AK-anchorage.description: Nestled between the Chugach Mountains and the Cook Inlet, Anchorage uniquely blends urban amenities and outdoor adventure. Abundant wildlife, breathtaking views and the tranquility of the Alaskan wilderness are only the tip of the iceberg when it comes to why Anchorage is a great place to live.

sections.location: Anchorage is a stunning city in south-central Alaska, accessible via Alaska Route 1. The nearest airport, Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport, is just southwest and offers international connections. Nearby major cities include: Fairbanks is 360 miles north, where you can chase the Northern Lights, visit the University of Alaska Museum or explore Chena Hot Springs. Juneau sits 570 miles southeast by air; spend time hiking Mendenhall Glacier, visiting Alaska State Museum and whale watching. Seward is just 125 miles south, home to the Kenai Fjords National Park, Alaska SeaLife Center and Resurrection Bay.
sections.economy: The last frontier doesn’t mean the buck stops when it comes to opportunities in Anchorage. The city is the economic hub of Alaska, and the largest industries include government, transportation and logistics and health care. Some of the largest employers in Anchorage include Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport, Providence Alaska Medical Center and Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson. The cost of living in Anchorage is higher than the national average due in large part to higher housing costs; however, the tax benefits of living in Anchorage help offset those costs. The median home value in Anchorage is $374,530, and the average household income is $136,834. Alaska residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Anchorage, AK? Because of the city’s lack of year-round sunlight, mental health and overall well-being are essential to the people here. There are several providers in the area, but some of the largest include Providence Alaska Medical Center, Alaska Native Medical Center and Alaska Regional Hospital. Each offers various medical services and specialties and is known for its comprehensive care.
sections.thingsToDo: Arts are abundant in Anchorage, and the city is filled with art galleries, museums and performance venues. The city is a vital hub for understanding Alaska Native heritage and is home to the Alaska Native Heritage Center, which showcases diverse Indigenous traditions through performances and exhibits, and the Anchorage Museum displays more than 600 rare or significant native artifacts and contemporary art.Kids love spending time observing wildlife at the Alaska Zoo and the Alaska Wildlife Conservation Center. The community comes together with many festivals throughout the year, including the NYO Games, a spring competition where Alaskan schoolchildren compete in traditional Native games. The state’s largest gathering of Indigenous peoples regularly takes place in Anchorage with the Alaska Federation of Natives Convention. The Charlotte Jensen Arts Market at Fur Rondy is Alaska’s oldest winter festival. Residents can also enjoy the beauty of the Alaska Botanical Garden year-round and engage in a thriving arts scene with numerous galleries and performance venues. What is something you can do for free in Anchorage, AK? There are plenty of scenic spots around the area. Drive down the Seward Highway — a 125-mile ride from Anchorage to the coastal Seward. What are some things to do in Anchorage, AK, with kids? Visit the Alaska Zoo, a home for orphaned and injured Arctic species and cold-climate animals from other zoos. Visitors can see brown bears, eagles, a polar bear, moose and more! The Discovery Center at the Anchorage Museum provides hands-on entertainment and an impressive planetarium. Where do locals shop in Anchorage, AK? Find a wide variety of local crafts at the Anchorage Market, the largest in the state. From May to September, find up to 300 vendors selling art, photography, jewelry and gorgeous treasures. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Anchorage, AK? The Alaska Center for the Performing Arts is the arts and entertainment hub in Anchorage. 5 Once-In-A-Lifetime Adventures That Are Easily Accessible From Anchorage
sections.outdoors: There are months when Anchorage averages nearly 20 hours a day of sunlight, so it makes sense that the city is a vital hub for Alaskan adventures, and there are hundreds of ways to explore the great outdoors. Residents can explore more than 11,000 acres over 223 municipal parks, including the Far North Bicentennial Park and Kincaid Park, which have trails for hiking, biking and wildlife viewing. The Tony Knowles Coastal Trail is a popular mainstay with stunning views of Cook Inlet and the surrounding mountains, while Chugach State Park offers vast wilderness areas for hiking and rafting. Surprisingly, water activities are abundant, with opportunities for salmon fishing in Ship Creek or whale watching in Turnagain Arm. Explore the glaciers via the Alyeska Tramway. Flightseeing tours offer aerial views of mountains and glaciers, and dog-sledding experiences are available year-round. Residents can enjoy the Northern Lights from September to April, a once-in-a-lifetime experience. Anchorage’s proximity to national parks like Denali, Katmai and Lake Clark further enhances its appeal to outdoor enthusiasts. 7 Outdoor Adventures You Can Only Have in Anchorage
sections.restaurants: Explore the last frontier through the local foodie scene. Known for its abundant seafood, restaurants range from casual eateries and food trucks to fine dining establishments. Local breweries and distilleries thrive, too. Favorite places to feast include Moose’s Tooth Pizza and Midnight Sun Brewing Co., and hidden gems like Middle Way Cafe and Hearth Artisan Pizza showcase the city’s innovative food culture. Even in the coldest weather, Wild Scoops draws crowds for their unique ice cream flavors, which highlights the city’s dedication to quality and creativity. What is one of the best places to eat in Anchorage, AK? Southside Bistro has two restaurants in one — an upscale dining room and a more casual bistro. What is the best coffee shop in Anchorage, AK? Kaladi Brothers Coffee started with one small cart downtown and now has more than a dozen locations in and around Anchorage. Where is the best happy hour in Anchorage, AK? After enjoying the arcade downstairs, venture up top to take in a crisp Alaska evening at Williwaw Social. Tasting the Last Frontier: How Alaska Became a Foodie Destination
sections.education: Local residents can take advantage of a variety of schools that cater to every academic preference and need. The Anchorage School District (ASD) is the primary public school provider in the area. Though the district’s individual schools offer varying performance, overall, ASD provides a smart start to families in the city. For private schooling options, residents can pick between nearly two dozen private institutions, including college preparatory schools and religiously affiliated schools. AL-auburn.description: A friendly, small-town atmosphere meets big-time college spirit in Auburn. This Alabama city offers residents a strong sense of community, passionate fans and beautiful green spaces to explore. Enjoy a relaxed pace of life with quality education and plenty of places to play.

sections.location: Auburn is a vibrant, college-spirited city tucked into eastern Alabama, about 55 miles east of Montgomery, where Interstate 85 runs through the rolling plains. The nearest major airport, Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, is 100 miles northeast. And Columbus Airport in Georgia is just 40 miles away, accommodating regional flights. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Montgomery, AL, is just 55 miles southwest, where visitors can visit the Alabama State Capitol and the Rosa Parks Museum or stroll the Riverfront Park. Atlanta, GA, sits 100 miles northeast, where you can dive into the Georgia Aquarium, sip at the World of Coca-Cola or cheer on the Braves at Truist Park. Birmingham, AL, is 110 miles northwest, where you can visit the Birmingham Museum of Art, explore the Civil Rights Institute, or cheer at Regions Field.
sections.economy: The dynamic local economy has experienced considerable growth in recent years and is significantly influenced by Auburn University. The three most prominent industries in Auburn are education, manufacturing and retail trade. The city’s largest employers include Auburn University, GE Aerospace and East Alabama Health. Additionally, the Auburn economy is supported by many businesses catering to the university and surrounding communities. The cost of living in Auburn is lower than the national average. The median home value in Auburn is $318,639, and the average household income is $95,318. Alabama residents pay a state income tax, and those living here can expect a 9.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Auburn, AL? Residents can access several top-notch health care providers and facilities, including those at East Alabama Medical Center (EAMC) and Auburn University Medical Center (AUMC). EAMC is located in nearby Opelika and offers a wide range of services and specialized care. It has also been recognized as one of America’s best-in-state hospitals. AUMC, on the other hand, provides health services to university students, faculty and staff, and the surrounding community and works in partnership with EAMC to offer a wide range of professional medical services.
sections.thingsToDo: Cheer on the home team! In addition to football, sports fans can cheer on the women’s and men’s basketball teams, gymnastics athletes, the baseball team and more. The largest park is Kiesel Park, which has a garden, pond, pavilion, 2.25-mile walking trail and the historic Nunn-Winston house. In addition to regular park hangouts at Kiesel, this is the spot that hosts some of the city’s most popular events, like the Fall Sundown Concert Series, The Auburn CityFest and an annual Easter egg hunt. Round up your friends for a night out. Some options include Bunkers, where groups can play golf on simulators or Piccolo 241, a tucked-away jazz and cocktail bar. If you’re feeling crafty, you can pour your own candles and choose from dozens of scents at Auburn Candle Company. If you’re interested in the arts, Telfair Peet Theatre hosts half a dozen performances throughout the year or enjoy live music with Sundilla Radio Hour. What is something you can do for free in Auburn, AL? Check out cool fossils and more behind-the-scenes collections when you join in on the free “First Wednesday” tour at the Museum of Natural History at Auburn University. What are some things to do in Auburn, AL, with kids? Visit the Auburn Public Library for story times, puppet shows and the “Movers and Shakers” program for little ones that incorporate books, music and movement. Or, head to the Auburn/Opelika Skate Park. On Saturday mornings, the skate park is reserved for remote control cars. Some other family-friendly attractions include Funtastic, which has play areas, arcade games and climbing walls and Bowlero, a bowling alley and arcade. Where do locals shop in Auburn, AL? Set in a beautiful vine-covered building, The Flower Store doesn’t just sell pretty arrangements, but it’s also a go-to destination for gifts, home decor and fine china. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Auburn, AL? The Gogue Performing Arts Center on Auburn’s campus is a state-of-the-art entertainment venue where you can catch musicals, dance shows, jazz performances and concerts. The center also has an amphitheater and lawn for outdoor shows.
sections.outdoors: Auburn is home to Chewacla State Park, a nearly 700-acre park where you can enjoy the soundscape of the cascading Chewacla Waterfall and cool down in its mists, plus go hiking and biking on the trails. The state park also features a large lake for swimming and kayaking. For a peaceful escape within the city, check out the Donald E. Davis Arboretum, a 13.5-acre botanic garden with walking trails and water features and annual events like an azalea festival, spring and fall plant sales and a photo contest, to name a few. A dozen parks are maintained by the city, including Dinius Park, which has plenty of shade, playground equipment and a couple of dog parks for the furry members of your family. Kreher Preserve & Nature Center is another community jewel that hosts nature walks, hikes, youth camps and more. The most beloved annual event is the family-friendly Leprechaun Hike each March when kids can tromp through the magical woods searching for leprechauns. Lake Martin, a 44,000-acre lake lined with 750 miles of wooded shoreline, is a popular place to spend a summer afternoon. Just 25 miles from Auburn, residents slip away to boat, fish, water ski and swim here.
sections.restaurants: Auburn’s dining scene ranges from the classics like Toomer’s Drugs, an institution since 1896 where you can get famous lemonade, to modern restaurants like Arrica, an Italian spot in a secret garden-like setting. One of the most unique dining experiences you can have here is at 1856, which is a teaching restaurant that has a chef and master sommelier in residence as well as a menu that sources produce and herbs from the rooftop garden that’s tended to by Auburn University’s Horticulture Program. Newcomers can discover their favorite cafe by taking a trip along the Auburn + Opelika Coffee Shop Trail. What is one of the best places to eat in Auburn, AL? If you love seafood, check out The Depot, a modern grilled gulf-coastal brasserie set in Auburn’s Historic Train Depot. This is your spot for the catch-of-the-day fish dishes, fresh oysters and starters like ceviche and blue crab dip. The restaurant was the first in the state to win a James Beard “Smart Catch Leader” award for sustainability. What is the best coffee shop in Auburn, AL? Make a coffee (or smoothie run) to Bitty & Beaus, a top-rated coffee shop in Auburn. The concept celebrates diversity and employs individuals with developmental and intellectual disabilities. Where is the best happy hour in Auburn, AL? Session Cocktails is known for its pre-Prohibition, classic drink list and moody vibes.
sections.education: Schools in Auburn offer a diverse educational landscape, with a strong emphasis on public and private school options. Auburn City Schools are generally well regarded and known for their academic excellence. ACS is Alabama’s fastest-growing school district and is consistently recognized for its academic achievements. For parents looking to take the private school path, the city has a selection of private schools, including religious-affiliated schools and independent institutions.Recognized for both its academic programs and passionate fan base, Auburn University plays a significant role in the city’s culture and economy. Auburn University has a strong reputation in various disciplines, including engineering and veterinary medicine. As a land-grant university, Auburn has a long-standing tradition of excellence in agricultural education. The Park at Auburn opened in 2008 on the university’s campus and is a research park that helps promote entrepreneurship. --> --> --> Browse the Top 100 Best Places to Live in the U.S. Auburn, AL 666 AL-hoover.description: Hoover’s Southern charm enchants residents. Coupled with affordability, championship golf courses, a low crime rate and well-maintained parks, Hoover has a family-friendly atmosphere. Residents take pride in their city, with a strong community spirit.

sections.location: Hoover, Alabama, is a thriving city nestled just south of Birmingham in Jefferson County, right along Interstate 459. Birmingham-Shuttlesworth International Airport is just 15 miles away, ready with flights for your travels. Ready to explore? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Birmingham is just 10 miles north, where you can visit the Birmingham Museum of Art, explore the Civil Rights Institute or cheer at Regions Field. Montgomery sits 85 miles south, offering the Alabama State Capitol, Rosa Parks Museum and scenic Riverfront Park. Huntsville is about 100 miles north, boasting the U.S. Space & Rocket Center, Botanical Garden and Monte Sano State Park.
sections.economy: The city has a diverse economy with a mix of industries, including health care, professional services and finance and insurance. Thanks to the city’s proximity to Birmingham, several companies have a significant presence here. Major employers include Regions Bank, Blue Cross & Blue Shield of Alabama and Hoover City Schools. Additionally, it's a major retail hub in the area, and Riverchase Galleria, a large shopping mall, is a significant economic driver. The cost of living in Hoover is much lower than the national average. The median home value in Hoover is $368,547, and the average household income is $145,629. Alabama residents pay a state income tax, and those living in Hoover can expect a 9.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Hoover, AL? Thanks to the 11 Jefferson and Shelby Counties hospitals, local residents have immediate access to world-class health care. Two notable providers are the UAB Hospital, the third-largest public hospital in the United States, and Children’s of Alabama, the country’s third-largest pediatric medical facility.
sections.thingsToDo: The city offers a range of activities, from sports to dining and shopping. Sports enthusiasts can enjoy the events at the Hoover Metropolitan Complex, including the SEC Baseball Tournament, which has been held here for 26 consecutive years. For some retail therapy, the Riverchase Galleria, one of the Southeast’s largest malls, provides a vast selection of shops and stores, while the Hoover Antique Gallery has unique finds. The city also plays host to several different community events, including the Celebrate Hoover Day festival, the Hoover Hayride and Family Night and the Christmas Tree Lighting Ceremony, ensuring there’s always something to enjoy. What is something you can do for free in Hoover, AL? Meander around Aldridge Gardens, a dreamy landscape dotted with gorgeous blooms like the Snowflake Hydrangea — the official flower of the city of Hoover. What are some things to do with kids in Hoover, AL? The Hoover Met Explore Playground and Splash Pad is a technicolor, whimsical, inclusive play area that allows all children to have fun and splash. Where do locals shop in Hoover, AL? Pick out some buried treasure at Hoover Antique Gallery, a multi-vendor retail spot in the heart of the city. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Hoover, AL? Most locals visit downtown Birmingham when looking for a show or concert. The Alabama Theatre is among the most popular haunts for residents itching for a night out.
sections.outdoors: The city is home to more than 30 public parks, a vital part of the city’s recreation system. With its nearly 350 acres of trails, rock formations and waterfalls, Moss Rock Preserve provides excellent hiking and climbing opportunities. Aldridge Gardens is a serene escape with 30 acres of landscaped gardens and walking trails. For those feeling adventurous, Black Creek Mountain Bike Park has 5 miles of trails designed for cycling, and the Cahaba River Blueway is the perfect place for canoeing, kayaking and fishing along the Cahaba River. The park system has several gems, like Bluff Park and Veterans Park, each offering trails and picnicking spots. Kids can play on the playgrounds or enjoy the fishing ponds at Veterans Park. Golfers can tee off at the renowned Robert Trent Jones Golf Trail.
sections.restaurants: Ranging from Southern staples at Farrelly’s Southern Bar & Kitchen to international delights from Silver Coin, the dining scene is delicious. Casual dining options with family-friendly menus are popular, including scrumptious sandwiches from Mr. P’s Deli and acclaimed burgers from the Whiskey Foxtrot Burger Dive. For upscale Italian, Tre Luna Bar & Kitchen can’t be missed. What is one of the best places to eat in Hoover, AL? Sweet potato casserole, stewed oxtails and mouthwatering classic Caribbean dishes await you at Jake’s Soulfood Cafe. What is the best coffee shop in Hoover, AL? Baba Java Coffee is a favorite spot among locals. Where is the best happy hour in Hoover, AL? Melt serves up happy hour specials from 4 to 6 every Monday to Friday!
sections.education: The city is known for its strong public school system, and Hoover City Schools serve the city. The district tends to have high graduation rates and a strong performance on standardized tests. Hoover City Schools are also known for their well-maintained facilities and impressive athletic programs. Families can choose between several well-rated public and private schools in the area. faqs.a: Meander around Aldridge Gardens, a dreamy landscape dotted with gorgeous blooms like the Snowflake Hydrangea — the official flower of the city of Hoover.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Hoover, AL?
faqs.a: The Hoover Met Explore Playground and Splash Pad is a technicolor, whimsical, inclusive play area that allows all children to have fun and splash.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Hoover, AL?
faqs.a: Pick out some buried treasure at Hoover Antique Gallery, a multi-vendor retail spot in the heart of the city.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Hoover, AL?
faqs.a: Most locals visit downtown Birmingham when looking for a show or concert. The Alabama Theatre is among the most popular haunts for residents itching for a night out.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Hoover, AL?
faqs.a: Sweet potato casserole, stewed oxtails and mouthwatering classic Caribbean dishes await you at Jake’s Soulfood Cafe.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Hoover, AL?
faqs.a: Baba Java Coffee is a favorite spot among locals.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Hoover, AL?
faqs.a: Melt serves up happy hour specials from 4 to 6 every Monday to Friday!

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Hoover, AL?
faqs.a: Although there aren’t any major colleges or universities located here, there are several higher education institutions in nearby Birmingham.

AL-huntsville.description: 3, 2, 1…Take off to an excellent life in Rocket City! Huntsville's top-rated schools, beautiful green spaces and iconic space center contribute to the city's high quality of life. Residents can appreciate the low cost of living and excellent job opportunities here but won't miss out on delicious dining options and amazing arts.

sections.location: Huntsville is a dynamic city in northern Alabama, about 100 miles north of Birmingham, accessible via Interstate 565 and U.S. Route 231. Huntsville International Airport is just 10 miles north, offering flights to Atlanta, Dallas and beyond. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Birmingham, AL, sits 100 miles south; visit the Birmingham Civil Rights Institute, explore Sloss Furnaces or catch a concert at the BJCC. Nashville, TN, is 110 miles north; explore the Country Music Hall of Fame, visit the Ryman Auditorium or stroll Broadway. Chattanooga, TN, is just 110 miles east; discover the Tennessee Aquarium, Ruby Falls and the Chattanooga Choo Choo.
sections.economy: The local economy is driven by its high-tech focus, which has resulted in a highly skilled workforce and a growing economy in the area. Primary industry sectors include aerospace/defense, information technology and advanced manufacturing. The U.S. Army/Redstone Arsenal is a major employer here, thanks to sites like NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center and FBI jobs. Other companies with a significant presence in the city include Boeing Company, Huntsville Hospital and Mazda Toyota Manufacturing. The cost of living in Huntsville is lower than the national average. The median home value in Huntsville is $302,276, and the average household income is $113,309. Huntsville residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Huntsville, AL? Huntsville’s largest health care providers are Huntsville Hospital Health System, Children’s of Alabama and Crestwood Medical Center. Huntsville Hospital is recognized as one of the best hospitals in Alabama.
sections.thingsToDo: Having fun in Huntsville is a given. See local artists at Lowe Mill ARTS & Entertainment and check out exhibits at the Huntsville Museum of Art. The downtown area features attractions like the historic Harrison Brothers Hardware store and the scenic Big Spring International Park, which hosts free concerts during the summer. The city also boasts a thriving performing arts scene, with the Huntsville Symphony Orchestra and Broadway shows at the Von Braun Center. Families can enjoy the Earlyworks Children’s History Museum, and history buffs can visit the U.S. Veterans Memorial Museum. Outdoor art is on display at the SPACES Sculpture Trail and the Secret Art Trail, and space fans can go to the Von Braun Astronomical Society’s planetarium. Shopping and entertainment options include Bridge Street Town Centre, and the city hosts various annual events and festivals. What is something you can do for free in Huntsville, AL? Big Spring International Park has koi fish, a pretty red bridge and cherry trees, all gifts from Japan. You can also take free yoga or workout classes in the park through the Healthy Huntsville program. What are some things to do with kids in Huntsville, AL? Spend the day looking for all 14 ducks hidden around Huntsville as part of the Lucky Duck Scavenger Hunt. Where do locals shop in Huntsville, AL? Huntsville is home to some great antique shopping. Railroad Station Antiques is a historic building with three floors of merchandise, including furniture, fine china, jewelry, rugs and more. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Huntsville, AL? Lowe Mill ARTS & Entertainment is the largest, privately-owned arts facility in the South, and the reworked building now houses 153 working studios for more than 200 artists, seven art galleries, four performance venues, a multi-use theater and restaurants.
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities are aplenty in Huntsville. The city boasts over 60 public parks, including family-friendly spots like Kids’ Space and Brahan Springs Park and natural preserves like Hays Nature Preserve, which features Alabama’s first certified Nature Explore Classroom. Hike at Monte Sano State Park, with its 20 miles of hiking trails and stunning overlooks, or stroll through the beautiful Huntsville Botanical Garden, home to diverse plant life and a seasonal butterfly house. For some more action, kayak or canoe on the Flint River, bike and walk at Indian Creek Greenway, or hike the trails through the Land Trust of North Alabama’s preserves. Golfers can enjoy the celebrated Hampton Cove Golf Course, part of the Robert Trent Jones Golf Trail. Burritt on the Mountain combines history with great city views.
sections.restaurants: Huntsville’s growing food scene is gaining popularity. The city boasts a collection of unique dining experiences, from the artisan chocolates at Pizzelle’s Confections to the diverse culinary offerings at Campus 805, which includes Earth and Stone Wood-Fired Pizza and Yellowhammer Brewing. Local favoritlives like The Moon Bakeshop and Cozy Cow provide delicious breakfast options, while Pane e Vino and Café 153 offer authentic Italian and casual lunch fare. The craft beer scene is a big deal, with the Downtown Huntsville Craft Beer Trail showing off local breweries. There’s a mixture of upscale dining, international cuisine and trendy eateries, ensuring there’s something to satisfy every palate. What is one of the best places to eat in Huntsville, AL? Cotton Row is a fine dining restaurant featuring American cuisine and strong Southern influences. The spot is located in a historic home in downtown and focuses on a seasonal menu with local ingredients. What is the best coffee shop in Huntsville, AL? Start the day strong with some quality coffee and a homemade bagel from Canadian Bakin. This locally owned bakery and cafe specializes in hand-rolled bagels and artisan breads. Where is the best happy hour in Huntsville, AL? Grab a beer at Green Bus Brewing, which always has something new on tap!
sections.education: Huntsville offers families various educational opportunities through its numerous public and private schools. Huntsville City Schools district represents public schools across the city and ranks solidly among other Alabama districts. For private education, the city is home to many private institutions, and roughly 20% of K-12 students in the city attend private schools, which is higher than the state average. faqs.a: Grab a beer at Green Bus Brewing, which always has something new on tap!

faqs.a: The city is home to several higher education institutions, including several HBCUs. Alabama A&M University and The University of Alabama - Huntsville are two of the largest schools.

AR-fayetteville.description: Nestled against the scenic Ozark Mountains, Fayetteville is a natural haven. The city's tight-knit (and welcoming!) community is characterized by outdoor activities, top-rated schools, acclaimed local cuisine and a strong sense of Razorback pride.

sections.location: Fayetteville is nestled in the Ozark Mountains of northwest Arkansas, about 190 miles northwest of Little Rock, linked by Interstate 49. The nearest airport, Northwest Arkansas National Airport, is 10 miles south, offering flights to Dallas, Chicago and beyond. When you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Tulsa, OK, is roughly 110 miles north, home to the Philbrook Museum of Art, the Gathering Place and the Woody Guthrie Center. Little Rock, AR, sits 190 miles southeast, boasting the Clinton Presidential Library, the Little Rock Central High School National Historic Site and the River Market District. Oklahoma City, OK, is just over 200 miles west, offering the Oklahoma City National Memorial, the Bricktown district and the Oklahoma City Zoo.
sections.economy: Educational services, retail trade and manufacturing are among the top industries in Fayetteville. The city is home to the University of Arkansas flagship campus, which employs many residents. Because the university is a major research institution, it’s also helped establish a culture of economic development within the city. Walmart is also a significant employer in the city because its headquarters are nearby in Bentonville, AR. For new workers or people trying to connect in the job force, the Northwest Arkansas Talent Network provides a warm welcome and introduction to career opportunities in the area. The cost of living in Fayetteville is lower than the national average. The median home value in Fayetteville is $318,319, and the average household income is $92,703. Arkansas residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9.75% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Fayetteville, AR? Washington Regional Medical Center is the city's most significant health care provider and has a high performance rating in over a dozen procedures and conditions. Residents can also take advantage of the nearby Mercy Hospital Northwest Arkansas.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents in Fayetteville are fans of all the city has to offer. The arts scene is characterized by art installations, galleries and live performances at venues like Walton Arts Center and TheatreSquared. Dickson Street is where it’s at for entertainment, with live music at George’s Majestic Lounge and several boutiques, galleries and restaurants. For bookworms, the Dickson Street Bookshop is universally loved by Fayetteville and book lovers beyond city limits. It was even named one of Buzzfeed’s “Bookstores That Will Literally Change Your Life.” Dickson Street, in particular, is the city’s shopping hub, and the sidewalks are dotted with boutiques, galleries, and bars and restaurants. Local shops and the Fayetteville Farmers Market offer unique finds. The city is also full of regional and national retailers. The Flying Possum has been a mainstay since 1976 and is the city’s original Birkenstock source. Fayetteville has a strong sense of community, hosting numerous annual events and festivals, including Bikes, Blues & BBQ and the Fayetteville Roots Festival. There is a mix of local and national retailers around town, giving residents options. For out-of-the-box experiences, ArtCourt and Boulders & Brews can’t be missed. What is something you can do for free in Fayetteville, AR? Dickson Street, in the entertainment district of Fayetteville, is a fun way to spend an afternoon without spending a dime. It has a lively atmosphere, several little shops, street art and perhaps even a free outdoor festival or concert. What are some things to do in Fayetteville, AR, with kids? Become a regular at the Fayetteville Public Library, which has a permanent art exhibit and rotating collections. The library has a calendar of events, from storytimes for kids to guided meditations and classes on of-the-moment topics like artificial intelligence for writers. Where do locals shop in Fayetteville, AR? The Fayetteville Farmers Market in the historic downtown square can’t be missed during the warmer months. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Fayetteville, AR? The Walton Arts Center is a much-loved performing arts center here. It has a reputation for hosting some of the region’s best concerts and theatrical performances and is the state’s largest and busiest performing arts venue.
sections.outdoors: Located in the Ozark Mountains, Fayetteville offers endless outdoor activities. With 37 city parks, 84 miles of trails and 15 natural areas, hiking, biking and walking are a breeze. The Razorback Greenway is a 36-mile trail connecting Fayetteville to neighboring cities, while Lake Fayetteville is great for fishing, kayaking and paddleboarding. Parks like Wilson, Lake Fayetteville and Sodalis Nature Preserve provide family-friendly spaces like playgrounds and nature trails. For residents seeking more adventurous recreational options, hiking trails at Devil’s Den State Park, the mountain biking skills course at Gregory Park and the 600-plus-acre Kessler Mountain Regional Park are must-visits. The Ozark Highlands Trail has challenging cycling routes, and national forests are perfect for exploring. Golfers can hit the greens at The Links at Springdale. Basically, if you like being outside, this city has you covered.
sections.restaurants: Fayetteville is for foodies, and the city’s ever-evolving dining scene focuses on fresh, local food. You’ll find farm-to-table spots like The Farmer’s Table Café and The Oven & Tap, and international eateries like Khana Indian Grill and Bordinos. Downtown is where a lot of the food action is, with food trucks, cozy cafes and upscale spots. The city’s commitment to local sourcing and innovation is evident in many establishments that highlight regional ingredients and reinvent traditional dishes. Plus, there’s a growing craft drink scene — breweries like Ozark Beer Company complement the city’s culinary offerings. Whether seeking Southern comforts or sampling something new, Fayetteville has a lot to offer. What is one of the best places to eat in Fayetteville, AR? Theo’s on Campbell Avenue is an upscale New American restaurant with a great cocktail bar and vibrant outdoor patio. Be sure to save room for dessert. What is the best coffee shop in Fayetteville, AR? Onyx Coffee Lab is a neighborhood shop with everything you want, from classic coffee favorites to light bites. You can even take latte art classes and a crash course coffee class, too! Where is the best happy hour in Fayetteville, AR? Play pinball and enjoy playful cocktails like rum-spiked Dole Whip at Pinpoint.
sections.education: More than 10,000 K-12 students are enrolled in public schools in the Fayetteville School District, which earns an overall quality ranking. The district includes Fayetteville Virtual Academy, Arkansas’ first (and only!) virtual district conversion charter school. Additionally, no other school district in the state has more Advanced Placement scholars. Residents can also take their pick of private and charter schools in the city, too. faqs.a: Theo’s on Campbell Avenue is an upscale New American restaurant with a great cocktail bar and vibrant outdoor patio. Be sure to save room for dessert.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Fayetteville, AR?
faqs.a: Onyx Coffee Lab is a neighborhood shop with everything you want, from classic coffee favorites to light bites. You can even take latte art classes and a crash course coffee class, too!

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Fayetteville, AR?
faqs.a: Play pinball and enjoy playful cocktails like rum-spiked Dole Whip at Pinpoint.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Fayetteville, AR?
faqs.a: Several colleges and universities are located here, including the University of Arkansas’ flagship campus.

AR-little-rock.description: Simply put, life in Little Rock rocks. Situated along the Arkansas River, Little Rock is home to scenic parts, an excellent arts scene and is a foodies paradise. A welcoming community and growing economy make Little Rock an ideal place to live and thrive.

sections.location: Little Rock is the state capital, nestled in central Arkansas along the Arkansas River, about 135 miles west of Memphis, accessible via Interstate 40. The nearest airport, Bill and Hillary Clinton National Airport, is just east and is a regional airport offering flights to several major cities, including Atlanta and Dallas. Nearby major cities include: Memphis, TN, is 135 miles east, buzzing with the excitement of Beale Street and home to attractions like Graceland and the National Civil Rights Museum. Fayetteville, AR, lies 190 miles northwest, home to the University of Arkansas, Walton Arts Center and Crystal Bridges Museum (in nearby Bentonville). Dallas, TX, sits roughly 320 miles southwest, boasting the Sixth Floor Museum, Dallas Museum of Art and AT&T Stadium.
sections.economy: From high-tech to health care, Little Rock’s job market is diverse and vibrant. Major industries include research, public services, finance and manufacturing. The city serves as the state’s economic hub, making it an appealing option for anyone looking to build their career in an environment that provides reasonable economic stability and job security. The largest employers in the area include the University of Arkansas for Medical Sciences (UAMS), the State of Arkansas and Dillard’s, whose corporate headquarters is in this city. If you’re new to town, request a relocation packet from the Little Rock Regional Chamber and upload your resume to their database, which will be shared with employers looking to hire new people. The cost of living in Little Rock is lower than the national average. The median home value in Little Rock is $224,033, and the average household income is $106,657. Arkansas residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.63% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Little Rock, AR? Little Rock residents have little to worry about when it comes to accessing quality health care. The city is home to numerous hospitals and providers, including Baptist Health Medical Center – Little Rock, UAMS Medical Center and Arkansas Children’s Hospital, which is nationally ranked.
sections.thingsToDo: From exploring the great outdoors to enjoying arts and culture, Little Rock residents will find plenty of things to do in their downtime. Former President Bill Clinton was born and raised in Arkansas, and the city is home to the William J. Clinton Presidential Library and Museum. This collection of 100,000 items includes gifts given to the Clintons from everyday people and heads of state with highlights like a handwritten letter from Mr. Fred Rogers and a NAFTA Drum representing the North American Free Trade Agreement. Little Rock Central High School is a stop on the Civil Rights Trail. Visitors can learn about the Little Rock Nine, a group of Black students who desegregated the school across the street from the still-open high school at the visitor center and museum, where rangers lead tours. At Esse Purse Museum, you can witness the progression of 20th-century American women through a unique lens: handbags of various decades, and the contents within them. What is something you can do for free in Little Rock, AR? One of the coolest things to do in Little Rock is also free! River Lights in the Rock includes three bridges that cross over the Arkansas River and connect Little Rock and North Little Rock. Every night, when the sun starts to go down, these bridges (Main Street Bridge, the Junction Bridge and the Clinton Presidential Park Bridge) put on a dazzling light show, with the colors and patterns changing every night. Snap some photos because the spectrum of colors reflects onto the river, which looks absolutely magical. What are some things to do in Little Rock, AR, with kids? Let your kids’ imaginations run wild at the Museum of Discovery, which has super fun exhibits like a 15-foot mirrored Kaleidoscope tunnel. This museum stands out for its inclusivity with sensory-friendly hours and a Sensory Cove, a peaceful space with calming colors, dimmable lights, tactile toys and a marble wall. The Little Rock Zoo is home to all kinds of animals, from alpacas to zebras! In addition to visiting the animals, families can watch penguin feedings, attend chats with zookeepers and sign up for clubs and classes.Little Rock also has several great splash pads for kids, but one of the neatest is at War Memorial Park because it has natural rocks built in. Where do locals shop in Little Rock, AR? Paper Arts Bookstore is an indie shop with a store in Pettaway Square and pop-ups throughout the state. The bookstore is also about to get rolling with a mobile Book Truck. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Little Rock, AR? The city loves live music, and plenty of spots are always rocking with tunes. One of the best places to catch some music is the River Market District, which is lined with bangers: Rev Room, Stickyz Rock’n’Roll Chicken Shack, Dugan’s Pub and Willy D’s Rock & Roll Piano Bar. 8 Fun Things To Do in Little Rock, ArkansasCentral Arkansas Arts Scene is Alive and Well9 Things You Didn’t Know About Little Rock, AR
sections.outdoors: Arkansas is called the Natural State for a reason! Outdoors enthusiasts have plenty to explore, from bike riding along the Arkansas River to getting out on a kayak or canoe at Big Dam Bridge and Two Rivers Park. Little Rock has over 60 city parks, and Riverfront Park is aligned with the Arkansas River, which makes for an excellent way to spend an afternoon wandering around. This 11-block-long greenspace links the city’s downtown to the river, crisscrossing with five bridges connecting downtown to Little Rock. The park is also home to more than 100 works of art, with its highest concentration within the Vogel Schwartz Sculpture Garden. Just west of the city is an adventurer’s paradise: Pinnacle Mountain State Park, which boasts 33 miles of trails, including 19 miles of challenging mountain bike trails. One of the state’s best parks is just over an hour away and is worth day trips: Petit Jean visitors can hike the scenic trails, feel the cool mist of a 95-foot tall waterfall, and descend down into a cave to discover ancient pictographs. For a peaceful day in nature close to home, visit the Witt Stephens Jr. Central Arkansas Nature Center along the Arkansas River. The grounds and trails include bird-feeding stations, and when you’re in the area, you can spot turtles, butterflies and migrating pelicans.
sections.restaurants: When you move to Little Rock, one of your biggest challenges will be to find your favorite local restaurant and watering hole — there are so many fantastic ones! The Root Cafe, which was featured on “Diners, Drive-ins, and Dives,” brings the community together for fun events like a pepper-eating contest with Dunbar Community Garden and serves dishes like biscuit sandwiches, burgers and brats and whipped pimento cheese slathered on bread. “The Root is the culinary version of a no-skip album,” one Redditor says. Community Bakery, which has been around since 1947, is a local institution and is the place for sweet treats and custom birthday cakes, as well as full menus for any meal of the day. Enjoy diverse cuisines like Honduran and Latin American dishes from El Sur Street Food. It’s worth finding Afrobites, a food truck serving authentic African food. Spirits lovers should definitely check out Rock Town Distillery for grain-to-glass tipples and specialty cocktails. What is one of the best places to eat in Little Rock, AR? Enjoy delectable Southern dishes and great views at Cypress Social, a top-rated and Redditor-recommended restaurant. Menu highlights include homemade cornbread with sun-dried tomato butter, fried catfish, chicken and andouille gumbo, corn fritters and collards … the list goes on. What is the best coffee shop in Little Rock, AR? Grab a cup of great gourmet coffee from Fidel & Co. Coffee Roasters. Where is the best happy hour in Little Rock, AR? Enjoy dollar-off beers and $6 cocktails at Brood & Barley in North Little Rock. Some of the most talked about sips are the sweet potato lemonade and “Siriusly Good Butterbeer.”
sections.education: Families here have the pick of the litter when it comes to finding a great educational fit. There is a large mix of public and private schools in the area, and the Little Rock School District serves a significant number of the city’s students. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: Little Rock residents have little to worry about when it comes to accessing quality health care. The city is home to numerous hospitals and providers, including Baptist Health Medical Center - Little Rock, UAMS Medical Center and Arkansas Children’s Hospital, which is nationally ranked.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: One of the coolest things to do in Little Rock is also free! River Lights in the Rock includes three bridges that cross over the Arkansas River and connect Little Rock and North Little Rock. Every night, when the sun starts to go down, these bridges (Main Street Bridge, the Junction Bridge and the Clinton Presidential Park Bridge) put on a dazzling light show, with the colors and patterns changing every night. Snap some photos because the spectrum of colors reflects onto the river, which looks absolutely magical.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Little Rock, AR, with kids?
faqs.a: Let your kids’ imaginations run wild at the Museum of Discovery, which has super fun exhibits like a 15-foot mirrored Kaleidoscope tunnel. This museum stands out for its inclusivity with sensory-friendly hours and a Sensory Cove, a peaceful space with calming colors, dimmable lights, tactile toys and a marble wall. The Little Rock Zoo is home to all kinds of animals, from alpacas to zebras! In addition to visiting the animals, families can watch penguin feedings, attend chats with zookeepers and sign up for clubs and classes.Little Rock also has several great splash pads for kids, but one of the neatest is at War Memorial Park because it has natural rocks built in.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: Paper Arts Bookstore is an indie shop with a store in Pettaway Square and pop-ups throughout the state. The bookstore is also about to get rolling with a mobile Book Truck.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: The city loves live music, and plenty of spots are always rocking with tunes. One of the best places to catch some music is the River Market District, which is lined with bangers: Rev Room, Stickyz Rock’n’Roll Chicken Shack, Dugan’s Pub and Willy D’s Rock & Roll Piano Bar.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: Enjoy delectable Southern dishes and great views at Cypress Social, a top-rated and Redditor-recommended restaurant. Menu highlights include homemade cornbread with sun-dried tomato butter, fried catfish, chicken and andouille gumbo, corn fritters and collards ... the list goes on.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: Grab a cup of great gourmet coffee from Fidel & Co. Coffee Roasters.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: Enjoy dollar-off beers and $6 cocktails at Brood & Barley in North Little Rock. Some of the most talked about sips are the sweet potato lemonade and “Siriusly Good Butterbeer.”

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Little Rock, AR?
faqs.a: The city has several public and private universities, including a satellite campus for the University of Arkansas and the University of Arkansas for Medical Sciences College of Medicine.

AZ-gilbert.description: Sun-soaked Gilbert is known for its location in the Sonoran Desert, well-planned neighborhoods and top-ranked schools, all of which contribute to its reputation as a suburban oasis. Residents can enjoy plenty of outdoor activities that come with living in Arizona, as well as a thriving economy and welcoming community.

sections.location: Gilbert is a sunny desert town in Maricopa County, southeast of Phoenix. U.S. Route 60 ties it to the Phoenix metro. Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport is around 20 miles away, a busy spot for flights near and far. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Phoenix is just 25 miles northwest, where you can explore the Desert Botanical Garden, visit the Heard Museum or catch a Suns game at Chase Field. Mesa sits 5 miles north, offering the Mesa Arts Center, Arizona Museum of Natural History and Usery Mountain Regional Park. Chandler is about 5 miles west, known for its lively downtown, the Chandler Museum and the quirky Ostrich Festival.
sections.economy: The economy is booming here. One of the reasons so many employers are picking Gilbert to launch or grow their operations is because of the strong talent pipeline: A high concentration of residents have graduate and professional degrees. There are many job opportunities in health care, technology and education fields, and Banner Health System, Mercy Gilbert Medical Center and the Town of Gilbert are all major employers. The cost of living in Gilbert is higher than the national average. The median home value in Gilbert is $496,010, and the average household income is $159,653. Arizona residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 2.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Gilbert, AZ? Mercy Gilbert Medical Center and Banner Gateway Medical Center are premier providers in the area and offer a wide range of medical services and specialties.
sections.thingsToDo: Get going in Gilbert! The city’s vibrant downtown, particularly the Heritage District, has more than 30 restaurants, live theater, a farmers market and a plentiful public art collection, including murals and integrated designs. History enthusiasts can explore the Gilbert Historical Museum housed in the original 1913 Gilbert Elementary School or pay their respects at the 9/11 Memorial. Beyond the urban attractions, the city stands out for its community-minded approach. Residents gather for an annual BARKtoberfest and at the International Sister Cities Garden in Gilbert Regional Park. The commitment to the arts is evident in galleries like Gallery 4 at HD South and public art displays around town. Visitors can also support local businesses by exploring unique shops like State Forty Eight, Studio 223 and Why I Love Where I Live. What is something you can do for free in Gilbert, AZ? Go stargazing at the Gilbert Rotary Centennial Observatory on Friday or Saturday nights. Seeing the galaxies through the high-powered telescope is free, but donations are appreciated. What are some things to do with kids in Gilbert, AZ? The area is packed with fun family activities, like exploring Gilbert Heritage District’s vibrant collection of shops, restaurants and seasonal events. The Gilbert Riparian Preserve at Water Ranch is another great spot for families who want to get outdoors. It’s packed with local wildlife, walking trails and beautiful scenery. If you want something more adventurous, consider the playgrounds, recreational facilities and mini amusement park at Freestone Park instead. Where do locals shop in Gilbert, AZ? Locals often frequent the bustling SanTan Village, a popular shopping center with a wide range of well-known retailers, boutique shops and dining options. Most people consider it a one-stop shop when they need to tackle errands, meet up with a friend or get in some good, old-fashioned retail therapy. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Gilbert, AZ? The Higley Center for the Performing Arts is the community’s local hub for live performances and cultural events. Throughout the year, you can find all manner of shows there, ranging from live music to plays and dance recitals.
sections.outdoors: Endless sunshine lures people outdoors in Gilbert. The city has an extensive network of trails — spanning 135 miles — perfect for hiking, biking and even horseback riding. Parks like Freestone District Park and Gilbert Regional Park are fun for the whole family, with lakes, playgrounds and sporting facilities. Looking for something more unique? Agritopia Farm is an urban farm that allows visitors to learn more about sustainable agriculture. The Riparian Reserve at Water Ranch is a haven for birdwatching, with more than 200 different species! Beyond the city limits, the Sonoran Desert beckons with mountain biking adventures and hiking trails in the San Tan Mountain Regional Park and Lost Dutchman State Park, offering stunning desert landscapes. Get your feet wet by kayaking, paddleboarding or tubing on the Lower Salt River.
sections.restaurants: The city has recently experienced a foodie boom, particularly within its Heritage District. From cozy spots like High Tide Seafood Bar & Grill and Joe’s Real BBQ to modern eateries like Liberty Market, there is something to satisfy every craving. Local agricultural roots shine at farm-to-table spots like Joe’s Farm Grill at Agritopia. What is one of the best places to eat in Gilbert, AZ? Enjoy tacos and margs at Joyride Taco House and order hatch green chili queso and corn fries with tomatillo ranch for the table. What is the best coffee shop in Gilbert, AZ? Mythical Coffee is a roaster and coffee shop serving coffee classics, seasonal sips and a light bites menu. Where is the best place for happy hour in Gilbert, AZ? Sip on $6 glasses of wine or $6 pitchers of beer until 5 p.m. at Postino East. Then, pair your vino with a bruschetta board or one loaded with charcuterie.
sections.education: Residents are served by both the Gilbert Public Schools and Higley Unified School District, which serve a combined nearly 50,000 students. Public schools in Gilbert rank higher than most in the state and have above-average math and reading proficiency scores. Private education is great here, too, and there are several different schools for families to choose from. AZ-tempe.description: Tempe’s year-round sunshine and location in the heart of the Sonoran Desert give this Arizona city a sunny disposition. Top-notch universities, a robust job market and a lively downtown district create an oasis of opportunity, culture and outdoor adventures.

sections.location: Tempe is in the heart of the Phoenix, AZ, metro area, just 5 miles east of downtown, tied together by the bustling arteries of Interstate 10 and U.S. Route 60. Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport is a mere 5 miles west, a major hub buzzing with flights to everywhere from Los Angeles to New York. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Phoenix is just 5 miles west, where you can explore the Desert Botanical Garden, visit the Heard Museum or catch a Suns game at Chase Field. Mesa sits 5 miles east, offering the Mesa Arts Center, Arizona Museum of Natural History and Usery Mountain Regional Park. Chandler is about 10 miles south, known for its lively downtown, the Chandler Museum and the Ostrich Festival.
sections.economy: A growing economy and employment opportunities in everything from finance and health care to technology and education mean residents can easily make a living in this charming college town. The largest industries contributing to the economy are technology, education and advanced manufacturing, and some of the area’s largest employers are Arizona State University, State Farm Insurance and Wells Fargo. Who are the major health care providers in Tempe, AZ? Honor Health Tempe Medical Center is a full-service acute care facility with a Level III Trauma Center and comprehensive medical services.
sections.thingsToDo: There are a ton of things to do in Tempe. The city’s rich arts scene features the ASU Art Museum’s contemporary exhibits, captivating performances at ASU Gammage and the lively Tempe Festival of the Arts. And, if you're feeling gutsy, the Tempe Center for the Arts is known for having one of the best open mics in the Southwest and hosts a variety of performances. Visitors can also explore the city’s history at the Tempe History Museum or embark on self-guided walking tours to discover public art and historical landmarks. Entertainment options abound, from catching a baseball game during spring training at Tempe Diablo Stadium to enjoying live comedy at ImprovMANIA. Catch an indie or mainstream film at Tempe Pollack Cinema, or enjoy one of their film festivals held throughout the year. Participate in some retail therapy at the unique boutiques of Mill Avenue District or find bargains at Arizona Mills and Tempe Marketplace. The city's event calendar is consistently full, featuring festivals like 2nd Sundays on Mill and the holiday boat parade on Town Lake, ensuring there’s always something exciting happening. What is something you can do for free in Tempe, AZ? Tempe is a college town, and since ASU is a state university, many of its campus amenities are free to the public. The ASU Art Museum, Ceramic Research Center, Lunar Exploration Museum & NASA’s Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Camera Operations Center Visitor Gallery, Mars Space Flight Facility and galleries for students in design, art and more are all great places to explore. What are some things to do in Tempe, AZ, with kids? Arizona SeaLife Aquarium and Legoland are right next to each other, and each sells a combined ticket that gets you into both attractions. Both will let your kids’ imaginations run wild while keeping the adults entertained. Where do locals shop in Tempe, AZ? There are a few popular shopping haunts here. Mill Avenue District is a popular combination shopping and dining destination with vintage stores and boutique shops. At the same time, Arizona Mills is more geared toward those looking for outlets and discount stores. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Tempe, AZ? For an intimate experience, catch a show at the Marquee Theatre in Tempe’s business and entertainment district. Local music enthusiasts often praise its spacious layout and intimate atmosphere.
sections.outdoors: Arizona has amazing activities, and Tempe is no exception. The city boasts numerous parks, including Tempe Beach Park and Kiwanis Park, which offer spaces for relaxation, picnics and various recreational activities such as kayaking, paddle boarding and swimming. For those seeking more adventurous pursuits, nearby mountains provide opportunities for hiking and biking, with popular spots like “A” Mountain (Hayden Butte), Papago Park Butte Loop and Evelyn Hallman Park offering scenic views and challenging terrain. Tempe Town Lake is perfect for paddle boarding and boating, and golfers can play at Ken McDonald Golf Course. The Desert Botanical Garden and nearby areas let you explore the desert’s beauty. Air Apparent offers a public art installation best viewed at sunrise or sunset. The city makes it easy to enjoy the Arizona sunshine, no matter what you like to do outdoors.
sections.restaurants: Tempe’s dining scene sure is tempting. The craft beer scene is big, with local favorites like Four Peaks and Pedal Haus. It also has a strong presence of international cuisine, with restaurants serving authentic Indian, Ethiopian, Vietnamese and Mexican dishes. For those seeking trendy spots and healthy options, establishments like The Original ChopShop and Morning Squeeze provide fresh juices, protein bowls and brunch favorites. There are many casual spots, like Casey Moore’s Oyster House and Culinary Dropout, which have small plates and cocktails. For sweets, check out Slickables and Sweet Republic. Coffee lovers can find expertly brewed specialty drinks at Cartel Coffee Lab and Press Coffee. Whether you want Southwestern food, something new, or a local beer, Tempe’s got you covered. What is one of the best places to eat in Tempe, AZ? The ambiance at Pier 54 is perfect for a fun night out. You’re outside on the water, and the sunset views are breathtaking. The menu is great for sharing, and they have an excellent dinner-for-two special that includes a complimentary bottle of wine. Or, take a walk on the wild side at Tempe's newest hotspot, Filthy Animal What is the best coffee shop in Tempe, AZ? The folks at Infusion Coffee and Tea Crafters are serious about their coffee. They have an onsite coffee lab, a barista academy, and more than 70 coffee bean and tea varieties. Come hungry and try the Boozy Sweet or Savory Waffle. Where is the best place for happy hour in Tempe, AZ? Social Hall has a “regular” happy hour from 4 to 6 p.m. during the work week and then offers a “reverse” happy hour Thursday through Saturday from 10 p.m. to midnight. Wash down bar favorites like wings and nachos with a spiked Capri-Sun cocktail.
sections.education: Tempe is home to Arizona State University, a public institution with a renowned research school. The university attracts around 75,000 students with over 350 programs, ranging from finance and psychology to biology and engineering. Because the school is also known for its Division I athletics programs, you can expect plenty of football, baseball, basketball, golf and soccer fans proudly wearing maroon and gold. When getting a smart start, local residents can take advantage of the Tempe School District and Tempe Union High School District. There are also plenty of private schools to pick from. CA-clovis.description: Clovis is a warm, welcoming community with highly rated schools, exceptional quality of life and a relatively low cost of living in the San Joaquin Valley. With a sunny climate and close proximity to natural wonders, Clovis residents enjoy a relaxed California lifestyle.

sections.location: Clovis is a charming city in central California, just east of Fresno along State Route 168. The nearest airport, Fresno Yosemite International Airport, is about 10 miles away. The city sits on flat farmland, but the Sierra Nevada Mountains loom nearby for outdoor escapes. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Fresno is just 10 miles west, where you can visit the Fresno Art Museum, explore Underground Gardens, or cheer at Chukchansi Park. Visalia sits 40 miles south, offering the Fox Theatre, ImagineU Children’s Museum, and scenic Mooney Grove Park. Modesto is about 90 miles northwest, tempting with a historic downtown, McHenry Mansion, and lovely Graceada Park.
sections.economy: The city has shown steady growth in recent years, benefiting from its proximity to Fresno. The local economy comprises several industries, including agriculture, education and retail trade. The Clovis Unified School District is one of the largest employers in the city. The cost of living in Clovis is higher than the national average. The median home value in Clovis is $448,689, and the average household income is $134,823. California residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.98% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Clovis, CA? The area has several health care providers and facilities for residents to choose from. Clovis Community Medical Center is a 24-hour emergency medical center providing many services. Other providers include Kaiser Permanente Clovis Medical Offices and other practitioners in Fresno.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents can appreciate all that comes with living in a community-focused area. The city’s charming downtown is a hub for social gatherings and has several quaint boutiques and restaurants. Annual events like the Clovis Rodeo and Clovis Fest, with its hot air balloon display, provide opportunities for community celebration. Explore the city’s history at the Dan Rouit Flat Track Museum and the Forestiere Underground Gardens in nearby Fresno, or take a leisurely stroll through the Clovis Botanical Garden or the Shinzen Japanese Garden. The Clovis Antique Mall and A Book Barn offer unique shopping experiences, while Blackbeard’s Family Entertainment Center and Wild Water Adventure Park provide fun for all ages. What is something you can do for free in Clovis, CA? There are plenty of free things to do in Clovis, but a favorite is to stroll around Old Town Clovis and surround yourself with a picturesque (and very Instagrammable) setting. What are some things to do with kids in Clovis, CA? Get some energy out at The Jungle Party House, located inside the Sierra Vista Mall, which features a jungle gym, a bounce house, a pool table, video games and arts and crafts. Where do locals shop in Clovis, CA? Clovis Book Barn, the largest used bookstore in the San Joaquin Valley, has multiple floors of titles — so you’d better set aside some time to browse. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Clovis, CA? Most residents catch live music at local restaurants in and around town, like country and rock performances at Old Town Saloon. For plays, many locals make a short jaunt to nearby Fresno and places like Roger Rocka’s Dinner Theater.
sections.outdoors: Clovis is California cool, providing residents with a wealth of outdoor activities, capitalizing on its proximity to the Sierra Nevada mountains and its own green spaces. Known as the “Gateway to the Sierra,” the city offers easy access to skiing and snowboarding during winter, as well as hiking, camping and fishing in the Sierra National Forest during warmer months. Residents can also enjoy local outdoor attractions such as the Clovis Botanical Garden, the Dry Creek Trail and Millerton Lake State Recreation Area, which offers boating and fishing. The city’s location allows for convenient day trips to iconic national parks like Yosemite, Kings Canyon and Sequoia, as well as large local parks like Woodward Park, enhancing the outdoor recreational opportunities for residents.
sections.restaurants: The local and diverse dining scene blends traditional flavors and contemporary culinary trends. Thanks to initiatives from the Clovis Culinary Center, there is a lot of culinary talent. Residents benefit and can enjoy a wide selection, from trendy brewpubs and barbecue joints to international flavors. Clovis commits to quality and local food sourcing, offering plenty of farm-to-table options. What is one of the best places to eat in Clovis, CA? Located in the heart of Old Town Clovis, the House of Juju serves the best bar food. (Seriously, try one of their signature burgers.) What is the best coffee shop in Clovis, CA? The Rodeo Coffee Shop has been locally owned and operated for over 50 years. This no-frills spot is a great way to start your day. Where is the best place for happy hour in Clovis, CA? On the Edge in Old Town Clovis has an impressive menu of coffee shop favorites and local beer and wine, plus a spacious patio and even live music.
sections.education: Families can take comfort in the fact that the area is known for its strong educational system, which the Clovis Unified School District primarily manages. The district has a large number of schools that are rated above average and has some of the best schools in the state. There are also several highly-rated private schools in both Clovis and nearby Fresno. CA-redding.description: Redding is a natural fit for residents who value a strong sense of community, a relaxed lifestyle and endless outdoor options. Nearby Shasta Lake and Lassen Volcanic National Park appeal to nature lovers and provide stunning scenic views. Redding has a robust economy and plenty of safe neighborhoods to choose from.

sections.location: Redding is a charming city in Northern California, approximately 160 miles north of Sacramento, easily accessible via Interstate 5 and State Route 273. Redding Municipal Airport is just 9 miles from downtown, offering convenient flights via United Express and Alaska Airlines. Ready to head out? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Sacramento sits 160 miles south, home to the state capital’s historic sites, the Sacramento Zoo and the Crocker Art Museum. San Francisco is 250 miles south, boasting the Golden Gate Bridge, Alcatraz and the vibrant neighborhoods of the City by the Bay. Portland, OR, is roughly 300 miles north, where you can enjoy the Rose City’s arts scene, craft breweries and Powell’s City of Books.
sections.economy: Retail trade, manufacturing, and government and public administration are among the largest industries in Redding. The city is also a regional health care hub; some of the largest employers are Dignity Health/Common Spirit and Mercy Medical Center Redding. The region is known as a launchpad for entrepreneurs looking to start a business or young professionals ready to start their careers. The cost of living in Redding is higher than the national average. The median home value in Redding is $362,940, and the average household income is $98,710. California residents pay a state income tax, and people living here can expect a 7.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Redding, CA? Redding residents are in good health, thanks to several area health care providers. Mercy Medical Center Redding, Shasta Community Health Center and the Patients’ Hospital of Redding provide top-notch care to patients in the area. 5 Reasons Remote Workers Thrive in Redding, CAYoung Professionals are Finding a Home in Shasta County, CA10 Reasons You Should Launch Your Business in Shasta County, CA
sections.thingsToDo: Rev up for lots of fun in Redding. The city mixes art with adventure and community spirit, packing a major punch. The Redding Cultural District is packed with more than 70 public art sites and galleries, including those at Old City Hall and Turtle Bay Exploration Park. The Cascade Theatre and Civic Auditorium host lively performances. The community comes together with events like First Fridays and annual festivals like Kool April Nites and the Freedom Festival. Historical sites like Shasta State Park, a preserved Gold Rush town, and Lake Shasta Caverns appeal to history buffs. Beyond the arts, the city offers leisurely pursuits from indie bookstores like Shop Around the Corner Books to live music and food trucks at The Park. Community engagement thrives via programs like Healthy Shasta and exploring downtown murals via Redding Bikeshare. For some family-friendly fun, Turtle Bay Museum and Fantasy Fountain provide fun for all ages. What is something you can do for free in Redding, CA? Take a walk or ride a bike across the striking Sundial Bridge above the Sacramento River. It’s a popular spot for birdwatching, especially in July, when thousands of swallows can be seen. What are some things to do in Redding, CA, with kids? Spend summer days at WaterWorks Park, where you can ride dragon-themed slides, bob in a wave pool and soak up the sun in the lazy river. Where do locals shop in Redding, CA? Channel your inner Kathleen Kelly from You’ve Got Mail and spend the afternoon browsing the stacks at Shop Around the Corner Books. The bookstore has an assortment of books, puzzles and games, and apparel. It even serves coffee and tea. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Redding, CA? There’s no shortage of arts here. The Redding Civic Auditorium hosts a variety of performing arts events, including comedy shows and touring musicians. The Cascade Theatre is a multiuse performing arts venue that hosts musicals, plays and ballets. The Arts Are Driving Redding, CA’s Economy Forward10 Reasons to Live in Redding, CAThe Cost of Living Diaries: Redding, CA
sections.outdoors: Redding is a sun-soaked adventure spot. Natural wonders like Whiskeytown National Recreation Area and Castle Crags State Park offer hiking and biking thrills, while the Sundial Bridge and Sacramento River Trail provide more scenic routes. Parks like Caldwell and Lema Ranch offer spaces for picnics, walks and relaxation. Nearly 60 parks and 40 miles of trails cater to both casual and avid adventurers. The McConnell Arboretum & Botanical Gardens and Turtle Bay Exploration Park offer leisurely escapes, while McCloud Falls and Whiskeytown Lake beckon for water adventures. With its extensive network of trails, including those at Mule Ridge and Swasey Recreation Center, and its proximity to natural wonders like Lassen Volcanic National Park, the city truly lives up to its reputation as a “basecamp for adventure.”
sections.restaurants: Residents are satisfied with the city’s evolving culinary scene, a fusion of American classics and international tastes. Upscale dining at View 202 offers modern American cuisine, and Raw is a popular sushi spot. Classic steakhouses like Jack’s Grill and contemporary American restaurants like C.R. Gibbs American Grille cater to more traditional tastes. Food truck hubs pump up the vibe with live music and delicious food. The culinary scene also embraces craft beverages and beers flowing from Woody’s Brewing Co. and Final Draft Brewing Co. International flavors are well-represented, with options ranging from Thai and Japanese to Chinese cuisine. Health-conscious diners find eats at spots like Wilda’s Grill and Roots Juice Bar. Top things off with a sweet treat from Taste & See Ice Creamery. What is one of the best places to eat in Redding, CA? Meet your friends at The Park, a food truck hub and popular yard games and live music spot. What is the best coffee shop in Redding, CA? Feast Coffee & Culture is a familiar favorite, lining the shelves of some of the city’s most beloved coffee shops. They operate Evergreen by Feast Coffee, a shop serving their delicious coffee blends and a menu of seasonal bites and coffee shop staples. Where is the best happy hour in Redding, CA? Happy hour starts every day at 3:30 p.m. at Market Street Blade & Barrel, a family-owned spot. Plenty of wine, beer and cocktails on the happy hour menu can be enjoyed with small bite specials, too!
sections.education: Residents can enjoy quality schools in their community. The city is a “school of choice” county, meaning families can choose any school that suits their children’s needs, regardless of where they live. The area is served by the Shasta Union High School District, which includes several well-regarded high schools, and the Shasta County Office of Education oversees the elementary and middle schools. Residents can also choose from a variety of private and charter schools.
