CO-aurora.description: With easy access to Denver and the Rocky Mountains, Aurora residents can enjoy an abundance of outdoor activities, excellent schools and a diverse population. The city has plenty of job opportunities and a thriving economy.

sections.location: Aurora is a diverse city just east of Denver, along Interstate 225 and Interstate 70, and is part of the Denver metro area. Denver International Airport, a central flight hub, is about 20 miles northeast. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Denver is just 10 miles west, where you can visit the Denver Art Museum or catch a concert at Red Rocks Amphitheatre. Colorado Springs sits 70 miles south, famous for the Garden of the Gods and the U.S. Air Force Academy. Fort Collins is about 70 miles north, home to Colorado State University and the Fort Collins Museum of Discovery.
sections.economy: Located within the Denver metropolitan area, Aurora has a dynamic economy and is the third-largest city in the state. Aerospace and defense, retail and health care are significant industries here. Buckley Air Force Base, the University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus and Children’s Hospital Colorado are all significant employers. Both UCHealth and Western Union have local headquarters. The cost of living in Aurora is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Aurora is $493,736, and the average household income is $116,542. Colorado residents pay a state income tax, and local residents expect an 8.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Aurora, CO? Due to the city’s proximity to Denver, Aurora residents have access to many highly ranked health care providers within city limits and nearby Denver. Some of the biggest systems include the UCHealth University of Colorado Hospital, Children’s Hospital Colorado and Intermountain Health Saint Joseph Hospital. Why Young Professionals Love Living in Adams County, COLooking to Relocate? 5 Reasons to Do Business in Adams CountyCheers to Thriving Entrepreneurs in Adams County, CO
sections.thingsToDo: Aurora, CO, has a little bit of everything — artsy, outdoorsy and family-friendly! The Aurora Fox Arts Center, numerous art galleries and plentiful public art installations all contribute to the city’s artistic vibe. For those wanting to get in touch with nature, there are plenty of places to stretch your legs and ample amounts of sunshine all year long. The Cherry Creek Reservoir is a family-friendly spot perfect for hiking, horseback riding and all sorts of water sports. For family-friendly fun, take a staycation to the Gaylord Rockies Resort’s indoor waterpark and seasonal events. Explore the city’s museums and attend any one of the community festivals. Looking to do a little retail therapy? Southlands Mall and the Stanley Marketplace are popular spots. History buffs can dig into the past at the Colorado Freedom Memorial and learn more about the city’s history. No matter what you’re into, this city has you covered. What is something you can do for free in Aurora, CO? Get a feel for life on the prairie in the 1800s at the Plains Conservation Center, which has a tipi camp and homestead village. Bring binoculars because you may spot bald eagles. Follow it up with a self-guided audio tour (and lots of selfies, too) of the Colfax Canvas Murals. What are some things to do in Aurora, CO, with kids? Drop in for storytime at Tattered Cover inside the famed Stanley Marketplace. Afterward, fuel up with an ice cream sandwich from Sweet Cow Ice Cream. Where do locals shop in Aurora, CO? Southlands is an outdoor mall with restaurants and shops, and it also hosts fun seasonal events like ice skating and farmers markets. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Aurora, CO? The Aurora Fox Arts Center is the premiere spot to catch a show here. The theater houses the Aurora Symphony Orchestra and Aurora Fox Theatre Company and offers residents professional, Broadway-quality performances. This Marketplace Is a Must-Visit in Colorado5 Reasons to Move to Adams County, COSoak in the Vibrant Arts Scene in Adams County, CO
sections.outdoors: Explore the outdoors in Aurora. With more than 99 parks and 100-plus miles of trails, getting in touch with nature is easy; try hiking, biking and wildlife spotting. Cherry Creek State Park and Reservoir stand out for watersports and activities like paddleboarding and fishing. Or, if you would prefer to stick to the dry land, set up camp in one of the dog-friendly zones. The Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge is 15,000 acres of fun, including observing bison and pronghorn. The Jewell Wetlands provide a peaceful escape and a butterfly garden. Beyond the parks, the city's proximity to the Rocky Mountains opens up opportunities for skiing, snowboarding and hiking, ensuring year-round fun. Whether you’re taking a relaxing walk in the park, spending a day on the water or planning a mountain escape, residents can have it all. Enjoy Year-Round Outdoor Activities in Adams County, COPark it Here in Adams County, CO
sections.restaurants: Aurora is a food lover’s dream, especially for residents who are into trying new flavors. With more than 400 independently operated ethnic restaurants, including 250 locally owned eateries, the city offers dishes from six continents, making it a prime destination for adventurous eaters. The Stanley Marketplace stands out, featuring diverse options from New York-style bagels to fresh sushi and Latin American dishes. But it isn’t just the food. Aurora has a growing craft brewery scene, with more than 15 microbreweries and several wineries and cideries. Lady Justice Brewing Company offers unique brews and supports community nonprofits so residents can feel good about going to happy hour. Farmers markets and international markets mean fresh produce and spices are readily available, too. What is one of the best places to eat in Aurora, CO? For a memorable meal, make a dinner reservation at award-winning Annette, a sweet restaurant built around a wood-fired grill that roasts fish and chicken to perfection. What is the best coffee shop in Aurora, CO? Legends Coffee was created to cultivate community and connect neighbors. They are known for their delicious coffee concoctions and sweet treats, including gluten-free and vegan options. Where is the best happy hour in Aurora, CO? Traveling Mercies is an oyster and cocktail bar that hosts a daily happy hour and offers an all-night Tuesday happy hour with $3 oysters. This Marketplace Is a Must-Visit in ColoradoLatin Savor in Adams County, CO
sections.education: Ask any of the locals if they think their hometown is a family-friendly place to live, and chances are they will reply with a resounding yes. The city boasts a variety of schools, and the Aurora Joint School District No. 28 is the primary public school district in town. The district offers advanced placement, international baccalaureate and gifted programs. The city also has more than two dozen private schools, including Crescent View Academy, one of the state’s best private schools. CO-colorado-springs.description: Colorado Springs enchants residents with its gorgeous landscapes and lively community. Nestled against the Rocky Mountains, Colorado Springs has abundant outdoor activities, a thriving arts scene and a strong economy, all while maintaining a low cost of living.

sections.location: Colorado Springs is a stunning city at the base of Pikes Peak in central Colorado, right along Interstate 25. It’s known for its natural beauty and military presence. Colorado Springs Airport is right in the city, making travel convenient. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Denver is just 70 miles north, where you can visit the Denver Botanic Gardens or the Molly Brown House Museum. Pueblo sits 45 miles south, offering the Historic Arkansas Riverwalk and the Sangre de Cristo Arts Center. Boulder is about 95 miles northwest, home to the University of Colorado Boulder and the Pearl Street Mall.
sections.economy: The city is known for its diverse economy, and some of the largest industries in the area are defense and aerospace, technology and cybersecurity, and tourism and hospitality. For example, the United States Department of Defense is one of the largest employers in the city due to the large number of military and air force bases there. The tech industry has expanded rapidly, too, drawing in companies like Hewlett-Packard Enterprise and Oracle. Also, The Broadmoor, a luxury resort, is a key employer in the city and brings in thousands of tourists every year. The cost of living in Colorado Springs is higher than the national average. Still, it’s remarkably more affordable to put down roots here than in Denver, which makes it an attractive option for growing families and young professionals willing to live outside the state capital. The median home value in Colorado Springs is $467,431, and the average household income is $117,271. Colorado residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.2% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Colorado Springs, CO? Colorado Springs is the largest city in Southern Colorado, and several major providers serve the city, including UC Health, which operates UCHealth Memorial Hospital, a high-performing facility in nearly a dozen procedures and conditions.
sections.thingsToDo: Colorado Springs delivers a vibrant mix: culture, outdoors and a strong community vibe. Downtown buzzes with dining, shopping and entertainment options. History and arts shine at the Colorado Springs Pioneers Museum and the Western Museum of Mining & Industry. The Ford Amphitheater hosts top-tier concerts set against a mountain view, making for a delightful night out. Old Colorado City offers shopping with its numerous boutiques and galleries, and the Garden of the Gods Trading Post features Native American crafts. The Cheyenne Mountain Zoo and Rock Ledge Ranch are kid-approved for family fun activities in the area. Looking for leisure? Enjoy spa treatments and golfing at the Broadmoor, the perfect place for a staycation. Dog-friendly spots like Red Rock Canyon Open Space and several local breweries are fun for furry friends. What is something you can do for free in Colorado Springs, CO? The picturesque Garden of the Gods would be a solid contender if Colorado had an official state postcard. Visitors can take in the beauty of the region’s iconic red rock formations against the backdrop of Pikes Peak. This city park offers free admission and provides various hiking trails, rock climbing opportunities and breathtaking views, making it a hot spot for outdoor enthusiasts and photographers. What are some things to do with kids in Colorado Springs, CO? Get an up-close view of a collection of waterfalls at The Broadmoor Seven Falls — afterward, snack on “bubble waffle” ice cream sundaes sold from a food truck. Where do locals shop in Colorado Springs, CO? Locals often shop at Old Colorado City, a popular historic district boasting an eclectic mix of boutiques, galleries and specialty shops. You can find all manner of locally owned businesses selling everything from unique gifts and handmade crafts to clothing and home goods. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Colorado Springs, CO? The Pikes Peak Center for the Performing Arts is a premier live music and performing arts venue. Located downtown, the center hosts many events, including concerts, Broadway shows and ballet performances. Go There: A Weekend Getaway to Colorado Springs
sections.outdoors: Colorado Springs is built for outdoor fun. Garden of the Gods has red rock formations for hiking, horseback riding and exploring. Pikes Peak offers a 13-mile hike or bike ride to the top — a more than 14,000-foot summit for those seeking a challenge! There are tons of trails, more than 350 miles in fact, ranging from relaxed walks to more challenging hikes like the Seven Bridges Trail and Cheyenne Mountain State Park. Adrenaline junkies can enjoy whitewater rafting or soar over the Royal Gorge on one of the nation’s highest zip lines. Families can explore Acacia Park’s cool playgrounds, and nature lovers can get lost in the mountains and forests. Residents never run out of outdoor activities, whether biking, hiking, climbing or just enjoying the views.
sections.restaurants: Colorado Springs has a food scene that just keeps growing, mixing local flavors and global influences and a solid craft drink scene. Restaurants like Four by Brother Luck and The Rabbit Hole focus on farm-to-table fare, using fresh, local ingredients. Breweries like Bristol Brewing Company and Goat Patch Brewing Co. are part of the local beer scene and are beloved staples. Residents can enjoy all kinds of food here: German from Edelweiss, Indian at Mirch Masala and sushi from Al Sushi & Grill. Coffee shops like Loyal Coffee and Switchback Coffee Roasters are mainstays. And you’ll never go wrong with a burger from Skirted Heifer or a wood-fired slice from Pizzeria Rustica. The Broadmoor has upscale dining with mountain views for a fancy night out. The city is known for local specialties like green chiles, which are creatively incorporated at spots like Bingo Burger. What is one of the best places to eat in Colorado Springs, CO? Dogs are welcome at tons of restaurants and brewery patios around the city. But there’s even a hybrid restaurant/dog park called Pub Dog, where dogs can hang out with you indoors as you eat (and they even have their own 3,000-square-foot off-leash area to romp and play). What is the best coffee shop in Colorado Springs, CO? Building Three Coffee works closely with its coffee farmers, resulting in a great product. There are several different blends on tap, each with unique tasting notes like milk chocolate, berries and citrus. Where is the best happy hour in Colorado Springs, CO? Head to the Principal’s Office for “Detention Hour” deals, including $7 signature cocktails like the “Teacher’s Pet” — a gin and strawberry drink — and other cocktails that earn an A for creativity. How to Eat & Drink Like a Local in Colorado Springs
sections.education: There are several public school districts locally, including Cheyenne Mountain School District No. 12, the best district in the state. Public Schools in Colorado Springs offer a variety of programs, including traditional, magnet and alternative schools, and some even have specialized programs in STEM, arts and language immersion. There are plenty of private schools to pick from, too. The city is home to Fountain Valley School and The Colorado Springs School, which Niche ranked in the top 10 best private schools in Colorado. CO-loveland.description: It's easy to fall in love with Loveland, CO. Stunning surroundings, excellent education and a community that loves celebrating all add to the city's charm. For nature lovers, the nearby Rocky Mountains and plenty of parks add to the allure of Loveland.

sections.location: This is a sweet city blending small-town charm with outdoor thrills. It’s tucked in northern Colorado along U.S. Route 34, just south of Fort Collins in the foothills of the Rocky Mountains. The closest airport, Denver International Airport, is about 55 miles away (the views make for a pleasant drive). When you’re set to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Fort Collins is just 15 miles north, where you can tour Colorado State University, visit the Discovery Museum or stroll Old Town Square. Denver sits 50 miles south, offering the Denver Art Museum, Botanic Gardens and the Denver Zoo. Boulder is about 30 miles southwest, tempting with Pearl Street Mall, CU Boulder and the stunning Flatirons.
sections.economy: Loveland, located in Northern Colorado, has a diverse economy with a mix of industries contributing to its growth and stability. Advanced manufacturing, aviation, bioscience, energy and natural resources, information technology, health and wellness and food manufacturing are among the top industries. Major employers include Banner Health, the City of Loveland and Hach Company. The cost of living in Loveland is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Loveland is $479,719, and the average household income is $115,225. Colorado residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.7% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Loveland, CO? Residents have access to various health care providers, including UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies, which is ranked as one of the overall best health care providers in Colorado by U.S. News & World Report, and McKee Medical Center.
sections.thingsToDo: Loveland is for lovers and artists. The well-known art scene features several sculpture parks, including Chapungu Sculpture Park, and hosts the world-renowned Sculpture in the Park event. There are nearly 50 custom-painted heart sculptures and other public artworks throughout the city, mirroring its status as a hub for sculptors and art enthusiasts. Beyond the arts, the city provides endless entertainment options, from concerts to family-friendly activities like laser tag, bowling and even indoor skiing. The community comes together at numerous free events, such as the Sweetheart Festival and Winter Wonderlights, fostering camaraderie. The downtown area offers boutique shops, and more retail options are available at The Promenade Shops at Centerra. Enjoy live music at venues like the Foundry Plaza and To the Fives Taproom, and explore literary treasures at Whampus Used Books. Family-friendly activities include train rides at North Lake Park, kids’ shows at Blue Arena and swimming at the Chilson Recreation Center. What is something you can do for free in Loveland, CO? Spend an afternoon exploring the 80-plus stone sculptures spread over 26 acres at Chapungu Sculpture Park. Or take a picnic at the 10-acre Benson Sculpture Park, which has more than 170 sculptures by world-renowned artists on display. The collection is valued at more than $5 million. What are some things to do with kids in Loveland, CO? Cheer on the Colorado Eagles (a minor league hockey team) at the Blue Arena at The Ranch Events Complex. Where do locals shop in Loveland, CO? Bookworms unite at Whampus Used Books, a cute little shop full of little reading nooks and a great selection of titles without breaking the bank. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Loveland, CO? The most scenic spot to see a concert is the Foote Lagoon Amphitheater, which has an island-like stage surrounded by water and stair-step seating.
sections.outdoors: Colorado is known for its stunning outdoor adventures; Loveland is no exception. Whether seeking a leisurely hike or an adrenaline-pumping adventure, its natural surroundings offer many options to connect with the outdoors. The city’s proximity to Rocky Mountain National Park allows easy access to world-class hiking and mountain vistas. Residents and visitors can explore Devil’s Backbone Open Space, which offers 17 miles of trails for hiking, mountain biking and wildlife viewing. Water activities abound at Boyd Lake and Carter Lake, where stand-up paddleboarding, boating and fishing are popular pastimes. For a more traditional Western experience, horseback riding and overnight stays are available at Sylvan Dale Guest Ranch. Fishing spots are plentiful along the Big Thompson River, and the more adventurous can enjoy whitewater rafting or kayaking. Beyond these activities, Loveland provides plenty of places for hiking and enjoying the surrounding area’s natural beauty. The city’s location near trails and fishing spots makes it an ideal base for outdoor exploration.
sections.restaurants: Take a bite out of the local dining scene, which is full of flavors and different dining destinations, ranging from cozy cafes and brunch spots to upscale dining and craft breweries. Downtown Loveland features restaurants with farm-to-table cuisine, international dishes and classic American meals. The city’s thriving craft beer culture and distilleries, as well as a meadery and a winery, contribute to a laid-back and welcoming atmosphere. Neighborhood eateries and establishments at The Promenade Shops at Centerra provide even more dining options, ensuring there’s something to satisfy every palate. What is one of the best places to eat in Loveland, CO? Can’t pick what’s for dinner? Look no further than Pourhouse! The bar and restaurant’s extensive menu has a little bit of everything, plus a full bar and live music three nights a week. What is the best coffee shop in Loveland, CO? Dark Heart Coffee Bar is a small-batch specialty coffee roaster known for its single-origin coffee roastery. Where is the best happy hour in Loveland, CO? Wicked Tequila Room is for agave lovers. It offers a creative menu of margs and other treats (think boozy snow cones and push-pops). Follow the bar on Facebook for drink specials, which include all-day happy hours on Sunday. Cheers to that!
sections.education: Families have choices when it comes to finding a school for their children. Thompson School District R-2J serves the more than 15,000 students enrolled, and although performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable 16:1 student-to-teacher ratio. There are also several private schools, including faith-based and independent education. Families also have access to Loveland Classical Schools, a tuition-free public charter school. faqs.a: Wicked Tequila Room is for agave lovers. It offers a creative menu of margs and other treats (think boozy snow cones and push-pops). Follow the bar on Facebook for drink specials, which include all-day happy hours on Sunday. Cheers to that!

CT-danbury.description: Danbury is home to a charming downtown, excellent schools, an arts community and great schools. Danbury is also just a hop, skip and a jump away from New York City. Danbury’s endless roster of things to do and relative affordability make it the perfect place to plant roots.

sections.location: Danbury is a delightful city nestled in Fairfield County, near the New York state line, about 60 miles northeast of New York City. Interstate 84 and U.S. Route 7 connect it to the region. The nearest major airport, Westchester County Airport, lies 30 miles southwest, offering regional flights, while Danbury Municipal Airport caters to smaller planes. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Bridgeport is 25 miles south, where you can explore the Barnum Museum, catch a show at the Downtown Cabaret Theatre or unwind at Seaside Park. Hartford is 50 miles northeast, home to the Mark Twain House, Connecticut Science Center and the Wadsworth Atheneum Museum of Art. New York City sits 60 miles southwest, where you can hit Times Square, roam Central Park or catch a Broadway show.
sections.economy: Danbury’s economy is made up of a mix of industries, including health care, retail trade and manufacturing. This quirky little town’s history in the manufacturing industry earned it the nickname “Hat City” after it became the central hub for hat production in the 19th century, and its manufacturing marvels are still crucial to the local economy today. Ethan Allen, a large furniture retailer, is headquartered in town and has a large manufacturing facility. Other large employers in Danbury include Nuvance Health, Boehringer Ingelheim (a pharmaceutical company) and the City of Danbury. The cost of living in Danbury is higher than the national average. The median home value in Danbury is $392,848, and the average household income is $123,224. Connecticut residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.35% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Danbury, CT? Residents have several options to choose from when it comes to finding a health care provider, including Danbury Hospital. Danbury Hospital is part of the Western Connecticut Health Network, and the 371-bed teaching facility has a Level II Trauma Center.
sections.thingsToDo: Danbury blends history, culture and family fun. History buffs can check out the Danbury Museum, the Railway Museum and the Military Museum. For art enthusiasts, there are shows at the Danbury Music Centre and Ives Concert Park, musicals at Richter and the Palace Theatre, plus modern art at the Aldrich Museum. Families can skate at the Danbury Ice Arena, have adventures at Thrillz Park or get a library card and participate in Danbury Library community programs. Outdoors, there’s hiking and picnics at Tarrywile Park and shopping at Danbury Fair Mall. Additionally, seasonal attractions like the Danbury Farmers Market add to the city’s diverse offerings. What is something you can do for free in Danbury, CT? The Danbury Farmers’ Market runs seasonally (late June through late October) on Saturdays at the Danbury City Center Green. You can also browse through the stacks at the Danbury Public Library and see the Sybil Ludington Statue, which depicts the young American Revolutionary War heroine on horseback. What are some things to do in Danbury, CT, with kids? The Danbury Railroad Museum tells the history of railroading with a collection of railroad artifacts and objects, including model trains and photographs and a rail yard full of locomotives, passenger cars and cabooses. The rail yard has regularly scheduled train rides and special themed excursions. Also, try duckpin bowling at Danbury Duckpin Lanes, a longtime alley with video games and a concession stand. Where do locals shop in Danbury, CT? Those looking to shop locally can find great reads at The Booksmiths Shoppe, which carries old and new titles. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Danbury, CT? Located in downtown Danbury, the Palace Theatre is a historic venue from 1928 that hosts various performances, including concerts, theatrical productions, dance shows and community events.
sections.outdoors: Explore the great outdoors here, which offers plenty of places to play, catering to nature enthusiasts and sports lovers. Tarrywile Park & Mansion, a sprawling 722-acre park, provides extensive hiking trails and scenic picnic spots, while Candlewood Lake, the largest lake in Connecticut, offers boating, fishing and lakeside relaxation opportunities. Bear Mountain Reservation features pet-friendly hiking trails with different difficulty levels, leading to picturesque views and access to Candlewood Lake. Lake Kenosia Park provides a more relaxed outdoor setting with fishing, walking paths and picnic areas. For golfers, Richter Park Golf Course offers an 18-hole public course with a scenic and challenging layout, while the nearby Golf Club at Oxford Greens provides a championship-level experience. Hikers and bikers can explore the trails at Bear Mountain Reservation, and families can enjoy the recreational facilities at Lake Kenosia Park, including a spray park and playground.
sections.restaurants: Dining in Danbury is a delight! This Connecticut hot spot has a diverse culinary landscape, blending classic American fare with various international cuisines. The city caters to diverse palates, from authentic Italian at Fusco’s Market to flavorful Mediterranean dishes at Souvlaki GR. Upscale dining experiences are often paired with local craft beers, such as those from Charter Oak Brewing, at events hosted by the Palace Theater. Additionally, a vibrant food truck scene adds to the city’s culinary variety, ensuring that residents can find satisfying options ranging from comfort food to unique culinary explorations. What is one of the best places to eat in Danbury, CT? Hanna’s serves delicious Middle Eastern/Lebanese dishes like stuffed grape leaves and gyros. What is the best coffee shop in Danbury, CT? Rumors Coffee and Tea offers hand-blended loose-leaf teas, locally sourced coffee and a menu of light bites. Where is the best happy hour in Danbury, CT? Rein Osteria's happy hour offers specials on a selection of wines by the glass, draft beers and cocktails. Mariposa Taqueria's happy hour happens all day for Taco Tuesday and then in the afternoons Wednesday through Friday, with specials on their tacos and margaritas.
sections.education: Danbury’s combination of affordability — comparatively lower than some neighboring areas — and high quality of life factors, like the strong educational system, make the city a haven for young families. Locals have access to many well-regarded public schools and a range of private options, and the city regularly ranks as one of the safest in Connecticut. Danbury School District has more than 12,000 students and offers a range of programs and schools, from traditional environments to magnet schools and a technical high school. Private schools include religiously affiliated institutions, non-sectarian schools and specialized private schools that cater to different learning styles. --> --> --> Browse the Top 100 Best Places faqs.a: There are a couple of colleges and universities here, including Western Connecticut State University and Naugatuck Valley Community College.

FL-coral-springs.description: Florida fun in the sun is a way of life in Coral Springs. Known for its excellent schools, safe neighborhoods and lush green spaces, Coral Springs residents can enjoy a strong sense of community and a top-notch quality of life.

sections.location: Coral Springs is a sunny city in southeastern Florida, just west of Fort Lauderdale. Part of the Miami metropolitan area, it’s accessible via the Sawgrass Expressway (State Route 869), keeping it linked to the coast. The nearest airport, Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport, is about 20 miles southeast. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Miami is 40 miles south, where you can lounge on South Beach or explore the Vizcaya Museum and Gardens. Fort Lauderdale sits 15 miles east, offering beautiful beaches and the historic Bonnet House Museum & Gardens. West Palm Beach is about 40 miles north, home to the Norton Museum of Art and the Palm Beach Zoo.
sections.economy: Coral Springs is a city experiencing growth, from an increase in available jobs to new commercial real estate developments. Many employers, including Broward County Schools, Fiserv and Broward Health of Coral Springs, reflect the city’s diverse economy. Some growing industries in Coral Springs include retail trade, health care, and social assistance and professional services. The cost of living in Coral Springs is higher than the national average. The median home value in Coral Springs is $466,482, and the average household income is $132,300. Florida residents do not pay a state income tax, and residents living here can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Coral Springs, FL? Broward Health Coral Springs serves the medical needs of residents living in the area; however, the city’s proximity to Fort Lauderdale provides plenty of options to keep them in good health.
sections.thingsToDo: One of the absolute coolest things you can do here (pun intended) is skate on the same ice that professional NHL players from the Florida Panthers practice on. The Florida Panthers IceDen is a 75,000-square-foot entertainment destination, and when they’re not working on their slap shots, the ice is open to the public for community skating. Those looking for an artsy afternoon can visit the Coral Springs Museum of Art, which showcases art by Floridians as well as national artists. Let the exhibits be your muse because you can sign up for workshops that range from ceramics to painting to botanical printing techniques and drawing. In nearby Fort Lauderdale, get out on the water on a water taxi, paddleboard or gondola and you’ll soon see why this part of the state is known as the “Venice of America.” Closer to home, families can cool off at Betti Stradling Park, which features a splash pad, a playground, sports courts, a skate park and sand volleyball courts. What is something you can do for free in Coral Springs, FL? Bring your binoculars to the Tall Cypress Natural Area and enjoy birdwatching along the boardwalk surrounded by cypress trees and lush ferns. What are some things to do with kids in Coral Springs, FL? Butterfly World is a top spot for kids. Thousands of butterflies take flight, and the attraction stakes its claim as the first butterfly house in the United States and the largest in the world. Monster Mini Golf is an all-out fun zone with glow-in-the-dark putt-putt, arcade games and laser tag. Families with teens can try their luck at one of the themed rooms (think jewel heist, prison break and space station) at Red Button Escape. Where do locals shop in Coral Springs, FL? Coral Square is an indoor shopping mall with department store anchors, more than 120 shops, 15 eateries and a children’s play area. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Coral Springs, FL? Coral Springs Center For The Arts hosts everything from tribute band concerts to comedians to musicals. Nearby in Fort Lauderdale, residents can catch touring Broadway musicals, operas, comedy shows and more at the Broward Center for the Performing Arts, which overlooks New River in downtown Fort Lauderdale.
sections.outdoors: Beach lovers are in paradise in sunny southern Florida. Head 30 minutes east, and you’ve got your pick of gorgeous public beaches, including Lauderdale-By-The-Sea, a cute retro town with punchy colors, mid-century architecture and reefs 100 yards off the shore. Or, head to Pompano Beach and unfurl a towel on the 3 miles of shoreline or bring your kids to some of the beach town’s tree-shaded playgrounds. A must-visit in Coral Springs is the Sawgrass Nature Center, home to birds, mammals, reptiles and native Florida plants. What sets this nature center apart is that it has a nonprofit wildlife hospital that treats and rehabs animals before releasing them back into the wild. The city maintains 49 parks and 337 miles of trails, providing ample space to stretch your legs. Some favorites among locals are Cypress, Betti Strandling and Dede Gilmore Memorial Parks. Cool off at the Coral Springs Aquatic Complex during the dog days of summer.
sections.restaurants: Residents here have a love for all things local. Options range from date-night spots like The Cook & The Cork to craft beer flights and wood-fired pizzas at Big Bear Brewing and kebabs and vegetarian sandwiches at Mediterranean hot spot La Marsa. The Walk of Coral Springs is a popular dining destination and is a place where fashion and food come together. There are more than a dozen dining options, including local favorites and national names. What is one of the best places to eat in Coral Springs, FL? The beauty lies in the simplicity of the fresh tomato pizza at Pizza Time. But there are also some delicious specialty pizzas like a gourmet lobster and spinach bisque pie and another for eggplant parmigiana lovers. What is the best coffee shop in Coral Springs, FL? Frida Coffee and Deli is a family-owned business proudly rooted in Argentina that sells empanadas alongside avocado toast, sandwiches and pastries. On hot days, coffee lovers can pop in for an iced or frozen beverage or an afternoon dessert like a pistachio affogato. Where is the best happy hour in Coral Springs, FL? For a jovial happy hour, head to Tacocraft, which has an impressive tequila list, a wide range of tacos, and snacky items like street corn and made-fresh guac.
sections.education: Families living in Coral Springs can take advantage of a diverse range of educational institutions in the area, including public, private and charter schools. The city is served by the Broward County Public Schools district, which oversees numerous schools. FL-gainesville.description: Gainesville is a quintessential college town. A diverse, artistic culture, plenty of outdoor activities and affordable housing add to the vibrant lifestyle Gainesville residents have come to enjoy. The city is anchored by a robust economy full of opportunities for all and is known for its innovative spirit — Gatorade was created here!

sections.location: Gainesville is a lively city in north-central Florida, about 70 miles southwest of Jacksonville, accessible via Interstate 75. Gainesville Regional Airport is just northeast, offering flights to Atlanta and Miami. Nearby major cities include: Jacksonville is just 70 miles northeast, home to the Jacksonville Zoo, Cummer Museum of Art and Jacksonville Beach. Orlando sits 115 miles southeast, where you can explore Walt Disney World, Universal Studios and Orlando Science Center. Tampa is 130 miles southwest, where you can explore the Tampa Museum of Art and Tampa Riverwalk or catch a Buccaneers game.
sections.economy: Gainesville is the largest city in north Central Florida, and as a result, there are endless economic opportunities here. With a growing roster of innovative companies, a culture of entrepreneurship and a strong support network to encourage and nurture startup enterprises, the city is fast becoming a global technology hub. Education, government and health care are the city’s largest industries. Its largest employers are the University of Florida, the Shands (UF Health) Health Care System and the City of Gainesville. The cost of living in Gainesville is lower than the national average. The median home value in Gainesville is $250,373, and the average household income is $77,511. Florida residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Gainesville, FL? Gainesville’s UF Health Shands Hospitals is one of the best health care providers in both the Gainesville area and the state. The UF Health Shands Children’s Hospital is nationally ranked for its neonatology, pediatric cardiology and heart surgery and pediatric diabetes and endocrinology programs.
sections.thingsToDo: Get out and about in Gainesville! This college town has plenty of fun to offer residents, from arts and culture at the Cade Museum for Creativity and Invention to live performances at the Phillips Center. The Harn Museum of Art offers free admission to its 10 gallery spaces and gardens. The city also hosts various annual festivals and events, including the Downtown Festival & Art Show and the Spring Arts Festival, adding to its appeal. The city has a memorable music scene, with venues like Heartwood Soundstage and The Wooly hosting live performances. The University of Florida’s sporting events, particularly football games in the fall, create a spirited atmosphere, and residents love to cheer on the team together. Outside college sports, the area has various other sporting events and venues, including the Alachua County Agriculture and Equestrian Center and the Gainesville Raceway, “Florida’s home for drag racing.” Families can enjoy attractions like the Santa Fe College Teaching Zoo and the Depot Park, while the city’s downtown area offers unique shopping and dining experiences. What is something you can do for free in Gainesville, FL? Birdwatching! Gainesville and Alachua County are part of the Great Florida Birding and Wildlife Trail. See if you can spot a Painted Bunting, beauties covered in bold primary colors. What are some things to do in Gainesville, FL, with kids? Looking for some kid-friendly fun? Look no further than the Cade Museum for Creativity and Invention, a modern space filled with fascinating science and tech exhibits. It’s named for Dr. James Robert Cade, the lead inventor of Gatorade, a piece of local trivia every new resident should know! Though the exhibits building is currently closed for an expansion, the Butterfly Rainforest at the Florida Museum of Natural History is a must-visit with kids. Where do locals shop in Gainesville, FL? The city has several different local farmers markets where you can stock up on produce. The Alachua County Farmers Market is a growers-only market where everything sold is grown within 50 miles. Another favorite is the Grove Street Farmers Market, where attendees can find fresh farm eggs, free-range beef and chicken, cow and goat milk and cheeses, organic coffee and a whole host of other produce and products. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Gainesville, FL? You’ve got to catch an UpStage performance on the University of Florida campus. The shows emulate New York jazz clubs, putting audiences and performers on the Phillips Center Main Stage for intimate shows. 12 Fun Things To Do in Gainesville, FL
sections.outdoors: Gainesville caters to nature enthusiasts and adventure seekers. Surrounded by natural landscapes, including Paynes Prairie Preserve State Park, where visitors can spot wild bison and horses, and Devil’s Millhopper Geological State Park, which features a 120-foot-deep sinkhole with a miniature rainforest. The city has more than 3,200 acres of recreation spaces, natural areas and facilities, so there is no shortage of parks and trails to explore. Favorites include those at O’Leno State Park and San Felasco Hammock Preserve State Park, perfect for hiking, biking and birdwatching. Getting on the water in Gainesville is easy since the city has easy access to nearby Ichetucknee Springs and Poe Springs for tubing, swimming and kayaking, and fishing spots like River Rise Preserve State Park and Lake Santa Fe. The Kanapaha Botanical Gardens are a tranquil place for picnics, while Sweetwater Wetlands Park offers opportunities to observe local wildlife. Additionally, dog owners can enjoy pet-friendly parks like Possum Creek Park.
sections.restaurants: Come hungry and leave happy. The city mixes upscale farm-to-table dining and casual, eclectic eateries, so it’s the ideal spot for college students on a budget who want to show off when their parents are in town to foot the bill. Residents love iconic spots like Spurrier’s Gridiron Grille, filled with Gators memorabilia, to trendy brunch at Afternoon and OAK, and unique experiences like vegan drag brunches at Curia on the Drag. Beyond traditional dining, Gainesville features a range of culinary experiences, including late-night eats, plenty of pizza joints and hidden gem restaurants. There is also a burgeoning brewery scene, with favorites like Blackadder Brewing Company, Swamp Head Brewery and First Magnitude Brewing Company offering a variety of local brews and hosting regular events. The emphasis on local ingredients is evident in many establishments, including Swamp Head Brewery’s use of Florida-sourced ingredients. Residents can explore the Alachua County Ale Trail to experience the brewery culture firsthand. What is one of the best places to eat in Gainesville, FL? Satchel’s is a Gainesville staple for pizza. This unique spot lets guests sit in a van, under a plane (yes, you read that right) or in a greenhouse. Satchel’s is known for its thin-crust pizzas with a wide array of toppings, hefty calzones, fresh salads and homemade soda concoctions. What is the best coffee shop in Gainesville, FL? Wyatt’s signature and seasonal cups of coffee will have you coming back for more. Baristas are knowledgeable about coffee, and it shows. Where is the best happy hour in Gainesville, FL? Cry Baby’s is an upscale dive bar known for their hand-crafted cocktails — ranging from classics to adventurous concoctions. 8 Great Restaurants in Gainesville, FL, You Must Try5 Breweries in Gainesville, FL, You Must Visit
sections.education: Education is a top priority for local residents. Managed by Alachua County Public Schools, Gainesville’s public school district ranks among the Top 15 in the state. Residents can also take their pick of private schools, too. The University of Florida is the state’s oldest (and largest) university and a center of education, with more than 300 undergraduate and graduate degree programs in 16 academic colleges. The university was ranked fifth among all public universities nationwide in recent years. FL-orlando.description: Orlando ignites imaginations and offers a world of wonder, from its world-class theme parks to a vibrant downtown. With year-round sunshine and a welcoming community, Orlando invites residents to explore every opportunity afforded to them here.

sections.location: Orlando is a world-famous city in central Florida, accessible via Interstate 4. Orlando International Airport, a global gateway, is just southeast of the city. Nearby major cities include: Tampa is roughly 85 miles west, where you can explore the Tampa Museum of Art and Tampa Riverwalk or catch a Buccaneers game. Daytona Beach is just 55 miles northeast, home to the Daytona International Speedway, Museum of Arts & Sciences and sandy beaches. Miami is 235 miles southeast, buzzing with the excitement of South Beach, Vizcaya Museum and Gardens and Little Havana’s vibrant culture.
sections.economy: Thanks to the dozens of colleges, universities and technical schools in the area, the local economy directly benefits from a strong talent pipeline to fill the needs of the city’s diverse workforce. The city is known for its tourism industry, particularly theme parks, but there are other significant industries in the city, too, including aerospace and aviation, defense and homeland security, and technology and innovation. Walt Disney World Resort, Universal Orlando Resort, Lockheed Martin and Publix Super Markets are among the largest employers. The cost of living in Orlando is just slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Orlando is $388,075, and the average household income is $110,315. Florida residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Orlando, FL? Orlando residents can access various top health care providers, including AdventHealth, Orlando Health and the University of Central Florida Health. In addition, some of the top hospitals in the area include Orlando Health-Orlando Regional Medical Center (nationally ranked in five children’s specialties), AdventHealth Orlando (nationally ranked in six adult specialties and one children’s specialty) and the Arnold Palmer Hospital for Children (nationally ranked in five children’s specialties).
sections.thingsToDo: This area has much to do beyond its famous theme parks, although they are a favorite spot for residents who can get discounted tickets and annual passes. Outside of the House of Mouse, art enthusiasts can explore the Rollins Museum of Art in nearby Winter Park, the Orlando Museum of Art and the CityArts gallery, while performing arts lovers can enjoy Broadway shows and concerts at the Dr. Phillips Center for the Performing Arts and Shakespearean plays at the Orlando Shakespeare Theater. History buffs can dive into the city’s heritage at the Orange County Regional History Center and the Hannibal Square Heritage Center. For something kid-approved, visit Gatorland, the Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex or explore the underwater world at SEA LIFE Orlando Aquarium. Beyond the cultural and historical attractions, there are plenty of places to shop, including Orlando International Premium Outlets and The Mall at Millenia. Families will love Discovery Cove and the Crayola Experience. Downtown Orlando is a community hub with a lively arts scene, as well as the Orlando Science Center and the Creative City Project. The downtown area also hosts many festivals and events throughout the year. Sports lovers can appreciate attending any professional sporting events in town, including soccer, basketball and volleyball games. What is something you can do for free in Orlando, FL? Spend an afternoon walking around CityArts Factory, which houses a rotating collection of contemporary art galleries. Or, take in the tranquility at Mead Botanical Garden. What are some things to do in Orlando, FL, with kids? Kids can learn about dinosaurs, test out their skills as a meteorologist and get a close-up look at the cosmos all at the Orlando Science Center. For those ages 13 and up, the Flight Lab has flight simulator stations outfitted with Oculus Rift VR headsets. Where do locals shop in Orlando, FL? East End Market is a two-story market and food hall home to a dozen merchants. Grab Japanese mochi donuts from Dochi, accessories from Freehand Goods and a cup of joe from Lineage Coffee Roasting. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Orlando, FL? Orlando Shakespeare Theater is one of the country’s premier Shakespeare companies and hosts year-round productions of the Bard’s work and other classics. Southern Pride: A Guide to Orlando’s LGBTQ Scene8 Reasons to Move to Orlando, FLLiving the Dream: I Moved to Orlando to Get a Job at Disney World
sections.outdoors: The city has abundant outdoor activities, letting residents appreciate Florida’s natural beauty. Orlando has nearly 150 parks, gardens, recreation areas and playgrounds. Lake Eola Park is a downtown jewel, providing a scenic setting for walks, picnics and swan-shaped paddleboat rides. The Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge has abundant opportunities to observe wildlife, including dolphins, manatees and birds. Hit the water at the Orlando Watersports Complex. Or explore the natural springs and hiking trails at Wekiwa Springs State Park, take scenic boat tours through Winter Park’s lakes or experience the Everglades on an airboat tour. Beyond these attractions, Orlando has several spots to hit the links, play pickleball or tennis at the USTA National Campus or explore with your four-legged friend at places like Barber Dog Park.
sections.restaurants: The local culinary scene has a mix of fresh seafood, an impressive vegan variety and farm-to-table experiences. There are plenty of dining destinations around town. Orlando’s dining landscape is cemented with upscale dining experiences on Restaurant Row, the gourmet offerings at East End Market and the sustainable culinary practices showcased at the Orlando World Center Marriott’s HyCube. Seafood lovers can indulge in fresh catches at Bar Harbor and Lombardi’s Seafood, while those seeking plant-based meals love Infusion Tea and Loving Hut. Several Michelin-starred dining options, like Soseki, offer an exquisite omakase experience and a growing emphasis on locally sourced ingredients, highlighted by urban farming initiatives like Fleet Farming and Infinite Zion Farms. Beyond its restaurant scene, Orlando has a growing craft beer culture. Breweries like Crooked Can Brewing Company feature citrus-infused IPAs. Check out cocktails from spots like The Courtesy Bar, offering unique drinks and cocktail-making courses. Residents can explore local wines at Lakeridge Winery and Vineyards in nearby Clermont and indulge in sweet treats at the Chocolate Museum & Cafe. What is one of the best places to eat in Orlando, FL? Check out Santiago’s Bodega for a wide array of tasty tapas, Mrs. Potato Restaurant for authentic Brazilian fare or Better Than Sex Dessert Restaurant for dessert that’s … well, you get it. What is the best coffee shop in Orlando, FL? Lineage is a specialty coffee roaster serving a global lineup of ethically sourced coffee beans. Where is the best happy hour in Orlando, FL? Try the ‘Envy,’ a smoked cocktail from Mathers Social Gathering, a super cool speakeasy housed on the third floor of a furniture store.
sections.education: The city has more than 35 colleges, universities and technical schools and offers a mixture of quality public and private schools, which makes it an excellent place for young families. Public schools in Orlando are managed by the Orange County Public Schools district, which offers a variety of academic programs, award-winning fine arts and competitive athletics. There are many esteemed private schools, too, including faith-based and preparatory schools, like Trinity, which has ranked as the top private and STEM school in the area. --> --> --> Browse the Top faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: Orlando residents can access various top health care providers, including AdventHealth, Orlando Health and the University of Central Florida Health. In addition, some of the top hospitals in the area include Orlando Health-Orlando Regional Medical Center (nationally ranked in five children’s specialties), AdventHealth Orlando (nationally ranked in six adult specialties and one children’s specialty) and the Arnold Palmer Hospital for Children (nationally ranked in five children’s specialties).

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: Spend an afternoon walking around CityArts Factory, which houses a rotating collection of contemporary art galleries. Or, take in the tranquility at Mead Botanical Garden.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Orlando, FL, with kids?
faqs.a: Kids can learn about dinosaurs, test out their skills as a meteorologist and get a close-up look at the cosmos all at the Orlando Science Center. For those ages 13 and up, the Flight Lab has flight simulator stations outfitted with Oculus Rift VR headsets.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: East End Market is a two-story market and food hall home to a dozen merchants. Grab Japanese mochi donuts from Dochi, accessories from Freehand Goods and a cup of joe from Lineage Coffee Roasting.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: Orlando Shakespeare Theater is one of the country’s premier Shakespeare companies and hosts year-round productions of the Bard’s work and other classics.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: Check out Santiago’s Bodega for a wide array of tasty tapas, Mrs. Potato Restaurant for authentic Brazilian fare or Better Than Sex Dessert Restaurant for dessert that’s ... well, you get it.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: Lineage is a specialty coffee roaster serving a global lineup of ethically sourced coffee beans.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: Try the ‘Envy,’ a smoked cocktail from Mathers Social Gathering, a super cool speakeasy housed on the third floor of a furniture store.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Orlando, FL?
faqs.a: The area has several colleges and universities, including the University of Central Florida (UCF), Valencia College and various private universities.

FL-tampa.description: Warm weather, distinctly different neighborhoods and coastal beauty entice residents to Tampa, FL. The economy here is booming, and job opportunities are as plentiful as the white, sandy beaches.

sections.location: Tampa is a sunny, vibrant city on Florida’s west coast, about 85 miles southwest of Orlando, accessible via Interstate 4 and Interstate 275. Tampa International Airport is just 6 miles west, offering flights to Miami, Atlanta and beyond. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Orlando is roughly 85 miles northeast, where you can explore Walt Disney World, Universal Studios and Orlando Science Center. St. Petersburg sits just 20 miles west, home to the Salvador Dalí Museum, the Sunken Gardens and the St. Pete Pier. Sarasota is about 60 miles south, boasting the Ringling Museum of Art, Siesta Key Beach and the Asolo Repertory Theatre.
sections.economy: Tampa is an economic engine for the state’s West Coast, powered by a diverse mix of industries. Financial services, manufacturing, health care, information technology, and defense and security are key industries in Tampa. BayCare Health Systems and Publix are top employers. The cost of living in Tampa is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Tampa is $414,677, and the average household income is $130,944. Florida residents do not pay a state income tax, and people living here can expect a 6.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Tampa, FL? Thanks to several area hospitals and health care providers, local residents are in good health. The H. Lee Moffitt Cancer Center and Research Institute is nationally ranked in its cancer treatments, and Tampa General Hospital was named by U.S. News & World Report as the best hospital in the area.
sections.thingsToDo: Downtown Tampa features the vibrant Tampa Riverwalk, connecting popular destinations like the Florida Aquarium and the Tampa Bay History Center. The city is a hub for sports enthusiasts, with professional teams in the NFL, NHL, MLB and MSC. Culture shines at the Tampa Museum of Art and the Straz Center for the Performing Arts. The Tampa Theatre offers classic movie magic. Families love ZooTampa at Lowry Park, known for its manatee rehabilitation program. Or visit the interactive Glazer Children’s Museum. Theme parks like Busch Gardens and Adventure Island deliver thrills, while Lettuce Lake Park is a green escape. The city hosts unique events like the Gasparilla Pirate Fest and the Tampa International Gay and Lesbian Film Festival, adding to its dynamic cultural scene. What is something you can do for free in Tampa, FL? Run, walk or bike on the waterfront Bayshore Boulevard — the world’s longest continuous walkway at 4.5 miles. Or tour the Yuengling Brewery and score some free beer samples. You can also do both, and that’s perfectly acceptable. What are some things to do in Tampa, FL, with kids? Glazer Children’s Museum is an interactive museum and home to “Big John,” an RV-sized triceratops skeleton. Take water lovers to see the friendly sea cows at Tampa Electric’s Manatee Viewing Center. These gentle giants are attracted to the warm, clean water near the power plant. Where do locals shop in Tampa, FL? Books and wine, what more could you ask for? Book + Bottle in nearby St. Pete combined a boutique wine bar with an independent bookstore, creating the perfect place to spend an afternoon getting lost in the stacks. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Tampa, FL? Catch a Broadway show at the Straz Center for Performing Arts, perched right on the Riverwalk. Bravo for the location!
sections.outdoors: Find your footing in this Florida city. Tampa is built for outdoor fun, thanks to an abundance of sunshine and water. Residents walk and bike ride along the Tampa Riverwalk and the Bayshore Boulevard, the world’s longest continuous sidewalk. Nature lovers can explore Lettuce Lake Park’s boardwalk trails and wildlife or kayak and paddleboard along the Hillsborough River. Alafia River State Park provides thrilling mountain biking trails, while the Florida Botanical Gardens offer a serene environment for walking among diverse flora. Water activities abound, with dolphin tours in Tampa Bay and ample spots for fishing and boating. Golfers can tee off at plenty of pristine courses, including TPC Tampa Bay, Rocky Point Golf Course and The Claw at USF. Picnic Island Park offers beachside picnicking and sunset views. The city also has dog beaches and family spots like ZooTampa, where you can see animals outside.
sections.restaurants: Tampa’s tantalizing cuisine draws inspiration from its history and location. Residents can expect strong Cuban and Spanish influences, especially in Ybor City, where spots like the Columbia Restaurant serve Cuban sandwiches and paella. Seafood is a must-try, with fresh catches from the Gulf like oysters and grouper. Food trends are big here. Bern’s Steak House is a classic for steaks and wine, and food halls like Armature Works and Sparkman Wharf have tons of local eats. The city’s craft beer scene is notable, with breweries like Cigar City Brewing attracting enthusiasts. The local coffee culture is also robust, with numerous independent coffee shops adding to the city’s diverse food and beverage offerings. Tampa’s food scene provides various dining experiences, from waterfront bistros to farm-to-table restaurants. What is one of the best places to eat in Tampa, FL? Grab friends and head to Pint and Brew, where you can pair a beer with a sandwich from the gourmet grilled cheese menu. (Get a little tomato soup dipper, too). What is the best coffee shop in Tampa, FL? Lady and the Mug is the perfect place to grab coffee with a friend or settle in with your laptop to get some work done. Of course, there are classic favorites, but the spot serves up delicious food, too, like a rainbow bagel! Where is the best happy hour in Tampa, FL? Grab your pup and your people for an afternoon at Hair of the Dog Park in Seminole Heights. Humans can drink beer at picnic tables while the dogs romp around under the mature trees that provide shade.
sections.education: Schools in Tampa are just as diverse as the city’s population. The public education system here is primarily managed by Hillsborough County Public Schools, one of Florida’s largest districts. The above-average district serves nearly 225,000 students K-12 and has a student-to-teacher ratio of 22 to 1. There are also numerous private schools in the area, including preparatory schools and religiously affiliated institutions. faqs.a: Grab your pup and your people for an afternoon at Hair of the Dog Park in Seminole Heights. Humans can drink beer at picnic tables while the dogs romp around under the mature trees that provide shade.

faqs.a: The city has several colleges and universities, including the University of South Florida and the University of Tampa.

GA-athens.description: Athens is alive with Southern charm and a lively arts scene. This vibrant college town is brimming with historic architecture, a thriving music scene and a whole lot of school pride.

sections.location: Athens is a vibrant city in northeastern Georgia, about 70 miles east of Atlanta, accessible via U.S. Route 78. The nearest airport, Athens-Ben Epps Airport, is local, but Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (80 miles west) offers more options. Nearby major cities include: Atlanta is just 70 miles west, home to the Georgia Aquarium, the World of Coca-Cola and the Atlanta Falcons and Braves. Greenville, SC, is about 100 miles north, a doable trip to explore the Greenville County Museum of Art, Fluor Field and Swamp Rabbit Trail. Augusta, GA, sits 100 miles east, offering the Augusta National Golf Club, Morris Museum of Art and Augusta Canal.
sections.economy: The city is known for having a robust economy, especially considering its lower unemployment rate than the rest of the country. The University of Georgia, Piedmont Athens Regional Medical Center and Clarke County School District are major employers in Athens, contributing to the city’s reputation as a hub for jobs in education, health care and government industries. Additionally, the presence of UGA fosters a growing tech startup scene, with companies specializing in software, biotechnology and other areas. The cost of living in Athens is generally lower than the national average. The median home value in Athens is $258,778, and the average household income is $80,760. Georgia residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Athens, GA? Piedmont Athens Regional Medical Center and St. Mary’s Health Care System-Athens are the area’s two largest health care providers. Both have high-performance ratings in several procedures and conditions.
sections.thingsToDo: From cheering on the University of Georgia bulldogs to catching live music and exploring the museums, gardens and local restaurants, residents will find endless fun ways to fill up their weekends here. The State Botanical Garden of Georgia is a community gem that’s free and open to the public and worth frequenting throughout the year to see what’s in bloom, from orchids to zinnias and tulips. The 2.5-acre Children’s Garden is a super fun spot for families, with a chestnut tree house, fossil wall, water-misting mushrooms, veggies growing in a garden and a replica of a North Georgia cave. Another can’t-miss attraction is The Georgia Museum of Art. Located on the UGA campus, it’s the official art museum of the state of Georgia. The permanent collection includes everything from Italian Renaissance paintings to Southern decorative arts, and repeat visitors can check out exhibits like “Waffle House” vistas that include photographs taken from inside the windows of the breakfast chain. What is something you can do for free in Athens, GA? Find the bulldog statues! A few dozen of these artistic bulldog statues are scattered throughout the college town thanks to the “We Let the Dawgs Out” public art program that raised money for AIDS Athens. Though the program ended years ago, many of the beloved bulldogs remain and are accompanied by a new generation of statues. What are some things to do in Athens, GA, with kids? Unleash your inner adventurer at Athens Climbing, an indoor rock climbing gym, hit the ramps at the skate and BMX park at Southeast Clarke Park and check out the Bear Hollow Zoo within Memorial Park. Where do locals shop in Athens, GA? Spend some time at Indie South, where you can shop a beautiful collection of home goods, crystals and jewelry and also book a tarot reading. Every Saturday, stock up on goods at the Athens Farmers Market. The market is also available on Wednesdays from April to November. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Athens, GA? Grab a pre-show cocktail at the rooftop bar at The Georgia Theater. BB King and The Police have played at this theater.
sections.outdoors: With more than 40 parks, five swimming pools and two nature preserves, Athens has plenty of green spaces to explore. Get out on two wheels! Athens is a great place for bikers, with mountain biking trails at the 113-acre Walker Park as well as popular multi-use trails like Firefly Trail that’s built on a former stretch of the Georgia Railroad where embers from wood-burning locomotives once flickered. Or try your luck at the Twilight Series Criterium — a weekend of bicycle races and events each April.For a park day, head to Bishop Park, which has a swimming pool, playground, basketball courts, walking trails, athletic fields and more. The State Botanical Gardens of Georgia is home to more than 290 acres of natural area and 32 acres of cultivated gardens, eight of which are specialty gardens. Within Memorial Park, the 1.25-mile Birchmore Trail is nestled in an urban forest and features installations built by local legend Fred Birchmore.
sections.restaurants: Food within the city is delicious and abundant, ranging from upscale restaurants and modern bars to comfort food and international cuisine. Southern Living Magazine even dubbed Athens with the title of “South’s Best Food City” a few years back. Get a taste of its acclaimed culinary scene at spots like Pretty Boy, a Vietnamese restaurant serving street food-style fare from Top Chef alum Kenny Nguyen, and Five & Ten, a modern Southern restaurant from James Beard Award-winning chef Hugh Acheson where you can (and should) order snacks like pimento cheese and tomato pie for the table. Sample the craft beer scene along the Athens Beer Trail and sip seasonal cocktails or a glass of wine to pair with Mediterranean dishes at The National. Creature Comforts is a staple for beer lovers. What is one of the best places to eat in Athens, GA? Cubano sandwiches and fish tacos draw lines to Cali-N-Titos, a Latin restaurant. What is the best coffee shop in Athens, GA? Buvez is a Euro-style cafe where you can order coffee and tea, of course. Where is the best happy hour in Athens, GA? Cocktails at The Old Pal are a must in Athens. This sweet spot is known for its seasonal cocktails, local beer and extensive whiskey list to whet your whistle.
sections.education: Local residents have an array of schools to pick from, both public and private. The Clarke County School District serves the Athens area and is rated above average with a student-to-teacher ratio of 14:1. There are several private school options in town, and they have a minority student enrollment higher than the state average. Though many of the private schools in town have a religious affiliation, others, like Athens Academy, an independent K3-12 school on a 152-acre campus, do not. As a high-energy college town, Athens is anchored by the University of Georgia, an academic powerhouse known for its top-tier honors college and standout pharmaceutical, education and business programs. The School of Public and International Affairs consistently ranks among the top programs in the country.
