GA-augusta.description: Southern charm meets sports in Augusta. This sweet Georgia city has mastered (pun intended!) being a great place to live with affordable housing, excellent schools and beautiful parks along the Savannah River.

sections.location: Augusta is a historic city in east-central Georgia, about 150 miles east of Atlanta, accessible via Interstate 20. Augusta Regional Airport is just south, offering flights to Atlanta and Charlotte. Nearby major cities include: Atlanta, GA, is just 150 miles west, where you can dive into the Georgia Aquarium, sip at the World of Coca-Cola or cheer on the Braves at Truist Park. Savannah, GA, sits 130 miles southeast, boasting the Savannah Historic District, Forsyth Park and Savannah College of Art and Design. Columbia, SC, is 75 miles north, a doable drive for a day trip to visit Riverbanks Zoo and Garden, South Carolina State Museum and Congaree National Park.
sections.economy: Augusta’s economy is robust and much, much more than the tourism that rolls through town every spring (though that tourism does bolster the economy!). The city has a strong economic foundation, with health care, manufacturing and government as the leading industries. Some of the leading employers in the area are Augusta University Medical Center, Fort Gordon and Savannah River Site. Fitting to its golfing reputation, Textron Specialized Vehicles and Club Car, both manufacturers of golf carts and other utility vehicles, have a heavy presence here. The cost of living in Augusta is lower than the national average. The median home value in Augusta is $163,376, and the average household income is $78,403. Georgia residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Augusta, GA? Piedmont Augusta Hospital, August University Medical Center and Doctors Hospital of Augusta all keep residents healthy.
sections.thingsToDo: Start your day off right at one of the great coffee shops along the Augusta Coffee Trail. For new residents, the Augusta Museum of History offers a fantastic introduction to the city. Explore the exhibits that dive into Augusta’s past, celebrate its iconic golf legacy and share the story of James Brown, the “Godfather of Soul,” who grew up in this Georgia city. Museum lovers can also purchase a membership to the Morris Museum of Art, which showcases art and artists of the American South. Staying active is easy here. Take a stroll (or a jog) along the Augusta Riverwalk that runs alongside the Savannah River, or join in on the pickleball craze at Dink’d Pickleball, which has indoor courts and serves burgers and pizza to pickleball players. Catch an Augusta GreenJackets game, a minor league baseball team, for something sporty. When it’s time to shop for home furnishings or a new outfit, head to the Surrey Center, a destination for stylish shops. Did you know that Augusta is the hometown of James Brown, the Godfather of Soul? At the Augusta Museum of History, the James Brown Exhibit features rare memorabilia and personal artifacts that give you a glimpse into his life via costumes, interviews, records, images and audio-visual stations that highlight concert performances, albums, studio recordings and more. Downtown, there’s a life-size bronze statue of the superstar that contains the world’s only James Brown Cam, which will take your photo and send it to your cell phone within minutes. What is something you can do for free in Augusta, GA? Visit the Phinizy Center for Water Sciences and be on the lookout for beavers, listen for songbirds and bring a pair of binoculars to spot woodpeckers, hawks and more. What are some things to do in Augusta, GA, with kids? Jump on trampolines and zipline over a bed of foam and try out the trapeze at Sky Zone or visit the Evans Town Center Park, which has a splash pad and playground. Where do locals shop in Augusta, GA? Find art from local makers at Art on Broad. Enjoy live music, fun runs and fresh produce at the Augusta Market. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Augusta, GA? Originally built as a 1940s movie house, the Miller Theater underwent a stunning $25 million renovation that preserved its historic charm. The venue draws crowds for concerts and films.
sections.outdoors: More than 60 parks and 1,500 acres of parkland are in Augusta, making outdoor adventures plentiful. The community is synonymous with golf, and locals can hit the links at beloved public courses like Forest Hill Golf Club, designed nearly a century ago by famed golf course architect Donald Ross. For those looking to explore nature, the Phinizy Center and Nature Park offers walking and biking trails that wind through scenic wetlands. Or rent a kayak and paddle along the Savannah River. For a fun group activity, gather up friends or neighbors to play disc golf at a spot like Pendleton King Park, home to an 18-hole disc golf course. To get the adrenaline pumping, the nearby Forks Area Trail System is a destination for mountain bikers. Other highlights include the Augusta Canal National Heritage Area and Augusta Common Park, which hosts concerts.
sections.restaurants: You’ll find no shortage of delectable Southern comfort food in Georgia, but the culinary scene is quite diverse. With a love for local, the restaurant scene ranges from a beloved hole-in-the-wall shop called Knuckle Sandwiches (try the PBR-B-Q pulled pork sammy with peach black pepper barbecue sauce) to the date-night Frog Hollow Tavern. Of course, save room for dessert: Locals love The Boll Weevil for its decadent cakes and pies. Sip spritzes and savor Italian dishes at Craft and Vine. The Treehouse is an organic, fresh-pressed juice joint with an art gallery for a healthy option. What is one of the best places to eat in Augusta, GA? Hunt down the Black Lotus Food Truck for tasty dishes like seafood stews and jerk chicken mac n’ cheese, or book it to bring the party to you for a ramen or hot pot party. What is the best coffee shop in Augusta, GA? Pop into Trellis Coffee Bar for a coffee break and the focaccia of the week. Where is the best happy hour in Augusta, GA? Enjoy a glass of wine or a hand-crafted cocktail with dill pickle deviled eggs and Vidalia onion dip at Finch & Fifth.
sections.education: Richmond County Schools, which serves nearly 30,000 students across 52 schools, is one of the largest districts in Georgia. There are three dozen private school options available to residents, including Augusta Preparatory Day School, an independent college prep school that consistently ranks in the top 10 private schools in the state.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Piedmont Augusta Hospital, August University Medical Center and Doctors Hospital of Augusta all keep residents healthy.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Visit the Phinizy Center for Water Sciences and be on the lookout for beavers, listen for songbirds and bring a pair of binoculars to spot woodpeckers, hawks and more.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Augusta, GA, with kids?
faqs.a: Jump on trampolines and zipline over a bed of foam and try out the trapeze at Sky Zone or visit the Evans Town Center Park, which has a splash pad and playground.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Find art from local makers at Art on Broad. Enjoy live music, fun runs and fresh produce at the Augusta Market.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Originally built as a 1940s movie house, the Miller Theater underwent a stunning $25 million renovation that preserved its historic charm. The venue draws crowds for concerts and films.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Hunt down the Black Lotus Food Truck for tasty dishes like seafood stews and jerk chicken mac n’ cheese, or book it to bring the party to you for a ramen or hot pot party.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Pop into Trellis Coffee Bar for a coffee break and the focaccia of the week.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Enjoy a glass of wine or a hand-crafted cocktail with dill pickle deviled eggs and Vidalia onion dip at Finch & Fifth.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Augusta, GA?
faqs.a: Augusta University, a major public research institution, is the largest university in the city. It houses the Medical College of Georgia, one of the largest medical schools in the country.

GA-roswell.description: Just an hour outside Atlanta, Roswell captivates residents with historic charm, scenic beauty and modern conveniences. Known for its parks, award-winning schools and a strong sense of community, Roswell offers a suburban lifestyle with all the amenities.

sections.location: Right along Georgia State Route 400, Roswell is parked just north of Atlanta in Fulton County. Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, one of the busiest airports in the world, is around 30 miles away. When you’re set to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Atlanta is just 20 miles south, where you can dive into the Georgia Aquarium, sip at the World of Coca-Cola or cheer on a local team at Mercedes-Benz Stadium. Sandy Springs sits 10 miles southwest, offering the Chattahoochee River National Recreation Area, Abernathy Greenway Park and great shopping. Marietta is about 15 miles west, known for its historic square, the Marietta Museum of History and Six Flags White Water.
sections.economy: Some of Roswell’s most prominent industries include health care, technology and manufacturing. Additionally, the automotive industry is a significant economic player, and the city has one of the highest concentrations of car dealerships in the Greater Atlanta area. Some companies with a large presence here include Kimberly-Clark, North Fulton Regional Hospital and the City of Roswell. The cost of living in Roswell is higher than the national average. The median home value in Roswell is $485,018, and the average household income is $184,331. Georgia residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.75% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Roswell, GA? Residents are in good health thanks to the number of highly ranked providers in the city and the Greater Atlanta area. For example, Wellstar North Fulton Hospital is Georgia’s ninth-largest hospital system, home to a Level II Emergency Cardiac Care Center.
sections.thingsToDo: Wondering about things to do in Roswell? We’re glad you asked. Local attractions are varied, from the gorgeous grounds at Barrington Hall to the boutique-lined streets of the Roswell Historic District. Residents appreciate all things local. On Saturdays from April through October, the Roswell Farmers and Artisans Market attracts visitors and features everything from fresh produce and artisanal goods to local crafts. It’s more than a place to buy groceries; it’s a community hub where residents gather to support local businesses, enjoy live music and savor the diverse flavors of the region. When it comes to letting loose, Roswell residents know how to have fun at one of the city’s many annual festivals. Some favorites are Roswell Roots, the Roswell Beer Festival and the Roswell Music Festival. Feeling fearless? Take a Roswell Ghost Tour. Tours run year-round on Friday and Saturday nights and seven days a week in October. What is something you can do for free in Roswell, GA? Learn all about the city’s history at Roswell Historical Society. What are some things to do with kids in Roswell, GA? Catch a movie, play putt-putt (or both!) at Area 51, Aurora Cineplex and The Fringe Miniature Golf. During August, take the kids to Miss Mary’s Ice Cream Crankin’, where you can sample more than 100 flavors of ice cream. Where do locals shop in Roswell, GA? Shop for a cause at Sunny & Ranney. The store sells designer furniture and home goods, and profits help pay for home makeovers for kids with special needs, creating accessible bathrooms, therapy rooms and bedrooms. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Roswell, GA? The Roswell Cultural Arts Center and Roswell Performing Arts shows are worth looking into for those hoping to spend a night out on the town.
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities in Roswell run the gamut. The city has more than 1,000 acres of parkland and 22 parks around town. Vickery Creek Trail at Old Mill Park is a scenic trail winding along the historic Roswell Mill Ruins and offering a peaceful escape into nature despite being within the city. Make sure to admire the Vickery Creek waterfall while you’re there. Chattahoochee River National Recreation Area offers walking, hiking and biking trails and kayaking, paddleboarding, tubing and fishing on the river. Take the kids to Leita Thompson Memorial Park or the Roswell Area Park, a large community park with walking trails, sports fields and picnic areas. Finally, rip through the shaded mountain biking trails at Big Creek Park.
sections.restaurants: Restaurants in Roswell are top-tier, and nearby Atlanta’s culinary influences bleed into the city. Residents will find everything from beloved Southern staples to international cuisine, with a wide array of restaurants, breweries and bars. Downtown is home to some of the best restaurants in the area, serving everything from sushi to barbecue to Italian. Osteria Mattone, 1920 Tavern and Canton St. Social are beloved favorites. Gate City Brewing Company anchors the brewery scene, and The Artillery Room @ Gate City provides a historic gathering space with craft cocktails. What is one of the best places to eat in Roswell, GA? Enjoy Southern fried chicken, catfish, collard greens and seasonal veggie plates in a cute restored cottage at Table & Main. Enjoy a nightcap at the restaurant’s bourbon bar. Bonus? Table & Main landed a spot on the Michelin Guide last year. What is the best coffee shop in Roswell, GA? Set up shop at Land of a Thousand Hills and grab a coffee, light bite or baked good … or maybe all three! There’s even a patio and lawn where you can enjoy your cup of joe. Where is the best place for happy hour in Roswell, GA? Hugo’s Oyster Bar is a Southern-inspired haunt with seafood dishes and some of the best craft cocktails in the area.
sections.education: The public education system here is managed by Fulton County School System, the fourth-largest in the state. Fulton County Schools are known for being incredibly diverse and having great athletic programs for students. Local families can also take their pick from nearly half a dozen private schools, ranging from independent and religious schools to college preparatory programs. And, because of the city's central location, there are plenty of other private schools to pick from in the area. IA-cedar-rapids.description: Surrounded by the scenic landscapes of the Cedar River, Cedar Rapids offers outdoor adventures, affordable housing and excellent schools. The city prioritizes affordability and innovation and emphasizes inclusivity, creating a diverse, tight-knit community.

sections.location: Cedar Rapids is a friendly city nestled in eastern Iowa, right along the Cedar River. It’s the second-largest city in the state and is intersected by Interstate 380, keeping it well-connected. The nearest airport, Eastern Iowa Airport, is just south of the town, making travel easy. When you’re ready to explore further, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Des Moines is just 125 miles west, where you can tour the Iowa State Capitol or visit the Blank Park Zoo. Davenport sits 65 miles east, offering the Figge Art Museum and the River Music Experience. Madison, WI, is about 170 miles northeast, home to the Wisconsin State Capitol and the Olbrich Botanical Gardens.
sections.economy: The city is known for being the largest corn processor in the United States and the entire world. It also has a reputation as one of the leading manufacturing regions in the country and one of the leading bio-processing and food ingredient centers in all of North America. But that’s not all. Businesses in Cedar Rapids thrive, and it’s an excellent place for young professionals to launch their careers. Rockwell Collins, an aviation and technology company, and Transamerica, a financial services company, are both headquartered here. And, if you’re new to town, Connect with an Iowan helps residents find opportunities that match their career goals and interests. The cost of living in Cedar Rapids is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Cedar Rapids is $174,452, and the average household income is $99,922. Iowa residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Cedar Rapids, IA? Residents are in good health and have access to several top-tier providers, including UnityPoint Health-St. Luke’s Hospital and Mercy Medical Center. Both have been recognized as high performers in several different procedures and conditions. Health Care Innovation is Thriving in Cedar Rapids, IA8 Reasons Why Business is Booming in Cedar Rapids, IACedar Rapids Companies Play Global Roles
sections.thingsToDo: The city is full of art, culture and community events, making it a delightful place to call home. The city’s arts scene includes venues like Theatre Cedar Rapids, CSPS and the Paramount Theatre, showcasing everything from contemporary plays to classic performances. The Cedar Rapids Museum of Art houses an impressive collection, including works by Grant Wood, and the city is home to Orchestra Iowa and Cedar Rapids Opera. Residents can also enjoy the cultural experiences at the National Czech & Slovak Museum & Library and explore the historic Czech Village. Beyond the cultural offerings, Cedar Rapids hosts a variety of community events. The Cedar Rapids Downtown Farmers Market and Market After Dark provide opportunities to enjoy local produce and artisan goods, while the Alliant Energy PowerHouse hosts concerts, sporting events and other large gatherings. Its development of areas like The District and Banjo Block provide contemporary living and entertainment options. Historical sites like Ushers Ferry Historic Village also offer a glimpse into the region’s past. What is something you can do for free in Cedar Rapids, IA? The Indian Creek Nature Center has miles of trails and is open year-round. In the winter, rent snowshoes or bring cross-country skis. For the rest of the year, visitors can spend time in the bird room, fish and enjoy the great outdoors in Cedar Rapids’ backyard. What are some things to do in Cedar Rapids, IA, with kids? Kids and adults alike will love getting up close and personal with the animals at the free petting farm at Old MacDonald’s Farm in Bever Park. Cool off at any of the 11 different splash pads in Cedar Rapids when the temperatures heat up. Where do locals shop in Cedar Rapids, IA? Czech Village and New Bohemia neighborhoods meet in The District, and the best place to find small, locally-owned businesses and restaurants is NewBo City Market. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Cedar Rapids, IA? Local arts and culture enthusiasts cite the Paramount Theatre as one of the best places to catch a show here. Located downtown, this historic theatre boasts stunning architecture and a phenomenal list of Broadway shows, concerts, dance performances and more. Recently, the Paramount underwent a $35 million renovation. 7 Free (or Cheap!) Things to Do in Cedar Rapids, IAImmerse Yourself in the Vibrant Cedar Rapids Arts Scene8 Fun Things To Do in Cedar Rapids, IA
sections.outdoors: The city has many outdoor activities that cater to nature enthusiasts and recreational adventurers. Residents and visitors can enjoy an extensive network of trails, including those at the Indian Creek Nature Center and the scenic Cedar River Trail, both of which are ideal for hiking, biking and walking. Mount Trashmore, a reclaimed landfill, provides unique panoramic views and recreational trails, while Palisades-Kepler State Park offers views of the Cedar River and opportunities for camping and hiking. Water activities are also popular, with the Cedar River providing ample boating, fishing and other water sports opportunities. Beyond the trails and waterways, Cedar Rapids has plenty of parks and natural spaces, like Beverly Park and Seminole Valley Park. The city also hosts various outdoor events and festivals, and the Prospect Meadows Sports Complex attracts baseball enthusiasts from across the country. Indian Creek Nature Center offers wildlife observation and educational programs. Explore These Fun Outdoor Activities in Cedar Rapids, IACedar Rapids is a Pickleball ParadiseExplore the Vast Network of Trails in Cedar Rapids
sections.restaurants: More than 60% of restaurants in Cedar Rapids are locally owned, and the city’s love of delicious food is evident. Many independent eateries are highlighted during the annual Cedar Rapids Restaurant Week, showcasing unique culinary offerings. The craft brewery scene is particularly vibrant, with community hubs like Iowa Brewing, Lion Bridge Brewing and Quarter Barrel offering craft beers, events and live music, while Big Grove Brewery and nearby establishments like Cedar Ridge Winery & Distillery and Millstream Brewing Co. contribute to the region’s beverage diversity. What is one of the best places to eat in Cedar Rapids, IA? Cobble Hill on Second Street is a locally-owned and operated contemporary American restaurant boasting everything from seriously delicious craft cocktails to decadent starters and even tastier entrees. Seasonality and availability drive the menu, so don’t be surprised when the offerings change. What is the best coffee shop in Cedar Rapids, IA? Located in the super hip NewBo District, Brewhemia is the place to get your morning caffeine hit. They also have Thrillist’s top-ranked breakfast burrito. Where is the best place for happy hour in Cedar Rapids, IA? Zeppelins Bar & Grill is a great place to grab a drink on the city’s northeast side. There are more than two dozen beers on tap and plenty of different wines and cocktails to choose from. Hungry? Where to Eat in Cedar Rapids6 Must-Try Breweries in Cedar Rapids, IAFeast on Fabulous Farm-to-table Fare in Iowa
sections.education: Local students are served by both the College Community School District and Cedar Rapids Community School District (CRCSD), the area’s primary district. Cedar Rapids Community Schools is the second-largest school district in Iowa and offers a range of programs and schools, including magnet schools with specialized themes. Private schooling options include religious, college prep and specialized options. IA-davenport.description: As Iowa's capital city, Davenport is home to several charming neighborhoods, a lively downtown and a burgeoning arts scene, offering residents a high quality of life and affordability. From riverfront parks to festivals, Davenport fosters a welcoming community.

sections.location: Davenport is a riverfront city perched in eastern Iowa, about 180 miles west of Chicago, where Interstate 80 and U.S. Route 61 meet along the mighty Mississippi. The nearest airport, Quad City International Airport, is just 10 miles south, a midsize hub with flights to Chicago, Dallas and beyond. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Chicago, IL, is just 180 miles east, where you can cruise the river to admire architecture, visit the Art Institute or root for the Cubs at Wrigley Field. Cedar Rapids sits 65 miles northwest, home to the National Czech & Slovak Museum, NewBo City Market and the Paramount Theatre. Des Moines is roughly 170 miles west, boasting the Iowa State Capitol, the Pappajohn Sculpture Park and the Des Moines Art Center.
sections.economy: Davenport is a midsize city with a steady, diversified economic market downtown and a thriving agricultural border. The largest of the Quad Cities, Davenport has a diverse economy with a mix of industries, including manufacturing, health care and retail trade. Genesis Health System, Davenport Community School District and Hy-Vee, a large grocery store chain, are all significant employers in town. Both Kraft Heinz and John Deere have manufacturing facilities in the area. And, if you’re new to town, Connect with an Iowan helps residents find opportunities that match their career goals and interests. The cost of living in Davenport is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Davenport is $164,976, and the average household income is $89,195. Iowa residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Davenport, IA? Genesis Health System’s Genesis Medical Center-Davenport-West Central Park is one of the highest-ranked hospitals in the area. Other major health care providers in the area include UnityPoint Health-Trinity, Mississippi Valley Surgery Center and Vera French Community Mental Health Center are other top providers.
sections.thingsToDo: Davenport is full of fun things to do. The downtown area is full of activity, featuring the Freight House Farmers’ Market and an entertainment district. Cultural sites like the Figge Art Museum, Putnam Museum & Science Center and the Adler Theater are all popular spots. Historical sites, including the German American Heritage Center, offer information on the city’s history. The Vander Veer Botanical Park is 33 acres of beautiful blooms and is believed to be the first botanical garden west of the Mississippi. The Riverwalk and Riverfront district provide the perfect backdrop of the Mighty Mississippi. Davenport residents can enjoy various events and festivals, like Red, White & BOOM! and the Beaux Arts Festival. Catch a game at Modern Woodmen Park, home of the River Bandits baseball team, or explore the city’s unique landmarks, like the Davenport Skybridge. What is something you can do for free in Davenport, IA? Take a walk on the Skybridge, a skywalk with an aerial riverfront view. At night, the color-changing LED lights make the experience extra magical. What are some things to do in Davenport, IA, with kids? Visit the Putnam Museum and Science Center, where science galleries, an Egyptian exhibit, and fossils and minerals on display pique the curiosity of little ones. Afterward, burn off some energy at Fejervary Park, which has a dinosaur-dig sandbox, water wall, spider web climber, and so much more. Where do locals shop in Davenport, IA? The Freight House Farmers’ Market has 150 vendors, chef demos and live music and is a great place to spend a morning browsing. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Davenport, IA? The arts are alive and well here. The Adler and Capitol Theatres are two of the city’s premier destinations for seeing concerts, theater and other artistic expressions.
sections.outdoors: Explore the great outdoors in Davenport, where many recreational activities are centered around the riverfront and parks. Residents can walk or bike the Mississippi Riverfront Trail and Duck Creek Parkway or visit one of the dozens of parks around town, like Sunderbruch Park. The Mississippi River provides spots for fishing, boating and kayaking. Parks like Fejervary Park & Aquatic Center and West Lake Park offer family-friendly fun with splash pads and green spaces. Residents can play disc golf at Credit Island Park or stroll along the Mississippi Riverfront Trail. Davenport has a robust park system, with more than 200 sunny days a year, so residents can capitalize on spending time out and about. Seasonal activities are available, with opportunities to enjoy the riverfront and sports facilities, ensuring that local residents can stay active year-round.
sections.restaurants: Davenport dining is delicious, from farm-to-table restaurants and bistros like Duck City Bistro and The Half Nelson to casual cafés and breweries like Front Street Brewery. Local spots like The Machine Shed offer farm-style meals, while places like Miss Phay Café feature Vietnamese dishes. The Mississippi River influences the cuisine, providing plenty of seafood options. Craft beer is available at various breweries, and unique experiences can be found at Cafe d’Marie for artisanal coffee and Me & Billy Kitchen and Bar for craft cocktails. What is one of the best places to eat in Davenport, IA? Head to The Half Nelson, a stylish restaurant with crab beignets, pistachio-crusted fish, steaks and Korean fried cauliflower. What is the best coffee shop in Davenport, IA? Grab an espresso from the drive-thru window at Main Street Coffee. The Davenport staple also serves jumbo muffins, breakfast burritos and more. Where is the best happy hour in Davenport, IA? Raw has a couple of drink specials throughout the week, including buy-one-get-one whiskey and tequila nights.
sections.education: Davenport’s top-notch school system and inviting people make it an easy enough place to establish deep and rich connections within the community. Davenport Community School District is one of the largest in the state — more than 14,000 students are enrolled. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable 14:1 student-to-teacher ratio. There are several private schools located here, too, and most are religiously affiliated. Rivermont Collegiate, a private non-sectarian school, is known for its rigorous academics and college preparation. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: Genesis Health System’s Genesis Medical Center-Davenport-West Central Park is one of the highest-ranked hospitals in the area. Other major health care providers in the area include UnityPoint Health-Trinity, Mississippi Valley Surgery Center and Vera French Community Mental Health Center are other top providers.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: Take a walk on the Skybridge, a skywalk with an aerial riverfront view. At night, the color-changing LED lights make the experience extra magical.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Davenport, IA, with kids?
faqs.a: Visit the Putnam Museum and Science Center, where science galleries, an Egyptian exhibit, and fossils and minerals on display pique the curiosity of little ones. Afterward, burn off some energy at Fejervary Park, which has a dinosaur-dig sandbox, water wall, spider web climber, and so much more.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: The Freight House Farmers’ Market has 150 vendors, chef demos and live music and is a great place to spend a morning browsing.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: The arts are alive and well here. The Adler and Capitol Theatres are two of the city’s premier destinations for seeing concerts, theater and other artistic expressions.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: Head to The Half Nelson, a stylish restaurant with crab beignets, pistachio-crusted fish, steaks and Korean fried cauliflower.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: Grab an espresso from the drive-thru window at Main Street Coffee. The Davenport staple also serves jumbo muffins, breakfast burritos and more.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: Raw has a couple of drink specials throughout the week, including buy-one-get-one whiskey and tequila nights.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Davenport, IA?
faqs.a: Several smaller colleges and universities in the city, including St. Ambrose University and Eastern Iowa Community College.

IA-iowa-city.description: Calling all creatives! Iowa City has cultivated an inviting and creative atmosphere, from its prestigious university to its rich literary legacy. There are plenty of outdoor activities, countless ways to flex your creative muscle and a low cost of living.

sections.location: Iowa City is a cultural hub in eastern Iowa, right along Interstate 80. It’s home to the University of Iowa, and the nearest airport, Eastern Iowa Airport in Cedar Rapids, is about 25 miles north, which is a quick drive for flights. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Cedar Rapids is just 25 miles north, where you can visit the National Czech & Slovak Museum or catch a show at the Paramount Theatre. Des Moines sits 115 miles west, offering the Iowa State Capitol and the Pappajohn Sculpture Park. Davenport is about 55 miles east, home to the Figge Art Museum and the River Music Experience.
sections.economy: Iowa City’s diverse economy includes various industries, including education, health care and government. The largest employer is the University of Iowa. Other major employers are the Iowa City Community School District, Proctor & Gamble and ACT, Inc. (yes, that ACT!), which is headquartered here. And, if you’re new to town, Connect with an Iowan helps residents find opportunities that match their career goals and interests. The cost of living in Iowa City is lower than the national average. The median home value in Iowa City is $260,681, and the average household income is $93,380. Iowa residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Iowa City, IA? Local residents are in good health, thanks mainly to the nationally ranked University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics, which also operates the nationally ranked Stead Family Children’s Hospital. Mercy Iowa City and UnityPoint Health are also major providers in the area. Iowa City Hospitals Care for the CommunityHow Tech Companies Are Transforming Iowa City’s Business ClimateIowa City Breeds Innovative Entrepreneurs
sections.thingsToDo: Iowa City residents can enjoy plenty of activities, from cultural to family-friendly. The city’s arts scene features the Englert Theatre, FilmScene and the Hancher Auditorium, hosting a variety of performances and events. As mentioned, the city is for literary lovers, and the Prairie Lights bookstore and The Literary Walk are must-visits for bookworms. Art lovers can visit the Stanley Museum of Art and enjoy the plentiful public murals throughout downtown. Iowa City is community-oriented, hosting numerous annual festivals, like the Iowa Arts Festival, Mission Creek Festival and the Iowa City Book Festival. Looking for family-friendly fun? The Iowa Children’s Museum and Iowa City Public Library are kid-approved. Of course, Iowa City is a college town, so sports enthusiasts can experience the excitement of Hawkeyes games and spend a Saturday at Kinnick Stadium. History buffs will appreciate the University of Iowa Museum of Natural History in town and nearby museums like the Johnson County Historical Society Museum and the Herbert Hoover Presidential Library and Museum. Plenty of spots for shopping and dining are around town, especially at the Pedestrian Mall. Visit the Iowa City Farmers Market for fresh produce and artisanal goods. What is something you can do for free in Iowa City, IA? Visit the Iowa Old Capitol Building on the University of Iowa campus. This historic building, which opened in 1842, served as the state capitol from 1846 to 1857 and now operates as a museum. What are some things to do with kids in Iowa City, IA? Pick strawberries, apples, pumpkins and more at Wilson’s Orchard and Farm. The farm also has a smokehouse where you can grab lunch and a Farm Market to pick up more produce. Where do locals shop in Iowa City, IA? Check out the local goods sold at Iowa City Farmers Market (May through October). Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Iowa City, IA? The Englert Theatre is a popular performing arts venue in the downtown area. Initially built in 1912, this self-described “vaudeville-era playhouse” seats 725 people and hosts incredible shows and performances all year round. Here’s Where to Find the Most Heartwarming Tradition in College Sports8 Reasons to Move to Iowa City, IowaIowa City: The Greatest Small City for the Arts
sections.outdoors: Love the outdoors? So do people who live in Iowa City! The city has a network of over 60 parks, trails and open spaces spanning 1,320 acres. Local favorites include spots like Hickory Hill Park, with its wooded trails and open meadows, and Terry Trueblood Recreation Area, where visitors can enjoy kayaking, paddleboarding and biking along Sand Lake. Lake Macbride State Park is an excellent spot for boating, fishing and hiking, while the Iowa River Corridor Trail offers waterfront walking and biking routes. Beyond the parks and waterways, Iowa City residents enjoy outdoor exploration through biking, and the Iowa City Cycling Club provides camaraderie and route inspo. Residents can also appreciate that this city has several dog-friendly spots, like Thornberry Off-Leash Dog Park. Additionally, the city features agritourism destinations, offering seasonal opportunities to experience Iowa’s farms and orchards.
sections.restaurants: Take a bite out of this city's diverse dining scene, which focuses on farm-to-table dining, unique flavors and a thriving craft beer culture. Local favorites include the Pullman Bar & Diner for brunch and Hamburg Inn No. 2 for a shake. The farm-to-table movement is anchored by spots like Trumpet Blossom Cafe, which emphasizes fresh, seasonal dishes, and the Iowa City Farmers Market, which highlights local produce and goods. Craft beer enthusiasts can enjoy local brews at Big Grove Brewery or sip coffee at independent cafes like The Java House. Unique spots like the divey rooftop bar at Joe’s Place add to the city’s eclectic culinary landscape. What is one of the best places to eat in Iowa City, IA? Slurp ramen and share sashimi, short ribs and pot stickers at Ramen Belly. What is the best coffee shop in Iowa City, IA? Fix! is the coffee shop inside The Chauncey, a high-rise in the downtown area. The cozy space makes it a great spot to work or gather with a group. Where is the best place for happy hour in Iowa City, IA? You can’t go wrong with happy hour at Micky’s Irish Pub on Dubuque Street. This long-loved staple offers plenty of craft brews, whiskeys and cocktails. 8 Reasons Why Iowa City Is the World’s Best Breakfast Destination
sections.education: Iowa City often ranks among the top college towns in the country, even coming in fifth, according to the American Institute for Economic Research; however, that doesn’t mean that this city doesn’t offer endless educational opportunities for young families, too. Iowa City Community School District is the fourth-largest district in Iowa, serving nearly 15,000 students. The district is also nationally recognized for its commitment to diversity and academic excellence, with students speaking more than 60 languages. Iowa City and the surrounding areas offer a dozen private schooling options, each known for their strong academics and small class sizes. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: Local residents are in good health, thanks mainly to the nationally ranked University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics, which also operates the nationally ranked Stead Family Children’s Hospital. Mercy Iowa City and UnityPoint Health are also major providers in the area.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: Visit the Iowa Old Capitol Building on the University of Iowa campus. This historic building, which opened in 1842, served as the state capitol from 1846 to 1857 and now operates as a museum.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: Pick strawberries, apples, pumpkins and more at Wilson’s Orchard and Farm. The farm also has a smokehouse where you can grab lunch and a Farm Market to pick up more produce.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: Check out the local goods sold at Iowa City Farmers Market (May through October).

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: The Englert Theatre is a popular performing arts venue in the downtown area. Initially built in 1912, this self-described “vaudeville-era playhouse” seats 725 people and hosts incredible shows and performances all year round.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: Slurp ramen and share sashimi, short ribs and pot stickers at Ramen Belly.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: Fix! is the coffee shop inside The Chauncey, a high-rise in the downtown area. The cozy space makes it a great spot to work or gather with a group.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for happy hour in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: You can’t go wrong with happy hour at Micky’s Irish Pub on Dubuque Street. This long-loved staple offers plenty of craft brews, whiskeys and cocktails.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Iowa City, IA?
faqs.a: There are several colleges and universities here, including the University of Iowa and Kirkwood Community College’s Iowa City Campus.

ID-boise.description: Located in the picturesque Treasure Valley, Boise is known as a crown jewel. Idaho's capital city is known for its strong job market, safety and affordability. This friendly city has no shortage of things to do, and Boise residents live a balanced life.

sections.economy: --> --> --> Weather in Boise, ID Average Temperatures 64 high / 42 low Average Annual Rainfall 13 in Average Annual Snowfall 19 in

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Boise?
faqs.a: Boise is a four-season destination with 200-plus days of sunshine and surrounding foothills that beckon skiers. However, with a high-desert climate, the days heat up quickly, and the nights cool down fast. The average highs and lows are 90°F and 55°F (summer) and 42°F and 24°F (winter).

faqs.q: Does Boise get a lot of snow?
faqs.a: The average annual snowfall in Boise is 19 inches. In winter, Boise typically experiences a mix of rain and snow, with the heaviest snowfall occurring between December and February.

faqs.q: Does Boise get a lot of rain?
faqs.a: The average annual rainfall in Boise is below the national average at just 13 inches a year.

faqs.q: How many sunny days does Boise get?
faqs.a: Boise averages 206 sunny days a year.

faqs.q: What are some things to do outside in Boise?
faqs.a: Just 16 miles outside the city, Bogus Basin is a popular spot for close-to-home skiing and snowboarding. In the summer months, it's a mountainous playground with a coaster, horseback riding trails and a bungee trampoline.

faqs.q: What is something free to do in Boise?
faqs.a: Anyone interested in art will appreciate Freak Alley Gallery, an outdoor urban gallery with a variety of eclectic street art and one of the best public art spaces in the country. You can also pay a visit to the Boise Art Museum Sculpture Garden outside the Boise Art Museum. If you want something a little more outside the box, you can take a walking tour around the exterior of the Old Idaho Penitentiary Historic Site.

faqs.q: What are some fun things to do with kids in Boise?
faqs.a: Kids will love the Aquarium of Boise, often described as "small but mighty," with more than 250 species of marine life ranging from sharks and stingrays to puffins and parrots.

faqs.q: What is the best open secret about Boise?
faqs.a: If you've ever bought groceries from Albertsons, you can thank Boise. Longtime Boise businessman Joe Albertson founded the beloved chain.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Boise?
faqs.a: Fervent readers will love Rediscovered Books, which has a “Human Rights Book Club” and puts the spotlight on talented Idaho authors.

faqs.q: What is the best restaurant in Boise?
faqs.a: Àlavita, an Italian restaurant that sources much of its menu from local farmers and ranchers, is a favorite spot to gather. Save room for a scoop of gelato or an affogato.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Boise?
faqs.a: A historic venue in downtown Boise, The Egyptian Theatre is known for its exquisite architecture and wide range of events, including concerts, film screenings and live performances.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Boise?
faqs.a: Bring your brainy co-workers and friends to Clairvoyant Brewing Company for trivia night or the $4 beer of the week.

faqs.q: What are the three largest industries in Boise?
faqs.a: Technology, manufacturing and health care dominate Boise's diverse industry. Micron Technology, Boise State University and HP, Inc. are major employers in the area. In addition, the Albertsons grocery chain is headquartered in Boise.

faqs.q: What is the sales tax rate in Boise?
faqs.a: In 2024, the sales tax rate in Boise is 6%.

faqs.q: Does Idaho have a state income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, Idaho has a state income tax.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Boise?
faqs.a: Boise is home to several major health care providers, including St. Luke's Health System, Saint Alphonsus Health System and Primary Health Medical Group. St. Luke's and St. Alphonsus Medical Centers are the number one and two ranked hospitals in the Boise area, respectively.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Boise?
faqs.a: There are several colleges and universities in Boise, ID; the most notable is Boise State University.

ID-meridian.description: Meridian’s location in the Treasure Valley means residents have hit the jackpot. A friendly community, excellent schools, family-focused events and abundant parks and outdoor spaces contribute to Meridian’s high quality of life and close-knit community feel.

sections.economy: --> --> Read the Magazine --> Weather in Meridian, ID Average Temperatures 65 high / 40 low Average Annual Rainfall 12 in Average Annual Snowfall 15 in

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Meridian?
faqs.a: With its semi-arid climate, Meridian gets hot, dry summers with temps that can reach the triple digits. In the winter, it sometimes snows. But it doesn’t rain much here, and spring and fall are usually comfortable. The average highs and lows are 85°F and 57°F (summer) and 32°F and 21°F.

faqs.q: Does Meridian get a lot of snow?
faqs.a: The average annual snowfall in Meridian is 15 inches per year.

faqs.q: Does Meridian get a lot of rain?
faqs.a: The average annual rainfall in Meridian is around 12 inches.

faqs.q: How many sunny days does Meridian get?
faqs.a: Meridian averages 210 sunny days a year.

faqs.q: What are some things to do outside in Meridian?
faqs.a: Just about everything! Meridian’s vast natural resources combined with its parks (including a state park and a water park) and ski areas allow endless possibilities for outdoor adventure, from active sports to just relaxing in nature.

faqs.q: What is something free to do in Meridian?
faqs.a: There are plenty of free activities in Meridian. Julius M. Kleiner Memorial Park is an excellent hangout for families, and thrill seekers enjoy free admission at Meridian Speedway on certain days or for specific events. Strolling downtown with its local shops and community events is another great way to pass the time.

faqs.q: What are some fun things to do with kids in Meridian?
faqs.a: Bring the little ones to The Village at Meridian, a mall with a European feel, and so much more – it also has movies, restaurants, an arcade, a play area and a "show fountain." And, if they need to run off some energy, there are plenty of places. Beyond its parks and green spaces, the city is situated near the Boise River and foothills, making it a great place for people interested in hiking, biking and water sports.

faqs.q: What is the best open secret about Meridian?
faqs.a: Since 1994, Meridian has held the title of the fastest-growing city in Idaho.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Meridian?
faqs.a: Artisans of Idaho is all about Idahoans supporting Idahoans. This self-professed “local artisan community marketplace” brings together area vendors to sell their handcrafted gifts and artwork to fellow locals.

faqs.q: What is a fun restaurant in Meridian?
faqs.a: If you want a fun night out, Epi's Basque Restaurant is a must-see for locals and tourists alike. The menu features dishes like paella, lamb stew and chorizo, all of which earn two thumbs up.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Meridian?
faqs.a: The Treasure Valley Children's Theater works to empower and inspire youth interested in the performing arts through classes, camps and workshops. Revolution Concert House hosts several concerts and events in nearby Garden City throughout the year.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Meridian?
faqs.a: Downtown’s Eighty Thirty Common offers a happy hour five days a week, which includes everything from wine and beer to cocktails and small bites.

faqs.q: What are the three largest industries in Meridian?
faqs.a: Meridian has three key industries: health care, education, and retail and manufacturing. For example, Scentsy, a wickless candle and fragrance company, is headquartered in Meridian. Other major employers include Blue Cross of Idaho and Jacksons Food Stores.

faqs.q: What is the sales tax rate in Meridian?
faqs.a: In 2024, the sales tax rate in Meridian is 6%.

faqs.q: Does Idaho have a state income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, Idaho has a state income tax.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Meridian?
faqs.a: Meridian is part of the Boise metropolitan area, and the city has several top-tier health care facilities, including Saint Alphonsus Medical Center - Meridian and St. Luke's Meridian Medical Center.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Meridian?
faqs.a: Several smaller colleges and universities are in Meridian, including Boise State University West Campus and the College of Western Idaho. Other larger colleges are nearby, including the Boise State University flagship campus in Boise and the University of Idaho in Moscow.

ID-nampa.description: Affordable housing, friendly neighborhoods and a strong sense of safety contribute to Nampa's reputation as a great place to live, work and play. A booming economy and small-town charm add to the appeal of this Idaho gem.

sections.location: Nampa is a growing gem in southwestern Idaho, about 10 miles west of Boise, along Interstate 84. The nearest airport, Boise Airport, is 10 miles east, offering flights to Seattle, Denver and beyond. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some larger nearby cities: Boise sits 10 miles east, boasting the Idaho State Capitol, the Boise Art Museum, and the Boise River Greenbelt. Meridian is just 5 miles east, where you can visit Settlers Park, the Meridian Symphony Orchestra and the vibrant downtown area. Caldwell is roughly 15 miles west, home of the College of Idaho, Indian Creek Plaza and the Caldwell Night Rode
sections.economy: The job market here has been growing steadily in recent years, with health care, manufacturing, food processing, technology and agriculture as the main sectors. Its location in the heart of Idaho’s agricultural region with fertile farmland and sunny weather makes agricultural products ripe for the picking. Generations of families have farmed the land, establishing a strong agrarian tradition. Some of the city's largest employers include the Nampa School District, St. Luke’s Nampa Medical Center and the City of Nampa. The cost of living in Nampa is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Nampa is $393,920, and the average household income is $91,316. Idaho residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Nampa, ID? Nampa’s proximity to Boise gives residents access to an extensive network of providers; however, there are plenty of great options right at home. St. Alphonsus Medical Center-Nampa and St. Luke’s Nampa Hospital rank highly in several specialties. Why I Moved My Business to Nampa, ID (and You Can, Too!)
sections.thingsToDo: Newbies are quick to learn there are plenty of things to do in Nampa. Downtown Nampa is a hub of activity and fun, with unique restaurants, public art and performance arts venues alongside breweries, coffee shops and boutiques. The city’s Western roots are celebrated at the Ford Idaho Horse Park, and the renowned Snake River Stampede Rodeo held annually at the Ford Idaho Center. For those interested in arts and culture, the Nampa Festival of the Arts and Heartland Studios showcase local artisans, while the Nampa Civic Center hosts theater productions and community events. Families can enjoy the Nampa Farmers Market for fresh produce and local goods and explore museums like the Warhawk Air Museum, the Nampa Train Depot Museum and the Canyon County Historical Museum, which offer insights into the region’s history. Venues like the Ford Idaho Center host concerts and sporting events, and the Hispanic Cultural Center of Idaho celebrates Hispanic heritage. What is something you can do for free in Nampa, ID? Spend some summer days at the city’s great pools, like Lakeview Waterpark, which has a two-story slide and fun spray features, or the Midway Splash Park, which has a windmill that sprays water. What are some things to do in Nampa, ID, with kids? One of Nampa’s most notable green spaces is Orah Brandt Park. Built in phases since 2018, the park features an ADA-inclusive playground with swings, slides and climbing areas. It also has walking paths, a splash pad and a rose garden, and plans are to add a nine-hole disc golf course, amphitheater and swimming pool. Where do locals shop in Nampa, ID? Everything sold at the Nampa Farmers Market is grown within a 100-mile radius of the city and picked at peak freshness. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Nampa, ID? Catch plays, musicals and even the occasional dog talent show at the Nampa Civic Center. 9 Reasons to Move to Nampa, IdahoNampa, Idaho: Meet the Hub of the ValleyLooking For a New Place to Live? Nampa Checks All the Boxes
sections.outdoors: Nampa’s never-ending outdoor activities appeal to all ages. The city boasts 27 parks and over 14 miles of pedestrian pathways, providing ample space for walking, jogging and biking. Parks like Lakeview Park, Lions Park and West Park offer playgrounds, sports fields and picnic areas, while the Nampa Dog Park provides a space for canine companions to play. Water enthusiasts can enjoy fishing and wildlife viewing at Wilson Springs Ponds and explore the scenic Lake Lowell at the Deer Flat National Wildlife Refuge. For more adventure, the nearby mountains have mountain biking and rock climbing, and there are ski resorts in the winter. The city also features golf courses like Centennial Golf Course and Ridgecrest Golf Club, and parks like Midway Park offer futsal courts. The Nampa Greenbelt provides a scenic pathway through the city, and the Nampa Recreation Center offers indoor activities like swimming and climbing. Explore the Great Outdoors in Nampa, IDPlay Time! Ready to Have Fun in Nampa, ID?
sections.restaurants: Nampa’s food scene is growing and changing, mixing local farm-fresh food with different types of restaurants, such as farm-to-table spots like Brick 29 Bistro, which uses seasonal ingredients, and comfort food places like The Tower Grill and LeBaron’s Honker Café, with big meals and pies. Nampa also has excellent Mexican food at places like Andrade’s and La Pantera Market, and steakhouses like Jaker’s Bar & Grill. The city’s craft beverage scene is also gaining popularity, with breweries like Crescent Brewery and distilleries like Koenig Distillery offering locally made beers and spirits. Nampa features international flavors from Vietnamese and Thai restaurants, as well as a thriving coffee and bakery culture with spots like Flying M Coffee Garage. Local, family-owned establishments like Mesa Tacos + Tequila add to Nampa’s unique culinary charm. What is one of the best places to eat in Nampa, ID? Holy Cow Idaho is known for its wings, burgers and creative appetizers (think beer-battered French fries and mac ’n’ cheese egg rolls). What is the best coffee shop in Nampa, ID? Flying M is a beloved coffee spot in the Boise metro area and was opened by local natives. Flying M is located inside a renovated car garage and houses the coffee shop, espresso and pastry bars, and even a roasting and tasting room. Where is the best happy hour in Nampa, ID? Craft Lounge serves classic, seasonal and nonalcoholic cocktails and also teaches cocktail classes. The bar’s happy hours are pretty generous, with all-day deals on Monday and happy hour deals from 3 to 6 p.m. Tuesday through Saturday. Where Is the Gateway to Idaho Wine Country?
sections.education: Residents have access to a variety of quality educational offerings. Nampa School District serves a majority of the city’s students. Several charter schools in the area offer different focuses and philosophies. Three of the best charter schools in Nampa are Idaho Arts, Legacy and Liberty Charter Schools. There are also several private options here, ranging from religiously affiliated to secular schools. One thing to consider about schools here is that Idaho has open enrollment policies, allowing families to apply to schools outside their assigned district, including charter schools. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: Nampa’s proximity to Boise gives residents access to an extensive network of providers; however, there are plenty of great options right at home. St. Alphonsus Medical Center-Nampa and St. Luke’s Nampa Hospital rank highly in several specialties.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: Spend some summer days at the city’s great pools, like Lakeview Waterpark, which has a two-story slide and fun spray features, or the Midway Splash Park, which has a windmill that sprays water.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Nampa, ID, with kids?
faqs.a: One of Nampa’s most notable green spaces is Orah Brandt Park. Built in phases since 2018, the park features an ADA-inclusive playground with swings, slides and climbing areas. It also has walking paths, a splash pad and a rose garden, and plans are to add a nine-hole disc golf course, amphitheater and swimming pool.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: Everything sold at the Nampa Farmers Market is grown within a 100-mile radius of the city and picked at peak freshness.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: Catch plays, musicals and even the occasional dog talent show at the Nampa Civic Center.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: Holy Cow Idaho is known for its wings, burgers and creative appetizers (think beer-battered French fries and mac ’n’ cheese egg rolls).

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: Flying M is a beloved coffee spot in the Boise metro area and was opened by local natives. Flying M is located inside a renovated car garage and houses the coffee shop, espresso and pastry bars, and even a roasting and tasting room.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: Craft Lounge serves classic, seasonal and nonalcoholic cocktails and also teaches cocktail classes. The bar’s happy hours are pretty generous, with all-day deals on Monday and happy hour deals from 3 to 6 p.m. Tuesday through Saturday.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Nampa, ID?
faqs.a: There are a handful of smaller colleges and universities in the city, like the College of Western Idaho; however, many more options are within driving distance, like Boise State University.

IL-bloomington.description: Surrounded by gorgeous green spaces and sweeping vistas, Bloomington offers a tranquil setting in a city with plenty of things to do. A strong economy, excellent schools and a thriving arts scene contribute to residents' high quality of life in this tight-knit community.

sections.location: Bloomington is a friendly, vibrant city in central Illinois along Interstate 55, about halfway between Chicago and St. Louis. The closest airport, Central Illinois Regional Airport, is right in town, making travel a snap. When you’re set to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Peoria is just 40 miles west, where you can visit the Riverfront Museum, explore the Caterpillar Center or cheer at Dozer Park. Springfield sits 65 miles southwest, offering the Lincoln Presidential Library, State Capitol and Washington Park. Champaign is about 50 miles southeast, buzzing with University of Illinois energy, Krannert Art Museum and Meadowbrook Park.
sections.economy: Young professionals find work-balance satisfaction with the combination of significant employers and a lower-than-average cost of living. Insurance, education and manufacturing are major industries that help shape the local economy. State Farm Insurance is headquartered here, and other major employers include Illinois State University, Rivian and COUNTRY Financial. The cost of living in Bloomington is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Bloomington is $195,487, and the average household income is $108,649. Illinois residents pay a state income tax, and those living in Bloomington can expect an 8.75% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Bloomington, IL? The city is home to several health care providers, including OSF St. Joseph Medical Center, which U.S. News & World Report recognizes as a high performer in two adult procedures and conditions — heart and kidney failure. Other major providers include Advocate Health Care and Carle BroMenn Medical Center, a Level II Trauma Center.
sections.thingsToDo: History buffs can explore the David Davis Mansion State Historic Site and the McLean County Museum of History, while art enthusiasts will appreciate visiting galleries and attending performances at the Bloomington Center for the Performing Arts and the Illinois Shakespeare Festival. For family-friendly fun, there’s ample entertainment at Miller Park Zoo, Children’s Discovery Museum and Grady’s Family Fun Park. Beyond its arts scene, the city has a fun downtown area with many unique shops. Throughout the summer, Bloomington connects residents through outdoor concerts and events, like a producers-only farmers market and First Fridays. What is something you can do for free in Bloomington, IL? The McClean County Museum of History is always free during the Downtown Bloomington Farmers’ Market on Saturday morning. The iconic building in the heart of downtown features five permanent and two rotating exhibit galleries with nearly 20,000 objects preserved at the museum. What are some things to do with kids in Bloomington, IL? The Children’s Discovery Museum in nearby Normal includes three stories full of exhibits and encourages children to interact at all ages and phases, from toddlers to pre-teens. Where do locals shop in Bloomington, IL? Independent bookstore Bobzbay sells new, vintage and gently used books in the downtown area. Visitors can trade in books for store credit, shop for novelties and attend events, including poetry readings and book signings. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Bloomington, IL? The Bloomington Center for the Performing Arts (BCPA) is a favorite performing arts center in the community. BCPA hosts concerts, theatrical productions and dance events like The Nutcracker throughout the year. Another beloved spot is The Castle Theatre, a historic venue hosting concerts and events for over 100 years.
sections.outdoors: Centered around its extensive park system and natural landscapes, Bloomington is booming with outdoor activities. The Constitution Trail spans 45 miles and is a popular spot for walking, jogging, cycling and rollerblading, connecting people to various parks and neighborhoods around town. Residents can participate in seasonal activities at places like Rader Family Farms, where they have pumpkin picking and corn mazes, or explore the natural beauty of Sugar Grove Nature Center and Tipton Park, which have many trails and wildlife observations. Golf enthusiasts can play a round at several courses, like The Den at Fox Creek. Families will love spending time at Miller Park Zoo. There are also plenty of ways to get on the water here, too. Boating and fishing at Evergreen Lake, disc golf at Maxwell Park and hiking at Moraine View State Recreation Area will keep residents busy. Ewing and Franklin Parks offer serene settings for relaxation and picnics. With over 60 parks and easy access to Lake Bloomington, residents have many ways to engage in outdoor recreation and enjoy the natural surroundings.
sections.restaurants: Residents are well-fed, with innovative farm-to-table fare and traditional tastes. Local establishments like Epiphany Farms Restaurant showcase seasonal dishes sourced directly from their farms. Some creature comforts include Lucca Grill, Rosie’s Pub, El Porton’s and Biaggi’s Italian. Beyond the restaurants, locals can take advantage of the growing beverage scene. Destihl Brewery offers craft beers, and Mackinaw Valley Vineyard provides local wines. The Eat Local BN community initiative promotes local eateries and fosters a sense of culinary community. What is one of the best places to eat in Bloomington, IL? Boasting a menu filled with seasonal eats prepared in farm-to-table style, Epiphany Farms Restaurant is one of the city's most beloved dinner spots. Try out their CSA program if you love it so much that you can’t get enough. What is the best coffee shop in Bloomington, IL? Locally owned Coffee Hound is dedicated to quality, craft-roasted coffees in Uptown Normal and Downtown Bloomington. Where is the best place for happy hour in Bloomington, IL? With a selection of cold brews, martinis and Rosie’s Rum Punch, plus a choice of burgers and flatbreads, it’s no wonder Rosie’s Pub gets high marks as a favorite place to meet.
sections.education: Local families can take advantage of the Bloomington Public Schools District, which provides comprehensive education to all students from elementary school to high school graduation. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable 14:1 student-to-teacher ratio. The city also has a great selection of private schools, many of which are religiously affiliated. Bloom Community School in nearby Normal is a progressive, independent private school that is a great option, too. faqs.a: There are several colleges and universities located here, including Illinois Wesleyan University. Illinois State University and Heartland Community College are in the greater Bloomington-Normal area.

IL-champaign.description: The Fighting Illini know what makes Champaign, IL, so special. This college town's emphasis on top-tier educational institutions has helped create ample job opportunities and a thriving economy. Champaign residents can enjoy affordable housing, outstanding arts and plenty of Midwestern charm.

sections.location: Champaign is a vibrant city in east-central Illinois, approximately 135 miles southwest of Chicago, easily accessible via I-74 and I-57. University of Illinois Willard Airport is just 6 miles from downtown, offering convenient flights via American Airlines. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Chicago, IL, is roughly 135 miles northeast, where you can cruise the river to admire architecture, visit the Art Institute or root for the Cubs at Wrigley Field. Indianapolis, IN, sits 100 miles southeast, boasting the Indy 500, the Children’s Museum and downtown’s cultural district. St. Louis, MO, is 200 miles southwest, where you can marvel at the Gateway Arch, wander the Missouri Botanical Garden or catch a Cardinals game.
sections.economy: The city's economy is as robust as the city’s residents. The local landscape is driven by its role as a significant educational and technological hub in the state. It's anchored by the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, which serves as a steady source of employment and an engine for research and innovation. The technology sector includes Research Park and several high-tech companies, and the health care, retail and manufacturing industries are also growing. The stable economy here also makes Champaign an excellent place for entrepreneurs to launch their businesses. Some of the largest employers in the area are the University of Illinois at Ubana-Champaign, Carle and Champaign Unit 4 School District. Residents looking to connect to the community can fill out the Champaign-Urbana Team’s Community Job Application, which will help them connect with a Welcome Crew member or even join a New to CU Mixer event. The cost of living in Champaign is lower than the national average. The median home value in Champaign is $199,871, and the average household income is $87,780. Illinois residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Champaign, IL? The Champaign-Urbana area is home to several health care providers, including the Carle Foundation Hospital, one of the best hospitals in the state overall. They are a leading health care provider, offering patients a wide range of medical services. Other providers include the OSF HealthCare Heart of Mary Medical Center and Presence Covenant Medical Center. I Grew My Business in Champaign, IL, and You Should, Too9 Reasons To Launch Your Startup in Champaign, ILI Came to Champaign, IL, for College and Stayed for My Career
sections.thingsToDo: Cheers to life in Champaign! The city pulses with a creative energy fueled by the university. There is a concentrated dose of arts and entertainment for residents to enjoy. Museums, often free, are part of the city’s landscape, alongside public art and a year-round calendar of live music and festivals. The downtown area is a hub for shopping experiences like Jane Addams Book Shop and The Literary, vinyl record stores like Exile on Main St., and creative reuse marketplaces like The Idea Store. There are plenty of cultural celebrations year-round, too, like Diwali and Lunar New Year, which help foster a community-forward Champaign. The arts scene is supported by organizations like 40 North, and public art installations like the “Outside of Ordinary” mural complement the vibrant Market at the Square. The city delivers rich experiences, blending academics and artistry. What is something you can do for free in Champaign, IL? Visit the Krannert Art Museum, a treasure trove of 11,000 works of art, plus rotating exhibits showcasing the works of students from the School of Art & Design at the University of Illinois. What are some things to do in Champaign, IL, with kids? See lights and laser shows and gaze at the galaxies at the Staerkel Planetarium on the Parkland College campus. The planetarium offers sensory-friendly shows, and children can join the “Galaxy Club.” Cap off the day by reserving a space to go camping and stargazing at Middle Fork River Forest Preserve, the state’s first International Dark Sky Park. Where do locals shop in Champaign, IL? Shopping on North Neil Street is a must. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Champaign, IL? The Virginia Theatre, a gorgeous historic theater and movie house, hosts intimate concerts, film series, ballet, comedy shows and more. Every April, the city hosts Roger Ebert’s Film Festival — aka Ebertfest. Ebert, the late Chicago Sun-Times TV and film critic, grew up in Urbana and attended the University of Illinois; he founded the festival in 1999, and the university’s College of Media has carried on his festival legacy ever since. 7 Places to Hear Live Music in Champaign, IL10 Reasons Champaign, IL, Is a Great Place To Live9 Things You Didn’t Know About Champaign, IL
sections.outdoors: There are so many things that you probably didn’t know about living in Champaign, but the most remarkable is that every single home in the city is within half a mile of a park or other green space. There are also six forest preserves nearby to enjoy hiking, biking and water activities like canoeing, fishing and kayaking. There are dozens and dozens of parks to choose from, including Hessel, West and Scott parks, which feature walking trails, playgrounds and plenty of space to spread out. The “Bark District” provides a dedicated off-leash area for dog owners. Beyond the city parks, residents can enjoy the natural beauty of the Arboretum on the University of Illinois campus, known for its gardens and spring cherry blossom blooms. The Boneyard Creek Walkway offers a scenic urban trail, while Homer Lake Forest Preserve provides a family-friendly escape with a natural playscape. The city’s dedication to accessible green space makes outdoor living a city-wide perk.
sections.restaurants: The culinary scene thrives on fresh, local produce and a global palate, courtesy of its agricultural setting and university influence. There is a spectrum of dining options, from local favorites like Black Dog Smoke & Ale House and Farren’s Pub & Eatery, which offers hearty meals, to establishments like Hamilton Walker’s, which provides upscale dining with steak and seafood. Champaign is known for its diversity, which carries over into the local cuisine. The international food scene is particularly strong, with restaurants like Golden Harbor, Sakanaya and Maize Mexican Grill offering authentic dishes from around the globe. Craft beer and cocktails flow throughout town, with breweries like Pour Bros. Craft Taproom and Riggs Beer Co., as well as cocktail bars like PUNCH! Bar. Caffeine and pastries fuel the city’s cafes and bakeries, making it a foodie destination where local meets international. What is one of the best places to eat in Champaign, IL? Nando Milano Trattoria is a traditional Italian restaurant that is great for a night out. The handcrafted menu features fresh pasta, breads and desserts — all made in-house — and a robust wine list. What is the best coffee shop in Champaign, IL? Caffe Paradiso is an independent, locally-owned shop that serves Intelligentsia coffee and espresso as well as fresh pastries, bread, sandwiches and soup daily. Patrons love the eclectic decor, cozy atmosphere and abundance of plant life inside and out. Where is the best happy hour in Champaign, IL? Choose from dozens of brews at Barrelhouse 34 while enjoying an evening with friends. Or grab an adult slushie from Watson’s Shack and Rail.
sections.education: Families can take advantage of the diverse educational opportunities the city affords its residents. The Champaign Unit 4 School District serves the city, and though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a low student-to-teacher ratio, which is ideal. There are also more than two dozen private schools in Champaign. Many schools here have received recognition for their achievements, and the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign contributes even more resources to the community. Families can also take advantage of the local libraries, museums and community centers that provide enrichment for residents of all ages. Many of the international alumni of U of I end up making Champaign their home, meaning the diversity trickles down to K-12 schools (and younger). While nearly every school in the area incorporates language programs that teach English, Spanish and French, about 17% of the city's population speaks a language other than English at home, from other Indo-European languages to Asian and Pacific Island languages and more.
