IL-evanston.description: Evanston exudes charm, blending historic beauty with a progressive spirit. Home to several universities, a stunning lakefront and a busy downtown, the city seamlessly combines intellect and diversity while maintaining affordability.

sections.location: Evanston is a charming city just north of Chicago, right on the shores of Lake Michigan. The area offers a college-town feel with big-city thrills nearby. Interstate 94 provides quick commutes and Chicago O’Hare International Airport is just 15 miles away. Ready to roam? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Chicago is just 15 miles south, where you can cruise the river to admire architecture, visit the Art Institute or root for the Cubs at Wrigley Field. Skokie sits 5 miles west, offering the Illinois Holocaust Museum, Northshore Sculpture Park and Harms Woods. Waukegan is about 25 miles north, boasting the Genesee Theatre, Waukegan Harbor and Illinois Beach State Park.
sections.economy: Because this is the first suburb you find north of the city, which is easily accessible by the Chicago Transit Authority, it’s inarguably a solid home base for those who want to save a little money and are planning to commute downtown for work. But it’s worth noting that Evanston presents plenty of jobs in industries like education, health care and research, as well as retail and professional services in the vibrant downtown district. Major employers in Evanston include Northwestern University, NorthShore University HealthSystem and the Evanston-Skokie School District. Additionally, Rotary International’s world headquarters are located here. The cost of living in Evanston is higher than the national average. The median home value in Evanston is $420,228, and the average household income is $174,974. Illinois residents pay a state income tax, and those living here can expect a 10.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Evanston, IL? Northwestern Medical is a major health care provider in the area. The Northwestern system has 11 hospitals and more than 200 locations in Chicago and Northern Illinois. The NorthShore University HealthSystem operates several hospitals and clinics locally. In addition, NorthShore is ranked among the nation’s top teaching hospitals. The community is also home to Ascension Saint Francis, which has a Level 1 Trauma and Heart Center.
sections.thingsToDo: Evanston has various cultural and historical attractions and plenty of community events to keep residents entertained all year long. History buffs can delve into the past at the Evanston History Center, housed in the chateau-style Charles Gates Dawes House, or explore the collections at the Halim Time & Glass Museum and the Mitchell Museum of the American Indian. Art lovers can immerse themselves in diverse exhibitions at The Block Museum of Art and the Evanston Art Center, while shoppers can peruse the unique boutiques and specialty shops in the area's distinct shopping districts, including Downtown Evanston, Central Street Evanston and Main-Dempster Mile. The local event calendar is full of fun festivals, street fairs and concerts to keep everyone entertained. The Downtown Evanston Farmers’ Market is held from May to November, and the annual arts celebration, Art in the Park, is a beloved tradition. Residents can also enjoy live performances at various venues. What is something you can do for free in Evanston, IL? Spend an evening stargazing through the historic telescope at Northwestern University’s Dearborn Observatory. The University hosts free Friday night tours for the public. In fact, Northwestern is so embedded into the tapestry of Evanston that the University was founded before the city itself! What are some things to do with kids in Evanston, IL? Adventurous kids will love Circus Summer Camp through The Actor’s Gymnasium. They can stretch their creativity through juggling, aerial skills, stilts and more! Where do locals shop in Evanston, IL? From May to November, the Downtown Evanston Farmers’ Market hosts more than 60 local vendors each Saturday morning. Local farmers and artisans sell fruits and veggies, arts and crafts, and baked goods. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Evanston, IL? SPACE is a popular live music venue that hosts a variety of performances, including concerts featuring local and nationally recognized artists. Northwestern University’s Wirtz Center for the Performing Arts is also a good choice for a night out. It is known for hosting theater productions, dance performances and musicals.
sections.outdoors: Enjoying the outdoors here is easy, with a wealth of activities primarily centered around the Lake Michigan shoreline. Residents can enjoy swimming, sunbathing and picnicking at several public beaches, including Clark Street Beach and Lighthouse Beach. The scenic Lakefront Trail provides opportunities for walking, jogging and biking with views of the Chicago skyline. Parks like Dawes Park, with its lagoon and open spaces, and the Ladd Arboretum, featuring many plant species and nature trails, offer serene escapes and recreational spaces. The Grosse Point Lighthouse stands as a historic landmark along the shoreline. Beyond the lakefront, Evanston provides plenty of dry land outdoor experiences, including exploring the Emily Oaks Nature Center, visiting the Merrick Rose Garden and biking the Skokie Lagoons North Loop. During warmer months, outdoor farmers markets offer local produce and culture, while winter provides sledding and ice skating opportunities at parks like Arrington Lagoon. Outdoor enthusiasts can also participate in group activities like hiking, walking and cycling while enjoying the natural beauty.
sections.restaurants: With nearly 90 cafes and restaurants, the local dining scene features everything from award-winning Italian and Japanese cuisine to French, Asian and American specialties, cementing its reputation as a certified foodie destination. From casual eateries like the Evanston Chicken Shack to flavorful spots like Mt. Everest Restaurant, there is a dining experience for every palate. The brewery scene is equally dynamic, with a burgeoning craft brewery and distillery presence. Despite its history as a dry town until 1972, the city now embraces its brewing culture. Residents can embark on brewery tours at Peckish Pig or sample whiskey and gin at Few Spirits, Evanston’s first distillery. Double Clutch Brewing Co. also offers tours, tastings and entertainment. What is one of the best places to eat in Evanston, IL? Campagnola is a beloved local Italian restaurant on Chicago Avenue. It boasts simple but decadent dishes highlighting local, seasonal ingredients in a cozy, warm atmosphere. It’s an excellent spot for a night out with friends or a date. What is the best coffee shop in Evanston, IL? Backlot Coffee is an independently owned specialty coffee company with three cafes and a roastery in the Chicagoland area. The menu is full of classic coffee favorites and specialty drinks, and some light bites for breakfast and lunch. Where is the best place for happy hour in Evanston, IL? Enjoy classic happy hour fare with half-price apps and draft beers at the ever-popular The Peckish Pig. Or consider the elevated cocktails at Ward Eight or Five & Dime for something different.
sections.education: Evanston schools are highly rated and offer plenty of options for families. The city’s districts are broken up into lower and high school districts. The Evanston Community Consolidated School District No. 65 is above average, with over 6,000 students in grades K-8. Nearly 4,000 high school students are served by the Evanston Township High School District No. 202. The district earns top scores in academics, activities, college prep and diversity and is one of the best districts in Illinois. There are also several private school options for families here, including religious, nonsectarian and college preparatory education styles. Roycemore School is an independent college preparatory school that has earned several accolades from Niche, including being in the top 3 for the best private K-12 schools in Illinois, best high schools for the arts in Illinois and best private high schools in Illinois. IL-naperville.description: Located on the outskirts of Chicago, Naperville residents have it all. Top-rated schools, pristine parks and a bustling downtown offer a high quality of life. Throw in a low cost of living and a tight-knit community, and it's easy to see why Naperville residents love living in this Midwest city.

sections.location: The community sits just west of Chicago, right along Interstate 88 — making it easy to zip in and out of the city. The city’s location in DuPage County grants Naperville residents easy access to Chicago O’Hare International Airport, a bustling hub for flights globally, roughly 25 miles away. Ready to explore further? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Chicago is just 30 miles east, where you can cruise the river to admire architecture, visit the Art Institute or root for the Cubs at Wrigley Field. Aurora lies 10 miles west, boasting a historic downtown, the Paramount Theatre and the free Phillips Park Zoo. Joliet is about 20 miles south, offering the Hollywood Casino, Rialto Square Theatre and the Des Plaines River Trail.
sections.economy: The community boasts a lively economic market with a wealth of job opportunities for those keen on building their careers alongside their families and personal lives. Technology, education, health care and agriculture are among the top industries in the city, which is further enhanced by the great public transportation system that reduces the amount of time residents spend in the car each morning. Edward-Elmhurst Health, Indian Prairie School District 204 and Nokia are all major employers in the area. The cost of living in Naperville is higher than the national average. The median home value in Naperville is $473,285, and the average household income is $210,168. Illinois residents pay a state income tax, and those living in Naperville can expect a 7.75% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Naperville, IL? The city has several highly rated health care providers, including Edward Hospital, a full-service hospital with a Level II trauma center, and DuPage Medical Group.
sections.thingsToDo: Known as one of the largest suburbs of Chicago, Naperville is chock-full of things to do. For those seeking entertainment, immerse yourself in the art world at the Naperville Fine Art Center & Gallery. Or, take a step back in time at the Naper Settlement, a 13-acre living history museum. Spend a day scanning the stalls at the Naperville Farmer’s Market, or let littles learn about local wildlife at Knoch Knolls Nature Center. The area has plenty to offer those seeking a little retail therapy. Downtown’s brick streets have a charming collection of boutiques, specialty stores and national retailers, while the Main Street Promenade features a solid mix of pedestrian-friendly shops and restaurants. What is something you can do for free in Naperville, IL? Stroll through the Naperville historic district, which features 280 houses in various architectural styles like Cape Cod, four square and, of course, Victorian. What are some things to do with kids in Naperville, IL? Take your kids to experience the magic of the DuPage Children’s Museum, where they can work with tools, explore toddler tunnels and create their next masterpiece. Where do locals shop in Naperville, IL? Peace is a locally-owned boutique focused on conscious consumerism and crafts ethically sourced and sustainable goods like jewelry and essential oils. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Naperville, IL? The Wentz Concert Hall at North Central College hosts a variety of performances each season, ranging from classical concerts and jazz ensembles to theatrical productions and more.
sections.outdoors: Nearly 90% of all local residents live within a 10-minute walking distance of a park, so we really mean it when we say the outdoor opportunities are endless. The Naperville Park District maintains and operates over 2,500 acres and 140 parks and offers more than 1,400 recreational, arts and environmental programs annually. Centennial Beach is a popular community swimming hole. Considered a beach, not a pool, this 15-foot-deep quarry holds 6.2 million gallons of water. Grab some friends and take your pups to Springbrook Prairie Forest Preserve, where your four-legged friend will love spending time in a designated off-leash area or exploring the 7 miles of trails. The East and West Branches of the DuPage River meet at Knoch Knolls Park, more than 200 acres of woodlands, prairies and wetlands with a nature center, several miles of multi-use trails, a 9-hole disc golf course, a nature-themed playground for children and fishing and kayaking. McDowell Grove Forest Preserve and Whalon Lake are favorites for nature lovers interested in hiking, biking or birdwatching. Affectionately known as the “Crown Jewel of Naperville,” the Riverwalk is a breath of fresh air that beckons morning walkers, families out for a stroll and runners looking to stretch their legs on the brick-paved paths along the DuPage River. Newcomers will find scenic views, fountains and art installations while strolling this nearly 2-mile path. Be sure to stop for an afternoon picnic at the Grand Pavilion.
sections.restaurants: Narrowing down a place for dinner here is no small task. Plenty of restaurants in town cater to a wide range of tastes and preferences. For a casual dining experience and classic deli food, check out Schmaltz Delicatessen or The Lucky Penny Diner and Deli. Looking for something more upscale? Consider choosing among Mesón Sabika, a Spanish tapas restaurant housed in a historic mansion; Catch 35, which specializes in seafood and steaks; or Jackson Avenue Pub, a popular sports bar with a pub-style menu and excellent cocktails. During winter, the city hosts an annual Naperville Restaurant Week with dozens of participating restaurants. What is one of the best places to eat in Naperville, IL? After decades of experimentation, Entourage serves a wide range of delicious dishes, blending the different cultures that define American food. Some favorites include classic chicken pot pie and a dry-aged wagyu bolognese with fresh pasta. What is the best coffee shop in Naperville, IL? Sparrow Coffee is for the coffee-obsessed and is the first cafe outpost of Chris Chacko’s coffee roasteries, popular in the Chicagoland area. Where is the best place for happy hour in Naperville, IL? Sip a fresh, flavorful cocktail (or two) at Zorba Cocktail Bar at Hotel Arista. The menu is full of delicious drinks, and you can unwind from the day around the patio’s fire pit.
sections.education: Education is a high priority for residents. Families benefit from the city’s exceptional educational system, which includes two public school districts: Naperville Community Unit School District 203 (NCUSD 203) and Indian Prairie School District 204 (IPSD 204). Year after year, both districts are ranked among the best in the state; this year, both were among the top 50 in the country, according to Niche. Local families can also choose from several private schools, too. IL-peoria.description: Peoria perfectly balances natural beauty and Midwestern charm. A strong sense of community, diverse recreational options and affordability contribute to the city's high quality of life. Peoria is full of character and opportunity, from charming neighborhoods to fun festivals.

sections.location: Peoria is a historic city along the Illinois River in central Illinois, about 165 miles southwest of Chicago, accessible via Interstate 74. General Wayne A. Downing Peoria International Airport is 5 miles west, offering flights to Chicago and Dallas. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Chicago is roughly 165 miles northeast, where visitors can stroll the Magnificent Mile, visit the Field Museum or catch a Cubs game. Springfield sits 70 miles south, home to the Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library, the Illinois State Capitol and part of Route 66. St. Louis, MO, lies 155 miles southwest, where you can marvel at the Gateway Arch, wander the Missouri Botanical Garden or catch a Cardinals game.
sections.economy: The city has a buzzing startup ecosystem powered largely by Distillery Labs, which serves as a launching pad for entrepreneurs looking for support, connections and resources. Distillery Labs connects people, ideas and resources so that entrepreneurs and innovators in the area can make advancements. In addition to aiding and providing space for startups, the facility can house corporate partners, community organizations and many other stakeholders. There are several thriving industries in the Greater Peoria area, including manufacturing, health care and education. Caterpillar, Inc., OSF Health Care and Peoria Public Schools are all major employers in the area. The cost of living in Peoria is lower than the national average. The median home value in Peoria is $151,100, and the average household income is $96,577. Illinois residents pay a state income tax, and those living in Peoria can expect a 9 to 10% sales tax rate in 2025, depending on where they are in town. Who are the major health care providers in Peoria, IL? OSF Saint Francis Medical Center, UnityPoint Health – Methodist | Proctor and the Children’s Hospital of Illinois are all top-tier health care providers available to local residents. Find Out Why Companies Love Greater PeoriaHealth Care Innovation in Greater Peoria Expands Access to PatientsLife and Business Intersect in Greater Peoria
sections.thingsToDo: Peoria delivers a cultural punch, mixing art and entertainment with family-friendly fun. The Riverfront Museum and Contemporary Art Center anchor the arts scene, while venues like the Civic Center and Peoria Players Theatre host performances. The symphony — one of the oldest in the country — is a historical gem and provides classical and contemporary performances. Outside of the arts, there are plenty of ways to have fun. The Peoria Zoo and Luthy Botanical Garden offer engaging experiences with nature, while the Peoria PlayHouse Children’s Museum and Elevate Trampoline Park cater to kids. History buffs can explore the city through the Peoria Historical Society tours and museums like the John C. Flanagan House Museum and the Charles Perdew Museum. Community events, from markets to fireworks, unite the city. Favorites include the Peoria RiverFront Market, Red, White & BOOM! Fourth of July festival and the Santa Claus Parade. Public art projects like Big Picture Peoria splash color across the cityscape. What is something you can do for free in Peoria, IL? A trip to Luthy Botanical Gardens is a great way to slow down and unwind. What are some things to do with kids in Peoria, IL? The Peoria Playhouse Children’s Museum is a hands-on, interactive museum that offers a variety of exhibits, programs and hands-on workshops. Three floors of fun are waiting to be explored, and exhibits like a light wall, science lab and musical bridge will delight. Where do locals shop in Peoria, IL? Northwoods Mall, Metro Centre and The Shoppes at Grand Prairie are among the top hot spots for local shopping among residents. These three destinations offer many national and regional retailers and plenty of dining options and service businesses. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Peoria, IL? Peoria Civic Center is the place to be for events, concerts and sporting events, while Peoria Players Theatre might be a better choice for those hoping to indulge in a musical or stage play. 8 Places to Live in Greater Peoria, ILLocal Arts Organizations Improve and Unite the Greater Peoria Region16 Festivals You Don’t Want To Miss Near Peoria, IL
sections.outdoors: Peoria’s outdoors delivers adventure from river thrills to scenic trails, catering to nature enthusiasts and those seeking recreational fun. The Illinois River sets the stage for boating and fishing, while Grand View Drive & Park and Luthy Botanical Garden provide tranquil escapes. Residents can explore the Rock Island Trail, a converted railway line perfect for hiking and biking, and enjoy dog-friendly spaces like Vicary Bottoms and Laura Bradley Park. Sports lovers find golf courses and pickleball courts aplenty. Get in touch with your natural side at Forest Park and Detweiller while stargazers peer from Northmoor Observatory. The riverfront’s walk and water charters offer fresh city views. Explore the Outdoors in Greater Peoria
sections.restaurants: The culinary scene blends Midwestern comforts with innovative dining and international influences. The city features a range of dining options, from steakhouses and casual eateries to farm-to-table restaurants and international establishments. Local favorites like Jim’s Steakhouse and Hearth provide classic and innovative American cuisine, while restaurants like Los Cabos Cantina & Grill and Thanh Linh Vietnamese Restaurant offer authentic Mexican and Asian flavors.Local brews flow at Industry Brewing Co., and Rhythm Kitchen combines food and live music. Unique eats at The Queen of Squash offer a fresh twist, and the Food + Wine Festival highlights the city’s evolving palate. What is one of the best places to eat in Peoria, IL? Casa de Arte is a taqueria that doubles as an art gallery. On the first Friday of every month, there is an opening reception for a local artist. What is the best coffee shop in Peoria, IL? Grab a coffee or a juice from Intuition Coffee Company. Where is the best happy hour in Peoria, IL? The Bar at Black Band Distillery has a limited happy hour, but the stellar food and cocktails at a discount make it worth scheduling a time to go. Take a Bite Out of Peoria: 6 Must-Try Restaurants
sections.education: Peoria Public Schools District is the primary public school provider in the cit. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable student-to-teacher ratio. There are plenty of prestigious private schools to pick from in Peoria, including Peoria Academy, a K-8 school that is the only accredited International Baccalaureate (IB) school in the Tri-County Area. faqs.a: The Peoria Playhouse Children’s Museum is a hands-on, interactive museum that offers a variety of exhibits, programs and hands-on workshops. Three floors of fun are waiting to be explored, and exhibits like a light wall, science lab and musical bridge will delight.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Peoria, IL?
faqs.a: Northwoods Mall, Metro Centre and The Shoppes at Grand Prairie are among the top hot spots for local shopping among residents. These three destinations offer many national and regional retailers and plenty of dining options and service businesses.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Peoria, IL?
faqs.a: Peoria Civic Center is the place to be for events, concerts and sporting events, while Peoria Players Theatre might be a better choice for those hoping to indulge in a musical or stage play.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Peoria, IL?
faqs.a: Casa de Arte is a taqueria that doubles as an art gallery. On the first Friday of every month, there is an opening reception for a local artist.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Peoria, IL?
faqs.a: Grab a coffee or a juice from Intuition Coffee Company.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Peoria, IL?
faqs.a: The Bar at Black Band Distillery has a limited happy hour, but the stellar food and cocktails at a discount make it worth scheduling a time to go.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Peoria, IL?
faqs.a: The city has several smaller colleges and universities, including Bradley University and Illinois Central College.

IN-bloomington.description: Bloomington, IN, is a vibrant college town known for its rich cultural scene, diverse dining options and thriving arts community. With scenic outdoor spaces, top-notch schools and a welcoming atmosphere, residents enjoy a balanced blend of academic excellence and small-town charm.

sections.location: Bloomington is a charming college town in southern Indiana, about 50 miles southwest of Indianapolis, where State Road 37 and State Road 46 converge. For regional flights, Monroe County Airport is 5 miles west, but Indianapolis International Airport is an easy 50-mile drive. When you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Indianapolis, IN, is 50 miles northeast; catch the Indy 500, explore the Children’s Museum or wander the White River State Park. Louisville, KY, is roughly 100 miles south; tour Churchill Downs, sip bourbon at Evan Williams or stroll Waterfront Park. Cincinnati, OH, sits 130 miles southeast; visit the Cincinnati Zoo, stroll Over-the-Rhine or admire the Roebling Suspension Bridge.
sections.economy: Heavily influenced by the presence of Indiana University, Bloomington’s economy reflects the active and youthful spirit on campus. The city has a strong presence in many sectors, but three of the largest are education, health care and manufacturing. Unsurprisingly, Indiana University and IU Health Bloomington Hospital are two of the largest local employers. Other large employers include the City of Bloomington, Monroe County Community School Corporation and Cook Group, a medical device manufacturer headquartered in the area. If you’re looking to start somewhere fresh and need a little financial incentive, the city offers new residents up to $8,000 to move there. The cost of living in Bloomington is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Bloomington is $448,943, and the average household income is $202,490. Indiana residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Bloomington, IN? The city is home to Indiana University Health Bloomington Hospital, which U.S. News & World Report recognizes as a high performer in three adult procedures and conditions, including stroke and maternity care. It has also been recognized as the best hospital in Indiana.
sections.thingsToDo: Bloomington is booming! The city’s downtown area, including Kirkwood Avenue and The Square, is where local shops, restaurants and bars are concentrated. Art enthusiasts can explore the Eskenazi Museum of Art and various art galleries, while live music venues and theaters like the Buskirk-Chumley Theater provide diverse entertainment options. History buffs have the Monroe County History Center and the Lilly Library, and families can enjoy attractions like the WonderLab Museum and Switchyard Park. The city has festivals all year, and being home to Indiana University means there are tons of cultural and sports events, like Hoosiers basketball games at Simon Skjodt Assembly Hall. What is something you can do for free in Bloomington, IN? You don’t have to be a student to spend the day on the Indiana University campus, which was founded in 1820 and is often cited as one of the prettiest in the country. Start a walking tour at the iconic Sample Gates, enjoy the beauty of landmark buildings and visit numerous free museums, including the Elizabeth Sage Costume Collection and Eskenazi Museum of Art. Wear your walking shoes — the campus is nearly 2,000 acres. Admission is free to the Lilly Library, a must-visit for literary lovers. The library’s collection includes 8.5 million manuscripts, 450,000 books, sheet music, mechanical puzzles and highlights such as the first printed edition of “The Canterbury Tales.” What are some things to do in Bloomington, IN, with kids? Karst Farm Park has four playgrounds (including a musical one!) and a fun splash pad. Where do locals shop in Bloomington, IN? Spend the morning perusing the vendors at the Bloomington Community Farmers Market. Grab a coffee and breakfast treat from one of the food vendors, then walk around to shop for produce, buy a bouquet of fresh flowers, and listen to buskers and people-watch. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Bloomington, IN? Live music is easy to come by in Bloomington. Catch a concert at The Bluebird or hear a local act and play pinball at the Orbit Room, a dive bar hosting comedy, trivia, author readings and more.
sections.outdoors: Get out and about in Bloomington, a place with a lot of outdoor options, thanks to its hills, forests and lakes. Hike, camp and ride horses in the Hoosier National Forest, or enjoy boating, fishing and swimming at Monroe Lake. Bloomington also has tons of trails, including the popular B-Line Trail, which is ideal for walking, jogging and biking. The more rugged trails at Morgan-Monroe and Yellowwood State Forests are also favorite spots. For more relaxed recreation, Bryan Park, Lower Cascades Park and Switchyard Park offer playgrounds, picnic areas and walking trails. Griffy Lake is a good place for fishing and canoeing, and the Tibetan Mongolian Buddhist Cultural Center is a peaceful place to relax. Golfers can play at the Pfau Course, and nature fans can check out the protected flora and fauna at the Beanblossom Bottoms Nature Preserve.
sections.restaurants: Bite into Bloomington, which boasts an eclectic food scene that comes from the innovative spirit of a college town. There’s everything from farm-to-table places like Farm Bloomington to all sorts of international foods and cozy cafes. Local breweries like Upland Brewing Co. and Cardinal Spirits distillery contribute to a thriving craft beverage culture, offering unique flavors and experiences. Many restaurants focus on local ingredients and sustainable practices, enhancing the quality and appeal of Bloomington’s culinary offerings. The city’s wine scene is in full bloom, with Oliver Winery producing nationally recognized wines. The city’s breweries and distilleries are not just places to drink but also hubs for community gatherings and learning, offering tours and tastings that highlight the dedication to craft. The mix of new flavors, good ingredients and friendly places makes the local food and drink scene something special. What is one of the best places to eat in Bloomington, IN? It has more than six dozen international restaurants. A favorite is Anyetsang’s Little Tibet Restaurant, which serves Thai, Indian and Tibetan cuisine in a restored house with a garden patio. What is the best coffee shop in Bloomington, IN? BloomingTea is a cozy tea shop with more than 100 loose-leaf teas to choose from and a private room if you need to hold a meeting. Start your morning at Verona Coffee Shop, a European-inspired spot that serves breakfast sandwiches and acai bowls. Where is the best happy hour in Bloomington, IN? Kirkwood and College avenues downtown are filled with popular spots to suit any mood, from upscale cocktails to beer and billiards, making it challenging to choose just one place. For a signature cocktail, try Serendipity Martini Bar or Uptown Cafe; for craft beer and spicy peanuts, The Alley Bar; for drinks and music, Root Cellar.
sections.education: Bloomington is a great place to raise a family for many reasons, including the community’s strong focus on education. Monroe County Community School Corporation is a highly rated public school district representing more than 10,000 students in the area. MCCSC offers a few specialty schools, like the Bloomington Project School, which offers project-based learning, and International Baccalaureate programs at some schools. There are excellent private schools here, too, ranging from religious to non-sectarian to specialized. The Harmony School is a private school for grades PK-12 known for its progressive approach to education. --> --> --> Browse the Top 100 Best Places to Live in the U.S. Bloomington, IN 690 Danbury, CT 690 Tempe, AZ 692 Bethlehem, PA 696 Eugene, OR 696 Kennewick, WA 697 Colorado Springs, CO 698 Asheville, NC 699 Aurora, CO 699 Gresham, OR 700 Madison, WI 700 Omaha, NE 701 Appleton, WI 704 Frederick, MD 704 Sioux Falls, SD 704 Fargo, ND 706 Lincoln, NE 707 Iowa City, IA 709 Durham, NC 710 Greenville, SC 710 College Station, TX 711 Norman, OK 711 Midland, TX 712 Ann Arbor, MI 715 Rio Rancho, NM 716 Coral Springs, FL 717 Cedar Rapids, IA 718 Charleston, SC 719 Rochester, MN 722 Bloomington, IL 727 Pittsburgh, PA 727 Nashua, NH 730 Clovis, CA 731 Vancouver, WA 731 Loveland, CO 744 Bloomington, MN 755 New Braunfels, TX 758 Raleigh, NC 758 Evanston, IL 759 Warwick, RI 761 Hoover, AL 763 Virginia Beach, VA 770 Clifton, NJ 771 Edmond, OK 778 Gilbert, AZ 791 Round Rock, TX 793 Olathe, KS 799 Columbia, MD 828 Troy, MI 829 Overland Park, KS 831 Roswell, GA 838 Naperville, IL 841 Sugar Land, TX 854 Carmel, IN 872 Flower Mound, TX 875 Augusta, GA 633 Chattanooga, TN 634 Rapid City, SD 634 Cincinnati, OH 636 Roanoke, VA 636 St. Louis, MO 636 Orlando, FL 638 Anchorage, AK 639 Gainesville, FL 639 Santa Fe, NM 641 Greenville, NC 643 Little Rock, AR 644 Tulsa, OK 644 Athens, GA 646 Columbia, SC 646 Green Bay, WI 648 Knoxville, TN 648 Peoria, IL 651 Redding, CA 653 Wilmington, NC 653 Champaign, IL 655 Worcester, MA 656 Abilene, TX 659 Lynn, MA 661 Murfreesboro, TN 661 Davenport, IA 662 Wichita, KS 662 Billings, MT 663 Columbia, MO 663 Auburn, AL 666 Manchester, NH 669 Salem, OR 671 Tampa, FL 673 Greensboro, NC 674 Huntsville, AL 674 Tyler, TX 676 Winston-Salem, NC 676 Louisville, KY 678 Duluth, MN 679 Richmond, VA 680 Spokane, WA 683 Minneapolis, MN 685 Lynchburg, VA 687 Nampa, ID 687 Fayetteville, AR 689 Return to Main List Explore All Top 100 Best Places Cities Top 100 Cities Map

faqs.a: Kirkwood and College avenues downtown are filled with popular spots to suit any mood, from upscale cocktails to beer and billiards, making it challenging to choose just one place. IN-carmel.description: Roughly 30 minutes outside of Indianapolis is Carmel, IN, a picturesque city with a stellar arts scene and top-rated schools. Carmel embraces quality of life, and residents in this tight-knit community have access to plenty of cultural events and outdoor activities.

sections.location: Carmel is in the central part of the state, just north of Indianapolis, and positioned along Interstate 465, the beltway that loops around Indy and keeps everything connected. Indianapolis International Airport is roughly 25 miles away, ready with flights to take you near or far. The city stretches across the mostly flat plains of the Midwest. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Indianapolis, IN, is just 15 miles south, where you can catch a Colts game, marvel at the Indianapolis Museum of Art or wander the historic Monument Circle. Fort Wayne, IN, lies 110 miles northeast, offering a beautiful botanical conservatory, the Fort Wayne Children’s Zoo and the Three Rivers Festival. Louisville, KY, is about 120 miles south, famous for the Kentucky Derby, the Louisville Slugger Museum and the scenic Waterfront Park along the Ohio River.
sections.economy: The area boasts a strong and diverse economy where the business community flourishes. The largest industries in Carmel are health care, technology and finance. A few of the top employers in Carmel include CNO Financial Group, Allegion, OPENLANE and Delta Faucet, all of which have national headquarters within city limits. Other major employers include IU Health North Hospital, Merchants Bank and Edward Jones. The Meridian Corridor has a large concentration of corporate office space and houses many corporate headquarters and regional offices. With all of this innovation, it’s easy to see why many entrepreneurs succeed in Hamilton County. The cost of living in Carmel is slightly below the national average; however, home prices in the area tend to be higher than other cities in Indiana. The median home value in Carmel is $448,943, and the average household income is $202,490. Indiana residents pay a state income tax, and those living in Carmel can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Carmel, IN? Residents can access several top-notch health care providers, including IU Health North Hospital and St. Vincent Carmel Hospital. Meet the Amazing Women Entrepreneurs & Makers of Hamilton County, Indiana
sections.thingsToDo: There are many things to do in Carmel, IN, and the city offers plenty of attractions. The local calendar of events caters to every interest under the sun, including Carmel PorchFest and Artomobilia, when Main Street transforms into an automotive wonderland filled with classic and vintage cars. Art-centric attractions are plentiful, from The Palladium to productions by the Carmel Community Players. The Carmel Arts & Design District is the perfect place to spend an afternoon looking for antiques, art, furniture and other treasures, or shop and eat your way through the Carmel City Center. Residents can visit the Carmel Farmers Market or the Winter Market, depending on the season. What is something you can do for free in Carmel, IN? Visit Flowing Well Park in spring to walk among the wildflowers before fishing in Cool Creek. Don’t forget empty containers to fill up at the artesian well before you leave! What are some things to do with kids in Carmel, IN? The Children’s Garden at Coxhall Gardens, equipped with a make-believe village and ringing bell tours, is perfect for little imaginations to run wild. Where do locals shop in Carmel, IN? All the most stylish homes are decorated with antiques, art and furniture from the Carmel Arts & Design District. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Carmel, IN? The Palladium at the Center for the Performing Arts is an architectural gem and a cultural hub for music and the arts. Whether you want to indulge in theater or classical or contemporary concerts, this world-class hall is hands down the best spot in town.
sections.outdoors: Miles of trails and greenways in and around the city make for an outdoor lover’s paradise. Lined with lush greenery, public art installations and charming bridges, the 27-mile Monon Trail is a scenic pathway that was a railway line in a past life, but today, it weaves its way from Indianapolis to Sheridan, passing through the heart of the city. There are over 700 acres of green space, including 20 parks and greenways. In partnership with the Indiana Wildlife Federation (IWF), Carmel is the first park system in the state to be certified as wildlife and habitat-friendly The area has an extensive network of parks and outdoor recreational spaces, and the local parks department has created an outdoor oasis in middle America. During the summer, secure a pass to cool off at The Waterpark’s one-of-a-kind FlowRider or float down the lazy river. Or, for some free fun, float, kayak or canoe along the White River. Back on dry land, golfers can enjoy hitting the links, thanks to the numerous golf courses in and around Carmel. There are seven courses inside the city and an additional 61 public, municipal and private golf courses within a 20-mile radius.
sections.restaurants: From upscale and fine dining favorites, like Prime 47, to farm-to-table fare from The Garden Table, you won’t go hungry at one of the dozens of restaurants in Carmel. Curb your cravings with global flavors, ranging from international cuisine to artisanal pizza, and explore a delicious dining scene that caters to a variety of tastes and budgets. For a night out, start with a snacky appetizer at 317 Charcuterie, followed by entrees at Vivante, an upscale French bistro inside Hotel Carmichael. Round out the evening with a sweet treat from Cake Bake Shop or a scoop of Graeter’s famous ice cream. The craft beer scene is hopping in Hamilton County, with 10 craft breweries. Kick back with a local brew or glass of wine from several spots around the city, including divvy, Sun King and Four Day Ray Brewing. Grab a nightcap at 3UP Rooftop Bar, which has great cocktails, small plates and excellent city views. What is one of the best places to eat in Carmel, IN? Anthony’s Chophouse, in the Arts and Design District, has a sophisticated vibe paired with upscale steaks, decadent seafood and a robust selection of wines. What is the best coffee shop in Carmel, IN? Located in a gorgeous converted home, Indie Coffee Roasters is the perfect spot to enjoy a great cup of coffee and tasty treats. Your four-legged friend will love it, too! Where is the best place for happy hour in Carmel, IN? Built as a Carnegie Library in 1913, Woodys Library Restaurant is a Carmel staple, and their daily happy hour food and drink specials sweeten the deal.
sections.education: Schools in Carmel are highly rated and regarded. Carmel Clay Schools consistently ranks as a top-rated school district in Indiana and has a strong reputation for academic excellence and achievements. Students perform well on standardized tests, have high graduation rates and have access to a wide range of academic programs, like AP courses, dual credit options and college prep. There are dozens of private school options in the greater Carmel area, including the highly awarded University High School of Indiana. IN-fishers.description: Located just minutes from Indianapolis, Fishers perfectly blends small-town suburban life and big-city amenities. With top-ranked schools, safe neighborhoods and plenty of parks and recreation, the city offers a family-friendly environment without skimping on affordability.

sections.economy: --> --> --> Weather in Fishers, IN Average Temperatures 61 high / 41 low Average Annual Rainfall 43 in Average Annual Snowfall 21 in

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Fishers?
faqs.a: Fishers has warm and sunny weather from May to the end of September (with highs in the mid-80s), proper cozy Midwestern falls and chilly winters (with lows in the teens). The average highs and lows are 84°F and 61°F (summer) and 35°F and 16°F (winter).

faqs.q: Does Fishers get a lot of snow?
faqs.a: The average annual snowfall in Fishers is 21 inches a year, with the heaviest chance for snowfall in January and February.

faqs.q: Does Fishers get a lot of rain?
faqs.a: The average annual rainfall in Fishers is about 43 inches. The wettest month is May.

faqs.q: How many sunny days does Fishers get?
faqs.a: Fishers averages 186 sunny days a year.

faqs.q: What are some things to do outside in Fishers?
faqs.a: Pack up the kids and grab a picnic basket for a relaxing afternoon at Geist Waterfront Park along the shores of the Geist Reservoir. The park, which opened in the spring of 2023, is 70 acres of waterfront property equipped with a playground, picnic shelters, walking trails and even beach access.

faqs.q: What is something free to do for fun in Fishers?
faqs.a: Perusing the Fishers Farmers Market, walking the shoreline at Geist Reservoir, attending a free concert at Nickel Plate District Amphitheater or picnicking at Flat Fork Creek Park are just a few of the many free activities available to locals in Fishers. Fishers Arts & Culture Commission hosts several free cultural activities, exhibitions and concerts throughout the year, too.

faqs.q: What are some fun things to do with kids in Fishers?
faqs.a: Spend the afternoon at Connor Prairie. This family-friendly living history museum captures what life in Indiana was like in the 1800s. When the outdoor exhibit opens, there are a variety of experiences to explore, including a balloon voyage!

faqs.q: What is the best open secret about Fishers?
faqs.a: Fishers may be small, but its passion for art is pretty spectacular. The city is home to several public art installations, including murals and sculptures. "Thanks for Being Weird With Me" by Nekoda Witsken in the Fishers District and "In this Together" by Travis Neal at Brooks School Park are just two of the many public art pieces found within the city.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Fishers?
faqs.a: The Fishers District is a great place to spend the afternoon shopping. Looking to overhaul your wardrobe? Blue Peppermint is a sweet local boutique. And, of course, IKEA is not far away if you've got a hankering for some of their famous Swedish meatballs.

faqs.q: What is a fun restaurant in Fishers?
faqs.a: Four Day Ray Brewing, a Fishers favorite, is tucked in the Nickel Plate District and boasts a seriously diverse menu with ample craft beers. The atmosphere is warm and welcoming, the outdoor patio is perfect for warm summer nights with a cold drink, and the rotating selection makes it worth circling back to regularly.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Fishers?
faqs.a: The Nickel Plate District Amphitheater, conveniently located in the heart of Fishers' charming downtown, often hosts live music events, concerts and community performances — especially during the warmer months. In the summer, it hosts a free concert series and a lively farmers market.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Fishers?
faqs.a: Locals have a hard time choosing just one coffee shop. Schoolhouse 7 (a former schoolhouse turned cafe), MOTW (a community-focused and charitable coffee shop) and Geist Coffee Company are a sampling of local favorites.

faqs.q: What are the three largest industries in Fishers?
faqs.a: Fishers' diverse economy includes several vital industries, from professional and technical services to health care and retail. Navient, Stanley Security Solutions, First Internet Bank and First Advantage have corporate offices in Fishers.

faqs.q: What is the sales tax rate in Fishers?
faqs.a: In 2024, the sales tax rate in Fishers is 7%.

faqs.q: Does Indiana have a state income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, Indiana has a state income tax.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Fishers?
faqs.a: Fishers is home to several major health care providers, including IU Health Saxony Hospital, St. Vincent Fishers Hospital and Community Health Network.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Fishers?
faqs.a: Ball State-Fishers Center is the main college in Fishers.

KS-olathe.description: Olathe is the epitome of Midwestern charm, with friendly neighborhoods, top-notch schools, a thriving job market and no shortage of things to do. The city’s close proximity to Kansas City perfectly balances suburban tranquility and urban conveniences.

sections.location: Olathe is a growing city just southwest of Kansas City with a friendly vibe. Interstate 35 keeps it tied to the region. Kansas City International Airport is around 35 miles away, offering flights for all your journeys. When you’re set to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Kansas City, MO, is just 20 miles northeast, where you can shop at the Country Club Plaza, visit the Kansas City Zoo, or cheer on the Kansas City Chiefs at Arrowhead Stadium. Overland Park sits 10 miles northeast, offering the Arboretum, Deanna Rose Children’s Farmstead and great dining spots. Lawrence is about 25 miles west, buzzing with University of Kansas life, historic Liberty Hall and lovely South Park.
sections.economy: This is a place that helps entrepreneurs get a jump-start on their business plans. Programs like GrowOlathe provide technical support to help young companies flourish. The city also promotes “economic gardening,” a concept that focuses on creating jobs by supporting existing companies in the community rather than just recruitment. Health care, professional services and retail trade are among the largest industries here. Garmin International and Honeywell are major employers in town. The cost of living in Olathe is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Olathe is $348,048, and the average household income is $135,678. Kansas residents pay a state income tax, and residents can expect a 9.48% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Olathe, KS? Olathe Medical Center is the largest hospital in the city, offering a wide range of medical services and rated a high performer in three adult procedures and conditions. Residents also access several highly rated health care providers in nearby Overland Park.
sections.thingsToDo: The city is always on the move — from historical sites to family-friendly entertainment and cultural attractions. For history buffs, step back in time at Mahaffie Stagecoach Stop & Farm Historic Site or explore the Ensor Farm Site and Museum, the home of Marshall Ensor, a local resident and pioneer of amateur radio. Car lovers will rejoice at the Kansas City Automotive Museum, showcasing a rotating car collection. The Museum of Deaf History, Arts & Culture is dedicated to educating visitors about the culture of the deaf and hard of hearing. For those seeking a self-guided adventure, the city offers historic walking tours that showcase beautiful homes and landmarks. The options for fun and entertainment are endless. Main Event offers 22 bowling lanes, laser tag and more than 100 arcade-style games. Shopping enthusiasts can hunt for finds at the antiques at Lone Elm Antique Mall. Check out the Olathe Farmers Market for fresh produce, plants, baked goods and more. Most of the items available are produced within 250 miles of town. There are several festivals throughout the year, too. Some favorites include the Olathe Summer Concert Series and the Johnson County Old Settlers Festival, a three-day event featuring crafts, parades and more. What is something you can do for free in Olathe, KS? Fourth Fridays in the downtown area is a community celebration with live bands during the summer. Food trucks, balloon artists and $1 science experiments for kids are also available. What are some things to do with kids in Olathe, KS? Take a step back in time at Mahaffie Stage Coach Stop & Historic Farm Site, which allows visitors to explore what life was like back in the heyday of the Santa Fe Trail in the 1860s. Where do locals shop in Olathe, KS? The Junque Drawer Studio consists of two homes and 22 rooms (yes, you read that right) of vintage treasures. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Olathe, KS? The Trilogy Cultural Arts Center is a state-of-the-art facility with several spaces to host performances, events and showcases. The center also offers several classes, including theatre and voice lessons.
sections.outdoors: Thanks to the scenic scapes, Olathe — the Shawnee word for “beautiful” — is a fitting name for this northeast Kansas city. Residents can enjoy 43 neighborhood community parks, nine linear parks, 57 miles of bike and pedestrian trails and 18 miles of bike lanes. Waterbugs rejoice! The city has several places to cool off in the summer. The Beach at Lake Olathe is a popular spot during the summer, equipped with locker rooms, outdoor showers, concessions, boat rentals and a floating obstacle course. Black Bob Bay Waterpark has two raft water slides, a lazy river, shallow water play structures and two pools. Back on dry land, residents can explore Ernie Miller Park & Nature Center, which features a 500-gallon aquarium, wildlife viewing room and miles of trails to meander the prairie and forest. Golfers can hit the links at Heritage Park Golf Course. Hike and bike along the Indian Creek Trail or at Lone Elm Park, where many Oregon, California and Santa Fe trail travelers spent their first night west of the Missouri River. The Olathe Prairie Center is a 300-acre tallgrass preserve and education site. Prairie Center Park offers a pedestrian trail, two playgrounds, 18-hole disc golf, a fishing pond, park shelters and fields and courts for soccer, baseball and basketball.
sections.restaurants: Nearby Kansas City is famous for its mouthwatering barbecue, and the foodie scene here is just as tantalizing. From casual eateries to city staples, there are various places to explore. Indulge in the Z-Man sandwich from Joe’s Kansas City Bar-B-Que or savor Mexican cuisine at Casa Amigos. J Bar is a foodie’s dream come true with a chef-driven menu. For a sweet treat, get your fix at Sylas & Maddy’s Homemade Ice Cream, which has more flavors than weeks in the year. Olathe also has a burgeoning craft beverage scene. Spots like Red Crow Brewing and Tall Trellis Brew Co. have redefined the local beer scene. What is one of the best places to eat in Olathe, KS? Romeito Cuban Restaurant is an unassuming spot that will blow your mind. There are specials on weekends, and their sandwiches can be enjoyed daily. What is the best coffee shop in Olathe, KS? Pour Coffee House is a coffee shop/zero-emissions coffee roaster in Olathe with some seriously delicious brews. Where is the best place for happy hour in Olathe, KS? You can’t get a more romantic view in Olathe (or more delicious sips) than at Stone Pillar Vineyard, which grows Old World French vines and French-American hybrids.
sections.education: The city offers diverse educational opportunities through its public and private schools. Olathe Public Schools is one of the most highly rated districts in the state, meeting the diverse needs of its almost 30,000 students. There are a number of private schools, more than half of which are religiously affiliated, that offer smaller class sizes and a focus on individualized learning styles. KS-overland-park.description: Situated in the heart of Kansas, Overland Park is a warm and welcoming community with picturesque neighborhoods, a strong economy, top-rated schools and plenty of things to do without skimping out on affordability.

sections.location: Just south of Kansas City, Overland Park is a thriving suburb along Interstate 35, a major route tying it to the KC metro. Kansas City International Airport is roughly 30 miles away, ready to keep Johnson County residents connected. Ready to roam? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Kansas City, MO, is just 10 miles north, where you can explore the Nelson-Atkins Museum, dig into barbecue or cheer at Arrowhead Stadium. Lawrence, KS, lies 30 miles west, home to the University of Kansas, historic Massachusetts Street and Clinton Lake. Topeka, KS, is about 60 miles west, boasting the Kansas State Capitol, an incredible arts scene and Lake Shawnee.
sections.economy: Benefiting from its location within the Kansas City metropolitan area, Overland Park has a long-standing reputation for being business-friendly and has seen consistent growth in various sectors. The city is home to several large companies, such as T-Mobile, and is a hub for finance, health care, and professional and technical services. The cost of living in Overland Park is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Overland Park is $386,658, and the average household income is $146,043. Kansas residents pay a state income tax, and residents can expect a 9.35% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Overland Park, KS? Overland Park Regional Medical Center, St. Luke’s South Hospital and Menorah Medical Center keep Overland Park residents healthy.
sections.thingsToDo: The fun is never done here. Residents have all kinds of fun things to do in their downtime, from enjoying a full-sized Tyrannosaurus rex and interactive exhibits at the Museum at Prairiefire to shopping for fruits, veggies, fresh-baked bread and jams at the Overland Park Farmers’ Market. Feeling crafty? Pay a visit to Ceramic Cafe, a paint-your-own pottery and glass fusing studio. The local creative spirit can be felt in the city’s arts and culture. There is something for all ages, from galleries that showcase local talent to public art installations and theater productions. The area's tight-knit community comes together several times throughout the year at the numerous festivals and events held in town, including the Overland Park Fall Festival. Overland Park blends upscale retail with charming local boutiques if some retail therapy is in store. Locals love the quaint charm of downtown with its locally-owned shops, cafes and galleries. What is something you can do for free in Overland Park, KS? Check out the murals all over town, which turn downtown Overland Park into an outdoor art gallery. What are some things to do with kids in Overland Park, KS? Bottle-feed baby goats at Deanna Rose Children’s Farmstead. Where do locals shop in Overland Park, KS? Perusing one of the many local shopping districts might land you in Oak Park Mall, a sprawling destination with renowned brands and department stores, or at Corbin Park and Hawthorne Plazas, retail villages that will entertain for hours. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Overland Park, KS? There are several places to enjoy live music or theater here, but the New Theatre Restaurant is a renowned dinner theater with a range of comedies, musicals and plays.
sections.outdoors: The area is like a playground for outdoor enthusiasts. The city lives up to its name, and — you guessed it — there are tons of parks. In fact, you could go to a new park every weekend and still not cover them all. Favorites include the large playground and splash pad at Roe Park or the pond, dog park and playground set on the 80 acres at Thomas S. Stoll Memorial Park. Consider buying an annual membership to the Overland Park Arboretum & Botanical Gardens, a 300-acre oasis. The arboretum hosts events like bird walks, wildflower walks, plant sales, seedling giveaways, wine tastings and a “Botanical Brewfest.” Looking for something more competitive? Hit the links at two of the best golf clubs in the KC Metro area — Sykes/Lady Overland Park and St. Andrews. Chicken N Pickle is an indoor/outdoor complex with pickleball courts, bocce ball and shuffleboard courts, an outdoor game yard and a game room with ping pong. During the winter, there is even an outdoor ice skating rink.
sections.restaurants: Kansas City-style barbecue is known for its thick, sweet sauce and slow-cooked meats – and is a staple of any diet. Restaurants in Overland Park will surely satisfy any craving, from laid-back brunches and kid-friendly patios to any type of cuisine you can imagine. Breweries, wineries and bars in Overland Park serve everything from local brews to specialty craft cocktails. Beer lovers enjoy experimenting at Brew Lab, which began as a homebrew supply shop and has evolved into a brewery, taproom and kitchen. Other sipping spots include Discourse Brewery, Aubrey Vineyards and Louie’s Wine Dive. What is one of the best places to eat in Overland Park, KS? Kansas City-style BBQ is a way of life here, and several delicious spots are in town. Q39 South, Jack Stack Barbecue and Joe’s KC BBQ can’t be missed. What is the best coffee shop in Overland Park, KS? Pilgrim Coffee is known for its cozy vibes and seasonal creations. A bonus? All syrups used in their drinks are made in-house. Where is the best place for happy hour in Overland Park, KS? Sip a beer (there are 50 or so to choose from) on the rooftop patio at The BrewTop Pub and Patio.
sections.education: The city is family-friendly, emphasizing amenities to keep kids occupied and excited morning, noon and night. This emphasis on a family-forward atmosphere also extends to the educational offerings available here. Overland Park is served by three school districts, all of which rank in the top 5 in the state, and Blue Valley Unified School District covers the largest area of town. With more than 22,000 students and a favorable 14:1 student-to-teacher ratio, Blue Valley schools consistently produce students achieving ACT scores exceeding both state and national averages. Several private school options include college prep and religious and alternative learning institutions. Hyman Brand Hebrew Academy and Saint Thomas Aquinas High School are highly favored private schools in town and across the state. KS-wichita.description: Located in the heart of Kansas, Wichita is known for its affordability, welcoming community and arts and culture scene. Residents have access to diverse opportunities, from a robust job market to a lively city center, exemplifying an authentic Midwestern experience.

sections.location: Wichita is a big-hearted, plains-proud city in south-central Kansas, about 200 miles southwest of Kansas City, where Interstate 35 and U.S. Route 54 meet. Wichita Dwight D. Eisenhower National Airport is just 7 miles west, a midsize hub offering flights to Dallas, Chicago and beyond. When you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Kansas City, MO, is 200 miles northeast, offering the National WWI Museum, savory BBQ at Gates and Chiefs games at Arrowhead Stadium. Oklahoma City, OK, sits 160 miles south, where you can explore the Oklahoma City National Memorial, wander Bricktown or visit the OKC Zoo. Tulsa, OK, is about 180 miles southeast, home to the Philbrook Museum of Art, the Gathering Place and the Woody Guthrie Center.
sections.economy: Wichita, the largest city in Kansas, has plenty of homegrown talent to fuel its diverse economy. Because of its size, the city is an important economic hub for the state, and three of the largest industries are health care, manufacturing and retail trade. Textron Aviation, Spirit AeroSystems and Ascension Via Christi, a health care provider, are major employers in the area. Koch Industries, a multinational conglomerate with a diverse portfolio of businesses that include refining, chemicals and agriculture, is headquartered here, and Pizza Hut got its start in the city (although its headquarters are elsewhere). The cost of living in Wichita is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Wichita is $191,957, and the average household income is $90,453. Kansas residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.50% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Wichita, KS? Ascension Via Christi has several high-performing facilities around Wichita. There are also specialty medical providers, like Kansas Heart Hospital and Kansas Spine and Specialty Hospital, that are located here and are high performers in several different procedures and condition categories.
sections.thingsToDo: With 20 museums and nearly 40 attractions within a 15-mile radius, local residents are never short of fun ways to fill a weekend itinerary. Exploration Place is a family-favorite science museum with live science demonstrations, laser shows set to tunes and super cool exhibits where you can rivet an airplane wing or measure the speed and humidity of a storm in a storm-chasing vehicle. Other top-notch museums to put on your must-visit list include The Kansas African American Museum, the Museum of World Treasures and the Kansas Aviation Museum. Other fun things to do include exploring the 20 acres of lush gardens at Botanica, catching a Wichita Thunder hockey game and checking out the impressive collection at the Wichita Art Museum. When you’re in the mood for some friendly competition, head to TopGolf or Chicken and Pickle to play pickleball on indoor or outdoor courts and stick around for trivia night or Sunday brunch. What is something you can do for free in Wichita, KS? Attention esteemed alums of the Pizza Hut BOOK IT! Program (and all other pizza lovers): You can visit the Pizza Hut Museum for free (!). It’s located on the Wichita State campus in the chain’s original restaurant and tells the tale of the two brothers who started the popular pizza chain in 1958. What are some things to do in Wichita, KS, with kids? Wichita and the surrounding area are packed with fun family attractions. Field Station: Dinosaurs bring dino fans face-to-face with life-sized, animatronic creatures, and Old Cowtown Museum, a living history museum with 54 buildings spread over 23 acres, shows what life was like from 1865 to 1880. Where do locals shop in Wichita, KS? Bradley Fair is an open-air shopping district that hosts fun events like family movie nights and concerts. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Wichita, KS? Dating back to 1922, Wichita’s Orpheum Theatre is a rare beauty showcasing atmospheric theater design. The iconic building is the stage for concerts, performances, films and more.
sections.outdoors: Wichita boasts more than 140 parks, including favorites like Sedgwick County Park, where you’ll find a playground, sledding hill, bike trails and a fishing pond among the fun attractions. For something a bit different, locals can try their hand (err, foot!) at footgolf, a unique hybrid of soccer and golf, at spots like Pine Bay Golf Course and Andover Municipal Golf Course. When summer hits, head to Splash Aqua Park, an inflatable park set on a 27-acre lake that features swings, ramps and trampolines. You can even hit up part of the Arkansas River Trail in the downtown area.
sections.restaurants: Home to 1,200 restaurants, locals can always find whatever they’re craving here. The restaurant scene ranges from homey comfort food spots like Doo Dah Diner to chic date night destinations like The Belmont, where you can grab a steak board with dips like creamy horseradish and chimichurri. Get your fill of schnitzel at a spot like Prost, which is in a recycled shipping container, and savor curries from Tuptim Thai. Explore the local beer and coffee scene via the Wicked Brew Tour (or draw inspiration from the circuit for your own self-exploration). Keep an eye on what’s happening at the National Institute of Culinary & Hospitality Education (NICHE), which opened a food hall in late 2024, starting with a coffee shop and bistro with stalls available for more restaurateurs. What is one of the best places to eat in Wichita, KS? Georges French Bistro is a friendly neighborhood restaurant that transports you to Paris with dishes like French onion soup, steak frites, escargot and creme brûlée. What is the best coffee shop in Wichita, KS? Can’t choose between a coffee break or a dessert outing? Check out Milkfloat for a caffeinated sweet treat: Affogato, which is espresso poured over ice cream. Where is the best happy hour in Wichita, KS? Dokum is a speakeasy-style spot in downtown Wichita. Enjoy specialty, hand-crafted cocktails with local live music four nights a week.
sections.education: Families in Wichita have several educational options to choose from. Wichita Public Schools is the area’s primary district, with a student population of more than 46,000, making it the largest district in the state. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable student-to-teacher ratio of 10:1. One thing to consider about schools here is that Kansas has open enrollment policies, allowing families to apply to schools outside their assigned district, including charter schools. When it comes to private schooling, there are several options for families to pick from in Wichita, each offering a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. The city is most notably home to Wichita Collegiate School, which was named the best private school in the state of Kansas by Niche. The school emphasizes STEM and college prep for its nearly 1,000 students. KY-lexington.description: Lexington lures residents with its bluegrass beauty and southern hospitality. While the city is most well-known for its equestrian heritage, Lexington's diverse cultural offerings, ample outdoor recreation, and busy downtown district can't be missed.
