faqs.q: What is the weather like in Lexington?
faqs.a: Lexington enjoys hot, humid summers and short, mild winters. Summer highs average above 77°F. July is the hottest month, with average highs around 85°F. The coldest month is January, with an average low of 27°F.

faqs.q: Does Lexington get a lot of snow?
faqs.a: The average annual snowfall in Lexington is below the national average at roughly 11 inches yearly.

faqs.q: Does Lexington get a lot of rain?
faqs.a: The average annual rainfall in Lexington is 46 inches, with May and June being the wettest months.

faqs.q: How many sunny days does Lexington get?
faqs.a: Lexington averages 186 sunny days a year.

faqs.q: What are some things to do outside in Lexington?
faqs.a: With bluegrass-covered rolling hills, a winding river and stunning pastures, Lexington is a beautiful place to enjoy year-round. Take in a round of golf at one of several area golf courses. Enjoy kayaking at Jacobson Park. Or visit a horse farm and add in a horseback riding excursion.

faqs.q: What is something free to do in Lexington?
faqs.a: Embrace Lexington's unique equine culture at Keeneland Thoroughbred Racing. You can view early morning workouts and stroll their beautifully landscaped grounds for free. If horses aren't your thing, visit the Lexington Art League, which offers free exhibitions of contemporary local and regional art in the historic Loudoun House.

faqs.q: What are some fun things to do with kids in Lexington?
faqs.a: Families will love the Kentucky Horse Park. With interactive museum exhibits, daily horse shows and holiday light shows, it's easy to find something kids will enjoy here.

faqs.q: What is the best open secret about Lexington?
faqs.a: In Lexington, home to some of the most famous horses in history, every Thoroughbred has at least one trait in common. No matter when they were born during the year, all Thoroughbreds in the Northern Hemisphere have a Jan. 1 birthday.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Lexington?
faqs.a: More than just a large, independent bookstore, Joseph-Beth is part of the fabric of Lexington. Featuring two stories of books and gifts and an on-site restaurant, Brontë Bistro, the Joseph-Beth Give Back Rewards program allows customers to select a community partner to receive a percentage of every purchase made in the store.

faqs.q: What is the best restaurant in Lexington?
faqs.a: Dudley's on Short is a sweet little spot with a big reputation as a dinner spot staple within the community. Located in the Northern Bank Building, built in 1889, this quirky two-story restaurant has won countless awards for its stellar American cuisine and excellent wine list.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Lexington?
faqs.a: Opened in 1887 and still going strong, the Lexington Opera House on West Short Street downtown is the perfect place to find all sorts of live performances, from Broadway productions to comedy shows. Then, of course, is Rupp Arena, which serves as both the home court for the Kentucky Wildcats basketball team and hosts dozens of nationally touring acts each year.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Lexington?
faqs.a: Lexington is in the heart of Bourbon Country, so why not visit one of the many spots in the Lexington Distillery District? This 25-acre property reimagined an abandoned distillery into a full entertainment spot with several working bourbon distilleries, various eateries and fun entertainment venues.

faqs.q: What are the three largest industries in Lexington?
faqs.a: Education, health care and manufacturing are key industries in Lexington. The University of Kentucky and Fayette County Public Schools are two of the city's largest employers. In addition, Tempur Sealy International, Valvoline and Lexmark International are also headquartered in Lexington.

faqs.q: What is the sales tax rate in Lexington?
faqs.a: In 2024, the sales tax rate in Lexington is 6%.

faqs.q: Does Kentucky have a state income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, Kentucky has a state income tax.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Lexington?
faqs.a: The University of Kentucky Albert B Chandler Hospital is one of the largest in Kentucky and the only hospital in the state to be ranked nationally in nearly a dozen adult specialties. Baptist Health is also recognized as one of the Best Regional Hospitals by U.S. News & World Report thanks to its high-performance ratings in multiple specialties.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Lexington?
faqs.a: Lexington has several major colleges and universities, including the University of Kentucky and Bluegrass Community & Technical College.

KY-louisville.description: It's not just bourbon and baseball in Louisville. This southern city is a haven for artists, is known for its iconic horse racing culture and has world-class dining, friendly locals and stunning parks. Louisville is a dynamic and inclusive place to call home.

sections.location: Louisville is a vibrant city in the Ohio River Valley in north-central Kentucky, about 110 miles south of Indianapolis, where Interstate 65 and U.S. Route 31 converge. Louisville Muhammad Ali International Airport is just 5 miles south, a central hub with flights nationwide. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Indianapolis, IN, is about 110 miles north, where you can catch a Colts game, marvel at the Indianapolis Museum of Art or wander the historic Monument Circle. Cincinnati, OH, sits 100 miles northeast; visit the Cincinnati Zoo, stroll Over-the-Rhine or admire the Roebling Suspension Bridge. St. Louis, MO, is roughly 240 miles northwest, boasting the Gateway Arch, the Missouri Botanical Garden and the Cardinals baseball team.
sections.economy: The city's economy is robust, with various positions available to job-seekers. The economy is also characterized by its diversity in industries, and the top ones are health care, manufacturing and trade, transportation and utilities. Several national brands have planted roots here — Ford Motor Company operates an assembly plant out of the city, and UPS has a major hub out of Louisville Muhammad Ali International Airport. Other major employers include Norton Healthcare, Baptist Health and Brown-Forman, a beverage company known for Jack Daniel’s. Additionally, Humana, General Electric Appliances and YUM! Brands (the umbrella company of KFC, Pizza Hut, Taco Bell and others) are all headquartered in Louisville. The cost of living in Louisville is significantly lower than the national average, especially compared to other large metropolitan areas. The median home value in Louisville is $247,510, and the average household income is $107,929. Kentucky residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Louisville, KY? Residents have their pick of providers, from the highly rated Baptist Health Louisville to the nationally ranked Norton Children’s Hospital.
sections.thingsToDo: Louisville has no shortage of fun things to do — more than 120 attractions are in town. Outside of obvious attractions like Churchill Downs, there are plenty of places where locals love to hang. The city's diverse neighborhoods each have their unique character, from the historic charm of Old Louisville to the artistic vibe of the Highlands. The city hosts numerous festivals and events annually, celebrating everything from culture and music to history and food. Artists can appreciate local galleries, KMAC and the 21c hotel. Book buffs love Carmichael’s Bookstore, the city’s oldest locally owned bookstore with two shops (plus a whimsical kid’s store) filled with hand-picked titles that reflect the neighborhoods. Spend the day exploring more than 25,000 square feet of local food, beverages and artisans at the Logan Street Market. Or take a sunset cruise aboard the Belle of Louisville, a rare Mississippi River-style steamboat. What is something you can do for free in Louisville, KY? The Speed Art Museum is Kentucky’s oldest and largest art museum, which is filled with amazing art and architecture, an impressive collection of Black and Native American works and interactive exhibits. It also hosts various community events, like a monthly yoga class. The museum offers free general admission every Sunday and the first Thursday of each month. Where do locals shop in Louisville, KY? The Nulu neighborhood of Louisville houses several locally owned boutiques, sweet shops and other specialty stores. Then, venture to The Highlands, where you’ll find many locally-owned businesses, including shops and galleries. Vintage lovers will rejoice with spots like hey tiger and Vintage Banana. What are some things to do in Louisville, KY, with kids? Kids will be mesmerized by the production process of the infamous Louisville Slugger at the Louisville Slugger Museum and can even get a photo with the giant baseball bat (120 feet tall!) outside. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Louisville, KY? Residents are highly entertained at the city’s dozens of performance arts venues. Catch a ballet at the Kentucky Performing Arts Center, a comedy show at the historic Louisville Palace or a national name at the KFC Yum! Center.
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities here are endless, with more than 120 parks and 13,000 acres of public land. Take a stroll or join a yoga class at Louisville Waterfront Park. Or visit E.P. “Tom” Sawyer State Park, a 554-acre park. Go ziplining or tackle a subterranean adventure course at Louisville Mega Cavern. Spend an afternoon hiking just outside town at Bernheim Arboretum and Research Forest. The Louisville Loop is a planned 100-mile trail system with paved and off-road sections for walking and biking that connects neighborhoods in Louisville and Jefferson County, KY. Cap off a day of outdoor adventures with a sunset on the Big Four Bridge, a bicycle and pedestrian bridge over the Ohio River connecting Louisville and Jeffersonville, IN. At night, it twinkles with LED lights and can be programmed to have a rainbow effect.
sections.restaurants: One of the country’s top foodie cities, Louisville offers plenty of restaurants that are sure to excite and delight. From classics like Derby pie to farm-to-table cuisine, chefs in town are known for putting a twist on traditional Southern dishes, creating a delightful array of creature comforts and new nibbles. Southern staples include The Eagle, Toasty’s Tavern and CASK Southern Kitchen and Bar. Check out Repeal Oak-Fired Steakhouse, La Chasse or Decade for more upscale offerings. There are plenty of cocktail bars here, too. Cozy up to a fire pit and take in sweeping city views at 8UP Elevated Drinkery & Kitchen. Bring Fido out for a night out in Germantown at 21st Amendment Tavern. Or grab a local brew from Against the Grain. What is one of the best places to eat in Louisville, KY? With a modern approach to Southern staples, 610 Magnolia's seasonal tasting menu is a foodie’s dream. What is the best coffee shop in Louisville, KY? Gralehaus is housed inside a 20th-century Victorian-style home and serves some of the best artisan roasts from across the country. Where is the best happy hour in Louisville, KY? Sit back in a pew (yes, a pew!) at Noche Mexican BBQ. Housed in a 1920s church, this happy hour spot highlights Latin American culture, and during the mid-afternoon, you can watch the sunset through the stained glass windows.
sections.education: Families can find the perfect fit for their kids in Louisville. There are a variety of educational options, including public, private and charter schools. Jefferson County Public Schools (JCPS) is the city's primary school district and one of the largest in the state, thanks to its student population of more than 95,000. JCPS offers a range of schools and programs, including traditional classroom environments and specialized magnet and charter schools. In terms of private education, Louisville has several outstanding options — according to Niche, three of the five best private schools in Kentucky are in this city. Creating a welcoming and inclusive environment is important for educators in Louisville. The de Paul School is a private school specifically designed for students of different abilities with learning differences. MA-lynn.description: Just minutes outside Boston, Lynn is a scenic city with affordable housing options, a growing arts scene and top-notch public schools and hospitals. Its close proximity to a major metro allows residents to enjoy plenty of big-city perks at a smaller price tag.

sections.location: Lynn is a coastal city nestled just 10 miles north of Boston, accessible via MA-1A and MA-129. The nearest airport, Boston Logan International Airport, is a mere 10 miles south, making it easy to connect to any destination. Nearby major cities include: Boston sits just 10 miles south, boasting the Freedom Trail, Boston Common and the Museum of Fine Arts. Salem is a mere 5 miles north, where you can delve into the history of the witch trials and enjoy the spooky charm. Cambridge is roughly 14 miles southwest, home to Harvard University and MIT.
sections.economy: Health care and social assistance, manufacturing and retail trade are essential to Lynn’s economy. Historically, manufacturing has been the most prominent of these industries, dating back to shoe production in the 1600s. Today, some of the largest employers in the city include General Electric (GE) Aviation, North Shore Medical Center and Lynn Public Schools. The city’s proximity to Boston and access to a regional workforce contribute to the area’s economic stability. The cost of living in Lynn is higher than the national average. The median home value in Lynn is $473,051, and the average household income is $95,272. Massachusetts residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Lynn, MA? Residents can access providers at BayRidge Hospital and Mass General Brigham Health Center.
sections.thingsToDo: The city has a rich tapestry of cultural experiences centered around its arts scene and community-focused events. The Lynn Museum & Historical Society is a historical exploration of the area, and the Lynn Auditorium has plenty of performances. Art enthusiasts can explore the Lynn Arts Cultural District, visit rotating exhibitions at GALA (Galleries at LynnArts) and witness the city’s transformation during the annual Beyond Walls Festival. The city celebrates its heritage through events like the Lynn Hip Hop Wall of Fame Festival and North Shore Latino Business Association events, while the Lynn Public Library hosts author talks and community discussions. Local theater productions and multicultural festivals at Lynn Commons enhance the city’s vibrant community spirit. What is something you can do for free in Lynn, MA? Take a walking tour of the downtown public art and grab some Instagram-worthy shots. The downtown area has a vibrant arts scene showcasing established and emerging artists and includes several public installations. What are some things to do in Lynn, MA, with kids? Lynn is the fifth-oldest colonial settlement in the Commonwealth and is chock-full of history. Spend an afternoon learning about both the city’s history and the early settlement days at the Lynn Museum & Historical Society. Admission is free for kids under age 12. Where do locals shop in Lynn, MA? Lucille’s Wine Shop. You can sip while you shop for bottles to take home because the shop has a full tasting room. The bottle shop also hosts themed tastings. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Lynn, MA? The Lynn Auditorium has hosted some of the music world’s biggest names and continues to host concerts, comedy and live theater.
sections.outdoors: Lynn is home to plentiful outdoor activities — it’s a perfect blend of sandy beaches and urban charm, offering a quick escape from the big city with its historic architecture and vibrant arts scene. The expansive Lynn Woods Reservation is home to more than 2,200 acres of trails for hiking, biking and wildlife observation. At the same time, the Lynn Shore Reservation and Red Rock Park provide scenic coastal views and waterfront relaxation. History enthusiasts can explore Dungeon Rock within Lynn Woods or visit High Rock Tower Park for panoramic city views. Residents can also enjoy beach activities at Nahant Beach Reservation, learn about the city’s maritime history at Lynn Heritage State Park, catch a baseball game at Fraser Field or relax in the central Lynn Commons. There are also bike paths that offer a scenic route for cycling along the waterfront, ensuring that residents can enjoy the city’s natural landscapes and historical landmarks.
sections.restaurants: You won’t leave this city hungry. The city has a dynamic foodie scene that blends traditional New England flare and inventive dishes. The city’s strong immigrant influences shine through in its Latin American, Southeast Asian and Middle Eastern restaurants. Seafood lovers can enjoy a fresh catch and classical coastal dishes, while casual diners and American eateries provide comfort food staples. Beyond the restaurant scene, the city has a growing craft food and drink scene highlighting local breweries, coffee shops and artisanal bakeries, creating community gathering spaces. Food trucks and pop-up events are also popular. What is one of the best places to eat in Lynn, MA? Nightshade Noodle Bar serves tasting menus that range from nine to 21(!) courses that feature Vietnamese and French-inspired seafood and noodle dishes. What is the best coffee shop in Lynn, MA? Grab a cappuccino and chocolate croissant at Union Cafè. Where is the best happy hour in Lynn, MA? The Blue Ox is a contemporary bistro featuring local, seasonal dishes, but the real stars of the show are their craft cocktails. There are old-school favorites and a rotation of seasonal sips to try, too.
sections.education: More than 15,000 students are enrolled in Lynn Public Schools, one of the largest districts in the state. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable student-to-teacher ratio of 12:1. The city is also home to KIPP Academy Lynn Collegiate. This charter school focuses on college readiness and character development. There are several private educational institutions, many with religious affiliations, in the area, too. MA-worcester.description: Worcester is a cozy community with a rich history and a vibrant, modern atmosphere in the heart of Massachusetts. With renowned cultural institutions, diverse neighborhoods and easy access to various outdoor activities, Worcester residents enjoy a dynamic community.

sections.location: Worcester is a vibrant city in central Massachusetts, about 40 miles west of Boston, easily accessible via major highways like I-290 and Route 9. Worcester Regional Airport is just 5 miles from downtown, offering convenient flights via American Airlines, Delta Air Lines and JetBlue. Ready to head out? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Boston is 40 miles east, boasting the Freedom Trail, Boston Common and the Museum of Fine Arts. Cambridge is roughly 45 miles east, home to Harvard University and MIT. Lowell sits 40 miles north, where you can discover the Lowell National Historical Park, Folk Festival and Memorial Auditorium.
sections.economy: Worcester is the second-largest metro in Massachusetts, giving way to a diverse economy with several key industries, including health care and social assistance, education and manufacturing. UMass Medical Center, City of Worcester, Worcester Public Schools and College of the Holy Cross are all significant employers. Both Polar Beverages and Hanover Insurance Group are headquartered here, too. The cost of living in Worcester is higher than the national average. The median home value in Worcester is $350,645, and the average household income is $94,205. Massachusetts residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Worcester, MA? Local residents are in good health. UMass Memorial Medical Center is rated high performing in 11 adult procedures and conditions. The hospital is part of the UMass Memorial Health Care system, which offers a wide range of medical services, including emergency care, cancer care, heart and vascular care, neurology and orthopedics. Why I Built My Business in Worcester (and You Can, Too!)Woman-owned Businesses in Worcester: Inclusive and InnovativeWant to Launch a Business in Worcester? Check Out These Resources
sections.thingsToDo: The city delivers a cultural mix where art meets history and entertainment. Art lovers can dive into museums and theaters like the Worcester Art Museum and the Hanover Theatre or wander streets splashed with eye-catching murals. The Canal District is a hot spot, buzzing with markets, breweries and Polar Park, home to the Worcester Red Sox. History buffs can visit the Worcester Historical Museum and the American Antiquarian Society, or explore the natural world at the EcoTarium and the New England Botanic Garden at Tower Hill. Live shows light up Mechanics Hall and the DCU Center, with the Jean McDonough Arts Center (JMAC) adding to the artsy vibes. Shoppers can find unique goods, and families can explore nearby attractions like Southwick’s Zoo and the living history museum at Old Sturbridge Village. The Worcester Cultural Coalition further enriches the city’s cultural landscape by promoting local arts and events, ensuring a dynamic and engaging community. What is something you can do for free in Worcester, MA? Spend an afternoon at the Worcester Art Museum. Admission is free on select days, and the museum has a vast collection of artwork from various cultures and periods. What are some things to do in Worcester, MA, with kids? Don’t miss the EcoTarium, a science and nature museum with three floors chock-full of fascinating exhibits — including animal habitats. Where do locals shop in Worcester, MA? Head to the Worcester Public Market, a year-round indoor market featuring local vendors, fresh produce, baked goods and artisanal products. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Worcester, MA? Built in 1904 and claiming to have something for everyone, The Hanover Theatre & Conservatory for the Performing Arts offers a wide range of music, dance, theater and other performances. Mechanics Hall is another local favorite. 8 Reasons to Move to Worcester, MARight at Home: Worcester, MA, is a Welcoming, Inclusive EnvironmentWhere to Live in Worcester, MA
sections.outdoors: The city's got a solid lineup for outdoor fun, whether relaxing in parks or hitting the water. Take it easy with walks and picnics at Elm Park, or check out any of the other 60-plus parks, including Salisbury and Green Hill. Lakes like Quinsigamond and Indian and Coes Pond provide space for boating, kayaking, fishing and swimming. During the summer, visit local beaches, too. Active individuals can take advantage of the more than 200 miles of hiking and biking trails. Mass Audubon’s Broad Meadow Brook is a significant urban wildlife sanctuary. In the winter, ski or tube at Wachusett Mountain and Ski Ward Ski Area or ice skate at the Worcester Common Oval and the Fidelity Bank Worcester Ice Center. The Greater Worcester Land Trust contributes to the city’s outdoor amenities, ensuring ample access to natural environments. Enjoy 4 Seasons of Fun Activities in Worcester, MAOutdoor Adventure Awaits in Worcester, MALooking for Outdoor Fun? Visit These 6 Parks in Worcester, MA
sections.restaurants: The food scene is a melting pot, where New England classics share the table with global flavors. The city boasts a robust collection of locally owned restaurants, highlighted by the Worcester Public Market's international offerings and the vibrant “Restaurant Row” on Shrewsbury Street. Diners and seafood spots provide classic fare, and the city’s significant immigrant population contributes to the cuisine with excellent Italian, Vietnamese, Japanese and Middle Eastern options. The farm-to-table movement is showcased at spots like Deadhorse Hill, and local favorites like Wormtown Brewery lead a booming craft beer culture. Cocktail lovers can sip cocktails from The Mercantile and Steel & Wire. Unique spots like Free Play Bar Arcade and School Yahd bring together food, drinks and entertainment. Or, satisfy a sweet tooth with treats from The Queen’s Cups or Meola’s Wayside Ice Cream. What is one of the best places to eat in Worcester, MA? Work your way through the 40 different kinds of tapas on the menu at Bocado Tapas Bar. What is the best coffee shop in Worcester, MA? Find ethically sourced coffee and a cozy environment with stocked bookshelves at Acoustic Java. The coffee shop also hosts live music and has a chess club. Where is the best happy hour in Worcester, MA? Peppercorn’s Grille & Tavern on Park Avenue is a hot spot for not only some seriously good Italian eats — we’re looking at you, thin-crust pizza — but also for happy hour deals and excellent cocktails. sections.education: This city is highly educated! With more than 24,000 students enrolled, Worcester Public Schools is the second-largest district in the state. The district is also highly diverse, offering specialized programs, including dual-language programs, and has a favorable student-to-teacher ratio of 14:1. There are also several private school options here, including several religious, college preparatory and alternative educational environments. Worcester Academy, an independent co-educational and boarding school, is on a gorgeous 71-acre campus and offers a real-world learning approach. faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Worcester, MA?

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Worcester, MA, with kids?
faqs.a: Don’t miss the EcoTarium, a science and nature museum with three floors chock-full of fascinating exhibits — including animal habitats.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Worcester, MA?

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Worcester, MA?

MD-columbia.description: Columbia's location in the heart of Howard County and its close proximity to Washington, D.C., entices residents to this community that blends nature, culture and innovation. Great schools, pristine parks and a focus on sustainability make it a great place to live.

sections.location: Columbia is a unique planned community tucked in Howard County between Baltimore and Washington, D.C., and Interstate 95 connects it to both cities. Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport is just 15 miles away, making travel easy. Ready to head out? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Baltimore is just 15 miles northeast, where you can explore the Inner Harbor, visit the National Aquarium or catch an Orioles game at Camden Yards. Washington, D.C., sits 25 miles south, packed with things to do, like the National Mall, Smithsonian museums and the U.S. Capitol. Annapolis, MD, is about 30 miles southeast, full of historic charm and home to the U.S. Naval Academy and Chesapeake Bay views.
sections.economy: The city's highly educated workforce and proximity to major metropolitan areas like Baltimore and Washington, D.C., fuels its economy. This planned community boasts a high quality of life, and industries like health care, education and professional and technical services have a strong presence in the area. Some of the largest employers in Columbia are Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory, Howard County General Hospital and Howard County Public Schools. The cost of living in Columbia is higher than the national average. The median home value in Columbia is $495,708, and the average household income is $171,110. Maryland residents pay a state income tax, and residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Columbia, MD? Howard County General Hospital is part of the Johns Hopkins Medicine System and one of the area's largest employers and health care providers. Residents can access several other wellness networks, including MedStar Health, Kaiser Permanente and LifeBridge Health.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents can enjoy a roster full of fun things to do, and the city offers an array of activities mainly centered around its downtown neighborhood. Color Bust Park is a community hub that offers free exercise classes, summer concerts and ice skating during the winter. From May to November, the Howard County Farmers Market meets three times a week for a producers-only market, and it sets up in Columbia on Sundays. Families will find ample entertainment in indoor play areas like Hyper Kidz and Play n’ Learn, as well as the ADA-compliant playground at Blandair Regional Park. When it comes to entertainment, the city’s commitment to the arts really shines at the annual Columbia Festival of the Arts LakeFest and through visits to the Columbia Art Center, which is set to reopen any day following renovations. Because of the city's convenient location, residents can also enjoy several regional attractions. A short drive to Baltimore provides opportunities to explore the Inner Harbor, visit the National Aquarium or attend an Orioles game. Take a trip south, and you’ll find all that D.C. has to offer, including 17 Smithsonian museums and galleries, the National Zoo and all the nation’s capital has to offer. What is something you can do for free in Columbia, MD? Explore the Howard County Farmers Market, which is open three times a week from May until November. You’ll love the local finds and live music at this producer-only market. What are some things to do in Columbia, MD with kids? Visit the Robinson Nature Center, which aims to connect people with nature. You’ll find a children’s discovery room, a digital planetarium and a bunch of educational exhibits. Where do locals shop in Columbia, MD? Locals often haunt the Mall of Columbia, a high-end shopping destination with more than 200 shops selling everything from fashion and accessories to electronics and home goods. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Columbia, MD? Looking for a place to catch a concert or enjoy a show? Merriweather Post Pavilion is a great spot for live music and performing arts. This iconic outdoor amphitheater, nestled in Symphony Woods, opened in the 1960s and has entertained residents with live performances ever since.
sections.outdoors: The area is an outdoor urban oasis, offering residents endless outdoor activities. Known as the Columbia Pathways, the city has an extensive network of interconnected pathways and over 100 miles of trails perfect for walking, jogging and biking, including the 2-mile loop around Lake Elkhorn. Lake Kittamaquandi and Lake Elkhorn are popular watering holes when it’s time to get out on the water, offering kayaking, canoeing and paddleboating. Columbia has more than 50 developed parks and 171 parks and tot lots, in addition to 114 miles of pathways, three artificial lakes, 41 ponds and 34 miles of stream valleys. Standouts include Centennial Park and Blandair Regional Park, both of which have playgrounds, sports fields and ADA-compliant facilities. Nearby in Ellicott City, Patapsco Valley State Park has hundreds of miles of hiking, biking and even horseback riding trails.
sections.restaurants: The city's foodie scene tantalizes every taste bud with a variety of flavors from around the world. From upscale spots like The Kings Contrivance to cozy cafes and fun food markets, residents can take a culinary journey. Endless ice cream and entertainment? Say less! That’s what you’ll get when you experience dinner and a show at Toby’s Dinner Theater. Columbia has a strong coffee shop culture and several different markets and restaurants that prioritize farm-to-table fare, like The Turn House. It also has a growing craft beer scene, with breweries like Sapwood Cellars and Black Flag Brewing Co. Indulge in specialty cocktails and local beer at Cured Table & Tap and Victoria Gastro Pub — their duck-fat french fries are a must. What is one of the best places to eat in Columbia, MD? Try 18th and 21st, with a speakeasy feel, live music and modern riffs on classic cocktails. Be sure to grab an order of the duck-fat fries. What is the best coffee shop in Columbia, MD? Start your day off at Roggenart, where you can pair your espresso with a Reese’s peanut butter croissant. Where is the best place for happy hour in Columbia, MD? Savor some tacos and a beer at Ale House Columbia.
sections.education: Columbia is part of the Howard County Public School System, which is recognized as the best school system in Maryland and one of the strongest in the nation. The nearly 58,000 students enrolled in Howard County schools have a 13:1 student-to-teacher ratio and plenty of clubs and activities to get involved in. The area also has many private school options, from independent to religious schools. MD-frederick.description: Frederick enchants residents with a well-preserved downtown, diverse cultural offerings, top-rated schools and a thriving arts scene. The city’s proximity to Washington, D.C., and relative affordability offer a perfect blend of small-town charm and urban amenities.

sections.location: Frederick is a historic city in north-central Maryland along Interstate 70 and Interstate 270. The nearest airport, Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport, is about 50 miles southeast. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Baltimore is just 45 miles east, where you can explore the Inner Harbor or visit the National Aquarium. Washington, D.C., sits 45 miles southeast, offering the National Mall and Smithsonian museums. Hagerstown is about 25 miles west, home to the Washington County Museum of Fine Arts and the Chesapeake & Ohio Canal.
sections.economy: Significant industries in Frederick include biotechnology and life sciences, information technology and manufacturing. Additionally, the city is home to Fort Detrick, so a substantial military presence bolsters the economy. AstraZeneca, RoosterBio and the Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research are other top employers. The cost of living in Frederick is higher than the national average, but that’s to be expected due to its close proximity to both Baltimore and Washington, D.C. The median home value in Frederick is $399,885, and the median household income is $126,045. Maryland residents pay a state income tax, and those living here can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Frederick? The city is home to several major health care providers, including Frederick Health and Frederick Regional Health System. In addition, Frederick Health Hospital is rated high-performing in half a dozen procedures and conditions.
sections.thingsToDo: The city’s proximity to Washington, D.C., might make visitors think of this Maryland spot as a sleepy bedroom community; however, Frederick is full of fun things to do and plenty of activities that keep residents booked and busy. The city appeals to residents looking for more than just a commuter lifestyle and blends history and innovation in a way that encourages creativity and inspires entrepreneurial-minded individuals to make things happen. The Frederick Historic District is home to more than 200 specialty shops, art galleries, breweries, distilleries and restaurants. Like many other cities, Frederick has its fair share of murals and street art, but unlike other cities, it has created a downtown public art trail that maps all of the pieces out for you and gives you insight into the history of the town. And that artistic flair extends throughout the city. A beloved local feature is the internationally recognized ‘Community Bridge,’ a trompe l’oeil mural. For the history buffs, there are plenty of historical sites around the city. It's known for its place in Civil War history, specifically for the Battle of Monocacy, which saved Washington, D.C. The site is now a national battlefield, which you can explore on a driving tour or walking trail. Or hop on your two-wheeler and start pedaling along the Frederick History Bicycle Loop, where you can see much of the historic district and get some fresh air. What is something free to do in Frederick? You won’t run out of choices for free entertainment here. Baker Park, an expansive park downtown, is full of walking paths and playgrounds. For history buffs, take a self-guided walking tour of downtown Frederick to learn about the city’s historical significance, which dates back to 1745. Local residents can fully take advantage of a vibrant arts and entertainment scene, with places like Weinberg Center for the Arts, the city’s premiere performing arts destination, regularly hosting performances throughout the year. What are some fun things to do in Frederick with kids? Take your creative kiddo to the Delaplaine Arts Center, where they can take one of the 250-plus classes each year. Be sure to also walk through any of their seven art galleries on site. Where do locals shop in Frederick, MD? The Curious Iguana is the literary hub here. The store is both a community gathering space and a business on a mission, donating portions of its proceeds to international nonprofits. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Frederick? For music lovers, the city hosts an annual Alive @ Five outdoor happy hour series. The event takes place at Carroll Creek Amphitheater every Thursday from May to September. Entry is less than $10, and food and drinks are available on-site.
sections.outdoors: After a long day in the office, nature lovers find that they feel right at home in Frederick. Carroll Creek Linear Park is downtown, boasting the world’s largest free public water garden and the Appalachian Mountains are not too far away. If you’re into cycling, you’ll especially love the many trails and the downtown coffee counter, Gravel & Grind, that doubles as a bike shop. During the summer, residents can beat the heat, grab a canoe or kayak and spend the afternoon floating down the Monocacy River. Pro tip: Don’t miss the city’s most unusual claim to fame, the country’s only high-wheel cycling race. It’s held every summer with a food-truck-filled Lawn Party for spectators to join in the fun. Frederick is also home to nearly 100 national, state, county and municipal park facilities that offer a wide variety of experiences and activities, like swimming, boating, camping, hiking and horseback riding. The city provides a range of nature programs in every environment you can think of — from urban city parks to wilderness reserves. Visit Gambrill State Park, where you can treat yourself to some stunning views of the Shenandoah Mountains as well as hike, bike, horseback ride or even reserve a space at the park’s quaint tea room.
sections.restaurants: The local dining scene is made up of everything from national favorites to hyper-local spots. The downtown main streets offer plenty of places to grab a bite and offer flavors for every palate. The culinary and farm-to-fork culture is thriving here, with more farms in Frederick County than any other county in Maryland. Cacique Fine Spanish & Mexican Cuisine provides a gorgeous ambiance and tasty, unique menu options like roasted duck, platanos maduros and brochettes of jumbo shrimp. However, the real star of the Frederick culinary scene is the city’s ample number of wineries, breweries and distilleries. There are more than 35 tasting rooms throughout the Frederick County area, so you’ll be sure to find something to whet your whistle. The Frederick Wine Trail covers the county’s area, from historic battlefields to happening local hangouts. Brewer’s Alley serves up some good brews and an interesting dash of history. Housed in a former market/town hall that was built in 1769, the building has served many purposes throughout the centuries, such as an opera house, an office building and, most peculiarly, it was used for a memorial service for President McKinley in 1901. What is one of the best places to eat in Frederick, MD? The Tasting Room stands out among the crowd as a must-visit for dinner. Housed in a beautifully restored historic building, this local hot spot blends modern flair with a cozy, intimate atmosphere. The menu is full of dishes emphasizing locally sourced ingredients and has an innovative cocktail menu. What is the best coffee shop in Frederick, MD? Gravel & Grind serves up the best cup of joe in town and has an incredible selection of bicycles for sale (including stock from Brooklyn Bicycle Co. and Surly Bikes). Where is the best happy hour in Frederick, MD? JoJo’s Restaurant & Tap House has all you could ever want when unwinding from work: a big draft list, a lively crowd and pub fries. Heavenly.
sections.education: Education is a high priority here, with a top-rated public school system and higher-ed institutions. The Frederick County Public Schools system is among the best in the state. In 2023, the system reported that a staggering 75% of its schools earned four or five stars on the Maryland School Report Card, which evaluates schools on a five-star scale based on student performance in reading, math, science and other areas. MI-ann-arbor.description: Go big, go bold, go blue in Ann Arbor. Located in southeastern Michigan, Ann Arbor is a thriving college town full of plenty of things to do, from top-notch schools to plenty of parks and recreation opportunities, all while maintaining a low cost of living.

sections.location: Ann Arbor is a lively city in southeastern Michigan, less than an hour west of Detroit, along Interstate 94. Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport, a central hub for flights far and wide, is approximately 25 miles east. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Detroit, MI, is just 45 miles east, where you can admire the Detroit Institute of Arts or visit the Motown Museum. Toledo, OH, sits 50 miles south, offering plenty of family-friendly fun, like the Toledo Museum of Art and the Toledo Zoo. Lansing, MI, is about 65 miles northwest, home to the Michigan State Capitol and the Potter Park Zoo.
sections.economy: Jobs in Ann Arbor are plentiful, and the economy is well-rounded and robust. Three of the largest industries in A2 are manufacturing, health care and automotive. In addition, several notable companies are headquartered here, including Domino’s Pizza, Borders Group and Esperion Therapeutics. Companies like ProQuest, NSF International, Google and Nokia have a significant presence here and are among the numerous companies flocking to the area. Compared to other tech hubs, the cost of living in Ann Arbor is low, and there’s an abundance of talent coming out of programs at U-M, Eastern Michigan University and Washtenaw Community College. The cost of living in Ann Arbor is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Ann Arbor is $430,301, and the average household income is $125,019. Michigan residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Ann Arbor, MI? Residents are in good health, and providers include the University of Michigan Hospitals — Michigan Medicine, St. Joseph Mercy Ann Arbor and C.S. Mott Children’s Hospital. Why Are Companies Flocking to Ann Arbor, MI?This City in Michigan is a Global Life Sciences HubCompanies Enjoy Deep Pool of Highly Skilled Employees in Ann Arbor, MI
sections.thingsToDo: Local attractions are plentiful! There are many ways to have fun here, including several annual festivals! Ask anyone who lives in A2, and they’ll tell you there is something magical about living in Ann Arbor … literally. Keep an eye out for fairies as several local businesses and homes build miniature “fairy doors” to serve as whimsical portals for their mythical residents. Ann Arbor is an artsy city. Filled with all kinds of colorful murals, graffiti and public art installations, there is an innovative vibe that encourages creativity throughout. The annual Ann Arbor Art Fair, the largest juried art fair in the country, is held each July with more than 1,000 artists. Of course, this is a college town, after all, and lots of entertainment opportunities are centered around the University of Michigan. People don’t mess around when it comes to experiencing ’The Big House’ — the nation’s largest football stadium! — during football season, but there are opportunities to cheer on the Wolverines in various sports all year long. What is something you can do for free in Ann Arbor, MI? University of Michigan Museum of Art is free and open to the public, with more than 20,000 objects from Western, Asian and African cultures and modern and contemporary art. What are some things to do with kids in Ann Arbor, MI? Ann Arbor Hands-On Museum is an interactive spot designed to entertain and educate children on various scientific and technological topics. Where do locals shop in Ann Arbor, MI? During the weekends, residents enjoy spending time at the Ann Arbor Farmers Market in the historic Kerrytown District. The market has been around for over 100 years and features more than 120 vendors across the state. Or, if you bleed blue, show off your school pride by visiting the M Den, the official retailer of Michigan athletics. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Ann Arbor, MI? The Ark is a legendary local venue known for its commitment to promoting folk and acoustic music. Its calendar features a variety of live performances from regional and national artists. 8 Reasons to Move to Ann Arbor, MiThings to Do in Ann Arbor, MI7 Must-Visit Festivals in Ann Arbor, MI
sections.outdoors: Located in the lower “hand” of the Mitten State, local residents have endless outdoor activities at their fingertips. Go kayaking or canoeing along the Huron River at the Delhi Metropark. You might even spot some turtles! The Argo Cascades are a series of drops and pools located near downtown and are one of the most popular warm-weather attractions. On dry land, the Border-to-Border Trail is a 35-mile, ADA-accessible paved pathway with half a dozen trail corridors connecting residents to cities and parks throughout Washtenaw County. Biking is a breeze here, with more than 79 miles of bike lanes across the city, earning the city the designation of a Bicycle Friendly Community by the League of American Bicyclists. The area has an additional 55 city and regional parks, including the 20,000-acre Waterloo Recreation Area, the largest park in Michigan’s Lower Peninsula. Nickels Arcade or the Matthaei Botanical Gardens are also popular spots for families. Walking and Biking in Ann Arbor Is a BreezeA River of Fun Runs Through Ann Arbor, MI
sections.restaurants: Local restaurants are as diverse as the residents — with more than 400 restaurants offering everything from American classics to award-winning Italian. Downtown Ann Arbor has a varied international food scene that will delight your senses. The local food economy, in particular, thrives with an emphasis on locally sourced ingredients. Families can feast at a number of local restaurants, and everyone knows that Zingerman’s Deli is an institution that will satisfy any sandwich craving. For the grown-ups, the city has embraced boutique spirits and artisan cocktail culture. Among the popular bars making an impact with their creative cocktails are the Raven’s Club and The Last Word. What is one of the best places to eat in Ann Arbor, MI? For a unique and flavorful night out, fill up your plate at Frita Batidos. In addition to being downright fun to say, this Cuban-inspired restaurant offers a variety of fritas (Cuban burgers) and batidos (shakes). What is the best coffee shop in Ann Arbor, MI? 19 Drips Coffee and Tea is a coffee and tea shop with a twist of Yemeni. Where is the best happy hour in Ann Arbor, MI? The breweries here are so dog-friendly that some even have custom menus for furry, four-legged visitors. Grizzly Peak Brewing Co., for instance, offers a special “Pups on the Patio” menu, with proceeds benefiting the Huron Valley Humane Society.
sections.education: Ann Arbor is a well-known college town geared to the 51,000 students at the University of Michigan on its 3,200-acre campus, giving Ann Arbor a pipeline of talent. In fact, the university is the city’s largest employer, followed by the school’s health care system. Ann Arbor Public Schools is a highly rated public school district, serving over 17,000 students and ranked as the best in the state. Several private school options are available for families, ranging from religiously affiliated to college prep. MI-troy.description: Just north of Detroit, Troy is a suburb full of modern conveniences, including highly-ranked schools, safe neighborhoods and a thriving economy. From upscale shopping to diverse dining to outdoor activities, Troy provides residents with abundant amenities.

sections.location: Troy, Michigan, is a vibrant city in southeastern Michigan, just north of Detroit. It is linked to the Motor City via Interstate 75. Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport, a major hub for flights far and wide, is around 35 miles away. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Detroit is just 20 miles south, where you can admire the Detroit Institute of Arts, catch a Tigers game at Comerica Park or browse Eastern Market. Warren, MI, sits 10 miles southeast, offering the Detroit Arsenal, the Warren Civic Center, and plenty of parks to unwind. Sterling Heights, MI, is about 5 miles east, known for its family vibe, the Sterling Heights Nature Center, and the fun Sterlingfest.
sections.economy: Troy has numerous companies involved in the automotive world. In fact, the city is a significant contributor to Michigan’s ranking as one of the largest high-tech workforce employers in the country. In addition, research and development, engineering, advanced manufacturing, financial services and health care contribute to the city’s economy. And, if you’re looking to make connections, nearby Detroit’s Let’s Detroit periodically offers networking and job-oriented events geared to students and young professionals. The cost of living in Troy is higher than the national average. The median home value in Troy is $385,318, and the average household income is $154,294. Michigan residents pay a state income tax, and residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Troy, MI? There are several health care providers here, including Beaumont Hospital, one of the city’s largest and most highly rated hospital systems. Why I Started a Business in Michigan, and You Should, Too
sections.thingsToDo: Residents can appreciate the suburb’s ability to blend historical charm and modern-day amenities. History enthusiasts will enjoy exploring Troy Historic Village, a 5-acre complex featuring 10 preserved 19th-century structures and a glimpse into the daily life of those who established homes and farms in the area in the 1800s. Families find entertainment at the interactive exhibits at the Stage Nature Center and Troy Family Aquatic Center, a seasonal water park. Troy Sports Center offers ice skating and hockey. The Troy Farmers Market showcases local produce and artisanal goods every Saturday. The Somerset Collection provides more than 180 retailers and 1.4 million square feet of shopping and dining for some retail therapy. Residents also have access to various area amenities, including the Detroit Zoo and the Cranbrook Institute of Science. What is something you can do for free in Troy, MI? Hit the courts! Play tennis or sand volleyball at Boulan Park. What are some things to do with kids in Troy, MI? Take a step back in time when iPads and Peppa Pig were a thing of the future. Not every city has a living and interactive historical village like this one does at the Troy Historic Village. Where do locals shop in Troy, MI? Looking for a bit of luxury? The Somerset Collection Mall is an upscale, super-regional shopping mall with over 180 stores. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Troy, MI? Ridgedale Players is one of the oldest community theaters in the state and a beloved destination for locals interested in supporting local performers — or indulging in a night out on the town.
sections.outdoors: The Mitten State packs a punch when it comes to outdoor activities, and this city is no exception. The area is a dreamland for the outdoorsy types, with lakes, streams, two golf courses, a nature center, a skate park, disc golf courses, tennis and basketball courts, soccer and baseball fields, play and picnic areas, and a thousand acres of parks — providing plenty of space for picnicking, sports and leisurely strolls. For those seeking more active experiences, golfing at Sanctuary Lake or Sylvan Glen is a must. Hike and explore the trails at the Stage Nature Center, a 100-acre natural preserve with 2 miles of trails. The Troy Community Center boasts a fantastic fitness facility where residents can find several ways to stay active.
sections.restaurants: From steakhouses to breweries and nearly everything in between, there are plenty of places to satisfy any palate. Grab a local slice from Alibi of Troy or sensational seafood at Ocean Prime. What is one of the best places to eat in Troy, MI? A melting pot of Asian cuisine is on the menu at Mon Jin Lau, and you can find sushi from this iconic restaurant in local grocery stores. What is the best coffee shop in Troy, MI? Global flavors meet local roasts at % Percent Coffee, where you can enjoy everything from a Japanese matcha latte to a traditional Turkish coffee. Where is the best place for happy hour in Troy, MI? Happy hour at the Yard House is Monday to Friday from 3 to 6 p.m., with late-night happy hour every Wednesday to Sunday from 10 p.m. to close. Get drink specials plus half-off apps and pizzas.
sections.education: If you’re looking to start a family, look no further. Thanks to highly rated schools, including a stand-out fine arts program, a college and branches of several universities, Troy is easily one of the best places in Michigan for families. The Troy School District is ranked in the top 100 in the country and is the third best in the state, with 20 schools and more than 12,000 students. With an 18:1 student-to-teacher ratio, students benefit from individualized attention. There are also 11 private schools in the area. MN-bloomington.description: Bloomington's location in the Twin Cities metro area makes it a prime location for young families and professionals. Top-notch schools, a robust economy and plenty of outdoor activities contribute to Bloomington residents' well-rounded lifestyle.

sections.location: Bloomington is just south of Minneapolis along Interstate 494. The Minneapolis-Saint Paul International Airport, a central international hub, is just 5 miles away. Ready to venture out? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Minneapolis is just 10 miles north, where you can visit the Walker Art Center, roam the Sculpture Garden or cheer at U.S. Bank Stadium. Saint Paul sits 15 miles northeast, offering the Minnesota State Capitol, Science Museum and the Cathedral of Saint Paul. Rochester is about 75 miles southeast, tempting with the Rochester Art Center and lovely Silver Lake Park. It’s also home to the Mayo Clinic.
sections.economy: The city’s location in the Minneapolis-St. Paul metro area primes the city for economic success. The largest industries here are health care, retail and manufacturing. The Mall of America, Health Partners and Best Buy are some of the major employers. To sweeten the deal, Dairy Queen’s headquarters is located here. The cost of living in Bloomington is typically lower than the national average. The median home value in Bloomington is $350,986, and the average household income is $121,162. Minnesota residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9.03% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Bloomington, MN? Bloomington’s proximity to Minneapolis gives residents many world-class providers to choose from. Closer to home, M Health Fairview Southdale Hospital is recognized as a high-performer in 10 different procedures and conditions.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents love living here thanks to the city’s array of activities, entertainment and cultural and educational experiences. Families can enjoy hands-on learning at The Works Museum, aquatic adventures at Great Wolf Lodge Water Park and year-round fun at Edinborough Park, an indoor play area in nearby Edina. The city has unique attractions like the Museum of Illusions, SEA Life Minnesota Aquarium, and both the Can Can Wonderland and Trapped Puzzle Rooms in St. Paul. The Northwest Airlines History Center is perfect for those interested in history and aviation. What is something you can do for free in Bloomington, MN? There are several free activities here, but one of the best is exploring the natural beauty of the Minnesota Valley National Wildlife Refuge. The refuge has walking trails and scenic overlooks, providing plenty of chances to spot native birds and enjoy fresh air. What are some things to do with kids in Bloomington, MN? Pack a picnic and head to Hyland Lake Park Reserve and Play Area, which features a three-story slide, ropes course and scenic lake to walk around. Take the kids to the Nickelodeon Universe indoor theme park at the Mall of America on a rainy day. Where do locals shop in Bloomington, MN? Stock up on local produce at the Bloomington Farmers Market, held every Saturday from June to October. During the summer, visitors can also enjoy a Midweek Music and Market on Wednesdays and even a monthly Indoor Market during the winter. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Bloomington, MN? The Mall of America occasionally hosts live performances in the Rotunda area, including musical and cultural performances. The best way to find out about upcoming shows is to check the mall’s event calendar. The Target Center in nearby Minneapolis hosts big-name artists.
sections.outdoors: Located in the iconic Land of 10,000 Lakes, this city has earned a well-deserved reputation as a beautiful place to call home. Because of these vast waterways, it’s easy for local residents to take a break in local parks and nature areas. Outdoor enthusiasts appreciate the city’s pride in maintaining its extensive park system. There are plenty of green spaces, trails and nature facilities for residents of all ages and abilities. Normandale Lake Park is considered a local treasure, featuring a lake for paddleboarding and kayaking in summer that transforms into a hub for ice skating and cross-country skiing in winter. Hyland Lake Park Reserve is another local favorite with miles of hiking and biking trails, a beach and spots for downhill skiing and snowboarding during the colder months. Another great destination is the Minnesota Valley National Wildlife Refuge, which boasts around 14,000 acres and an abundance of opportunities for birdwatching.
sections.restaurants: Minnesota is full of mouthwatering foodie destinations, including Bloomington. Blending established local favorites and new ventures, there is an array of international cuisines, including Indian dishes at Aroma Indian Cuisine and Chinese menus from Mandarin Kitchen. Italian fare is a favorite, with Ciao Bella offering classic dishes and Luna di Luna providing hearty pasta in a family-friendly atmosphere. Long-standing establishments like Wally’s Roast Beef contribute to the area's charm. The city supports culinary entrepreneurs through initiatives like “Hatch Bloomington,” fostering local startups like the Scramblin’ Egg food truck. Residents also have easy access to the broader offerings of the nearby Twin Cities. What is one of the best places to eat in Bloomington, MN? Grab your pals for a delicious dinner at Hazelwood Food & Drink, where your order for the table should definitely be the brie cheese curds. What is the best coffee shop in Bloomington, MN? Mugshots Coffee Company in the Valley West Shopping Center is known for its great coffee, modern atmosphere and excellent eats. Where is the best place for happy hour in Bloomington, MN? Choose from one of the 24 rotating beers on tap at NorthStar Tavern.
sections.education: Bloomington is known for its quality school systems, which makes it an appealing destination for families. The city schools often rank high in state assessments and offer a good balance of academic and extracurriculars to elementary, middle and high school students. The Bloomington Public School District is one of the most diverse districts in Minnesota and offers a 20:1 student-to-teacher ratio for the more than 10,000 students enrolled. There are also plenty of private schools to choose from, with roughly 20 in the area. MN-duluth.description: With stunning views of Lake Superior and endless outdoor adventures, Duluth, MN, is the natural home for outdoor enthusiasts. Housing, education and health care are top priorities for residents, fostering a strong sense of community in this scenic city.

sections.location: Duluth is a scenic city on the north shore of Lake Superior, about 155 miles north of Minneapolis, accessible via U.S. Route 2 and Minnesota State Route 61. Duluth International Airport is just 5 miles south, offering convenient flights. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some nearby cities: Minneapolis-St. Paul sits 155 miles south, boasting the Mall of America, the Minnesota State Capitol and the Guthrie Theater. Superior, WI, is just 5 miles east; visit the Fairlawn Mansion, the Richard I. Bong Memorial Bridge and Barker’s Island Park. Thunder Bay, Canada, is 150 miles north, boasting the Sleeping Giant Provincial Park, the Fort William Historical Park and Prince Arthur’s Landing.
sections.economy: Economic diversity and stability play a major role in the city's attraction to newcomers. The city has retained much of its maritime and shipping heritage, but it’s evolved to become a hub for industries such as health care, education and tourism. Institutions like the University of Minnesota Duluth and St. Luke’s Hospital are major employers, and the growing craft brewery scene and thriving tech and start-up culture provide additional opportunities for young professionals and families alike. The cost of living in Duluth is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Duluth is $234,322, and the average household income is $94,972. Minnesota residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.88% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Duluth, MN? The city is home to two first-rate hospitals, which provide residents with access to more than 50 specialty care areas and 12,000 health care professionals. Essentia Health and St. Luke’s Hospital set Duluth apart and make the city a destination medical region.
sections.thingsToDo: Life here is anything but dull. History, arts and entertainment come together in this Minnesota marvel. Explore the city’s past at sites like the St. Louis County Depot and Glensheen Mansion, or delve into art at the Duluth Art Institute and the Tweed Museum of Art. The performing arts scene thrives with productions at the Duluth Playhouse, live improv at Zeitgeist and concerts by the Duluth Superior Symphony Orchestra. Families find fun at the Great Lakes Aquarium, the Lake Superior Zoo and the Lake Superior Railroad Museum. Or gather for free outdoor movies at Leif Erikson Park. Canal Park is great for shopping, dining, and arts and culture. Browse local spots like Indigenous First Art & Gift Shop and Hucklebeary. The city also hosts various events and festivals, and its friendly, welcoming atmosphere, coupled with iconic landmarks like the Aerial Lift Bridge, contributes to its appeal. What is something you can do for free in Duluth, MN? Visit the Tweed Art Museum on the University of Minnesota Duluth campus. Tweed is a collecting and teaching museum with a global collection of over 10,000 objects spanning eight centuries. Admire all types of mixed-media pieces, including paintings, photographs, sculptures and ceramics. One of the coolest pieces you’ll see is the mural in the elevator! What are some things to do with kids in Duluth, MN? Spend the day at the Great Lakes Aquarium, a three-story waterfront structure with various exhibits. Kids will especially love ‘Otter Cove,’ where otters play, and the ‘Isle Royale,’ which features an 85,000-gallon tank with lake sturgeon, burbot and more. Where do locals shop in Duluth, MN? Hucklebeary is a cute shop for cards and party supplies and hosts craft and movie nights. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Duluth, MN? The Duluth Homegrown Music Festival can’t be missed. The festival is spread over multiple venues, two cities and eight days, and more than 150 acts come together to entertain.
sections.outdoors: Outdoor amenities are out of this world in Duluth. The shores of Lake Superior characterize the stunning natural landscape. The city has more than 200 miles of trails, like the Lakewalk and the Ely’s Peak hike, with amazing views. Parks like Leif Erikson Park, Congdon Park and Piedmont Park provide family-friendly spaces with playgrounds and recreational areas. During the warmer months, Park Point offers sandy beaches for relaxation and swimming, while Enger Park and Lester Park Golf Courses provide scenic golfing experiences. Winter turns the area into a cold-weather playground with unexpected opportunities to have fun, including cross-country skiing at Lester-Amity Ski Trail and downhill skiing at Chester Bowl. Birdwatching is popular year-round, especially at Hawk Ridge Nature Reserve. The city’s extensive parkland and trails, coupled with its proximity to natural attractions like the Superior Hiking Trail and Enger Tower, make it a haven for outdoor enthusiasts.
sections.restaurants: The local dining scene is growing by the day and is characterized by its blend of hearty Northern cuisine with fresh, locally sourced ingredients and a thriving craft beverage culture. Casual spots like The Duluth Grill cater to regional dishes, while upscale destinations like Lake Avenue Restaurant & Bar focus on local flavors. Seafood lovers can enjoy fresh catches at The Boat Club Restaurant, while Northern Waters Smokehaus offers artisan smoked fish and meats. Craft beer is a big deal here, with breweries like Bent Paddle Brewing Co. and Canal Park Brewing Company offering a variety of local brews. For something sweeter, Duluth Cider has creative ciders. Places like Sir Benedict’s Tavern on the Lake have good food and great views, making Duluth a fun place to eat and drink. What is one of the best places to eat in Duluth, MN? Ride the Pizza Party Express train, which operates evenings in summer and fall. What is the best coffee shop in Duluth, MN? Wussow’s Concert Cafe is a cozy coffeehouse with a full live music calendar. Duluth Coffee Company grew out of a love for coffee with a small roaster and delivering beans from a pickup truck. The cafe is a great place to spend time or just grab a pick-me-up before work. They also mentor those considering opening a coffee shop and offer barista training. Where is the best happy hour in Duluth, MN? Fitger’s is right on the waterfront, within walking distance from downtown, and serves award-winning ales and lagers and elk burgers. (And normal burgers, too.)
sections.education: Residents can take advantage of a range of educational options, including a comprehensive school system, private schools and charter schools. The Duluth Public School District serves over 8,000 students across grades PK-12, with a student-teacher ratio of 19:1. The district has worked to make improvements in recent years to increase proficiency in academics across the board. The city is also home to several top-notch private schools, including Marshall School, one of the state’s highest-ranked college prep schools. MN-minneapolis.description: Minneapolis is an artsy community full of natural beauty and urban sophistication. Diverse neighborhoods, a strong job market, parks and recreation and a commitment to sustainability and affordability contribute to the laidback lifestyle in Minneapolis.

sections.location: Minneapolis is a vibrant city in east-central Minnesota, known as the “City of Lakes,” with over 20 lakes within its borders. It’s accessible via major highways like Interstate 94 and U.S. Route 169. The nearest airport, Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport is just 10 miles south, offering flights worldwide. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: St. Paul is just 10 miles east, home of the Minnesota State Capitol, the Science Museum of Minnesota and the Xcel Energy Center. Duluth is roughly 155 miles north, where you can enjoy the scenic beauty of Lake Superior, the Duluth Harbor and the Aerial Lift Bridge. Rochester sits 85 miles southeast, boasting the Mayo Clinic, the Olmsted County History Center and the Rochester Art Center.
sections.economy: Business is booming, Minneapolis! The area has several Fortune 500 companies, and the city is a major economic center in the Midwest. Health care, education and professional, scientific and technical services are significant industries here. In addition, several major companies have headquarters here, including Target Corporation, Ameriprise Financial, U.S. Bancorp, General Mills and Xcel Energy. The cost of living in Minneapolis is generally lower than the national average. The median home value in Minneapolis is $361,884, and the average household income is $118,800. Minnesota residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9.03% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Minneapolis, MN? Minneapolis is home to several world-class hospitals that offer high-quality medical care, including Abbott Northwestern Hospital (ranked nationally for its neurology and neurosurgery program) and M Health Fairview University of Minnesota Medical Center (ranked nationally for its adult cancer and pediatric nephrology programs).
sections.thingsToDo: Minneapolis has a mixture of cultures, fun and experiences, making it a great place to live. The city’s vibrant arts scene features renowned institutions like the Walker Art Center and the Minneapolis Institute of Art, alongside a thriving theater community anchored by the Guthrie Theater. For family-friendly fun, there’s the Minnesota Children’s Museum, which provides interactive exhibits, and outdoor spaces like the Walker Art Center’s Open Field, which offers room to play and explore. Beyond its cultural and family-oriented attractions, the city boasts unique experiences like the Mill City Farmers Market, offering local produce and artisan goods, and the Lyndale Avenue area, known for its independent boutiques. The city also hosts various events and festivals, and its parks, such as Pullen Park and James Madison Park, are popular community gathering spaces. What is something you can do for free in Minneapolis, MN? Stroll through Minneapolis Sculpture Garden, which has over 40 sculptures, including the photogenic Spoonbridge and Cherry. What are some things to do with kids in Minneapolis, MN? Take a trip to the Como Zoo & Conservatory, where you’ll spot an Arctic fox, a Galapagos tortoise and the zoo regulars — lions, tigers and bears! Where do locals shop in Minneapolis, MN? The city is home to several designated shopping districts, and because there is no tax on clothing or shoes in Minnesota, you can take advantage of them all! The North Loop, Downtown and Uptown are among the most popular shopping districts and offer a mix of local shops and national brands. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Minneapolis, MN? Catch a matinee performance of a Broadway musical at Hennepin Theatre Trust.
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities are a way of life here. The city sits on the banks of the Mississippi River, with a mix of flat and hilly terrain, perfect for biking, hiking and water sports. It’s known as the “City of Lakes,” and 98% of residents live within a 10-minute walk to a park. With nearly two dozen lakes and plentiful parks, like Chain of Lakes Regional Park and Minnehaha Park, there are endless opportunities for year-round recreation. In the summer, boating, kayaking, swimming and biking are popular pursuits, and in the winter, the city transforms into a playground for ice skating, cross-country skiing and hockey. Kayak the Mississippi and get great views from the Stone Arch Bridge. The city’s commitment to green spaces is evident in its many parks, such as Lake Nokomis and Theodore Wirth Park, which offer playgrounds, hiking trails and recreational facilities.
sections.restaurants: Mealtime is a breeze when you have as many options as people do in Minneapolis. The city boasts a vibrant and eclectic food scene, blending traditional Midwestern flavors with a rich tapestry of international cuisines and innovative culinary experiences. The city’s commitment to farm-to-table dining is evident in restaurants that emphasize locally sourced, seasonal ingredients, while diverse neighborhoods contribute to a global culinary landscape, offering everything from Ethiopian and Korean to Vietnamese cuisine. Food halls like Midtown Global Market showcase this variety, providing a hub for diverse food vendors. Beyond restaurants, Minneapolis has a thriving craft beer scene, with breweries offering locally brewed beers that complement the city’s diverse food offerings. Iconic local dishes, like the Jucy Lucy and walleye, are must-tries, and the city’s cafes and bakeries provide delightful pastries and brunch options. The ever-evolving food scene also includes trendy pop-ups, food trucks and farmers markets, ensuring there’s always something new and exciting to discover. What is one of the best places to eat in Minneapolis, MN? Chef/Owner Gavin Kaysen is a James Beard award-winning chef from Minneapolis who opened Spoon and Stable, a popular spot in the heart of the North Loop Neighborhood inside an old horse stable. The restaurant focuses on seasonality and offers a rotating menu. After dinner, head to Edward’s Dessert Kitchen for sweet treats like pies, cheesecakes and tarts. What is the best coffee shop in Minneapolis, MN? Dogwood Coffee Co. is a local staple, with a couple of locations around the MPLS area. Where is the best happy hour in Minneapolis, MN? Centro's happy hour is from 3 to 6 p.m. Sunday to Friday and offers several wine and cocktail specials and snacks at a discount. Plus, you can even sample oysters at $2 a piece.
sections.education: Minneapolis Public Schools is one of the state’s most diverse districts; nearly 30,000 students are enrolled. The district’s 17:1 student-to-teacher ratio allows for more individualized learning, and though performance varies from school to school, overall, it’s quality education. In terms of private schools, the city is home to several religious, independent and college prep programs. Minnehaha Academy, Breck School and DeLaSalle High School are three of the best private schools in the state.
