faqs.a: When you can’t decide between a happy hour or a caffeinated pick-me-up, head to The Times Bar and Coffee Shop, which serves classic cocktails, craft coffee and boozy caffeinated drinks. Battery Park Book Exchange and Champagne Bar is the perfect union of books and bubbly. Guests can look through a huge selection of used books, enjoy live music and sip champagne all the while.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Asheville, NC?
faqs.a: Rye Knot is a brewery and distillery that makes for a perfect post-work gathering space. You can even take home a cut of meat from the curing cabinet and make a tasty steak at home.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Asheville, NC?
faqs.a: There are several colleges and universities located here, but the University of North Carolina – Asheville is the most notable.

NC-cary.description: Between the Western Carolina mountains and East Coast beaches is Cary, a cozy community with a high quality of life, thanks to a thriving economy, top-ranked schools, picturesque neighborhoods and a number of outdoor activities.

sections.economy: --> --> --> Weather in Cary, NC Average Temperatures 71 high / 49 low Average Annual Rainfall 46 in Average Annual Snowfall 6 in

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Cary?
faqs.a: Cary residents enjoy mild winters and hot, humid summers. Winter temperatures average highs of 58°F, and the coldest month of the year is January, with an average low of 33°F and high of 51°F. Summers average daily temperatures above 81°F, with July being the warmest month.

faqs.q: Does Cary get a lot of snow?
faqs.a: The average snowfall in Cary is minimal at just 6 inches annually.

faqs.q: Does Cary get a lot of rain?
faqs.a: The average annual rainfall in Cary is 46 inches. Typically, August is the wettest month, and April is the driest.

faqs.q: How many sunny days does Cary get?
faqs.a: Cary averages 216 sunny days a year.

faqs.q: What are some things to do outside in Cary?
faqs.a: Outdoor enthusiasts have their pick of activities. Hike the Company Mill Trail through wooded Umstead State Park, enjoy a day fishing on the lake at Fred G. Bond Metro Park or take in a show at the outdoor Koka Booth Amphitheatre. Kayaking, mountain biking and ropes courses are also popular.

faqs.q: What is something free to do for fun in Cary?
faqs.a: Want to enjoy a night on the water? Take in Band, Bites and Boats at Bond Park's boathouse. This free concert series also offers local food vendors and the option to enjoy the music while out on the lake.

faqs.q: What are some fun things to do with kids in Cary?
faqs.a: A unique experience that families love to enjoy in the winter is the annual North Carolina Chinese Lantern Festival. Walk along this half-mile loop filled with huge LED lantern displays created in China. There are also Chinese acrobat performances that will amaze the kids!

faqs.q: What is the best open secret about Cary?
faqs.a: The baked goods at La Farm Bakery are melt-in-your-mouth good. The bakery’s authentic goods give residents the feeling they are in France.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Cary?
faqs.a: The lucky locals in Cary have many choices when it comes to shopping. Many young families and creatives enjoy the historic charm of Downtown Cary with its myriad boutique stores and local businesses, while the Cary Farmers Market is a must for anyone shopping for local goods and produce.

faqs.q: What is the best restaurant in Cary?
faqs.a: Herons at The Umstead Hotel and Spa in Cary is hands-down one of the best dinner destinations in the city. A serene atmosphere is paired with upscale dining, and everything on this contemporary American menu is guaranteed to be a home run. Herons has a reputation for featuring locally sourced ingredients, making it especially appealing to anyone keen on indulging in localism.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Cary?
faqs.a: Tucked within Symphony Lake Park, Koka Booth Amphitheatre is an outdoor venue that hosts concerts, cultural performances, festivals and more. It's the perfect place to take a date for a night of music under the stars, but it's equally fun to go with kids or friends.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Cary?
faqs.a: There are many dog-friendly bars, restaurants and shops here, including BottleDog Bites & Brews, a hybrid taproom and bottle shop specializing in "BottleDogs," aka hot dogs wrapped in bacon! Dogs love hanging out on the patio here.

faqs.q: What are the three largest industries in Cary?
faqs.a: Key industries in Cary include technology, health care and education. Because of Cary's proximity to the Research Triangle, several technology companies have established themselves in the city.

faqs.q: What is the sales tax rate in Cary?
faqs.a: In 2024, the sales tax rate in Cary is 7.25%.

faqs.q: Does North Carolina have a state income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, North Carolina has a state income tax.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Cary?
faqs.a: WakeMed Cary Hospital is the largest health care provider in Cary and is an acute care hospital offering services such as emergency care, heart and vascular care, and orthopedics.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Cary?
faqs.a: Wake Technical Community College is in Cary.

sections.economy: Checkr says Charlotte ranks in the top eight of 2025's Best US Cities for Job Opportunities and Earning Potential. The city is continuously ranked among the fastest-growing metro areas, it’s the nation’s second-largest banking hub after New York City home to Bank of America, Wells Fargo’s East Coast headquarters, and Truist Financial. Fortune 500s cluster across the skyline, from Honeywell’s sleek Uptown headquarters to Duke Energy, Nucor, and Lowe’s, each defining sector-leading innovation while fueling civic pride. Add in logistics leaders, aerospace manufacturers, and data companies, and you have an economy that’s not only strong but surprisingly diverse. Part of Charlotte’s draw is the seamless integration of career and personal life. Major industries continue to grow including finance, health care, renewable energy, advanced manufacturing, and yet, people still have time to explore their passions. Whether it’s joining a cycling group through the city’s expansive greenways or attending local gallery nights, the workday gives way naturally to a fulfilling after-hours life. Local employers increasingly reflect this ethos of inclusion and balance. Newer developments emphasize green buildings, public art, co-working plazas, and walkable access to dining, not merely to attract talent, but to keep it happy. That focus on lifestyle makes Charlotte less transactional and more transformational. It’s a city where careers and creativity truly coexist.
sections.thingsToDo: Charlotte welcomes you with kinetic energy and comfort, inviting you to explore at your own pace. Whether you're kayaking on Lake Norman, biking the shaded trails of Anne Springs Close Greenway, or catching a rooftop concert in Uptown, there’s truly no wrong way to experience the city’s vibrant blend of urban convenience and outdoor adventure. Start your day along Freedom Park’s seven-acre lake, a favorite for morning jogs, playground time, or just soaking up sunshine (Freedom Park). On weekends, you might explore the historic artsy vibe of NoDa, home to live music venues and local galleries (NoDa Neighborhood).​ If food is your passion, Charlotte is packed with standout local restaurants. Dig into Italian-Japanese fusion at Yunta in South End or enjoy a tasting menu at L’Ostrica, a top spot for innovative global cuisine. For a lighter bite and coffee, South End’s Not Just Coffee is a cozy spot to meet friends or get work done.​ Social scenes revolve around local breweries, like the expansive beer gardens at The Olde Mecklenburg Brewery and community events at Town Brewing Co., where music, markets, and food trucks keep the calendar full year-round. Don’t miss annual events like Charlotte Craft Beer Week and block parties throughout LoSo and Plaza Midwood.​ Beyond the city’s hustle, enjoy dozens of miles of greenways — Little Sugar Creek Greenway is a local favorite for walking or cycling through some of Charlotte’s most beautiful neighborhoods.​ Whether your Charlotte day takes you from a morning trail run to new restaurants, from laid-back breweries to live outdoor concerts, the city is designed so that you can move, connect, and thrive wherever you land. Every neighborhood has its own story, and every resident discovers a new favorite place before long. Read The Ultimate Things To Do in Charlotte Bucket List
sections.education: Charlotte offers a wealth of educational options for learners of all ages, making it a vibrant destination for families, students, and lifelong learners. Public & Private K-12 Schools Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools (CMS) is one of the largest public school districts in the Southeast, offering strong neighborhood schools, magnet programs, and specialized academies in STEM, performing arts, and more. Top-rated public elementary schools include Hawk Ridge, Polo Ridge, and Elon Park, all recognized for their academic excellence and innovative learning environments.​ If you’re seeking private schools, top options include Charlotte Country Day School, Charlotte Latin School, Charlotte Catholic High School, Charlotte Christian School, and Providence Day School—all known for their strong academics, small class sizes, and extracurricular depth.​ Higher Education Charlotte is home to 11 four-year nonprofit colleges and universities, totaling nearly 50,000 students. The largest is the University of North Carolina at Charlotte (UNC Charlotte), a top-tier urban research institution with over 170 majors, an R1 research designation, and robust innovation partnerships across the city. Davidson College, just north of Charlotte, is a nationally ranked liberal arts college known for interdisciplinary learning and leadership. Queens University of Charlotte, located in Myers Park, offers a personalized curriculum and numerous study-abroad programs, while Johnson C. Smith University serves as one of the state’s top Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs).​ Career Training & Adult Education Charlotte’s landscape also includes outstanding job training and career pathway programs. For example, Year Up United provides job skills, internships, and placement services for young adults, and Junior Achievement offers students financial literacy and career exploration experiences. Professional Development Charlotte hosts innovative teaching initiatives and conferences each year, such as the Charlotte AI Summit for Smarter Learning at UNC Charlotte and the Families Learning Conference, which focus on cutting-edge education technologies and community learning practices.​ Whether you're planning for grade school, looking ahead to college, or considering a career change, Charlotte delivers high-quality, diverse educational resources across public, private, and specialty learning environments.

faqs.q: What is Charlotte, North Carolina best known for?
faqs.a: Charlotte, NC is best known as a major banking and financial services hub, often called the “Queen City.” It’s also recognized for its fast-growing population, vibrant arts scene, diverse neighborhoods, and dynamic mix of Southern charm and urban energy.​

faqs.q: Is Charlotte a good place to move for jobs?
faqs.a: Yes. Charlotte consistently ranks among the top U.S. cities for job seekers, especially in banking, healthcare, tech, and advanced manufacturing. Major employers here include Bank of America, Atrium Health, Lowe’s, Truist, and numerous innovative start-ups.​​

faqs.q: What is the cost of living like in Charlotte?
faqs.a: Charlotte’s cost of living is generally lower than most large metro areas, especially compared to cities like New York or San Francisco. Residents enjoy affordable housing, reasonable utilities, and no city income tax, making it attractive for families and young professionals.​​

faqs.q: What are the best neighborhoods in Charlotte?
faqs.a: Popular neighborhoods include South End (trendy, artsy, transit-friendly), University City (proximity to UNC Charlotte), Plaza Midwood (eclectic, vintage shops, and nightlife), Ballantyne (suburban communities), and Myers Park (tree-lined streets, historic homes).​​

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Charlotte?
faqs.a: Charlotte enjoys four distinct seasons with mild winters and warm summers. Spring and fall bring comfortable temperatures and beautiful foliage, while summer is perfect for lake and pool days.

faqs.q: Are there good schools and universities in Charlotte?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Charlotte is home to strong public and private K-12 schools, and higher education options like UNC Charlotte, Queens University of Charlotte, Johnson C. Smith University, and nearby Davidson College.​

faqs.q: What cultural attractions does Charlotte offer?
faqs.a: Beyond sports and outdoor recreation, explore the Mint Museum, Discovery Place Science, Bechtler Museum of Modern Art, Harvey B. Gantt Center, live theater at Blumenthal Performing Arts Center, and numerous annual cultural festivals.​

faqs.q: How far is Charlotte from the beach or mountains?
faqs.a: You can reach the Blue Ridge Mountains in two hours’ drive and the Atlantic coast (such as Myrtle Beach, SC or Wilmington, NC) in about three and a half hours, making it easy to enjoy weekend getaways.

faqs.q: Is Charlotte family-friendly?
faqs.a: Yes. Charlotte features many parks, family attractions, highly rated schools, and a strong sense of community. Festivals, children’s museums, and neighborhood events make it a great place to raise a family.​

NC-durham.description: Durham is a city where innovation and creativity thrive. With historic neighborhoods, natural beauty, nationally recognized universities, an outstanding arts scene and delicious dining options, Durham fosters a community with diverse interests.

sections.location: Durham is a dynamic city in the heart of the Research Triangle, right along Interstate 40 and Interstate 85. The nearest airport, Raleigh-Durham International Airport, is about 15 miles south, a convenient spot for flights. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Raleigh is just 25 miles southeast, where you can explore the North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences or catch a game at PNC Arena. Chapel Hill sits 10 miles west, known for the University of North Carolina and the Morehead Planetarium. Greensboro is about 55 miles northwest, home to the International Civil Rights Center & Museum and the Greensboro Science Center.
sections.economy: Located in the heart of North Carolina’s Research Triangle, Durham is home to several Fortune 500 companies. The city’s mix of corporations and energetic startups lure people into the job market. The economy is deeply rooted in technology, health care and education. Duke Energy, one of the largest energy companies in the country, is headquartered here and provides electric and gas services to millions of customers across six states. The cost of living in Durham is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Durham is $377,377, and the average household income is $116,388. North Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Durham, NC? Duke University Health System is one of the top health care providers in the country and offers a wide range of specialties and services. The health system includes several hospitals and outpatient centers in the area and is consistently ranked among the top hospitals in the country by U.S. News & World Report.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents can enjoy a dynamic lifestyle in this North Carolina city. The city’s cultural scene is anchored by DPAC, which hosts Broadway shows, concerts and comedy shows, and the Carolina Theatre, a historic venue for films and performances. Immerse yourself in the local arts at the Durham Arts Council, the Durham Civil Rights Mural or the Nasher Museum of Art, which has contemporary and classical exhibits. The Duke Homestead State Historic Site and the Hayti Heritage Center are local favorites. The Museum of Life and Science provides interactive experiences, and the Carolina Theatre is a gorgeous historic venue for films. The city is community-oriented thanks to the number of events and recreational opportunities. The Durham Bulls Athletic Park offers the excitement of minor league baseball, and the Durham Farmers’ Market provides a taste of local produce and goods. Take curious kiddos to the Duke Lemur Center or the Scrap Exchange, a creative reuse arts center that collects materials from over 250 local industries for crafting. The revitalized American Tobacco Campus and Historic Brightleaf Square offer unique shopping and dining experiences, while the 9th Street District is home to several independent shops and bookstores. What is something you can do for free in Durham, NC? From utility boxes to walls, artists have transformed Durham into a color-saturated outdoor museum with murals that celebrate Black stories and beautify the area. Take a self-guided tour of the murals with the help of an inspiration guide. A good starting place is the Visitor Info Center, where you can pose with a 2,500-pound bronze bull. What are some things to do with kids in Durham, NC? The Duke Lemur Center is home to more than 200 animals across 14 species. The center also studies, cares for and protects lemurs, the most endangered mammals on the planet. Where do locals shop in Durham, NC? Bookstore meets bar at Letters Bookshop. Literary lovers can also subscribe to the shop’s book subscription service, which comes with one new and used book each month. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Durham, NC? There are several venues for live music and performing arts here, but two of the most popular are the Durham Performing Arts Center (DPAC) — the state’s largest performing arts center — and Motorco Music Hall, a quirky local haunt located in a former car dealership. 8 Reasons to Move to Durham, NCSouthern Pride: A Guide to Durham, NC’s LGBTQ Scene20 Things You Must Do Your First Year Living in North Carolina
sections.outdoors: North Carolina is known for its outdoor adventures, and Durham is an outdoor enthusiast’s dream. The city has an extensive network of parks and trails, including the American Tobacco Trail, a 22-mile rails-to-trails project perfect for walking and biking, and the Eno River State Park, which has nearly 30 miles of hiking trails. The Sarah P. Duke Gardens are a tranquil escape with 50-plus acres of preserved gardens and 5 miles of accessible pathways. The Duke Forest is also an excellent spot for hiking and mountain biking. For those looking to get their feet wet, Lake Michie and Falls Lake State Recreation Area offer fishing, boating, swimming and camping. Durham Central Park is a community hub that hosts outdoor concerts and events. Residents can also enjoy the Al Buehler Trail, a 3-mile loop around the Washington Duke Inn & Golf Club, offering a scenic route for walking and jogging.
sections.restaurants: The city has blossomed into a foodie haven in recent years. The city’s revitalized districts, like the American Tobacco Historic District, now house a mix of restaurants and bars, earning Durham the title of “South’s Tastiest Town.” There is an emphasis on community and local sourcing around town, and spots like Bull City Burger and Brewery and Fullsteam Brewery champion “farm to tank” and “Southern Beer Economy” philosophies, respectively. The city has a diverse menu of dining experiences, from French-inspired cuisine at Rue Cler to the eclectic offerings at Durham Food Hall. The Bullpen social district is a favorite spot for food and beverage offerings, too. Durham’s craft beer scene is equally impressive, with spots like Ponysaurus Brewing Company contributing to its lively atmosphere. What is one of the best places to eat in Durham, NC? Saltbox Seafood Joint, helmed by James Beard-nominated chef Ricky Moore, is a must-try. Locals love the “Hush-Honeys” — fried, honey-glazed cornmeal fritters. What is the best coffee shop in Durham, NC? Liturgy Beverage Company is a staple, and the owners know what they are doing since they had their meet-cute in a coffee bar! Where is the best happy hour in Durham, NC? More than 300 whiskeys from around the world join local beers on the menu at Dram and Draught, which also serves tasty mocktails. sections.education: This city is a highly educated place to live. The Durham Public Schools District has undergone redistricting in recent years, and performance varies from school to school. However, several top-notch public, private, magnet and charter schools are in the area. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: Duke University Health System is one of the top health care providers in the country and offers a wide range of specialties and services. The health system includes several hospitals and outpatient centers in the area and is consistently ranked among the top hospitals in the country by U.S. News & World Report.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: From utility boxes to walls, artists have transformed Durham into a color-saturated outdoor museum with murals that celebrate Black stories and beautify the area. Take a self-guided tour of the murals with the help of an inspiration guide. A good starting place is the Visitor Info Center, where you can pose with a 2,500-pound bronze bull.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: The Duke Lemur Center is home to more than 200 animals across 14 species. The center also studies, cares for and protects lemurs, the most endangered mammals on the planet.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: Bookstore meets bar at Letters Bookshop. Literary lovers can also subscribe to the shop’s book subscription service, which comes with one new and used book each month.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: There are several venues for live music and performing arts here, but two of the most popular are the Durham Performing Arts Center (DPAC) — the state’s largest performing arts center — and Motorco Music Hall, a quirky local haunt located in a former car dealership.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: Saltbox Seafood Joint, helmed by James Beard-nominated chef Ricky Moore, is a must-try. Locals love the “Hush-Honeys” — fried, honey-glazed cornmeal fritters.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: Liturgy Beverage Company is a staple, and the owners know what they are doing since they had their meet-cute in a coffee bar!

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: More than 300 whiskeys from around the world join local beers on the menu at Dram and Draught, which also serves tasty mocktails.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Durham, NC?
faqs.a: Several major colleges and universities are located here, including Duke University, Durham Technical Community College and North Carolina Central University.

NC-greensboro.description: Greensboro offers a perfect mix of Southern charm and modern amenities. A thriving arts scene, excellent schools and beautiful parks are paired with a diverse dining scene and vibrant community that make Greensboro a welcoming place; it’s a great place for families and young professionals.

sections.location: Greensboro is a vibrant city in central North Carolina, about 90 miles west of Raleigh, accessible via Interstate 40 and Interstate 85. The nearest airport, Piedmont Triad International Airport, is just 9 miles west, offering convenient flights. When you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Raleigh is 90 miles east; explore the North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences, visit the State Farmers Market or catch a show at the Duke Energy Center. Charlotte sits 90 miles southwest, home of the NASCAR Hall of Fame, the Mint Museum and the Bank of America Stadium. Winston-Salem is just 30 miles west; tour the Reynolda House Museum of American Art, explore Old Salem or hike at Pilot Mountain State Park.
sections.economy: Greensboro is a hub for health care and medical services, education and research, and manufacturing. Companies like Honda Aircraft Company and Volvo Group Trucks Operations also have a strong presence in town. The technology sector continues to evolve, with startups and tech firms contributing to the city’s innovative network. And if you’re thinking of moving back home to Greensboro and need help figuring things out, Boomerang Greensboro has everything you need to know. The cost of living in Greensboro is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Greensboro is $236,451, and the average household income is $93,798. North Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.75% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Greensboro, NC? Residents are in good health thanks to providers like Moses H. Cone Memorial Health, a high-performing hospital in several procedures and conditions.
sections.thingsToDo: Greensboro mixes history with arts and culture. History buffs can visit the Civil Rights Center & Museum, commemorating the Greensboro sit-in, and the Greensboro History Museum. The arts scene has places like the Carolina Theatre and the Weatherspoon Art Museum, home to 20th-century American art. The Greensboro Science Center offers an aquarium, a zoo and interactive exhibits for family-friendly entertainment, while the Miriam P. Brenner Children’s Museum provides hands-on learning experiences. Beyond the usual museums, the city has unique attractions like Elsewhere Museum, an artist residency in a former thrift shop, and independent bookstores like Scuppernong Books, which hosts literary events. There’s shopping at Four Seasons Town Centre, basketball games with the Swarm and public art along the greenway. Outdoor enthusiasts can find golf courses at Bryan Park Complex and explore the Piedmont Triad Farmers Market. What is something you can do for free in Greensboro, NC? Head to Lake Brandt Marina for a day of kayaking or fishing. What are some things to do in Greensboro, NC, with kids? The Greensboro Science Center will fascinate kids. Watching sharks in the shark tanks, seeing baby penguins, swinging through treetops — who could ask for more? Where do locals shop in Greensboro, NC? Downtown Greensboro is a vibrant shopping district with 50 locally-owned shops, galleries and antique stores. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Greensboro, NC? From Broadway to opera to concerts, the Steven Tanger Center for the Performing Arts is Greensboro’s prime spot to see a show.
sections.outdoors: Get out and about in Greensboro. The city features numerous parks, including Center City Park and LeBauer Park, which provide green spaces for relaxation and community events. The Downtown Greenway is a 4-mile trail for walking, biking and running with art and history along the way. For those seeking natural beauty, Greensboro’s four public gardens — Greensboro Arboretum, Tanger Family Bicentennial Garden, Gateway Gardens and Bog Garden at Benjamin Park — offer lush landscapes and diverse flora. Get out on the water by kayaking and canoeing on lakes like Lake Higgins and Lake Jeanette, while golfers can tee off at courses like Greensboro National Golf Club. Dog owners can appreciate the Triad Dog Park and LeBauer’s off-leash area. Keeley Park has mountain bike trails and a playground, and the Ice House offers skating.
sections.restaurants: The local food scene has been growing and changing in recent years. Residents can enjoy everything from casual spots like Hops Burger Bar and The Pho Shop to fancier places like Green Valley Grill and Chef and the Farmer, which use fresh, local ingredients. Places like The Old Town Draught House have Southern food and craft beers, while Chez Genèse offers a unique dining experience that helps the community. The brewery scene is significant, with places like Pig Pounder and Joymongers offering different beers and vibes. You can also find food from around the world, like Ethiopian cuisine at Lalibela and global dishes at Table 16. Basically, there’s something for everyone, whether you want comfort food, something new or a good local beer. What is one of the best places to eat in Greensboro, NC? The 1618 Midtown serves modern cuisine with hand-curated wines in a fun but relaxed atmosphere. What is the best coffee shop in Greensboro, NC? Borough Coffee is community-minded and pays employees a livable wage. They have a standalone location, but what sets Borough apart is the mobile trike used to pedal to neighborhoods needing a great coffee shop. Where is the happy hour in Greensboro, NC? One Thirteen Brewhouse and Rooftop Bar has a custom-built bar and a large stage for live music on the first floor, a bourbon and whiskey bar on the second floor, and open-air rooftop bars with entertainment and great city views, making it the ideal spot to sit back and relax after a long day at work.
sections.education: Local families are served by Guilford County Schools, which is the third-largest district in North Carolina with more than 66,000 students. There are several highly ranked high schools within the district, including The Early College at Guilford and STEM Early College at N.C. A&T, ranked No. 1 and No. 2 in the state, respectively. The city also has a variety of private schools, including both college preparatory and religiously affiliated options. NC-greenville.description: A dynamic and welcoming city, Greenville is home to East Carolina University and provides residents with an affordable cost of living, vibrant arts and music scenes and plenty of parks and green spaces, a recipe for creating the perfect college town.

sections.location: Greenville is a growing city in eastern North Carolina, about 85 miles east of Raleigh, accessible via U.S. Route 264. Home to East Carolina University, students and residents alike can travel easily — the nearest airport, Pitt-Greenville Airport, is nearby, but Raleigh-Durham International Airport (90 miles west) offers more flights. Nearby major cities include: Raleigh, NC, is 85 miles west; enjoy exploring the North Carolina Museum of Art and State Farmers Market or catch a show at Duke Energy Center. Wilmington, NC, sits 120 miles south, a doable trip to visit the historic downtown, Battleship North Carolina and Wrightsville Beach. Norfolk, VA, is about 130 miles northeast; spend the day exploring the Chrysler Museum of Art, Virginia Zoo and Norfolk Botanical Garden.
sections.economy: Greenville is the economic hub of Eastern North Carolina. It has a robust health care industry and is home to East Carolina University, which brings a dynamic energy to the city and fosters job opportunities in academia. The university also collaborates with local schools, offering resources and expertise that benefit students and teachers. The city is also an emerging tech hub for companies like Mr. Beast and Grover Gaming. Major employers include Vidant Medical Center, East Carolina University, Pitt County Schools and the City of Greenville. The cost of living in Greenville is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Greenville is $236,303, and the average household income is $81,489. North Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Greenville, NC? The health care system is buoyed by ECU Health Medical Center, which received a high-performance rating in 15 different procedures and conditions. Why I Built My Business in Greenville-Pitt CountyReceive Top-Notch Health Care in Greenville and Pitt CountyEastern North Carolina Draws Entrepreneurs and Companies
sections.thingsToDo: At the Jaycee Park Center for Arts & Crafts, residents of all ages can sign up for classes and learn how to paint, draw, sew, crochet, make pottery and more. The center also holds special events like “Paint in the Park” at Town Common. Or, learn a new skill like glass blowing through classes offered by East Carolina’s GlasStation, which is 15 west of the main campus in Farmville. Check out works by featured artists at City Art Gallery, a fine arts gallery. The spirit of Greenville-Pitt County comes alive through its live music and collection of vibrant festivals and events. Emerge Gallery & Art Center hosts the African American Concert Series, a free, standing-room-only event on the second Friday of each month. PirateFest, a two-day extravaganza on the Tar River, transforms the downtown area into a pirate’s paradise. Play pickleball or join the fitness classes at the Greenville Aquatics and Fitness Center, or catch up with friends (or meet some fellow locals) at a spot like Uptown Brewing Company. The craft brewery hosts “vinyl nights,” when you can bring your favorite records to the brewery and they’ll spin it on the house turntable. Stock up on farm-fresh produce and artisanal goods — all grown or made within 50 miles of Greenville — at the Dickinson Avenue Farmers & Makers Market. What is something you can do for free in Greenville, NC? Check out the Greenville Metal Zoo, which is an outdoor sculpture park featuring art by local artist Jonathan Bowling. His artistic animals can be found throughout the town, too. What are some things to do in Greenville, NC, with kids? Check activities off from your fall bucket list at Briley’s Farm Market, where families can pick out pumpkins, go on hayrides, wander through corn mazes and more. For adventurers, Black Jack Motocross features a trail just for kids. Little ones can learn to ice skate at the indoor rink at Carolina Ice Zone. Play some putt-putt Captain Jack’s Mini Golf course for a fun family night out. Pro tip: You get free popcorn with each game. Where do locals shop in Greenville, NC? When it comes to beautifying your outdoor space, Carolina Seasons Nursery, which opened in 1980, is a top spot for plants and flowers, and professional gardeners can help you develop a green thumb. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Greenville, NC? Enjoy theater performances at the Magnolia Arts Center, which also holds youth theater classes in the summer. 13 Fun Things To Do in Greenville, NCEnjoy Plenty of Great Art in Greenville, NC7 Reasons to Move to Greenville, NC, Right Now
sections.outdoors: On the weekends and after work, Greenville’s Greenways are a great place to unwind. The network includes 9 miles of trails for a scenic walk, run or bike ride, with paths that trace the Tar River, loop around the East Carolina University campus and connect to some of the city’s public parks — there are more than 25 of them! Mild temps in Eastern North Carolina also mean golfers can play year-round at courses like Bradford Creek Public Golf. For an on-water adventure, rent a kayak or stand-up paddleboard from an outfitter like Knee Deep Adventures and paddle along Greenville’s stretch of the Tar River. 10 Outdoor Activities in Greenville, NC, to Explore
sections.restaurants: One of the best ways to get acquainted with the city's culinary scene is along the Pitt County Brew & ’Cue Trail, where you can sample some of the region’s iconic barbecue dishes at spots like B’s Barbecue and wash it all down with a cold pint. Another suggestion? Check out the festival calendar. The Ayden Collard Festival celebrates the leafy green vegetable, and there’s even a collard green eating contest! As far as beloved restaurants in town, locals say the go-to takeout spot for dishes like pork shoyu ramen and mango chicken is Sakura Asian Express, and they rave about the lunch buffet at Cinnamon Bistro, a local Indian restaurant. What is one of the best places to eat in Greenville, NC? Nino’s Cucina Italiana serves delicious Italian dishes made from century-old family recipes. Enjoy homemade pasta dishes like cacio e pepe and shrimp fra diavolo. Save room for the limoncello cake. What is the best coffee shop in Greenville, NC? The Scullery in downtown Greenville serves fresh-roasted coffee and homemade ice cream in a cozy setting. It’s also known for connecting with the surrounding community by holding fundraisers and showcasing works from local artists. Where is the best happy hour in Greenville, NC? Split salsa and a specialty cocktail at Side/Bar. 10 Great Restaurants in Greenville, NC, You Must Try
sections.education: Local families benefit from various educational offerings, including those from the Pitt County Schools district and various private school offerings. Pitt County Schools is the largest district in the city, with more than 24,000 students enrolled and is one of the best districts for athletics in the state. faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Greenville, NC?
faqs.a: East Carolina University is the largest higher-ed institution here. East Carolina University is a major driver of Greenville’s identity. The school brings a lively atmosphere to the city, contributing to its cultural vibrancy and economic growth. A four-year medical doctor degree and six doctoral programs are available at the Brody School of Medicine. The university also collaborates with local schools, offering resources and expertise that benefit students and teachers.

NC-raleigh.description: Southern charm radiates throughout Raleigh, NC. Part of the Research Triangle, residents can take advantage of a thriving economy and excellent quality of life that is anchored by top-notch universities, cool neighborhoods and a fabulous arts scene.

sections.location: This state capital is in central North Carolina, perched along Interstate 40, which ties it to the other cities in the Research Triangle, Durham and Chapel Hill. Raleigh-Durham International Airport is about 15 miles away, serving up flights for all your plans. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Durham is just 25 miles northwest, where you can tour Duke University, stroll Sarah P. Duke Gardens or cheer at Cameron Indoor Stadium. Chapel Hill sits 30 miles west, buzzing with UNC energy, historic Franklin Street and the NC Botanical Garden. Greensboro is about 75 miles west, offering the Science Center, Civil Rights Museum and Bicentennial Garden.
sections.economy: As the state capital, Raleigh is a center for government and politics and a thriving place to live. Of course, there’s a higher concentration of government and related jobs, which broaden employment opportunities. And because it's located in the Research Triangle, there are plenty of technology jobs. Professional and business services, education and health services and technology are the largest industries here. Some of the largest employers include the State of North Carolina, Duke University and Health Services, IBM and Cisco Systems. The cost of living in Raleigh is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Raleigh is $456,019, and the average household income is $131,172. North Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Raleigh, NC? Residents have easy access to major hospitals and health care systems, including the nearby, nationally ranked Duke University Hospital. Within the Raleigh city limits, UNC Rex Hospital and Duke’s Raleigh Hospital are also available to keep residents healthy.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents won’t want for things to do. Art enthusiasts can explore the Contemporary Art Museum (CAM Raleigh), showcasing emerging artists, and the North Carolina Museum of Art, featuring an outdoor park and extensive collections spanning ancient to contemporary works. History and science buffs can explore exhibits at the North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences and the James B. Hunt Library, a technologically advanced architectural marvel. Families can enjoy interactive exhibits at Marbles Kids Museum and outdoor fun at Pullen Park, which features a carousel, a train and pedal boats. Beyond museums and parks, the city has vibrant markets like The Raleigh Market, offering a variety of goods from antiques to handmade crafts and unique shopping experiences at places like Raleigh Denim. The city hosts numerous events, including the First Friday Gallery Walk and outdoor movie screenings at the Raleigh Municipal Rose Garden. With a thriving arts and music scene and an array of local boutiques and breweries, Raleigh provides dynamic experiences for residents. What is something you can do for free in Raleigh, NC? Go for a picnic at the Raleigh Rose Garden, which has 60 beds of roses that are typically in bloom from about Mother’s Day weekend to mid-November. The garden is a popular proposal spot. What are some things to do with kids in Raleigh, NC? Pullen Park is a blast! Beyond the playgrounds are pedal boats, a carousel, an arts center with classes and youth galleries, and an outdoor cafe where visitors can grab a bite to eat. During the fall, take the kids to the North Carolina State Fair, the state’s largest annual event. Where do locals shop in Raleigh, NC? Look like a local and grab the perfect pair of custom jeans at Raleigh Workshop. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Raleigh, NC? In nearby Chapel Hill, become a repeat visitor to Carolina Performing Arts, where you can see theater and dance at Memorial Hall or an immersive performance or installation at CURRENT ArtSpace + Studio. Closer to home, in the heart of downtown Raleigh, is the Red Hat Amphitheater. The amphitheater’s season runs from April to October and hosts a variety of artists. 6 Reasons To Visit Raleigh, NC, the Smithsonian of the SouthSouthern Pride: A Guide to Raleigh’s LGBTQ Scene
sections.outdoors: Get out and about in Raleigh with a range of outdoor activities. The city has an extensive network of greenways, including the 27.5-mile Neuse River Greenway, perfect for walking, biking and kayaking, and the Capital Area Greenway, with more than 100 miles of interconnected trails. Umstead State Park offers plenty to do with over 6,000 acres for those seeking something more adventurous, while the Mountains-to-Sea Trail provides scenic hiking, particularly around Falls Lake. The city also features a handful of unique outdoor experiences, like the Ann and Jim Goodnight Museum Park, which combines art installations with scenic walking and biking paths, and Yates Mill County Park, which offers hiking trails and a historic gristmill.
sections.restaurants: Raleigh has emerged as a culinary destination in recent years, earning accolades for its thriving foodie scene. From Southern comforts to international flavors, the city offers a rich tapestry of dining experiences anchored by award-winning chefs. Food halls like Morgan Street Food Hall and Transfer Co. Food Hall have become popular hubs, offering a variety of cuisines under one roof. With a flourishing craft brewery culture, its beverage scene is equally impressive. The city boasts over 40 breweries, which are celebrated through the Raleigh Beer Trail and the Raleigh Beer Garden, which holds a Guinness World Record for its extensive beer selection. With establishments like Standard Beer & Food offering on-site brews, locally sourced food, and numerous other bars and breweries throughout the city, it caters to casual drinkers and craft beer aficionados. What is one of the best places to eat in Raleigh, NC? Get your friend group together for a night at Morgan Street Food Hall, where you can find everything from sushi burritos to Indian curry dishes and chicken and waffle sandwiches at Iyla’s Southern Kitchen. Enjoy a nightcap at the secret gin and absinthe bar. What is the best coffee shop in Raleigh, NC? Blackbird Books & Coffee is the perfect place to enjoy a new book and a delicious coffee. They also carry a rotation of sweet and savory pastries from Yellow Garden Bake Shop. Where is the best place for happy hour in Raleigh, NC? The Raleigh Beer Garden has more than 350 (!) beers on tap. Parlez-Vous Raleigh? 6 Raleigh-Durham Restaurants That Will Make You Think You’re in France
sections.education: Wake County Public School System serves the city and its surrounding areas, and it's one of the largest and most highly ranked school systems in North Carolina. There are also several private schools in the area, too. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: Residents have easy access to major hospitals and health care systems, including the nearby, nationally ranked Duke University Hospital. Within the Raleigh city limits, UNC Rex Hospital and Duke’s Raleigh Hospital are also available to keep residents healthy.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: Go for a picnic at the Raleigh Rose Garden, which has 60 beds of roses that are typically in bloom from about Mother’s Day weekend to mid-November. The garden is a popular proposal spot.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: Pullen Park is a blast! Beyond the playgrounds are pedal boats, a carousel, an arts center with classes and youth galleries, and an outdoor cafe where visitors can grab a bite to eat. During the fall, take the kids to the North Carolina State Fair, the state’s largest annual event.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: Look like a local and grab the perfect pair of custom jeans at Raleigh Workshop.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: In nearby Chapel Hill, become a repeat visitor to Carolina Performing Arts, where you can see theater and dance at Memorial Hall or an immersive performance or installation at CURRENT ArtSpace + Studio. Closer to home, in the heart of downtown Raleigh, is the Red Hat Amphitheater. The amphitheater’s season runs from April to October and hosts a variety of artists.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: Get your friend group together for a night at Morgan Street Food Hall, where you can find everything from sushi burritos to Indian curry dishes and chicken and waffle sandwiches at Iyla’s Southern Kitchen. Enjoy a nightcap at the secret gin and absinthe bar.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: Blackbird Books & Coffee is the perfect place to enjoy a new book and a delicious coffee. They also carry a rotation of sweet and savory pastries from Yellow Garden Bake Shop.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for happy hour in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: The Raleigh Beer Garden has more than 350 (!) beers on tap.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Raleigh, NC?
faqs.a: North Carolina State University, Wake Technical Community College and several smaller institutions are located here.

NC-wilmington.description: Coastal living doesn't have to come at a cost in Wilmington, NC. Picturesque beaches, a laid-back lifestyle, and a flair for film all add to why this Southern city is a great place to live. Wilmington residents enjoy a high quality of life and are highly educated.

sections.location: Wilmington is a charming coastal city in southeastern North Carolina, about 130 miles south of Raleigh, accessible via U.S. Route 17. Known for its historic downtown and film industry ties, it blends riverfront charm and beachy vibes. Wilmington International Airport is just north of the city, offering flights to Atlanta and Charlotte. Ready to roam? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Raleigh is roughly 130 miles northwest, where you can enjoy exploring the North Carolina Museum of Art and State Farmers Market or catch a show at Duke Energy Center. Myrtle Beach, SC, sits 75 miles south, home to golf courses, the SkyWheel and Broadway at the Beach. Charleston, SC, is just 175 miles south, where you can stroll the Battery, visit Fort Sumter or explore the French Quarter.
sections.economy: The city has a diverse economy, with several major industries contributing to the area’s growth and development. These industries include tourism and hospitality, health care and social assistance, and film and television production. The city is lovingly called “Wilmywood,” as it has been the filming location for numerous movies, TV shows and commercials. The cost of living in Wilmington is lower than the national average. The median home value in Wilmington is $365,418, and the average household income is $102,848. North Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Wilmington, NC? Novant Health New Hanover Regional Medical Center is one of the best hospitals here and one of the highest-ranked hospitals in North Carolina. Additionally, Novant Health’s Betty H. Cameron Women’s & Children’s Hospital is beloved by residents.
sections.thingsToDo: Blending history, art and family fun with the coastal coolness that comes with being a beach town, the city's wonders are endless. History buffs will love visiting spots like Bellamy Mansion and Latimer House. Dive into the local lore by visiting the Cape Fear Museum of History and Science or the Wilmington Railroad Museum. Art thrives in Wilmington and is showcased at the Cameron Art Museum and Thalian Hall, as well as through local art galleries and events like the Azalea Festival. For family-friendly fun, the Children’s Museum of Wilmington and attractions like the Battleship North Carolina provide engaging adventures. Downtown’s Riverwalk and boutiques offer fun places for shopping and dining, and year-round festivals keep the city buzzing. What is something you can do for free in Wilmington, NC? How does a long walk along the river sound? Take a stroll through the city’s historic River District, home to 2 miles of scenic walkways, breathtaking views of the Cape Fear River from the Riverwalk, and a variety of local restaurants, stores and attractions, including the USS North Carolina Battleship. What are some things to do in Wilmington, NC, with kids? At the Wilmington Railroad Museum, kids can climb into a caboose, see model trains and learn about railroad history. Where do locals shop in Wilmington, NC? Shop goods from more than 50 local artisans at City Gypsy Boutique, which also hosts art classes and unicorn-themed birthday parties. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Wilmington, NC? The Museum School at Cameron Art Museum offers ongoing education to visitors of all ages and skill levels and celebrates over 50 years of art education through exhibits and community programs.
sections.outdoors: The port city is a gateway to area beaches, boasting a huge historic district, a mile-long riverwalk and plentiful opportunities to get outside, including the area’s more than 50 golf courses and nearby beaches. The Riverwalk is a perfect spot for scenic strolls, and Airlie Gardens, home to a 500-year-old oak tree and a butterfly house, showcases the area’s natural beauty. Beaches and golf courses, including the revitalized Wilmington Municipal Golf Course, designed by Donald Ross, and the scenic Beau Rivage Golf & Resort, define Wilmington’s landscape. Feeling active? Greenfield Lake is your playground for kayaks and trails, while the Cross-City Trail stretches 15 miles of pure adventure. Parks like Long Leaf and Halyburton are packed with playgrounds and trails, perfect for kids. Catch a Wilmington Sharks baseball game or visit Jungle Rapids Family Fun Park, where outdoor adventures and coastal vibes collide.
sections.restaurants: Take a bite out of this community's food scene. It’s a flavor fiesta where the ocean’s bounty meets Southern charm. Savor expertly prepared seafood at Catch and Cape Fear Seafood Company, then indulge in a waterfront meal at The Pilot House. But the adventure doesn’t stop there. Indochine’s lush garden setting offers an escape, and RX Restaurant & Bar elevates Southern comfort to new heights. Savorez ignites palates with Latin American flair. The local craft beer scene bubbles with local flavor, thanks to breweries like Wilmington Brewing Company and Front Street Brewery. More than 40 local eateries and pubs add to the city’s food and drink scene. Unique experiences, like Bottega Art + Wine’s art-infused wine bar and the Port City Brew Bus beer tours, make Wilmington a captivating destination for those seeking a culinary adventure. What is one of the best places to eat in Wilmington, NC? The outdoor space at Indochine – A Far East Café practically doubles as a botanical garden. The Thai and Vietnamese restaurant has a cute gift shop in a bungalow where you can shop while you wait for a table. Be sure to order a dumpling sampler for the group. What is the best coffee shop in Wilmington, NC? Hidden Grounds Coffee will start your morning with great coffee and equally fantastic vibes. The shop has a cute backyard oasis. You deserve a little treat, like a double espresso garnished with a toasted marshmallow. Where is the best happy hour in Wilmington, NC? Located inside a more than a century-old firehouse, Edward Teach Brewery features a two-story, ship-shaped bar and a variety of beers on tap, including their signature Scallywag Session IPA.
sections.education: The city's New Hanover County Schools district serves more than 25,000 PreK-12 students and operates 42 schools. The district has a favorable student-to-teacher ratio of 15:1 and is ranked within the top 20 school districts in North Carolina. There are several private schools here, too, from religious to STEM-focused. Cape Fear Academy is an independent school with a Pre-K-12 student population and a curriculum centered around college preparedness. faqs.a: The community’s most well-known university is the University of North Carolina – Wilmington, part of the prestigious University of North Carolina system. Other notable institutions include Miller-Motte College and Cape Fear Community College.

NC-winston-salem.description: Located in the foothills of North Carolina, Winston-Salem combines artistic flair with entrepreneurial innovation. The city’s thriving arts scene, vibrant downtown and easy access to outdoor activities offer residents a dynamic place to call home.

sections.location: Winston-Salem is a vibrant city in central North Carolina, about 100 miles west of Raleigh, accessible via Interstate 40 and U.S. Route 52. The nearest airport, Piedmont Triad International Airport, is just 15 miles east, offering convenient flights. Geographically, it sits in the Piedmont region, with rolling hills and the Yadkin River flowing through, providing scenic views and opportunities for water sports. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Raleigh is roughly 100 miles east; enjoy exploring the North Carolina Museum of Art and State Farmers Market or catch a show at Duke Energy Center. Charlotte sits 90 miles southwest, offering the NASCAR Hall of Fame and the U.S. National Whitewater Center. Greensboro is just 30 miles east; discover the Greensboro Science Center, the International Civil Rights Center and Museum, and the Greensboro Coliseum Complex.
sections.economy: The city’s economy is innovative and thriving. The city’s downtown central business district is bustling, and the Innovation Quarter is a growing biotechnology research park making a name for itself nationally. Health care and life sciences, advanced manufacturing, and education are three of the top industries in Winston-Salem. HanesBrands, Reynolds American Inc. and BB&T Corp. are all headquartered here. Even better? The beloved Krispy Kreme Doughnuts were founded in Winston-Salem in 1937. The cost of living in Winston-Salem is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Winston-Salem is $244,376, and the average household income is $92,390. North Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Winston-Salem, NC? The city is home to several high-performing health care providers. Wake Forest Baptist Medical Center is a nationally ranked academic medical center that provides advanced health care services. Novant Health is a network of hospitals and medical centers in the area. Finally, Forsyth Medical Center is a leading hospital that offers comprehensive medical services. Why Winston-Salem is a Great Place to Build Your StartupWhy Is Winston-Salem a Great Place to Live and Work?Why Minority-Owned Businesses in Winston-Salem Thrive
sections.thingsToDo: The city is full of wonders, especially when it comes to history and art. Explore the preserved history of the city at Old Salem and Historic Bethabara districts and delve into American art at the Reynolda House Museum of American Art. The arts district is alive and well, with galleries and murals, and the monthly First Friday events are a must. The Sawtooth School for Visual Art provides classes and exhibitions for residents to take. Catch a play or enjoy a show at the North Carolina Black Repertory Company and Theatre Alliance, or enjoy live music at spots like The Ramkat. Winston-Salem is a tight-knit community that celebrates its diverse cultural heritage through events like the Juneteenth Festival and the Bookmarks Festival of Books & Authors. Residents can also enjoy numerous annual events, like the RiverRun International Film Festival, showcasing international cinema, and the Carolina Classic Fair. Root, root, root for the home team at a Winston-Salem Dash baseball game. Or shop til you drop at the Winston Junction Market, where you can browse local artisan goods. Downtown is a walkable hub of shops, restaurants and culture. What is something you can do for free in Winston-Salem, NC? In the heart of Innovation Quarter is Bailey Park, a public green space that often hosts free yoga classes, food trucks, festivals and movie presentations. Then, spend your afternoon walking around the downtown district. Do it for the ’gram! The Downtown Arts District is the best place to spot some of the city’s many murals and public art installations. What are some things to do with kids in Winston-Salem, NC? Winston-Salem’s youngest residents can spend time at Kaleideum, a children’s museum that blends art and science. It’s opening a brand-new museum in the heart of downtown. Where do locals shop in Winston-Salem, NC? Body and Soul is a cultural gift shop that celebrates African American culture, from books and skin care to art and clothing imported from or inspired by Africa. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Winston-Salem, NC? Tucked away in the Industry Hill neighborhood near a smattering of great restaurants, The Ramkat is the place to be if you want to enjoy a night of live music, from country and indie to jazz and cover bands. 9 Fun Things to Do in Winston-Salem, NCThings to Do Before You Move to Winston-Salem, NCEnjoy These 7 Family-Friendly Activities in Winston-Salem, NC
sections.outdoors: Get out and about in Winston-Salem, which caters to relaxation and adventure. With more than 3,500 acres of parks and 25 miles of greenways and trails, residents can engage in hiking, biking and other outdoor pursuits. Salem Lake has a 7-mile trail around it, where kayaks and boats are available to rent. Parks like Reynolda Park, Tanglewood Park and Country Park offer playgrounds, walking trails and green spaces for family outings and picnics. Pilot Mountain State Park is close by for thrill-seekers, and it has hiking and great views. Golfers can play a round at Kimberly Park or one of Tanglewood’s two courses. Old Salem Museums & Gardens is a cool mix of history and the outdoors. Reynolda Gardens has lovely gardens and tranquil nature trails. Get Outside in Winston-Salem11 Must-Do Outdoor Activities in Winston-Salem, NCExplore the Bountiful Outdoor Activities in Winston-Salem, NC
sections.restaurants: Savor the varied flavors of the local dining scene. Mixing Southern staples and culinary experiences, there is a wealth of locally owned restaurants, coffee shops and breweries, particularly in the downtown area, making it a haven for food enthusiasts. Enjoy Moravian cookies at Winkler Bakery in Old Salem or try Texas barbecue at East of Texas. Winston-Salem’s culinary landscape includes farm-to-table establishments, international cuisines and craft beverage spots. Restaurants like The Katharine and Nana’s serve fancy Southern dishes, while The Porch and Mary’s Diner offer more laid-back meals. The local beer scene is strong, with breweries close to downtown, perfect for winding down after a day out. What is one of the best places to eat in Winston-Salem, NC? Mozelle’s Fresh Southern Bistro specializes in Southern fare with a twist in the West End neighborhood. Serving lunch, dinner and brunch, Mozelle’s offers a range of dishes, from shrimp and grits to vegetarian options like tomato pie. What is the best coffee shop in Winston-Salem, NC? Krankies Coffee roasts its own beans in the heart of downtown. With plenty of outdoor seating, visitors can enjoy freshly made coffee drinks along with specialty biscuits and sandwiches. Where is the best place for happy hour in Winston-Salem, NC? Reboot Arcade Bar is the city's first arcade bar and has more than 60 vintage arcade favorites, including Ms. Pac-Man and Donkey Kong. Savor These 8 Great Places to Eat in Winston-Salem, NC5 Chefs Shaking Up Winston-Salem’s Food SceneEat Like a Local in Winston-Salem, NC
sections.education: The Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools (WS/FCS) district serves 81 different schools and a student population of more than 52,000 pre-K-12 students. WS/FCS is the fourth-largest school system in North Carolina. Several private school institutions are also in the area, including Salem Academy, a private, all-girls boarding and day school. faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Winston-Salem, NC?
faqs.a: The talent pipeline is fueled by Winston-Salem’s many educational institutions, including Wake Forest University; Winston-Salem State, a top-ranking HBCU; and Salem College, the country’s oldest women’s college.

ND-fargo.description: Fargo, known for its Midwestern charm and urban sophistication, is a stunning spot along the banks of the Red River. With its strong job market, affordable cost of living and top-rated schools, it's no wonder people love living here.

sections.location: Fargo is a welcoming city in the eastern part of the state, right along the Red River, which forms the border with Minnesota. It’s accessible via Interstate 94 and Interstate 29, keeping it well-connected. Hector International Airport is right in the city, making travel a snap. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Minneapolis, MN, is just 235 miles southeast, where you can shop at the Mall of America or visit the Minneapolis Institute of Art. Sioux Falls, SD, sits 245 miles south, famous for Falls Park and the Washington Pavilion. Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada, is about 220 miles north, offering The Forks and the Canadian Museum for Human Rights.
sections.economy: Fargo also has a strong economy and a growing job market. Health care, educational services, and trade, transportation and utilities all shape the local economy. Some of the largest employers in Fargo include Sanford Health, North Dakota State University (NDSU), Essentia Health and Marvin Windows, a manufacturer of windows and doors with a facility here. New to town? Let Fargo’s community champions work with community employers and a welcoming committee to help you transition. Also, check out the Find the Good Life in ND Relocation Help Desk. The cost of living in Fargo is lower than the national average. The median home value in Fargo is $273,770, and the average household income is $100,732. North Dakota residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Fargo, ND? Residents are in good health thanks to providers like Sanford Health and Essentia Health, which operate several hospitals in the area. Sanford Medical Center Fargo and Essentia Health-Fargo are high performers in several categories.
sections.thingsToDo: Fargo packs arts, culture and entertainment into a city full of character. The Plains Art Museum and downtown murals turn the city into an open-air gallery. The historic Fargo Theatre screens indie films and hosts concerts. The Hjemkomst Center dives into Scandinavian heritage with a full-scale Viking ship replica, and downtown buzzes with unique boutiques like MINT + BASIL and vintage shops. Monthly events like Fargo-Moorhead First Fridays showcase local art and culture. For family-friendly fun, the Red River Zoo is home to rare cold-climate animals, and the Children’s Museum at Yunker Farm offers hands-on exhibits. Live performances thrive at Trollwood Performing Arts School, while the Fargo Film Festival draws cinephiles. Root, root, root for the home team at the Fargodome. History buffs can explore the Comstock House, and pop culture enthusiasts can visit the infamous Fargo wood chipper at the Visitors Center. The community comes together in the winter with the annual Frostival celebration. What is something you can do for free in Fargo, ND? Check out Bonanzaville, a historic village with over 40 buildings showcasing the region’s history. Some special events may have admission fees, but exploring the grounds is a fun way to spend an afternoon. What are some things to do in Fargo, ND, with kids? Davies Recreational Pool doubles as a water park with slides, a playground and a 400-gallon drop bucket that tips every four minutes. Where do locals shop in Fargo, ND? Locals often shop in the downtown area, where you can find a good mix of boutiques, specialty stores and eclectic shops. Pro tip: Spend a few hours perusing the dozens of great shops along Broadway. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Fargo, ND? The Fargo Theatre is a historic venue that regularly hosts live music, films and arts performances. Those more interested in the alt/indie music scene should catch a show at The Aquarium. Can We Talk About How Cool Fargo Is?Why Fargo, ND is a Great Place to LiveCity Spotlight: Why Fargo Is One of America’s Best Places to Live
sections.outdoors: The city makes the most of every season with outdoor activities for all interests. Warmer months bring scenic walks and bike rides along the Red River, plus playgrounds and splash pads at Lindenwood and Island Park. Kayaking, canoeing and fishing are favorites on the river, while golfers hit the greens at Rose Creek Golf Course. For family fun, Thunder Road Family Fun Park offers go-karts, mini-golf and laser tag. Winter turns the city into a snowy playground with cross-country ski trails, ice skating, curling, and broomball leagues. Residents bundle up for snowshoeing, sledding and ice fishing on the Red River while Island Park transforms into a frosty retreat. Just beyond Fargo, Buffalo River State Park offers year-round hiking and wildlife viewing. Special events like the Fargo AirSho add even more excitement to the city’s outdoor lineup. 4 Seasons of Fun in North Dakota5 Winter Sports You Must Try in North Dakota
sections.restaurants: Farm-to-table spots like Rosewild spotlight local ingredients, while Drekker Brewing Company fuels the city’s craft beer culture. International flavors shine with Ethiopian dishes at Dash & Rosa and inventive ramen at Slurp Ramen. Downtown Fargo is packed with eateries, from The Toasted Frog to The Boiler Room, plus cozy cafés like Nichole’s Fine Pastry and Twenty Below Coffee Co. Wurst Bier Hall nods to the city’s German roots. At the same time, Camp Lonetree and CRAVE offer unique dining experiences. Brewhalla showcases local bites, Marge’s Diner serves classic flavors and coffee lovers can sip on expertly brewed drinks at Young Blood Coffee Co. and Atomic Coffee. What is one of the best places to eat in Fargo, ND? Vinyl Taco is a popular spot for delicious tacos and a lively, upbeat atmosphere. They boast a great selection of craft beers and cocktails, and the restaurant has a solid reputation for its funky, music-themed decor. What is the best coffee shop in Fargo, ND? Find a darn good matcha latte and immaculate vibes at Youngblood Coffee Roasters, where ethically sourced beans and fair wages are prioritized. Where is the best happy hour in Fargo, ND? Grab some beer at Drekker Brewing Company’s taproom. This beloved spot is located inside a renovated railroad shop from 1883.
sections.education: Families here have many educational options, including a mix of public, private and charter schools. Fargo Public Schools is one of the largest public school districts in the state. West Fargo Public Schools also serves certain parts of the city, particularly in the western and southwestern areas of the city. There are several religious and independent private schools, often with a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. Something to keep in mind is that North Dakota has open enrollment policies, allowing families to apply to schools outside their assigned district, but availability can be limited. NE-lincoln.description: Lincoln, the capital of Nebraska, is an economic epicenter with top-rated schools, plentiful parks and green spaces and friendly residents who love their city. Lincoln blends small-town charm and big-city amenities to create a welcoming, inclusive community.

sections.location: Lincoln, the state capital of Nebraska, is in the southeastern part of the state along Interstate 80. It’s home to the University of Nebraska-Lincoln, giving it a lively, college-town feel. Lincoln Airport is right in the city, making travel super convenient. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Omaha is just 55 miles northeast, where you can visit the Henry Doorly Zoo or stroll the Old Market district. Kansas City, MO, sits 185 miles southeast, famous for the Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art and world-class barbecue. Des Moines, IA, is about 190 miles east, home to the Iowa State Fair and the Des Moines Art Center.
sections.economy: The city's diverse economy is made up of a variety of employers and industries. The city has several state government offices and agencies, including the State Capitol, a significant source of employment in the community. The University of Nebraska-Lincoln is another major employer contributing to education, research and cultural development. Health care also plays a crucial role, with Bryan Health and CHI Health St. Elizabeth offering a range of medical and administrative positions. You can also find opportunities in insurance with companies like Allstate and Ameritas or manufacturing roles with businesses like Kawasaki and Zoetis. The cost of living in Lincoln is lower than the national average. The median home value in Lincoln is $265,255, and the average household income is $101,268. Nebraska residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Lincoln, NE? Local residents are in good health thanks to Bryan Medical Center, Select Specialty Hospital – Lincoln and Lincoln Surgical Hospital. Bryan Health is a nonprofit health system that provides a range of medical services and centers in the area.
sections.thingsToDo: Living in Lincoln means you’ll never be bored. Art lovers can enjoy the city’s vibrant arts scene, which features the Sheldon Museum of Art, the Burkholder Project’s multi-level galleries and the Lux Center for the Arts, which offers exhibitions and workshops. There’s also the Great Plains Art Museum and First Friday art walks. The Nebraska State Capitol and Quilt Museum offer history, while the Lied Center and Nebraska Repertory Theatre have great shows. The city fosters a strong sense of community, which is evident in events like the Haymarket Farmers’ Market and the welcoming atmosphere at venues like the Branched Oak Observatory. The Haymarket District is a historical spot with several shops and restaurants, and the University of Nebraska-Lincoln’s sports teams provide year-round excitement. Outdoor enthusiasts can enjoy Star City Shores Aquatic Center activities and explore the city’s parks and green spaces. What is something you can do for free in Lincoln, NE? Pioneers Park Nature Center offers guests free admission to a variety of walking trails, wildlife observation areas, and educational exhibits that are fun for kids. It’s an excellent place for nature lovers and families to explore. What are some things to do in Lincoln, NE, with kids? It’s no surprise that the Lincoln Children’s Zoo is a beloved destination among families in the area. The variety of animals and interactive experiences make it an excellent place for children of all ages to appreciate and learn about wildlife. Where do locals shop in Lincoln, NE? A beloved favorite is Eyes of the World Imports, which sources fair-trade goods and home decor from around the world. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Lincoln, NE? The Bourbon Theatre is a locally loved venue housed in a renovated movie theater from the 1930s. They regularly host live music performances, including concerts by local and national artists, and boast a generous menu of craft cocktails.
sections.outdoors: Live life outdoors in this area. With 6,000 acres of parks and open space, including 132 parks and 134 miles of trails, the city has more parkland per capita than most other U.S. cities. Pioneers Park Nature Center is a scenic spot for hiking and wildlife spotting, while the MoPac Trail offers a longer route into the countryside. Holmes Lake Park is popular for fishing, kayaking and picnics, with a trail around the lake. Wilderness Park has over 1,400 acres of wooded trails. Sunken Gardens and disc golf at Roper Park are local favorites. The Children’s Zoo is a hit for families, and Antelope Park has gardens and trails for kids to run around. Golfers can hit the links at Hidden Valley Golf Club or Jim Ager Golf Course or catch a Saltdogs baseball game at Haymarket Park. Lincoln’s abundance of green spaces and trails makes it an ideal city for outdoor enthusiasts.
sections.restaurants: Lincoln has a food scene that’s growing fast, with flavors from all over the world and local favorites, too. With over 30,000 immigrants and refugees from 150 countries, residents find a wide range of international eats alongside classic American dishes. Places like The Oven and the UNL Dairy Store highlight the quality of Nebraska’s local ingredients, while Well & Good and Hub Cafe focus on farm-to-table meals. The craft drink scene locally has gained momentum in recent years, with breweries like Boiler Brewing Company and Kinkaider Brewing Co. Taproom offering unique, locally crafted beers. Wine lovers can visit Capitol View Winery and James Arthur Vineyards and enjoy pairings at Fireworks and The Oven Cellar. There are cozy cafes like Meadowlark and The Coffee House and dessert spots like Goldenrod Pastries and 402 Creamery. Go on a global journey at Licorice International, which offers an extensive selection of licorice. For unique dining, try Screamer’s Dining and Cabaret for entertainment or Lazlo’s Brewery & Grill for historic ambiance. What is one of the best places to eat in Lincoln, NE? Go for dinner and a show. Screamers Family Restaurant is an entertaining option, and the restaurant was the brainchild of an alum of the University of Nebraska’s show choir, The Scarlet and Cream Singers. What is the best coffee shop in Lincoln, NE? The Mill is a locally-owned coffee shop and roaster with half a dozen locations around town, making it a staple. Where is the best place for happy hour in Lincoln, NE? Need a martini? Barrymore’s is the spot to place your order. Tucked away inside Rococo Theater, this cozy cocktail spot was the theater’s original backstage area many moons ago.
sections.education: Home to the University of Nebraska – Lincoln, it’s no surprise that education and excellent schools here are second nature. There are various educational options, including public, private and charter schools. With a student population of nearly 42,000, Lincoln Public Schools is a highly-rated (and attended!) district. LPS has several focus programs, which are magnet schools with specialized themes. These focus programs include the arts and humanities, science and career academies. LPS schools also strongly focus on early childhood education, offering programs for preschool and kindergarten students. The city also has many religious and independent private schools, often with a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. Among the most highly ranked schools are Pius X High School, Parkview Christian Schools and Lincoln Christian School.
