faqs.a: The city has several major colleges and universities, including the University of Nebraska - Lincoln, Nebraska Wesleyan University, Union College and Southeast Community College.

NE-omaha.description: Midwestern charm and a vibrant city vibe come together in Omaha. The city is known for its thriving arts and culture scene, world-class culinary experiences, strong job market, affordable housing and, of course, the steaks!

sections.location: Omaha is a bustling city in eastern Nebraska, set along the Missouri River, which forms the border with Iowa. It’s accessible via Interstate 80 and Interstate 29, keeping it well-connected. The nearest airport, Eppley Airfield, is right in the city, making travel a breeze. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Lincoln is just 55 miles southwest, where you can visit the University of Nebraska-Lincoln or the Sunken Gardens. Kansas City, MO, sits 185 miles southeast, famous for the Country Club Plaza and the National World War I Museum. Des Moines, IA, is about 140 miles east, home to the Iowa State Fair and the Greater Des Moines Botanical Garden.
sections.economy: Omaha is the largest city in Nebraska and serves as a major economic hub for the Midwest. As a result, its economy is rooted in several industries, including health care, finance and insurance and trade, transportation and utilities. The city is known as "Silicon Prairie," and it's home to several young tech startups and more than half a dozen Fortune 100 companies, including Berkshire Hathaway, Mutual of Omaha and Union Pacific Railroad. Additionally, the city is seeing a lot of growth. The cost of living in Omaha is lower than the national average. The median home value in Omaha is $253,521, and the average household income is $108,027. Nebraska residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Omaha, NE? Nebraska Medicine-Nebraska Medical Center, Nebraska Methodist Hospital and several CHI Health locations all keep local residents in good health.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents know you should only move here if you’re ready to have a lot going on. Families and young professionals have a lot to explore with top-notch attractions like the Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium and the Joslyn Art Museum, which showcase impressive art collections and natural wonders. Families love the Omaha Children’s Museum and the Kiewit Luminarium, while history buffs dig the Durham Museum, housed in a restored Union Station. Old Market is a cool spot for shopping and eating, with unique shops and the Omaha Farmers Market. Plus, there is a long list of restaurants you need to try. The arts scene is music to our ears, particularly when it relates to indie rock, with venues like Steelhouse Omaha and Slowdown hosting live performances. Neighborhoods like the Blackstone District are lively, and the Old Market is always buzzing. Residents can also catch shows at the Orpheum Theater and Holland Performing Arts Center, and there are community events all year. What is something you can do for free in Omaha, NE? Biking the Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge, which spans the Missouri River, is a fun way to take in scenic views of the river and skyline. Even better? It’s one of the longest pedestrian bridges connecting two states (Nebraska and Iowa) — which means if you stand in the right spot, you can actually be in two states simultaneously. What are some things to do with kids in Omaha, NE? Visit the monkeys, giraffes, sea lions and other animals at Omaha’s Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium. Kids can also ride the zoo’s train and carousel. Where do locals shop in Omaha, NE? The Bookworm is a 6,000-square-foot, independently-owned store. Booksellers keep you up-to-date with what books they’re currently loving with a “We’re Reading” website feature. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Omaha, NE? Conveniently tucked downtown, the Holland Performing Arts Center is considered the premier local venue that hosts classical concerts, Broadway shows and more. Go There: A Weekend Getaway to Omaha, NEWant a Swanky Broadway Weekend Without Breaking the Bank? Skip NYC and Go to Omaha (Yes, Really)
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities abound, with an extensive park system and scenic riverfront. The city’s parks, such as Elmwood Park and Hanscom Park, provide ample green spaces for hiking, picnicking and leisurely strolls, making them popular destinations for residents seeking outdoor recreation. The Gene Leahy Mall at the Riverfront is a revitalized urban park with giant porch swings, hammocks, a sculpture garden and playgrounds, all set against the backdrop of the Missouri River. The Riverfront itself is a big draw for people living in Omaha, with walking and biking trails along the Missouri River and views from Lewis and Clark Landing. Residents can enjoy both active pursuits and relaxing moments amidst Omaha’s natural beauty.
sections.restaurants: Take a bite out of Omaha’s food scene, with classic Midwestern fare, innovative experiences and a growing craft beverage culture. Let’s get real: You can’t go to Omaha and not eat a steak at one of the city’s many fantastic steakhouses. The city is known for great steakhouses, like Monarch Prime, and other options, like Neapolitan pizza at Dante and farm-fresh ice cream at Coneflower Creamery. The Old Market district provides a charming setting for culinary exploration, with eateries like Ika San Ramen and Yokocho offering flavorful noodle dishes and creative cocktails. Farm-to-table spots like Gather, which grows its own food, and upscale establishments like Le Bouillon and Committee Chophouse offer something for every budget and palate. Other locally sourced spots include Kitchen Table and 402 Eat + Drink, which focus on local ingredients. The Blackstone District is home to trendy spots like Early Bird. Brunch culture is huge here, with Jam’s and Louie M’s Burger Lust Cafe serving big breakfasts. What is one of the best places to eat in Omaha, NE? At Kinaara, a beloved Indian restaurant, you’re faced with impossibly difficult choices: Famed Butter Chicken or Grandma’s Fish and Mango Curry? Of course, this is the kind of place you’ll want to go with friends who are keen on sharing dishes! But definitely start with some chili-garlic naan. What is the best coffee shop in Omaha, NE? Bring your pup and enjoy a cup of joe on the patio at Archetype Coffee. Where is the best place for happy hour in Omaha, NE? Omaha Dog Bar is a watering hole for pups and their people. (There’s even a dog menu with treats like frozen pumpkin puree).
sections.education: The area has several great school districts for families, including Omaha Public Schools (OPS), Millard Public Schools and Westside Community Schools. Millard is one of the best districts in Nebraska. However, OPS is the largest district in both Omaha and Nebraska and serves most of the students (more than 51,000!) in the area. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable student-to-teacher ratio of 16:1. Omaha also has many religious and independent private schools, often with a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. Brownell Talbot College Preparatory School is among the most well-regarded, thanks to its strong academic reputation and focus on college preparation. There are also dueling all-boys and all-girls college preparatory schools — Creighton Preparatory School and Duchesne Academy of the Sacred Heart. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: Nebraska Medicine-Nebraska Medical Center, Nebraska Methodist Hospital and several CHI Health locations all keep local residents in good health.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: Biking the Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge, which spans the Missouri River, is a fun way to take in scenic views of the river and skyline. Even better? It’s one of the longest pedestrian bridges connecting two states (Nebraska and Iowa) — which means if you stand in the right spot, you can actually be in two states simultaneously.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: Visit the monkeys, giraffes, sea lions and other animals at Omaha’s Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium. Kids can also ride the zoo’s train and carousel.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: The Bookworm is a 6,000-square-foot, independently-owned store. Booksellers keep you up-to-date with what books they’re currently loving with a “We’re Reading” website feature.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: Conveniently tucked downtown, the Holland Performing Arts Center is considered the premier local venue that hosts classical concerts, Broadway shows and more.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: At Kinaara, a beloved Indian restaurant, you’re faced with impossibly difficult choices: Famed Butter Chicken or Grandma’s Fish and Mango Curry? Of course, this is the kind of place you’ll want to go with friends who are keen on sharing dishes! But definitely start with some chili-garlic naan.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: Bring your pup and enjoy a cup of joe on the patio at Archetype Coffee.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for happy hour in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: Omaha Dog Bar is a watering hole for pups and their people. (There’s even a dog menu with treats like frozen pumpkin puree).

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Omaha, NE?
faqs.a: Several colleges and universities are located here, including Creighton University and the University of Nebraska – Omaha.

NH-manchester.description: History comes alive in Manchester. Nestled along the banks of the Merrimack River, residents have access to plenty of historic landmarks, excellent schools, a thriving arts and culture scene and proximity to a variety of outdoor activities and things to do.

sections.location: Manchester is a vibrant, gritty city perched in southern New Hampshire, about 50 miles north of Boston, where Interstate 93 and U.S. Route 3 crisscross. Manchester-Boston Regional Airport is right in the city, a convenient midsize hub offering flights to spots like Chicago and Orlando. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Boston, MA, sits 50 miles south, boasting the Freedom Trail, Boston Common and the Museum of Fine Arts. Portland, ME, is 95 miles northeast, where you can savor lobster rolls at the Old Port, visit the Portland Head Light or browse indie shops on Congress Street. Concord, NH, is just 15 miles north, home of the New Hampshire State House at the McAuliffe-Shepard Discovery Center and White Mountain National Forest nearby.
sections.economy: Manchester’s robust economy is experiencing significant growth, and the city boasts a burgeoning tech sector, health care industry, financial services, and various retail and hospitality opportunities. Major local employers are Elliot Hospital, Southern New Hampshire University, Fidelity Investments and Catholic Medical Center. The cost of living in Manchester is generally higher than the national average. The median home value in Manchester is $371,951, and the average household income is $109,314. While New Hampshire has no income tax, residents are taxed on interest and dividends. And because New Hampshire doesn’t have a state sales tax, no local sales taxes are applied in Manchester. Who are the major health care providers in Manchester, NH? Local residents have access to several major health care providers. Both Catholic Medical Center and Elliot Hospital are highly-ranked hospitals in the state. Plus, the city's growing wellness scene makes it easy for residents to prioritize their health. 8 Reasons To Build Your Small Business in Manchester, NHThe Cost of Living Diaries: Manchester, New HampshireWe’re Hiring: 5 Industries in Manchester, NH, That Are Growing
sections.thingsToDo: Manchester mixes culture and entertainment, creating a community-centered city with plenty of things to do every day. Art enthusiasts love the Currier Museum of Art, featuring works by renowned artists and tours of the Frank Lloyd Wright-designed Zimmerman House, or take a self-guided tour of the MHT Art Walk to view local sculptures and murals. Catch a show at the Palace Theatre or visit the Millyard Museum to learn more about local history, from its Native American roots to its industrial past. For fun, there’s “Art After Work” at the Currier and trivia nights around town. The New Hampshire Fisher Cats provide professional sports entertainment, and unique shops like The Terracotta Room offer local finds. The city fosters a strong spirit through various festivals and events and lively local establishments. What is something you can do for free in Manchester, NH? Channel your inner artist with a stroll through Cat Alley. The alley underwent a revitalization a few years ago and now showcases 18 new murals and some restored originals. During the summer months, Veteran’s Memorial Park hosts a variety of free movies and concerts. Where do locals shop in Manchester, NH? Pick up your plant babies and crystals at The Terracotta Room, a cute, locally-owned shop. What are some things to do in Manchester, NH, with kids? Livingston Park is a favorite, with a playground, a pool outfitted with a water slide and a nearby pond with a walking trail. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Manchester, NH? The Palace Theatres (yes, plural!) is a charming historic spot in downtown Manchester that is home to four performing arts venues that host a variety of performances, including Broadway shows, concerts and comedy acts. 10 Things You Must Do in Manchester, NH7 Reasons Manchester, NH, Is a Great Place To Live
sections.outdoors: Make your move to Manchester to explore the great outdoors. From relaxing to non-stop movement, there are plenty of places and parks to explore around town. The city boasts seven parks, including Prout Park with its new pickleball courts, and Derryfield Park, which offers sports fields and cross-country skiing trails. Water bugs can boat or kayak on Lake Massabesic or paddle along the Merrimack River past old mills. Hit the pavement at spots like the Piscataquog River Trail and the South Manchester Rail Trail, perfect for walking, running and biking. Take a time out at Cedar Swamp Preserve, which offers a peaceful nature escape within the city. For more action, there are two disc golf courses — designed by a world champion — and the McIntyre Ski Area offers skiing and snow tubing during the winter months. Golfers can enjoy the greens at Derryfield Country Club, and Crystal Lake is an excellent spot for swimming and hiking. The city’s proximity to regional attractions like Hampton Beach and the mountains makes it a hub for seasonal outdoor recreation. Weekend Itineraries: Explore Manchester, NH, Throughout the Year10 Activities for Outdoor Lovers in Manchester, NH
sections.restaurants: There is more than meets the eye when it comes to Manchester’s dining scene, blending classic New England comfort with modern culinary trends and a thriving craft beverage culture. Residents can enjoy a collection of diverse dining options, ranging from Puritan Backroom, famous for its chicken tenders and a stop for political figures, to international flavors at Alas de Frida, which serves authentic Mexican cuisine, including birria specialties. Fine dining can be found at The Foundry, emphasizing farm-to-table dishes, while Piccola Italia impresses with traditional Italian fare and tableside Caesar salads. The craft cocktail and brewery scene is growing, with more than a dozen local spots. Tours like the Manchvegas Brew Bus make it easy to hop between places like Pipe Dream, Great North and Candia Road. Casual taprooms like The Hop Knot and family-friendly brewpubs like Backyard Brewery add to the city’s lively atmosphere. What is one of the best places to eat in Manchester, NH? Cotton, located in the historic Millyard district, is a casual but upscale restaurant with a diverse menu and stylish atmosphere. They err on the side of classic American dishes and drinks with creative twists. What is the best coffee shop in Manchester, NH? Grab a coffee, juice or smoothie from The Bridge Cafe in downtown Manchester. Where is the best happy hour in Manchester, NH? Strange Brew Tavern has killer specials on Tuesdays as well as comedy and karaoke nights throughout the week! 8 Foodie-Recommended Restaurants in Manchester, NHWhat’s Brewing in Manchester, New Hampshire
sections.education: A major bonus for families in Manchester is that New Hampshire has open enrollment policies, allowing families to apply to schools outside their assigned districts, including charter schools. Manchester School District is the area’s leading public school district and the largest in the state. The district has a strong Career and Technical Education program at the Manchester School of Technology. Though performance varies from school to school, a favorable 12:1 student-to-teacher ratio provides the opportunity for more individualized education. Manchester has several religious and non-sectarian private schools offering smaller class sizes and focusing on college prep. Area charter schools are publicly funded but operate independently from the Manchester School District, offering more flexibility in curriculum and teaching methods. NH-nashua.description: History and hospitality meet in Nashua, inviting residents to join a tight-knit community and providing many amenities while maintaining affordability. A vibrant downtown, parks and recreation and proximity to Boston are just a few of the perks of living here.

sections.location: Nashua is a charming city in southern New Hampshire, mixing small-town appeal with urban access. It’s situated along U.S. Route 3, just south of Manchester. The nearest airport, Manchester-Boston Regional Airport, is about 20 miles away. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Manchester is just 15 miles north, where you can visit the Currier Museum, explore the Millyard Museum or cheer at Delta Dental Stadium. Boston, MA, sits 40 miles southeast, offering the Freedom Trail, Boston Common and the Museum of Fine Arts. Concord is about 30 miles north, tempting with the State House, McAuliffe-Shepard Center and lovely White Park.
sections.economy: Many people would describe the local job market as robust and diversified. The city benefits from its proximity to the high-tech corridor of southern New Hampshire, with several technology companies and manufacturing firms, especially in the defense and aerospace industries. Health care, finance and retail are other significant players in the local economy. The city has a reputation as a business-friendly community, making it an attractive place for new businesses and entrepreneurs. The cost of living in Nashua is higher than the national average. The median home value in Nashua is $396,310, and the average household income is $129,545. While New Hampshire has no income tax, residents are taxed on interest and dividends. And, because New Hampshire doesn’t have a state sales tax, no local sales taxes are applied in Nashua. Who are the major health care providers in Nashua, NH? Some major health care providers here include St. Joseph Hospital and Southern New Hampshire Medical Center.
sections.thingsToDo: New England charm is alive and well in Nashua. This sweet city’s amenities are primarily centered around the historic downtown and Nashua River, and Main Street has more than 100 shops and 35 restaurants, while the tax-free shopping environment attracts visitors from across the region to do some retail therapy at spots like Pheasant Lane Mall and Merrimack Premium Outlets. The arts are alive here, with attractions like the Nashua Performing Arts Center. The city also supports a thriving arts community, with galleries like Bilancia Gallery and events like the International Sculpture Symposium. Beyond retail and arts, Nashua has many entertainment options. Residents can enjoy local produce and artisan goods at the Nashua Farmers Market, catch a baseball game at the historic Holman Stadium or explore unique attractions like The Butterfly Place in nearby Westford, MA, and the Nashua Historical Society. What is something you can do for free in Nashua, NH? The city sponsors a whole program of free events called SummerFun that includes yoga in the park, nighttime concerts in Greeley Park, daytime classical concerts at the public library, a farmers market and more. The library hosts several events, including an annual Tiny Film Festival and a city-wide book club. What are some things to do with kids in Nashua, NH? Mine Falls Park is a local favorite. It features walking trails, bike paths and scenic picnic spots, all adding to a great afternoon of outdoor adventure. Many local families would also like to explore the Children’s Museum of New Hampshire, which is located nearby in Dover. They can take advantage of the many interactive exhibits and educational activities designed to engage budding minds there. Where do locals shop in Nashua, NH? In the morning, “The Monster” breakfast sandwich and a coffee from Riverwalk Bakery & Café is the perfect way to start your day. Then, shop the city’s cute boutiques, like Camaraderie. Bonus: New Hampshire has no sales tax, so residents can shop until they drop. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Nashua, NH? Many Nashua residents enjoy live music at local restaurants, bars and taverns. However, the city is known for being the home of Symphony NH. The symphony hosts various live concert events.
sections.outdoors: Get outdoors in Nashua. The city is primarily centered around the parks and natural landscapes. Mine Falls Park has more than 300 acres and 20 miles of trails along the Merrimack River and is a local favorite for hiking, biking, fishing and kayaking. Greeley Park has ample space for picnicking, tennis and seasonal events, like disc golf. The Nashua River Trail is a scenic spot for cycling and walking and extends into Massachusetts, while the Spit Brook Road Conservation area has scenic hikes and wildlife observation. For those looking for a more serene experience, the Reflection Garden & Labyrinth provides a sweet spot for meditation, and the Nashua National Fish Hatchery offers insight into fish conservation. Residents can enjoy seasonal activities like ice skating at Roby Park, and dog owners can visit the community-run Nashua Dog Park.
sections.restaurants: Eat your way through Nashua, enjoying classic New England fare, international flavors and modern dining experiences. The city has everything from fresh seafood and farm-to-table eateries to cozy diners and fusion restaurants. Local coffee shops like Riverwalk Bakery & Café are the perfect place to start the day, while spots like Surf offer fresh seafood. Martha’s Exchange and Red Arrow Diner are local flavors for those seeking comfort food. Try something more adventurous at spots like Pho Tai Loc. Beyond its restaurants, Nashua’s beverage scene is bubbling, with local breweries like Liquid Therapy and Omnium offering craft beer selections. Pressed Café is a trendy spot for healthy options and casual brunch, and Stella Blu offers craft cocktails. What is one of the best places to eat in Nashua, NH? Located along the Nashua River, Surf Seafood is a favorite among locals and is known for its creative seafood dishes, lively atmosphere and charming patio. Whether you want a place to hang out with friends after work or the perfect spot to meet up with a date, Surf is a solid choice. What is the best coffee shop in Nashua, NH? Bonhoeffer’s Cafe and Espresso is a nonprofit, direct-trade coffee shop that roasts its beans in-house. With a big fireplace and decadent breakfast options, it’s the perfect place to while away for hours. Where is the best place for happy hour in Nashua, NH? Stella Blu makes a mean martini and delicious small plates.
sections.education: A major bonus for families in Nashua is that New Hampshire has open enrollment policies, allowing families to apply to schools outside their assigned districts, including charter schools. The Nashua School District is the primary public school system in Nashua, and the city is also home to the Academy for Science & Design Charter School, the second-highest-ranked school in the state. Nashua also offers private school options, including 2nd Nature Academy, which focuses on nature-based and experiential learning. NJ-clifton.description: Clifton’s location in picturesque Passaic County and proximity to New York City make the city a haven for young families and professionals. Charming neighborhoods, excellent schools and plenty of outdoor activities contribute to Clifton’s strong sense of community.

sections.location: Clifton is located just west of New York City in Passaic County and offers suburban ease with world-class city vibes. New Jersey Route 3 links it to the Big Apple. Newark Liberty International Airport, about 15 miles away, is a central hub for flights everywhere. When you’re set to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: New York City is just 15 miles east, where you can hit Times Square, roam Central Park or catch a Broadway show. Paterson sits 5 miles north, offering the Great Falls, Paterson Museum and historic Lambert Castle. Jersey City is about 10 miles southeast, boasting waterfront views, Liberty State Park and the Liberty Science Center.
sections.economy: The city’s proximity to New York City and position as a regional commercial hub in New Jersey create an environment for economic prosperity. Three of the largest sectors in the area are health care, manufacturing and retail trade. Some of the largest employers are the City of Clifton, Clifton Public Schools and Passaic County. ClassLink, an education technology company, is headquartered in the area. The cost of living in Clifton is higher than the national average. The median home value in Clifton is $438,054, and the average household income is $131,314. New Jersey residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.63% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Clifton, NJ? Several health care providers are located here, including St. Joseph’s University Medical Center and the Clifton Health Center. St. Mary’s General Hospital is in nearby Passaic, NJ.
sections.thingsToDo: Its location to the Big Apple gives residents access to plenty of amazing amenities; however, there are tons of awesome things to do right at home. Residents can explore art and sculptures at the Clifton Arts Center and Sculpture Park or delve into local history at the Hamilton House Museum or Lambert Castle in nearby Paterson. Clifton residents are also community-minded, coming together for events throughout the year, like the Clifton Farmers Market and celebrations at Clifton Stadium. And, for kids, the Sea Life Aquarium is nearby and provides an opportunity to enjoy marine life. Shop ’til you drop at Clifton Commons and nearby spots like American Dream Mall. What is something you can do for free in Clifton, NJ? Stroll outside the Clifton Arts Center and you’ll happen upon Sculpture Park, which features 30 unique sculptures. Free to visitors, you can come to see these masterpieces from dawn until dusk every day. What are some things to do with kids in Clifton, NJ? Explore Weasel Brook Park, a 19-acre park with plenty of meadows, winding paths and, of course, a large play structure for the littles. Where do locals shop in Clifton? Locals often frequent the Main Avenue district, a popular spot featuring a mix of small businesses, local boutiques and specialty shops. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Clifton, NJ? The community's convenient location gives residents access to a wealth of music and performing arts. The historic Wellmont Theater, in nearby Montclair, hosts comedy and musical acts. Plus, in nearby New York City, residents can take in everything from a Broadway show to indie bands in underground venues and even an epic performance at Madison Square Garden.
sections.outdoors: The city packs a punch when it comes to recreation — there are 39 parks in the area! Main Memorial Park is a picture-perfect setting with a lake, playground and tennis facilities. The awe-inspiring Paterson Great Falls National Historical Park is about 10 minutes outside Clifton. Take in some breathtaking scenes and hike around for a quick getaway from the city. For a challenge, the Giant Stairs Hike is a demanding trail that pays off with stunning views.
sections.restaurants: Clifton is known as a melting pot, and that reputation spills over into the city’s delicious and diverse dining scene that reflects its multicultural community. Iconic establishments like Rutt’s Hut — popular for its “ripper” hot dogs — sit with traditional spots like Matthew’s Italian and tapas bars Spuntino. Seafood lovers can enjoy the fresh catch of the day at Sharky’s Wings and Raw Bar or American classics at The Barrow House. Craft breweries like Ghost Hawk Brewing Company and local distillery Silk City Distillers contribute to local beverage offerings. What is one of the best places to eat in Clifton, NJ? Grab casual fare at Rutt’s Hut. This local institution is a beloved walk-up hotdog stand that has served fried dogs since 1928. What is the best coffee shop in Clifton, NJ? El Coquí Café is a family-owned coffee shop. Be sure to try their flavor of the month specialty coffee! Where is the best place for happy hour in Clifton, NJ? With rankings for the best sports bar in New Jersey and the best wings, you can’t really go wrong stopping by Sharky’s for an after-work brew.
sections.education: Families thrive here, where education is a top priority. Clifton Public Schools is the main district serving nearly 11,000 students and offering a range of programming and schools, including magnet and technical schools. The city has a few religious and independent private schools, often with a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. There is also the Classical Academy Charter School, which is centered around a classical education and offers a rigorous curriculum. One thing to note is that New Jersey has a limited school choice program that allows students to attend schools outside their district of residence, but options are limited and based on availability. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: Several health care providers are located here, including St. Joseph’s University Medical Center and the Clifton Health Center. St. Mary’s General Hospital is in nearby Passaic, NJ.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: Stroll outside the Clifton Arts Center and you’ll happen upon Sculpture Park, which features 30 unique sculptures. Free to visitors, you can come to see these masterpieces from dawn until dusk every day.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: Explore Weasel Brook Park, a 19-acre park with plenty of meadows, winding paths and, of course, a large play structure for the littles.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Clifton?
faqs.a: Locals often frequent the Main Avenue district, a popular spot featuring a mix of small businesses, local boutiques and specialty shops.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: The community's convenient location gives residents access to a wealth of music and performing arts. The historic Wellmont Theater, in nearby Montclair, hosts comedy and musical acts. Plus, in nearby New York City, residents can take in everything from a Broadway show to indie bands in underground venues and even an epic performance at Madison Square Garden.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: Grab casual fare at Rutt’s Hut. This local institution is a beloved walk-up hotdog stand that has served fried dogs since 1928.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: El Coquí Café is a family-owned coffee shop. Be sure to try their flavor of the month specialty coffee!

faqs.q: Where is the best place for happy hour in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: With rankings for the best sports bar in New Jersey and the best wings, you can’t really go wrong stopping by Sharky’s for an after-work brew.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Clifton, NJ?
faqs.a: Although there aren’t any major colleges or universities located here, several higher education institutions are within driving distance.

NJ-jersey-city.description: Located along the banks of the Hudson River, Jersey City dazzles residents with its stunning skyline views and easy access to New York City. With a relatively low cost of living, gorgeous parks and no shortage of things to do, Jersey City is a melting pot of cultures.

sections.economy: --> --> Read the Magazine --> Weather in Jersey City, NJ Average Temperatures 63 high / 47 low Average Annual Rainfall 46 in Average Annual Snowfall 27 in

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Jersey City?
faqs.a: The climate here offers mild winters and hot summers. Things heat up in the summer, with July being the hottest month. The average highs and lows are 80°F and 65°F (summer) and 38°F and 25°F (winter).

faqs.q: Does Jersey City get a lot of snow?
faqs.a: The average annual snowfall in Jersey City is around 27 inches per year.

faqs.q: Does Jersey City get a lot of rain?
faqs.a: The average annual rainfall in Jersey City is 46 inches, with springtime typically being the wettest season.

faqs.q: How many sunny days does Jersey City get?
faqs.a: Jersey City averages 210 sunny days a year.

faqs.q: What are some things to do outside in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Enjoy some of the best views of the New York City skyline while walking, jogging or biking along the Hudson Riverfront Walkway. Spend some time on the water with an Urban Paddle kayaking tour, or charter a boat. Pick up lunch at one of the many farmers markets, such as Hamilton Park Farmers Market, and enjoy a picnic.

faqs.q: What is something free to do in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Summer in the City is the longest-running concert series in Jersey City and offers free concerts and outdoor movies. In addition, fishing is free twice a year with no license needed!

faqs.q: What are some fun things to do with kids in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Calling all space enthusiasts! The Liberty Science Center in Liberty Park boasts the largest planetarium in the U.S. and the fourth-largest globally.

faqs.q: What is the best open secret about Jersey City?
faqs.a: The sky's the limit in Jersey City. While the Liberty Science Center itself is well-known, you might not know that the state-of-the-art planetarium within it is the largest planetarium in the Western Hemisphere. It puts on breathtaking shows and stargazing experiences, making it a must-see for locals and visitors alike.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Central Avenue is a bustling commercial corridor boasting a mix of shops and businesses. Visitors can find everything from small boutiques to grocery stores and specialty shops in this part of town.

faqs.q: Where is a fun place to eat in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Check out the several European-style Biergartens, such as Smorgasbar, Zeppelin Hall Biergarten and Restaurant, and Hudson Hall, which feature extensive brew lists, live music and delicious food.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Jersey City?
faqs.a: White Eagle Hall is a restored historic venue in the Village neighborhood downtown that hosts everything from concerts and comedy shows to theater productions and more. They even have their own restaurant.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Jersey City?
faqs.a: While each neighborhood has its favorite shops, Modcup has several storefronts around the city. Treehouse in Westside offers creative drinks that are perfect for coffee enthusiasts looking for a clever twist.

faqs.q: What are the three largest industries in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Many industries, including financial services, health care and transportation and logistics, shape Jersey City's diverse economy.

faqs.q: What is the sales tax rate in Jersey City?
faqs.a: In 2024, the sales tax rate in Jersey City is 6.63%.

faqs.q: Does New Jersey have a state income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, New Jersey has a state income tax.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Jersey City has several hospitals and health care facilities, including Jersey City Medical Center and CarePoint Health. Hudson Regional Hospital also serves the region.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Jersey City?
faqs.a: Jersey City has several smaller colleges and universities, including New Jersey City University, Hudson County Community College and Saint Peter's University.

NM-rio-rancho.description: Beneath the cerulean skies of New Mexico, Rio Rancho epitomizes the Land of Enchantment. Stunning desert landscapes, affordable housing, excellent schools and endless outdoor activities (thanks to year-round sunshine!) cultivate a strong sense of community.

sections.location: Rio Rancho is a growing city just northwest of Albuquerque, right along New Mexico State Road 528, with Interstate 25 nearby for easy access. The nearest airport, Albuquerque International Sunport, is about 15 miles south, making travel a snap. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Albuquerque is just 10 miles south, where you can experience the Albuquerque International Balloon Fiesta or visit the Petroglyph National Monument. Santa Fe sits 60 miles northeast, tempting with art galleries and the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum. Las Cruces is about 225 miles south, home to the New Mexico Farm, Ranch Heritage Museum and the stunning Organ Mountains.
sections.economy: The city's proximity to Albuquerque may influence the number and types of businesses setting up shop in this city, but the economy is strong and diverse all on its own. Health care, retail trade and educational services are among the largest industries, and major employers are Rio Rancho Public Schools, Presbyterian Rust Medical Center and the City of Rio Rancho. Intel also has a large manufacturing facility in the area. The cost of living in Rio Rancho is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Rio Rancho is $297,034, and the average household income is $108,235. New Mexico residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.44% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Rio Rancho, NM? Local residents are in good health. UNM Sandoval Regional Medical Center is the most notable hospital in the area and was given the U.S. News & World Report’s high-performing distinction in the pneumonia category. Presbyterian Medical Group is also a major medical provider in the area. Rio Rancho, NM and Sandoval County: A Perfect Place to Call HomeNew Mexico’s Modern Day Manufacturing is Tech-DrivenThe Future is Now in Rio Rancho, NM and Sandoval County
sections.thingsToDo: Residents have plenty of entertainment options right at their fingertips. The Santa Ana Star Center and Rio Rancho Events Center host various concerts, sporting events and community gatherings, providing ample entertainment options. Art enthusiasts can explore local talent at the Artists of New Mexico Galleria. At the same time, history buffs can delve into the region’s past at sites like Casa San Ysidro & San Ysidro Church and the Coronado Historic Site. Nearby Albuquerque offers additional attractions, including the ABQ BioPark Zoo, Cliff’s Amusement Park and the Indian Pueblo Cultural Center, providing opportunities for family outings and cultural immersion. What is something you can do for free in Rio Rancho, NM? Go for a hike or run at Mesa Contenta Park Lookout Area, a 1.1-mile out-and-back trail. What are some things to do in Rio Rancho, NM, with kids? A Park Above is a 6-acre inclusive and accessible playground. The park was constructed to meet universal design standards and be more usable for everyone. The park’s design goal is to create an inclusive social play environment for children of all ages and abilities. Where do locals shop in Rio Rancho, NM? For bookworms, FriendShop and FriendsShop Too are part of the Friends of Libraries and Literacy. This all-volunteer nonprofit supports libraries and funds literacy programs for children, teens and adults in Sandoval County. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Rio Rancho, NM? Rio Rancho Event Center is often considered the local live performance and entertainment hub. It is also a popular spot for sporting events and trade shows. Rio Rancho, NM and Sandoval County Inspire Artistic Expression
sections.outdoors: Rio Rancho residents won’t get restless with the amount of outdoor recreation available to them. Geographically, the city sits amid high desert terrain with rolling hills, and the Rio Grande flows nearby. Capitalizing on its scenic location and extensive park system — there are more than 50! — the spots for hiking and biking are stunning. Hit the Willow Creek Trail or the Paseo del Bosque Trail along the Rio Grande for waterfront action. The nearby Jemez Mountain Trail is an excellent spot for hiking, and the Petroglyph National Monument and Alameda Open Space provide insights into the region's natural and cultural history. Enjoy family-friendly outings at parks like Loma Colorado Park and A Park Above, or experience the natural beauty of the Sandia Mountains and Cabezon Peak. Seasonal activities include exploring the Albuquerque International Balloon Fiesta at Balloon Fiesta Park and engaging in sports at the McDermott Athletic Center.
sections.restaurants: The city has a delightful dining scene, creatively blending New Mexican flavors. Local favorites showcase the region’s signature chile sauces, and other establishments like Weck’s and Turtle Mountain Brewing Company provide hearty American dishes and craft brews. The city’s beverage scene includes local breweries like Brew Lab 101 Beer & Cider Co. and nearby wineries along the Corrales Wine Loop. Culinary exploration is encouraged through events highlighting the city’s diverse dining establishments. What is one of the best places to eat in Rio Rancho, NM? Hot Tamales is the real deal when it comes to Mexican food. What is the best coffee shop in Rio Rancho, NM? Piñon Coffee House is a local favorite with a drive-thru, serving specialty lattes (think Mexican Spiced Chocolate). Where is the best happy hour in Rio Rancho, NM? Pair wood-fired pizzas and calzones with tasty microbrews at Turtle Mountain Brewing Company.
sections.education: The area offers many amenities that make it great for young families, and quality public and private schools are a major draw. Rio Rancho has various educational options, including a mix of public, private and charter schools. The Rio Rancho Public Schools district is among the best in the state and has a student population of more than 17,000. The district is renowned for providing a well-rounded education and exceptional academic, fine arts and athletics programs. There are also a couple of charter schools here, including a STEM-focused setting and dual-language immersion school. The area also has many religious and independent private schools, often with a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. faqs.a: Although there aren’t any major colleges or universities located here, there are several higher education institutions in nearby Albuquerque, including the University of New Mexico.

NM-santa-fe.description: Paired with breathtaking landscapes and Southwestern charm, It's easy to see why residents are charmed by the Land of Enchantment. Santa Fe is home to world-class art galleries, delicious dining options and artistic inspiration around every corner.

sections.location: Santa Fe is a historic city in north-central New Mexico, about 60 miles northeast of Albuquerque, accessible via Interstate 25. Santa Fe Regional Airport is close, but Albuquerque International Sunport (65 miles southwest) has more options. Nearby major cities include: Albuquerque sits just 60 miles southwest, where you can explore the Petroglyph National Monument and Old Town or ride the Sandia Peak Tramway. Taos is 70 miles north, boasting the Taos Pueblo, Rio Grande Gorge Bridge and world-class skiing. Las Cruces lies 275 miles south, home to New Mexico Farm and Ranch Heritage Museum, the Organ Mountains and White Sands National Park (nearby).
sections.economy: The city has a unique economy heavily rooted in tourism, government and health care industries. Some of the most significant employers in Santa Fe are the State of New Mexico, Christus St. Vincent Regional Medical Center and Los Alamos National Laboratory. While it may not be a hub for major corporate headquarters, the city is known for its high concentration of art galleries and studios and other tourism-related businesses, like restaurants and hotels, creating a landscape ripe for local businesses. The cost of living in Santa Fe is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Santa Fe is $446,470, and the average household income is $108,676. New Mexico residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.19% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Santa Fe, NM? Christus St. Vincent Regional Medical Center is one of the best hospitals in the state and the largest hospital in the city. It offers a comprehensive range of services, including emergency care, surgery, oncology and cardiology. New Mexico: Come for the Job, Stay for the LifestyleThese Top Industries in New Mexico Are Powering the Economy
sections.thingsToDo: The city's reputation as “A City Different” sets the city apart, and there are plenty of things to keep residents entertained. The city is a hub for artists, with over 250 galleries, particularly along Canyon Road. Cultural experiences range from exploring the historic Santa Fe Plaza and the Palace of the Governors to immersive art experiences. Residents can also enjoy unique events like the Folk Art Market and the Santa Fe Indian Market and attend performances at the Santa Fe Opera House. But beyond the art, Santa Fe has opportunities to delve into Native American and Spanish colonial history at the Museum of Indian Arts & Culture and the Museum of Spanish Colonial Art. Residents can shop at the Santa Fe Farmers Market, relax at spas like Ten Thousand Waves and visit significant religious sites like Loretto Chapel and Santuario de Chimayo. The numerous festivals and markets contribute to its distinct way of life, and residents love living in a tight-knit community. What is something you can do for free in Santa Fe, NM? Spend an afternoon strolling around the Canyon Road Arts District. The area is home to more than 80 art galleries and several boutiques and restaurants in a half-mile stretch. What are some things to do with kids in Santa Fe, NM? Kid adventurers of all ages can climb at the Santa Fe Climbing Center. And, if your little monkeys want to swing from the ceiling, LiggettVille Adventure Center has a ropes course and sip rail. Where do locals shop in Santa Fe, NM? Peruse the Santa Fe Farmers Market, which is open every Saturday of the year and has additional mid-week markets during the spring, summer and fall. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Santa Fe, NM? Become a concert regular at Meow Wolf, which is like a creativity vortex. The neon sci-fi museum championed by George RR Martin (of “Game of Thrones” fame) has one of the coolest concert venues in the region. Go There: A Splendid Weekend Away in Santa Fe, NM
sections.outdoors: Stretch your legs in any of the open spaces in Santa Fe. Whether you’re looking for adventure or a peaceful natural experience, there is something for everyone. During the winter, residents can ski and snowboard at Ski Santa Fe, which surprisingly gets a large amount of snow. Year-round hiking and biking enthusiasts can explore the extensive network of trails, including the scenic Dale Ball Trails and the landscapes of Bandelier National Monument and Kasha-Katuwe Tent Rocks National Monument. Beyond these activities, local residents can raft on the Rio Grande, fish in local rivers and streams and golf on courses set against desert landscapes. Horseback riding is a traditional way to explore the area’s trails, and hot-air ballooning gives a new perspective of the region. Nature lovers can also enjoy the Randall Davey Audubon Center and the Santa Fe Botanical Garden, and drives like the Turquoise Trail provide beautiful views of the surrounding landscapes. 5 Ways to Explore the Outdoors in New MexicoSleep Under the Stars! Great Places to Camp in New Mexico
sections.restaurants: Native American, Mexican and Spanish flavors influence the local culinary scene, and spots like The Shed, Tia Sophia’s and La Choza offer traditional New Mexican dishes. A growing farm-to-table movement around town and spots like Vinaigrette and The Compound highlight local, seasonal ingredients. The city has a surprising number of specialty shops, like Chocolate + Cashmere.Beyond its restaurants, Santa Fe has a thriving craft beverage scene, with breweries like Santa Fe Brewing Co. and Rowley Farmhouse Ales and wineries such as Vivac Winery contributing to the city’s vibrant food and drink culture. Sip your way down the Margarita Trail, which highlights over 40 different varieties of the beloved cocktail. What is one of the best places to eat in Santa Fe, NM? Izanami, a Japanese restaurant at 10,000 Waves, features an impressive sake collection, and you can order a tasting flight here. What is the best coffee shop in Santa Fe, NM? ICONIK is a Santa Fe staple, serving some of the best specialty coffee in the state and light bites, specialty teas and matcha. Where is the best happy hour in Santa Fe, NM? La Mama is a natural wine bar, a restaurant and a market that sells tinned fish, olives and everything else you need to throw one heck of an at-home happy hour. Cheers to Craft Beer in New MexicoSanta Fe Unveils ‘Margarita Trail’ — Here’s Where to Stop and Sip
sections.education: Families can enjoy a highly educated environment and a community that places a significant emphasis on the importance of education, which is reflected in the variety of educational options, including a mix of public, private and charter schools. Santa Fe Public Schools (SFPS) is the main public school district in the area, serving more than 11,000 students and offering a wide range of programming. Several magnet and charter schools have specialized themes and dual language programs. The city has many religious and independent private schools, often with a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. The Santa Fe School for the Arts & Sciences offers students a well-rounded education, focusing on the arts and sciences. faqs.a: Peruse the Santa Fe Farmers Market, which is open every Saturday of the year and has additional mid-week markets during the spring, summer and fall.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Santa Fe, NM?
faqs.a: Become a concert regular at Meow Wolf, which is like a creativity vortex. The neon sci-fi museum championed by George RR Martin (of “Game of Thrones” fame) has one of the coolest concert venues in the region.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Santa Fe, NM?
faqs.a: Izanami, a Japanese restaurant at 10,000 Waves, features an impressive sake collection, and you can order a tasting flight here.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Santa Fe, NM?
faqs.a: ICONIK is a Santa Fe staple, serving some of the best specialty coffee in the state and light bites, specialty teas and matcha.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Santa Fe, NM?
faqs.a: La Mama is a natural wine bar, a restaurant and a market that sells tinned fish, olives and everything else you need to throw one heck of an at-home happy hour.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Santa Fe, NM?
faqs.a: Several higher-education institutions are located in the city, including Santa Fe Community College and the Institute of American Indian Arts.

OH-cincinnati.description: Cincinnati’s reputation as the ‘Queen City’ makes it a crown jewel of the Midwest. Cincinnati fosters a strong sense of community with arts, world-class dining and a passionate fanbase. The city has plenty of things to do and maintains a low cost of living.

sections.location: Cincinnati is a historic city in southwestern Ohio, about 110 miles southwest of Columbus, accessible via Interstates 75 and 71. Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky International Airport is 15 miles south with global connections. Nearby major cities include: Louisville, KY, sits 100 miles southwest, famous for the Kentucky Derby Museum, Muhammad Ali Center and massive Louisville Slugger. Columbus, OH, is 110 miles northeast, where you can explore Columbus Zoo, Franklin Park Conservatory and Short North Arts District. Indianapolis, IN, is roughly 115 miles northwest, where you can catch a Colts game, marvel at the Indianapolis Museum of Art or wander the historic Monument Circle.
sections.economy: Cincinnati is Ohio’s largest metropolitan area, and, as a result, the city has a highly diverse economy. The city is a major center for jobs in the health care, finance and insurance and manufacturing sectors. The largest employers include the University of Cincinnati, Kroger, Procter & Gamble (P&G) and General Electric Aviation. In addition, Kroger, P&G and Fifth Third Bank call Cincinnati home, with headquarters in town. The cost of living in Cincinnati is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Cincinnati is $235,965, and the average household income is $91,343. Ohio residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Cincinnati, OH? Cincinnati Children’s Hospital Medical Center is consistently ranked as one of the country’s top children’s hospitals, offering a wide range of pediatric specialties and services. The University of Cincinnati Medical Center, Christ Hospital and the TriHealth System hospitals provide quality health care for adults. 9 Reasons to Start a Business in Cincinnati
sections.thingsToDo: This Midwest gem is where residential, business and entertainment combine to create quite an appealing lifestyle. Residents living in Cincinnati are met with a fantastic performing arts scene (think the Ballet, Opera and Symphony Orchestra), a plethora of sporting events (football, baseball, soccer), great shopping, a calendar full of events and festivals — the list goes on and on. This city thrives on the arts and is one of only a handful of U.S. cities with professional companies in all five arts: ballet, opera, fine arts museum, theater and symphony. If seeing one of the professional companies doesn’t suit your fancy, just take a stroll around the city to enjoy its amazing public art. The city hosts BLINK, the nation’s largest immersive art experience, every other year. Learn more about Cincinnati’s vital role in the Underground Railroad and helping enslaved people reach freedom by visiting the National Underground Railroad Freedom Center. Pay a visit to one (or several) of Cincinnati’s numerous farmer’s markets, including Hyde Park Farmers’ Market, Northside Farmers Market and Findlay Market, then spend the afternoon lunching, patio-drinking and shopping in the historic Over-the-Rhine neighborhood. What is something you can do for free in Cincinnati, OH? The Cincinnati Art Museum offers free admission year-round! They also provide free and low-cost programming for adults, teens and families. What are some things to do in Cincinnati, OH, with kids? Thanks largely (pun intended!) to a hippo named Fiona, the Cincinnati Zoo & Botanical Garden is well known. Born prematurely in 2017, Fiona was severely underweight and struggled to survive. However, she has thrived since and become a massive draw for this 70-acre complex, one of the oldest zoos in the country, dating back to 1875. Besides Fiona, the zoo is home to more than 1,900 animals representing more than 500 species. Where do locals shop in Cincinnati, OH? Initially opened in 1855, The Findlay Market, located just blocks from downtown, is an iconic market hosting 50 full-time shops selling everything from meats and local vegetables to ethnic foods and flowers. Weekends offer even more to explore as farmers markets are set up in the outdoor space, and there are often street performers and entertainment. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Cincinnati, OH? Riverbend Music Center is an outdoor amphitheater along the banks of the Ohio River, attracting national tours and hosting local musicians, too. Why I Moved Back to CincinnatiGo There: Queen of the Midwest Cincinnati is Sure to Captivate You8 Reasons to Move to Cincinnati, OH
sections.outdoors: Residents have a range of activities right at their fingertips. The city has 364 parks within its limits, including five regional parks, 70 neighborhood parks and 34 different natural areas. Residents can enjoy scenic walks and picnics at parks like Eden Park, Washington Park and Mt. Airy Forest, which offers hiking, biking and a wheelchair-accessible treehouse. The Cincinnati Riverwalk and the Purple People Bridge provide picturesque views of the Ohio River and the city skyline, while the John A. Roebling Suspension Bridge connects Cincinnati to nearby Covington, KY. Hit the water on the Ohio River and surrounding waterways by kayaking, boating and fishing. Green Acres Kayak and Miami Whitewater Forest offer kayak rentals, and Wilton Woods Harbor provides watercraft rentals on Winton Lake. Take a trip and enjoy a riverboat tour, too. Hiking trails are abundant, and favorites include the Cincinnati Nature Center and East Fork State Park.
sections.restaurants: The Over-the-Rhine district is a hub for eclectic dining and nightlife, featuring everything from craft breweries to trendy restaurants. The city is known for its unique local specialties, like Skyline chili, goetta — a German-American breakfast staple — and Graeter’s Ice Cream. It also offers many international cuisines, from Mexican at Mazunte to Italian at Pepp & Dolores. The local craft brewery scene is strong, with breweries like Rhinegeist and MadTree Brewing leading the city’s craft beer movement. A burgeoning wine scene has sprouted over the last few years, with local wineries like Meiers Wine Cellars and urban wineries like Revel OTR offering unique tasting experiences. What is one of the best places to eat in Cincinnati, OH? The Aperture is a James Beard Semi-Finalist, focusing on Mediterranean and North African influences and is known for its live-fire cooking. What is the best coffee shop in Cincinnati, OH? Kings Arms Coffee Roasters has delightful lattes, cappuccinos, pour-overs and tons of different syrups. They also have some of the best teas and beans you can find. Where is the best place for happy hour in Cincinnati, OH? Grab a unique cocktail or two from Homemakers Bar, a spot where retro meets modern and cocktails and conversation flow freely.
sections.education: Cincinnati places a significant emphasis on the importance of education, which is reflected in the variety of educational options, with a mix of public, private and charter schools. Cincinnati Public Schools, the primary school district serving the city, has a student population of more than 35,000 and is the second-largest district in the state. CPS offers several charter and magnet schools with specialized themes, including STEM, arts and language immersion. The city also has a strong presence of religious and independent private schools, often with a long history of high-quality education and smaller class sizes. faqs.a: Riverbend Music Center is an outdoor amphitheater along the banks of the Ohio River, attracting national tours and hosting local musicians, too.

faqs.a: The Aperture is a James Beard Semi-Finalist, focusing on Mediterranean and North African influences and is known for its live-fire cooking.

faqs.a: Kings Arms Coffee Roasters has delightful lattes, cappuccinos, pour-overs and tons of different syrups. They also have some of the best teas and beans you can find.

faqs.a: Grab a unique cocktail or two from Homemakers Bar, a spot where retro meets modern and cocktails and conversation flow freely.

faqs.a: There are several major colleges and universities located here, including the University of Cincinnati, Xavier University, Cincinnati State and the Art Academy of Cincinnati.

OK-edmond.description: On the outskirts of Oklahoma City is Edmond, a city that charms residents with its friendly atmosphere and exceptional quality of life. A low crime rate, great schools, bustling local businesses and plenty of outdoor activities create a welcoming, safe environment.

sections.location: Edmond is a charming city just north of Oklahoma City. Interstate 35 connects it to OKC and beyond, and Will Rogers World Airport is roughly 25 miles away. Ready to explore? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Oklahoma City is just 15 miles south, where you can visit the OKC National Memorial, stroll Bricktown or cheer at Chesapeake Energy Arena. Norman sits 30 miles south, buzzing with University of Oklahoma life, the Sam Noble Museum and Lake Thunderbird. Tulsa is about 100 miles northeast, with the Philbrook Museum, Tulsa Zoo and scenic River Parks.
sections.economy: Edmond boasts an increasingly diversified economy, with health care, educational services and professional services among the top industries. Health care, in particular, thrives here, with two hospitals, a surgical center, and many doctors’ offices, significantly boosting the local economy and making Edmond a flourishing community with a longer life expectancy for residents. Petra Industries, a leading wholesale distributor of electronics, technology products and accessories, is headquartered here. Some significant employers in the area include the University of Central Oklahoma, Edmond Public Schools and the City of Edmond. The cost of living in Edmond is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Edmond is $317,536, and the average household income is $150,964. Oklahoma residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Edmond, OK? Integris Health Edmond and Summit Medical Center-Edmond are top-tier health care providers in the area. Additionally, residents can access providers in nearby Oklahoma City.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents and visitors can enjoy a range of activities, from cultural experiences at the Armstrong Auditorium and UCO Jazz Lab to family-friendly fun at Sky Zone Trampoline Park and Pelican Bay Aquatic Center. History enthusiasts can dive in at the Edmond Historical Society & Museum and the Rodkey House. Looking for some fun? There are plenty of festivals to entertain the whole family, including holiday light displays at Edmond Electric’s Luminance and seasonal ice skating. The Edmond Farmers Market provides local produce and crafts, and venues like Edmond Unplugged and Unpluggits Paint & Play offer unique social and creative experiences. What is something you can do for free in Edmond, OK? Go on a self-guided bicycle public art tour, which features over 20 art pieces on a 7.5-mile trek. You can also choose your own adventure and take a shorter route, too! What are some things to do with kids in Edmond, OK? The Children’s Learning Center at the Edmond History Museum offers a miniature pioneer town complete with a general store, train station, post office and prairie house with dress-up clothes. Where do locals shop in Edmond, OK? An offshoot of the popular Midtown OKC location, Commonplace Books Edmond is a picturesque place to pick up the latest and greatest literature. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Edmond, OK? The Armstrong Auditorium is a world-class performing arts center hosting the best classical, folk and jazz artists.
sections.outdoors: With 26 parks, 300 miles of trails, playgrounds, sports courts and disc golf, there are plenty of places to explore the local green spaces. E.C. Hafer Park is a popular spot for picnicking. Residents and their four-legged friends can enjoy an afternoon at the Edmond Dog Park. Mitch Park has several trails and a disc golf course, while Arcadia Lake offers residents the opportunity to fish, boat, hike and camp. The Edmond Parks and Recreation Department hosts several events throughout the year, including an annual Concerts in the Park series. Sports lovers can practice skateboarding at the Mathis Skate Park, catch a game at the Edmond Route 66 Softball Complex or hit the links at the KickingBird Golf Club.
sections.restaurants: Take a bite out of the city's growing foodie scene. This Oklahoma city will satisfy every craving with more than 250 restaurants offering everything from upscale dining to casual eats. Downtown is a hub for foodies, where you can even take a food tour to explore the local cuisine. Enjoy fine wine and steaks at Boulevard Steakhouse, classic Italian fare from Sparrow Modern Italian and fresh seafood at Sedalia’s Oyster & Seafood. Charleston’s Restaurant, Hidalgo’s Mexican Restaurant & Cantina and Café Icon Sushi & Grill are local favorites for more relaxed meals. What is one of the best places to eat in Edmond, OK? Savor every flavor at The Edmond Railyard, a food hall in the heart of town with eight different restaurants — perfect for an indecisive group. What is the best coffee shop in Edmond, OK? Cafe Evoke is a local favorite specializing in espresso drinks and a great spot to enjoy a chef-driven brunch menu and pastries. Where is the best place for happy hour in Edmond, OK? The Patriarch, a 110-year-old house-turned-craft-beer-joint, has one of the best patios in town.
sections.education: Education is a high priority for local residents. Edmond Public Schools, the primary public school district here, is one of Oklahoma’s largest and most highly ranked districts and is known for its high academic standards. Edmond Public Schools has a student population of more than 26,000, numerous schools that have earned a National Blue Ribbon School distinction and a strong focus on technology integration in the classroom. There are also several private schools nearby, including religiously affiliated institutions and non-sectarian schools. Keystone Adventure School and Farm combines academics with outdoor and farm-based learning experiences, giving students experiences both in and out of the classroom. OK-norman.description: Norman’s location in the heart of Oklahoma and college town energy contribute to the city’s close-knit community, characterized by a vibrant arts scene, ample outdoor spaces and a spirited sports culture. The city has a low cost of living and plenty of things to do.

sections.location: Norman is a friendly city in Cleveland County just south of Oklahoma City, along Interstate 35. It’s home to the University of Oklahoma, giving it a lively, college-town vibe. The nearest airport, Will Rogers World Airport in Oklahoma City, is about 20 miles north, ready for your travel plans. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Oklahoma City is just 20 miles north, where you can visit the Oklahoma City National Memorial or stroll through the Myriad Botanical Gardens. Tulsa sits 105 miles northeast, offering the Philbrook Museum of Art and the Gathering Place Park. Dallas, TX, is about 190 miles south, home of the Sixth Floor Museum, Dallas Museum of Art and AT&T Stadium.
sections.economy: The local economy is rooted in educational services, health care and retail trade, and the presence of the University of Oklahoma significantly influences the city’s economic landscape. Some significant employers are the University of Oklahoma, Norman Regional Health System and Norman Public Schools. The city has a thriving local business economy, with numerous restaurants, shops and entertainment venues. The cost of living in Norman is generally lower than the national average. The median home value in Norman is $248,548, and the average household income is $93,801. Oklahoma residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.76% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Norman, OK? Residents are in good health thanks to providers like Norman Regional Health System, which has two campuses in the city. In addition, Norman Regional Hospital is rated as a high performer in five adult procedures and conditions.
sections.thingsToDo: Life in Norman is buzzing with excitement and activities, largely thanks to the youthful energy that bleeds over from the university. The city’s cultural scene is rich, with attractions like the Fred Jones Jr. Museum of Art and the Jacobson House Native Art Center. Music is big here, with performances at venues like The Blue Note and the annual Norman Music Festival, which draws more than 70,000 people for three days of fun. Residents can also enjoy a variety of community gatherings throughout the year, including the May Fair Arts Festival, Jazz in June and the Norman Pride Festival. Beyond the cultural offerings, the city has plenty of places to play, like experiencing the excitement of the University of Oklahoma Sooners football games at Oklahoma Memorial Stadium. Explore the historic Campus Corner District, shop at the Norman Farm Market or spend some time in the revitalized downtown district with tons of quaint cafes, shops and entertainment. What is something you can do for free in Norman, OK? There are free concerts in Legacy Park in the spring and Lions Park in the summer. Lions Park also has free movies in the warmer months. In September, the Cleveland County Fair is a great way to wind down the summer. What are some things to do with kids in Norman, OK? Check out the massive dinosaur skeletons at Sam Noble Oklahoma Museum of Natural History or get immersed in meteorology at the National Weather Museum and Science Center. Also, check out HeyDay Norman, which has bowling, laser tag, an arcade, mini golf and a ropes course. Where do locals shop in Norman, OK? Locally owned boutiques line Norman’s Main Street neighborhood. Green Feather Book Company is the city's oldest bookstore and hosts various events, like book clubs and poetry nights. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Norman, OK? See a show at the iconic historic Sooner Theatre. Or enjoy a concert at The Depot, a former train depot that now hosts rotating gallery exhibits, live music series and poetry readings.
sections.outdoors: The Sooner State is full of stunning scenes, and this city is no exception. The city has nearly 70 parks, including the massive Lake Thunderbird State Park, which provides hiking, boating, fishing and camping opportunities. At the same time, Sutton Wilderness offers 160 acres of prairie grass and wildlife, and Griffin Community Park features a disc golf course, lake, dog park and jogging trails. The Discovery Cove Nature Center houses interactive exhibits on local wildlife and ecosystems, and Andrews Park has several community events like outdoor movie nights. Hit the links and enjoy a round at Jimmie Austin Golf Club. Or, enjoy the weather on the numerous trails throughout town, which provide ample space for hiking, biking and walking. Take the kids to Andrews Park to enjoy the splash pad, the large wooden playground structure at Reaves Park and the wide open spaces at Normandy Park.
sections.restaurants: Take a bite out of the fabulous local foodie scene. Iconic establishments like The Mont, known for its signature “Swirl” (half sangria, half margarita!) drink, and The Diner, offering Tex-Mex comfort food, anchor the city’s dining landscape. Victoria’s Pasta Shop and Gabarino’s provide Italian fare, while Pepe Delgados and Tarahumara’s Mexican Cafe are Mexican favorites. Restaurants like The Moon Sushi & Asian Bistro and Sweet Basil offer Asian flavors, while Ray’s Smokehouse BBQ caters to barbecue enthusiasts. The craft beer scene is brewing delicious beers all over town, and you can experience it firsthand on the Norman Ale Trail. The Bluebonnet Bar is an excellent spot for cocktails and live music. There are plenty of brunch spots, with places like Neighborhood JAM. To satisfy a sweet tooth, visit Lohmann’s Good Things or Apple Tree Chocolate. What is one of the best places to eat in Norman, OK? Interesting cocktails, fresh food, weekday brunch options and a gorgeous space make Scratch Kitchen a place you’ll return to again and again. What is the best coffee shop in Norman, OK? Gray Owl is a beloved local spot and a worker-cooperative coffee shop in downtown Norman. This cozy coffee shop serves specialty coffee, loose-leaf tea and baked goods. Where is the best happy hour in Norman, OK? Cocktails at The Winston start at $4 at 3 p.m. and go up $1 for the next two hours. Beer drinkers will like the $2.25 drafts at The Library Bar and Grill. Plus, college students can say they were at “The Library” if their parents ask about any questionable transactions on the bank statement!
sections.education: The area offers a variety of educational options for families, with a strong mix of public and private schools. Norman Public Schools is the primary school district serving the city, and it is one of the largest in the state, often lauded for its commitment to academic excellence. Norman Public Schools consistently rank highly in the state and have a strong track record of success, including national Merit Scholars and Blue Ribbon Schools. But it isn’t all work and no play for the nearly 16,000 students enrolled in Norman Schools — the district is known for its strong emphasis on extracurriculars, including athletics and fine arts. Le Monde International School is a public charter school offering language immersion programs in French and Spanish. Local private schools are equally excellent and varied in educational styles. The city has several religiously affiliated private and non-sectarian schools with smaller classes and a focus on individualized learning. It also also has some specialized private schools, including Evergreen: an Acton Academy, which uses learner-driven, project-based approaches to education, and Terra Verde Discovery School, which focuses on environmental sustainability and entrepreneurialism.
