faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: Residents are in good health thanks to providers like Norman Regional Health System, which has two campuses in the city. In addition, Norman Regional Hospital is rated as a high performer in five adult procedures and conditions.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: There are free concerts in Legacy Park in the spring and Lions Park in the summer. Lions Park also has free movies in the warmer months. In September, the Cleveland County Fair is a great way to wind down the summer.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: Check out the massive dinosaur skeletons at Sam Noble Oklahoma Museum of Natural History or get immersed in meteorology at the National Weather Museum and Science Center. Also, check out HeyDay Norman, which has bowling, laser tag, an arcade, mini golf and a ropes course.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: Locally owned boutiques line Norman’s Main Street neighborhood. Green Feather Book Company is the city's oldest bookstore and hosts various events, like book clubs and poetry nights.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: See a show at the iconic historic Sooner Theatre. Or enjoy a concert at The Depot, a former train depot that now hosts rotating gallery exhibits, live music series and poetry readings.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: Interesting cocktails, fresh food, weekday brunch options and a gorgeous space make Scratch Kitchen a place you’ll return to again and again.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: Gray Owl is a beloved local spot and a worker-cooperative coffee shop in downtown Norman. This cozy coffee shop serves specialty coffee, loose-leaf tea and baked goods.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: Cocktails at The Winston start at $4 at 3 p.m. and go up $1 for the next two hours. Beer drinkers will like the $2.25 drafts at The Library Bar and Grill. Plus, college students can say they were at “The Library” if their parents ask about any questionable transactions on the bank statement!

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Norman, OK?
faqs.a: There are several major colleges and universities in the area, but the University of Oklahoma is the most notable.

OK-tulsa.description: Tulsa is an unexpected haven for artists and outdoor enthusiasts. The Tulsa Arts District and world-class museums add to the city's creative energy, and residents can appreciate the area's affordable cost of living, A+ schools and plenty of parks to play in.

sections.location: Tulsa is a dynamic city in northeastern Oklahoma, about 100 miles northeast of Oklahoma City, accessible via Interstate 44 and U.S. Route 412. Tulsa International Airport is just northeast, offering flights to Dallas and Chicago. Nearby major cities include: Oklahoma City, OK, sits 100 miles southwest, home to the Oklahoma City National Memorial, Bricktown and OKC Thunder. Fayetteville, AR, is just 115 miles east, where you can explore the Clinton House Museum, Botanical Garden of the Ozarks and the University of Arkansas. Kansas City, MO, is 250 miles northeast, offering the National WWI Museum, savory BBQ at Gates and Chiefs games at Arrowhead Stadium.
sections.economy: The city has a lot of talent and, as a result, a diverse economy with a strong foundation in a variety of industries, including health care, manufacturing and retail. Saint Francis Health System, Hillcrest Healthcare System and Tulsa Public Schools are major employers in Tulsa. Williams Companies and ONEOK, Fortune 500 Companies, and QuikTrip, a convenience store and gas station chain, are all headquartered here. American Airlines also has a maintenance base in town. Remote workers looking to move should check out Tulsa Remote, a relocation program offering a $10,000 grant to help talent make Tulsa their new headquarters and home. The cost of living in Tulsa is lower than the national average. The median home value in Tulsa is $213,956, and the average household income is $94,117. Oklahoma residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.517% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Tulsa, OK? There are several top-notch health care providers here. St. Francis Hospital – Tulsa is generally considered the area’s best hospital and has earned a high-performance rating in 10 different procedures and conditions. Other excellent health care options include Ascension St. John Medical Center and the Children’s Hospital at Saint Francis.
sections.thingsToDo: The city is a vibrant hub for arts and culture. The Philbrook Museum of Art, set in a gorgeous Italian Renaissance-style villa, is a local treasure. With an impressive art collection, 25 acres of gardens and a packed events calendar, visitors can enjoy everything from children’s storytimes and meditations to wine-and-hip-hop nights. Other top museums to explore include Greenwood Rising, which honors the legacy of Black Wall Street, and the Tulsa Air and Space Museum, where you can learn about the history of flight from a theater inside an airplane.For music lovers, the iconic Cain’s Ballroom is the place to be for live performances. You can also immerse yourself in the life of folk singer Woody Guthrie at the Woody Guthrie Center, where you’ll get a glimpse of his lyric journal or visit the Bob Dylan Center for unprecedented access to the voice of a generation. Soon, the Oklahoma Museum of Popular Culture will tell the story of Oklahoma’s creatives. What is something you can do for free in Tulsa, OK? View Jazz-age treasures at the free Tulsa Art Deco Mini-Museum and pick up a complimentary Deco District walking tour map for a self-guided tour of Tulsa’s marvelous downtown architecture. (Curators are building a bigger museum, but the sneak peek of the collection is free for now). What are some things to do in Tulsa, OK, with kids? Tulsa families are regulars at the Gathering Place (one of the country’s best parks!), but more weekend fun can be found at spots like the Tulsa Zoo and the Discovery Lab, an interactive science museum. Where do locals shop in Tulsa, OK? Utica Square is an outdoor mall with 60 stores, including Saks Fifth Avenue, Anthropologie and Amber Marie & Co., a lifestyle boutique. On Saturdays, stroll the stalls of the Tulsa Farmers’ Market, one of the best in the state. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Tulsa, OK? Head to the Tulsa Arts District, where you can catch live music and comedy shows at Soundpony, a beloved dive bar, and, a few doors down, enjoy a show at the iconic Cain’s Ballroom, which has been nicknamed “Carnegie Hall of Western Swing.” Southern Pride: A Guide to Tulsa, OK’s LGBTQ SceneTulsa: A City for Creators, Dreamers and DoersHow Tulsa’s Black Wall Street Gallery Is Confronting Decades of Racism and Charting a Path Forward
sections.outdoors: Parkgoers have lots of ground to cover in Tulsa — there are 135 parks and trails in town! The Gathering Place has gotten a lot of national attention, and for good reason. Opened in 2018 on 100 acres of land near the Arkansas River, the Gathering Place has everything you could imagine in a good park: trails, gardens, an outdoor concert arena and ONEOK Boathouse to launch kayaks. But the main attraction is their covetable, sprawling playground. The Chapman Adventure Playground inside the Gathering Place is a whopping 5 acres of playspace for children to run, scream and, let’s face it, get covered in dirt, water and sand (all the best things in the entire world for a child). Learn about plants at the Trust Sky Garden, soar on swings 56 feet above Peggy’s Pond, rent a kayak, listen to a concert — the options are limitless. For sports enthusiasts, LaFortune Park offers tennis, croquet, golf and more. Turkey Mountain's 45 miles of dirt trails are calling your name if you’re into trail running, hiking or mountain biking. This Midwestern City Is Home to the Coolest Park in America
sections.restaurants: Local residents on the hunt for their favorite steakhouse can try out contenders like Bull in the Alley, The Hemingway, Spudder and Mahogany Prime Steakhouse. But Tulsa offers more than just mouthwatering rib-eyes. Dive into the flavors of Mexico City at Noche, savor Native American dishes at Nātv in nearby Broken Arrow or enjoy grilling at your table at Mr. Kim’s Asian-inspired steakhouse. For a group outing, head to Mother Road Market, where everyone can find something to love — from Brazilian dishes at Doctor Kustom to nostalgic burgers at Howdy Burger and Nashville hot chicken at Chicken and the Wolf. Check out the Takeover Cafe Residency, where up-and-coming food entrepreneurs host pop-ups and win over new fans. What is one of the best places to eat in Tulsa, OK? Maple Ridge Grocer is a stylish all-day spot serving breakfast sandwiches, burgers, vegetarian tacos and creative cocktails and mocktails. What is the best coffee shop in Tulsa, OK? Chimera is known for its specialty coffees that are roasted in-house, and its menu has a variety of vegan and gluten-free options. Where is the best happy hour in Tulsa, OK? Bird And The Bottle is home to the seemingly inflation-proof $4 martini and really tasty and crispy chicken wings (try the chipotle dry rub with Gorgonzola).
sections.education: The city offers a variety of educational options for young families, including a mix of public, private and charter schools. Tulsa Public Schools is the largest public district in the area, with more than 33,000 students enrolled, serving most of the city’s students. Union Public Schools is another large public school district serving parts of Tulsa. The area has several private school options, from religiously affiliated institutions to college preparatory and non-sectarian schools. KIPP Academy has both a middle and high school option for residents to choose from, too. OR-eugene.description: Eugene is a haven for outdoor enthusiasts, surrounded by lush forests and the Willamette River. Its stunning natural beauty, laid-back atmosphere and strong sense of community contribute to the city's high quality of life.

sections.location: Eugene is a vibrant, nature-loving city nestled in the lush Willamette Valley, roughly 110 miles south of Portland, perfectly positioned along Interstate 5 for easy road trips. Eugene Airport sits just 5 miles northwest of downtown, a small but bustling hub offering flights to major West Coast cities like Seattle and San Francisco. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Portland is 110 miles north; the “City of Roses” tempts with the Portland Art Museum, a legendary food truck scene and shopping on Hawthorne Boulevard. Salem sits 65 miles north; Oregon’s capital charms with the historic Oregon State Capitol building, Riverfront Park and family-friendly museums. Bend is roughly 130 miles east, a paradise for outdoor enthusiasts.
sections.economy: Eugene has a robust economy comprising several industries, including manufacturing, technology and wood products. Lumber is the largest industry in the area, where several manufacturers produce lumber and wood products. Additionally, the area is the nation’s leading producer of softwood lumber and plywood products. Some of the large employers in the area include PeaceHealth Medical Group, the University of Oregon and Burley Design. The cost of living in Eugene is higher than the national average. The median home value in Eugene is $455,903, and the average household income is $98,945. Oregon residents pay a state income tax; however, the state doesn’t have a sales tax, so local residents can expect a 0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Eugene, OR? Local residents are in good health thanks to access to various providers, including PeaceHealth Medical Group, the Oregon Medical Group and PeaceHealth’s Sacred Heart Medical Center at the University District.
sections.thingsToDo: The best way to experience Eugene is to immerse yourself in the community and explore the many things to do across the city. Downtown Eugene buzzes with activity, featuring the historic 5th Street Public Market, Kesey Square’s lively atmosphere and the popular Saturday Market, a haven for handmade crafts and local food. The arts are big here, with places like the Jordan Schnitzer Museum and many performing arts centers. The University of Oregon provides ample opportunities to have fun, too. The campus is home to Hayward Field and the Museum of Natural and Cultural History. Residents can learn about local history at the Lane County Historical Museum. The Owen Rose Garden offers a peaceful escape with thousands of rose varieties and historic cherry trees. The city has events all year, from festivals to sports, that bring in people from everywhere. What is something you can do for free in Eugene, OR? A fantastic free activity in Eugene is spending a few hours exploring Hendricks Park. This 80-acre park is nestled in the city and is a scenic spot renowned for its rhododendron garden, bursting with more than 6,000 varieties of spring blooms and an extensive network of trails. The park also provides breathtaking views of the Willamette Valley, which makes it an excellent place for a leisurely stroll among 200-year-old Douglas firs. What are some things to do in Eugene, OR, with kids? Aspiring pilots adore spending time at the Oregon Air and Space Museum, where a collection of vintage airplanes and aviation memorabilia is displayed. Where do locals shop in Eugene, OR? Smith Family Bookstore is one of the oldest independent bookstores in Oregon. With six rooms spread over two floors, literature lovers can easily spend a day getting lost in the stacks. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Eugene, OR? The Hult Center downtown is considered a cultural hub that hosts many performances, including concerts, Broadway shows, ballets and symphony performances. Music aficionados are particularly fond of its Silva Concert Hall and the Soreng Theater, both praised for their excellent acoustics and intimate atmospheres.
sections.outdoors: Get out and about in Eugene. This Oregon city is an outdoor playground with amazing nature all around. Geographically, the city rests on mostly flat terrain with gentle, rolling hills cradled by the serene Willamette River, which invites kayakers, anglers and picnickers to its banks. Surrounded by dense forests and sprawling parks like Alton Baker, nature is always within reach. The city is a gateway to the Willamette Valley’s scenic beauty, with options ranging from hiking Spencer Butte for panoramic views to exploring the 12-mile Ridgeline Trail and the basalt columns at Skinner Butte Park. Cyclists love the Covered Bridge Bikeway, while water lovers can kayak or paddleboard at Alton Baker Park and explore the McKenzie River. Nature fans can see birds of prey at the Raptor Center or walk through Hendricks Park. In winter, Willamette Pass provides ample opportunities for skiing and snowboarding. Day trips offer more adventures, including exploring the Oregon Coast’s craggy shorelines, hiking to Sahalie and Koosah Falls, soaking in Belknap Hot Springs, and discovering the scenic Old McKenzie Highway with its Proxy Falls and Dee Wright Observatory views.
sections.restaurants: Eat your way through Eugene. This Oregon spot is all about local farms and craft drinks, and its location helps chefs make those farm-to-table fantasies come true. Being close to the Willamette Valley means great wineries, like J. Scott Cellars, are easy to find. The beer scene is also big, with breweries like Oakshire Brewing being community spots. The city embraces farm-to-table dining, showcasing fresh, local ingredients in restaurants like Agate Alley Bistro, which blends Latin and Asian flavors. Beyond the breweries and wineries, Eugene’s culinary scene features a mix of casual eateries, international cuisine and food carts, offering everything from hearty breakfasts at Off the Waffle to tacos at Tacovore. The 5th Street Public Market and local grocers like The Kiva provide access to artisanal goods and fresh produce, reflecting the city’s commitment to sustainability and local sourcing. Eugene has a mix of everything from brewpubs and upscale restaurants, so there’s something for everyone. What is one of the best places to eat in Eugene, OR? The culinary pros at Rye source ingredients from local farms and purveyors to produce a menu of elevated, perfect-for-sharing starters, salads and mains. What is the best coffee shop in Eugene, OR? Order a sea salt–zinged caramel latte or a decadent affogato (a double espresso poured over vanilla bean ice cream) at Meraki Coffee & Tea. Where is the best happy hour in Eugene, OR? Meet up for $4 happy hour bowling with friends at Lane 25.
sections.education: Eugene is a college town through and through, which is further represented by the endless educational institutions available to residents. Bethel School District and Eugene School District both serve the city. Eugene Schools emphasize diversity, equity and inclusion by providing student resources, including library services and secondary education programs. There are also excellent private schools here, including the Oak Hill School, a stunning school set on a 72-acre campus, and The Village School, a public charter school inspired by Waldorf principles. OR-gresham.description: Right outside of Portland, Gresham is a suburban dream for outdoor enthusiasts. This Oregon city is near the Columbia River Gorge, giving residents access to stunning natural beauty and plenty of trails and parks. Gresham’s low cost of living and friendly neighborhoods make it a welcoming place to call home.

sections.location: Gresham is a welcoming city just east of Portland, along U.S. Route 26, and part of the Portland metro area. Portland International Airport is about 15 miles west, making flights easily accessible. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Portland, OR, is just 15 miles west, where you can visit Powell’s City of Books or the Portland Japanese Garden. Vancouver, WA, sits 20 miles northwest, offering Fort Vancouver National Historic Site and the Pearson Air Museum. Salem, OR, is about 55 miles south, home to the Oregon State Capitol and the Willamette Heritage Center.
sections.economy: The city's great location near Portland — Oregon’s most populous city — sets businesses up for success. In recent years, the city has actively been working to attract and retain talent, and that work is paying off. The three largest industries here are health care, manufacturing and retail. Some of the largest employers include Adventist Health Portland, Gresham-Barlow School District and the City of Gresham. Costco also has a significant presence in the city. Local efforts to promote economic development in recent years and its relatively affordable housing market contribute to the city’s economic stability and potential future growth. The cost of living in Gresham is higher than the national average. The median home value in Gresham is $448,551, and the average household income is $99,770. Oregon residents pay a state income tax; however, the state doesn’t have a sales tax, so local residents can expect a 0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Gresham, OR? Adventist Health Portland is a major health care provider in the area, earning a high-performance rating in five different procedures and conditions. Residents can also take advantage of the wealth of health offered in nearby Portland.
sections.thingsToDo: Catch a space exploration show at Mount Hood Community College’s Planetarium Sky Theatre, learn about the area’s history at the Gresham History Museum, which is housed in a beautiful brick Tudor Revival building, and see artifacts from the city's early pioneer days at the Zimmerman House. Portland’s proximity opens up all kinds of fun weekend itineraries, too, like a visit to Rose City Book Pub, where you can enjoy a beer and books, and a visit to the International Rose Test Garden, where, from May to October, 610 different varieties of roses bloom. The city is also centrally located near some of the Pacific Northwest’s gems. For a weekend day trip, head to the Oregon Coast: Cannon Beach, which is often named one of the most beautiful beaches in the United States, is reachable in under two hours. What is something you can do for free in Gresham, OR? Stroll around the Gresham Japanese Garden, a peaceful spot with plants native to Oregon and Japan. What are some things to do in Gresham, OR, with kids? Go skating at the Main City Skate Park or play futsal (a sport that’s like soccer, but played on a court instead of a field) at a spot like Vance Park. Families can play on glow-in-the-dark lanes at Mount Hood Lanes during “Cosmic Bowling” nights. In warmer months, splash around at the Children’s Fountain, which has separate splash pad areas for big and little kids. Where do locals shop in Gresham, OR? Poppy & Dale Vintage is a beautifully curated vintage shop with one-of-a-kind finds that will make your home look like it’s from the pages of a design magazine. The sweet store is named after the owner’s grandfathers. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Gresham, OR? Music Mondays is a free concert series at the Arts Plaza in July and August. Local vendors sell beer, wine, hot dogs, tacos, churros, ice cream and more.
sections.outdoors: The great outdoors is synonymous with the Pacific Northwest, and the city lives up to PNW’s reputation. Its park system comprises more than 300 acres of parkland, 840 acres of natural areas and miles of trails. There are dozens of parks throughout town, too! Hogan Butte Nature Park and Main City Park are local favorites. Witness the beauty of Multnomah Falls, a magical 620-foot waterfall, and for added adventure, lace up your hiking boots and huff it up to the top of the falls. You’ll gain 600 feet of elevation (which is like climbing 60 flights of stairs but on steep terrain). A more moderate 2.4-mile hike on the Gresham Butte Saddle Trail delivers stellar views of Mount Hood. In warmer months, head 15 minutes out of town to Oxbow Regional Park on the Sandy River Gorge and go for a float in a tube or get out on the water in a kayak.
sections.restaurants: There are no surprises here — Portland’s coffee culture spills over into Gresham, and you’ll have no problem finding a favorite coffee shop for remote work or meeting up with a friend for a cup of joe. Sample the local food scene in the city's historic downtown, where you can choose between a couple of dozen bagel varieties and enjoy jazz music at the aptly named Jazzy Bagels, savor Lebanese and Mediterranean dishes at Nicholas Restaurant or grab a burger and fries at The Local Cow. In nearby Portland, visiting the famed food carts at Cartopia is a must-do. What is one of the best places to eat in Gresham, OR? Devour the famous birria tacos at Birrias Tamazula. The Mexican spot also has birria ramen and tasty ceviche dishes. What is the best coffee shop in Gresham, OR? With comfy seating, community tables and a kid’s area, Legacy Coffee is a beloved third place in the downtown area, caffeinating locals. Where is the best place for happy hour in Gresham, OR? MadCow Brewing Company has weekly specials that can’t be missed. We recommend partaking in Wednesday’s Pizza and a Pint deal for $15.
sections.education: Local schools are diverse in both their offerings and student populations. The Gresham-Barlow School District, where more than 11,000 students are enrolled, is the primary public school district in the area. The district offers a range of programs and schools and focuses on providing multiple pathways for students to succeed, including alternative and virtual learning options. A couple of local private schools provide more individualized curriculum and attention; these schools range from religiously affiliated schools to non-sectarian and college preparatory options. Charter schools are also a popular option for area students. OR-salem.description: Oregon's capital city, Salem, is a laid-back community where residents can enjoy a high quality of life in the heart of the Willamette Valley. It's easy to get around here and spend time enjoying the city's natural resources and exploring the variety of unique and affordable neighborhoods.

sections.location: Salem is the state capital, nestled in the Willamette Valley about 50 miles south of Portland and accessible via Interstate 5. Salem Municipal Airport offers local flights via small planes, but Portland International Airport is 50 miles north, offering more flights. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Portland is 50 miles north; the “City of Roses” tempts with the Portland Art Museum, a legendary food truck scene and shopping on Hawthorne Boulevard. Eugene sits 65 miles south, home to the University of Oregon, the Jordan Schnitzer Museum of Art and the Willamette River Greenway. Corvallis is roughly 30 miles south, boasting Oregon State University, Avery Park and the Corvallis Museum.
sections.economy: Salem is the capital of Oregon, making it an economic epicenter. The three largest industries are government, health care and retail trade, which should be no surprise. Businesses here find a welcoming and supportive economy and some of the city’s largest employers are the State of Oregon, Salem Health and the City of Salem. The cost of living in Salem is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Salem is $391,619, and the average household income is $102,168. Oregon residents pay a state income tax; however, the state doesn’t have a sales tax, so local residents can expect a 0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Salem, OR? Salem Hospital is one of the best hospitals in Oregon and is a high performer in cancer-related specialties. Other providers include Salem Health West Valley, Oregon State Hospital and Kaiser Permanente.
sections.thingsToDo: Salem is so much fun. The city’s downtown area features Riverfront Park, home to the hand-carved Riverfront Carousel and the Gilbert House Children’s Museum, providing interactive fun for kids. History enthusiasts can delve into the city’s past at the Willamette Heritage Center, showcasing pioneer life and the region’s industrial history. Art lovers can explore the city’s extensive public art collection and visit the Jordan Schnitzer Museum of Art at Willamette University. Salem also hosts numerous year-round festivals, including the World Beat Festival and the Salem Art Fair & Festival, celebrating diverse cultures and artistic expressions. Families can enjoy the A.C. Gilbert’s Discovery Village and the Kroc Center’s recreational facilities, while the Salem Saturday Market offers local produce and artisanal goods. The city’s blend of museums, parks and community events ensures a variety of engaging activities for residents and visitors alike. What is something you can do for free in Salem, OR? Pack a picnic to enjoy at Riverfront Park along the Willamette River. The park is like the community’s living room and hosts all kinds of events, from holiday tree lightings to Fourth of July celebrations and summer movies in the park. What are some things to do in Salem, OR, with kids? Take a $2 ride on Salem’s Riverfront Carousel. In addition to the majestic horses, whimsical characters include a duck, a dragon and a llama. Where do locals shop in Salem, OR? Dine and shop downtown at the recently renovated Reed Opera House. The circa 1870 brick building is full of cute shops and restaurants, from plant specialists and hat stores to sushi and an Italian bakery focused on cannoli. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Salem, OR? Salem is home to several live and performing arts venues, including the Pentacle Theatre for community theater and the Elsinore, which hosts a variety of comedy and musical acts. Salem Cinema is a great date night (or solo date) spot. The theater shows independent and foreign films.
sections.outdoors: Oregon offers many outdoor amenities, and Salem caters to leisurely explorers and adventurous individuals. Minto-Brown Island Park, with its expansive 1,200 acres and 29 miles of trails, provides ample space for walking, biking and jogging, as well as an off-leash dog area and wildlife viewing opportunities. For a more immersive nature experience, Silver Falls State Park features the iconic Trail of Ten Falls, a 7.6-mile hike showcasing ten stunning waterfalls, and offers additional trails for hiking, horseback riding and biking. Beyond the parks, residents can explore the vineyards of Eola Hills Wine Cellars through “wiking,” a unique blend of wine tasting and hiking. The region’s natural beauty extends to its numerous parks, offering diverse landscapes and recreational opportunities. For family fun, the Enchanted Forest theme park provides a whimsical escape with rides and attractions.
sections.restaurants: Salem’s food scene celebrates the region’s agricultural heritage, and it benefits from its location in the heart of the Willamette Valley, providing access to fresh, locally sourced ingredients featured in its farm-to-table restaurants and bustling farmers markets like the Salem Saturday Market. Local favorites like Word of Mouth Bistro showcase homespun comfort food, while the Yard Food Park offers a vibrant array of food trucks catering to diverse tastes with everything from Thai to barbecue. The city's proximity to world-class wineries, particularly those producing Pinot Noir, enhances its culinary appeal, with destinations like Bethel Heights Vineyard offering scenic tasting experiences. The city’s craft beverage scene is also thriving, with local breweries and cideries adding to the mix. International flavors abound, with restaurants serving authentic Thai, Mexican and Italian cuisine, and dessert options like Slick Licks offering refreshing treats made with natural ingredients. Salem’s dining landscape balances casual, family-friendly eateries with more refined dining experiences, creating a welcoming and evolving food destination. What is one of the best places to eat in Salem, OR? Bo & Vine serves smash burgers, a variety of fry piles (think nachos, but with french fries), and — get this — a wine shake with pinot gris, strawberry puree and Tillamook vanilla ice cream, with a crown of whipped cream and chocolate drizzle. What is the best coffee shop in Salem, OR? Archive Coffee & Bar is a coffee shop by day and a craft cocktail bar by night. The obvious order is an espresso martini made with house-made coffee liqueur and Archive’s espresso. But other tasty drinks are on the menu, like an old fashioned with Japanese whisky and half a dozen mocktails. Where is the best happy hour in Salem, OR? Stop by Coin Jam and enjoy a cocktail while playing vintage arcade games.
sections.education: Students in Salem get a smart start via the city’s numerous educational institutions. Salem-Keizer Public Schools (SKPS) serve almost 40,000 students in Salem and nearby Keizer and is the second-largest school district in the state. The district offers several programs, including Migrant Education, English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) and Dual Language programs, all of which help support its diverse student population. There are a number of private schools here, including the Heritage School, which offers progressive education for grades 1-8 and emphasizes learning by doing and a thematic curriculum tailored to each PA-bethlehem.description: Bethlehem, located in the picturesque Lehigh Valley, is a vibrant city that blends history and modernity. From an incredible arts scene, ample outdoor activities and spaces, a welcoming community and a low cost of living, it's easy to see the magic of Christmas City.

sections.location: Bethlehem is a picturesque city tucked into the rolling Lehigh Valley, about 60 miles north of Philadelphia, where Interstate 78 and U.S. Route 22 crisscross, linking it seamlessly to the region. The nearest airport, Lehigh Valley International Airport, is a quick 5 miles northeast, a midsize hub with flights to spots like Charlotte and Chicago. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Allentown, PA, is just 5 miles west; catch a Lehigh Valley Phantoms hockey game, browse the Allentown Art Museum or enjoy a concert at the PPL Center. Philadelphia, PA, sits 60 miles south, tempting with the Liberty Bell, Independence Hall and the iconic Philly cheesesteak. New York City is 90 miles east; from Broadway shows to Central Park picnics and the Statue of Liberty, the Big Apple delivers endless excitement.
sections.economy: Bethlehem is small but mighty, making it a draw for professionals to plant roots. Key industries in the city include health care, manufacturing and educational services. St. Luke’s University Health Network, Lehigh University and Bethlehem Area School District are among the largest employers. And to sweeten the deal? Just Born Quality Confections, the candy manufacturer known for Peeps marshmallows, is headquartered here. Living a stone’s throw from major players in the country’s economy, such as New York City and Philadelphia, doesn’t hurt either. The cost of living in Bethlehem is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Bethlehem is $249,745, and the average household income is $99,964. Pennsylvania residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Bethlehem, PA? Thanks to top hospitals like St. Luke’s University Hospital – Bethlehem Campus, Good Shepherd Specialty Hospital and Lehigh Valley Health Network’s Highland Avenue campus, Bethlehem residents can stay in good health. St. Luke’s is a nonprofit health care system whose flagship hospital is in the city. It offers a wide range of medical services and is regionally ranked as one of the best hospitals in Northeastern Pennsylvania.
sections.thingsToDo: Old meets new in Bethlehem. You can check out its Moravian history at places like the Moravian Church Settlements, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and the Moravian Museum. Or discover its industrial past at the National Museum of Industrial History and the Colonial Industrial Quarter. With its preserved 18th-century architecture, Downtown Bethlehem is an excellent spot for enjoying shops and galleries, particularly on First Friday events on the South Side. Residents also have access to a lot of arts and entertainment. Places like SteelStacks, the Banana Factory and the Zoellner Arts Center host concerts, festivals and shows. Annual events like Musikfest and Christkindlmarkt draw crowds from far and wide, while the Crayola Experience offers family-friendly fun. What is something you can do for free in Bethlehem, PA? Enjoy sunny days and get active by visiting the Bethlehem Skateplaza. The plaza is a 23,000-square-foot park for bikers and skaters. What are some things to do in Bethlehem, PA, with kids? Learn about America’s industrial machines and early innovators at the National Museum of Industrial History. More things to do at SteelStacks include toddler story times and a family movie series. Where do locals shop in Bethlehem, PA? Bethlehem’s Main Street is a hub for local shops and boutiques. You can find unique items, gifts and locally crafted goods. Be sure to tuck into Moravian Book Shop, the oldest continuously operating bookstore in the world, founded in 1745. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Bethlehem, PA? SteelStacks, a campus formerly owned by Bethlehem Steel, now hosts more than 1,000 concerts a year and eight annual festivals. While they often put on paid events, you can also attend any of their 750 free performances throughout the year.
sections.outdoors: Bethlehem residents have bunches of fun when it comes to exploring the great outdoors. Mixing city trails with nature, it’s easy to experience the outdoors in a city setting. Geographically, the city stretches across gentle hills with pockets of flat land. The Lehigh River carves its way nearby, a playground for kayakers, fishermen and anyone craving a riverside walk under the shade of towering trees. Ride a bike or walk along the South Bethlehem Greenway and Sand Island Park, which runs alongside the Lehigh River. For those seeking more adventurous pursuits, kayaking on the Lehigh River and hiking sections of the Appalachian Trail are easily accessible just outside the city. Monocacy Park and Illick’s Mill & Monocacy Creek Park provide peaceful settings for nature walks and fishing. The cooler temperatures in the winter don’t deter residents, and the city has found ways to offer outdoor activities all year long. Dutch Springs has a water playground and scuba diving in a former quarry during the summer. Or explore the Lost River Caverns nearby. In winter, SteelStacks turns into an ice skating rink, offering residents seasonal outdoor fun.
sections.restaurants: Bite into Bethlehem’s foodie scene, which is growing by the day. Historic establishments like The 1758 Sun Inn, where old meets new, offer contemporary food in a historic building. Or tantalize your taste buds with Greek food at Yianni’s Taverna. Local wineries like Black River Farms offer tastings, or sample craft drinks at Fegley’s Bethlehem Brew Works and Colony Meadery. Local residents can enjoy farm-to-table fare at Bolete. Looking for something hyper-local? The Bethlehem Farmers’ Market provides fresh, local produce and artisan foods. What is one of the best places to eat in Bethlehem, PA? Share Spanish-style tapas and pitchers of raspberry mint or blood orange berry sangria at Tapas on Main. What is the best coffee shop in Bethlehem, PA? Bitty & Beau’s is a coffee shop run by people with disabilities. It is a “human rights movement disguised as a coffee shop.” The Bethlehem shop is one of 23 nationwide. Where is the best happy hour in Bethlehem, PA? During the week, enjoy the happy hour at Zest Bar & Grill, where they serve discounted classic cocktails, local drafts and snacks. Be sure to make a reservation for Sunday brunch on the rooftop, too.
sections.education: Brainiacs can rejoice in Bethlehem. The Bethlehem Area School District (BASD) serves nearly 13,000 students and offers a range of programs and school types, from traditional K-12 classrooms to magnet schools and charter schools. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable student-to-teacher ratio. Residents also have their pick of private schools, including Moravian Academy, a historic independent school with a Moravian heritage. PA-pittsburgh.description: Steel City’s got it. Renowned universities, world-class health care and a vibrant arts and culture scene all contribute to Pittsburgh’s reputation as a historical city with an eye for innovation. From pro sports to plenty of parks, Pittsburgh is welcoming for all.

sections.location: Pittsburgh is a vibrant city in the western part of the state, right where the Allegheny, Monongahela and Ohio rivers meet, giving it riverfront charm. It’s well-connected by major highways like Interstate 376 and Interstate 79, making it easy to navigate. The nearest airport, Pittsburgh International Airport, is just 20 miles west of downtown, perfect for jetting off on your next adventure. When you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some other major cities: Cleveland, OH, is just 135 miles northwest, where you can rock out at the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame or admire masterpieces at the Cleveland Museum of Art. Philadelphia sits 305 miles east, tempting you with the Liberty Bell, Independence Hall and the iconic Philly cheesesteak. Washington, D.C., is about 245 miles southeast, packed with Smithsonian museums, the U.S. Capitol and the National Mall’s monuments.
sections.economy: The local economy has transitioned from an industrial past to a forward-thinking future. The largest economic sectors are health care, education and professional and business services, and the city serves as a significant economic center for the region. Major employers include The University of Pittsburgh Medical Center (UPMC), the University of Pittsburgh and Allegheny Health Network. Several notable companies are headquartered here, including PNC Financial Services, PPG Industries and H.J. Heinz Company. The cost of living in Pittsburgh is slightly lower than the national average. The median home value in Pittsburgh is $206,135, and the average household income is $100,657. Pennsylvania residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Pittsburgh, PA? The city has several highly regarded hospitals, including UPMC Presbyterian Shadyside, Allegheny General Hospital and UPMC Children's Hospital of Pittsburgh. Quiz: Which Pittsburgh Neighborhood is Right for You?From Steel City to STEM City: Why Smart Millennials Are Making Pittsburgh Home
sections.thingsToDo: Pittsburgh is a dynamic city offering a range of experiences, from its rich history and vibrant arts scene to its passionate sports culture. History enthusiasts can explore landmarks like the Fort Pitt Block House and Meadowcroft Rockshelter & Historic Village while learning about the city’s role in the French and Indian War and the lives of its earliest inhabitants. Museums like the Heinz History Center and the Andy Warhol Museum can’t be missed, while the Duquesne Incline offers panoramic views of the city’s iconic rivers and numerous bridges. The local commitment to the arts is evident in its numerous museums, galleries and performance venues, including The Frick Pittsburgh and the Mattress Factory, and its lively music scene. Sports fans can cheer on the Steelers, Pirates and Penguins, experiencing the city’s “black and gold” spirit firsthand. Beyond its cultural and sporting attractions, local residents can enjoy unique shopping experiences, with vintage and retro shops offering hidden treasures. What is something you can do for free in Pittsburgh, PA? Exploring the eclectic shops, ethnic markets and street art in the city's iconic Strip District is a fun way to pass the time. Sample local treats, browse boutiques and soak up the energetic atmosphere without spending a dime. What are some things to do with kids in Pittsburgh, PA? The Children’s Museum of Pittsburgh is a special place and even has an exhibit dedicated to promoting kindness and empathy. Where do locals shop in Pittsburgh, PA? Renowned for its historic charm and specialty shops, the Strip District offers everything from fresh produce and ethnic groceries at spots like Pennsylvania Macaroni Company and Lotus Food Company to unique finds at antique stores like Mahla & Co. Antiques. Beyond the Strip District, neighborhoods like Lawrenceville and Shadyside are popular among locals for boutique shopping. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Pittsburgh, PA? The historic Benedum Center for the Performing Arts in the downtown Cultural District is a beautifully restored theater with plenty of performances throughout the year, including Broadway shows, concerts, ballets and operas. Many consider it a cornerstone of the local arts and culture scene.
sections.outdoors: The area offers a surprising array of outdoor activities. The city’s extensive park system, featuring gems like Frick Park and Schenley Park, provides ample opportunities for hiking, biking and leisurely strolls. For water enthusiasts, kayaking on the Allegheny River and tiki boat tours offer unique perspectives of the city, while fishing enthusiasts can venture to nearby Ohiopyle State Park for excellent trout fishing on the Youghiogheny River and Meadow Run. The Great Allegheny Passage, a 150-mile trail, allows for extended biking adventures, and the city’s Healthy Ride bike-sharing program makes urban cycling accessible. Beyond its parks and rivers, the city boasts attractions like Kennywood Amusement Park and the Phipps Conservatory and Botanical Gardens. With 15,000 acres of public parks and green spaces within its urban core and numerous state parks in the surrounding region, Pittsburgh provides a diverse range of outdoor experiences for residents and visitors alike.
sections.restaurants: The local culinary scene is a tapestry of global flavors, spanning its 90 neighborhoods and gaining national and international attention. From upscale fine dining and farm-to-table restaurants to casual eateries, the city caters to every palate. The legacy of H.J. Heinz, who introduced tomato ketchup to America, reflects the city’s ongoing spirit of food innovation. Today, Pittsburgh has a thriving food scene. The city’s craft brewery scene is equally dynamic, with numerous breweries popping up across the downtown area. The Pittsburgh Brewery Trail features over 30 spots, offering various brews, from IPAs and stouts to experimental varieties. What is one of the best places to eat in Pittsburgh, PA? Tucked away in a restored former church, Church Brew Works is a brewery and restaurant on Liberty Avenue that serves everything from house-brewed ales and pizza to pierogis and brats, making it one of the hottest spots for dinner in the city. What is the best coffee shop in Pittsburgh, PA? Everyone loves Commonplace Coffee, and with multiple locations, it’s easier than ever to settle in with a good brew and beloved novel for an afternoon pick-me-up, no matter what part of town you’re in. Where is the best place for happy hour in Pittsburgh, PA? Lorelei is a popular wine and cocktail bar, beer hall and bottle shop all in one. The spot serves up wood-fired pizza and is located in the East Liberty neighborhood. Understanding Pittsburgh History Through 4 Unique (and Delicious) Festivals
sections.education: There are more than 40 school districts in Pennsylvania’s Allegheny County, and Pittsburgh Public Schools is the largest of those districts, serving more than 20,000 students in the city. The Pittsburgh Public Schools district offers a range of programs and schools outside of the traditional K-12 setting, including a number of charter and magnet schools specializing in different themes like STEM, arts and language immersion. There is also a strong presence of independent private schools here, including Shady Side Academy, which has several rankings in everything from the best boarding schools in the country to one of the best STEM schools in Pennsylvania. RI-warwick.description: Warwick is tucked along Narragansett Bay's shores and is a hidden gem with a coastal allure. Picturesque beaches, a friendly community and high-achieving schools add to the allure of Rhode Island's second-largest city, full of opportunities at an affordable price.

sections.location: Warwick is a picturesque city in central Rhode Island, just south of Providence. It’s situated along Interstate 95 and T.F. Green Airport, which is in town, making travel a breeze. Plus, two-thirds of New England’s population is accessible within a 75-mile radius of Warwick. When you’re set to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Providence is just 10 miles north, where you can visit the Roger Williams Park Zoo, explore the RISD Museum or catch a show at PPAC. Newport sits 25 miles south, with historic mansions, the Cliff Walk and the Newport Folk Festival. Boston, MA, is about 60 miles northeast, boasting the Freedom Trail, Boston Common and the Museum of Fine Arts.
sections.economy: The economy of Rhode Island is shaped mainly by manufacturing and service-based industries, so it is no surprise that health care, retail trade and manufacturing are among the largest sectors here. The largest employers in Warwick include Kent Hospital, Metropolitan Life Insurance and United Parcel Service (UPS), which has a facility in the area. The cost of living in Warwick is higher than the national average. The median home value in Warwick is $332,783, and the average household income is $112,764. Rhode Island residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 7.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Warwick, RI? Local residents can stay in good health at any of the providers in the Providence metropolitan area. Kent Hospital is part of the Care New England Health System and has been nationally recognized in several areas, including stroke care, joint replacement and cardiac care.
sections.thingsToDo: Welcome to Warwick, where you’ll find historic charm and modern amenities paired with coastal beauty. History enthusiasts will love the city’s rich history. They can explore landmarks like the Goddard and Aldrich mansions, the Clouds Hill Victorian House Museum and the historic Pawtuxet Village, the oldest in New England, which hosts the annual Gaspee Days Celebration. Arts abound at the Warwick Center for the Arts, featuring rotating exhibits and performances, and the GAMM Theatre, while aviation buffs can visit the Rhode Island Aviation Hall of Fame at T.F. Green Airport. For unique finds, Mary’s Paperbacks and local boutiques provide alternatives to the larger Warwick Mall. Beyond historical and artistic attractions, the city offers plenty of family-friendly fun, including trampoline parks and the classic carousel at Warwick Mall. What is something you can do for free in Warwick, RI? Warwick has played a significant role in our nation’s history. Notably, it was the site of the Gaspee Affair, one of America’s first acts of rebellion against the British government before the Revolution. Rhode Island, one of the 13 original colonies, is steeped in history. The Walking Tour of Historic Apponaug Village, with a free booklet available at City Hall from the Department of Tourism, features more than 30 historic structures along a leisurely stroll. What are some things to do with kids in Warwick, RI? Go on a story walk! The library program places an illustrated children’s book, displayed page by page, along walking paths and green spaces. Then, while enjoying the sweeping views of Greenwich Bay from Oakland Beach, stop by Iggy’s Doughboys & Chowder House for a sweet treat for the kids. They will surely love the “doughboys” — doughnuts topped with sugar, cinnamon sugar or powdered sugar. Where do locals shop in Warwick, RI? Read, trade and save at Mary’s Paperbacks. This family-owned bookstore has been recycling gently used books since 1976 and has more than 40,000 previously loved books in stock. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Warwick, RI? Venture into downtown Providence to catch a show at the Providence Performing Arts Center (PPAC). Originally built in 1928 as the Loew’s Theater, the PPAC is now a world-class facility that hosts Broadway shows, plays and concerts year-round.
sections.outdoors: Warwick’s outdoor scene is wonderful, capitalizing on its coastal location and numerous parks. Residents can enjoy picturesque beaches like Oakland Beach and Goddard Memorial State Park, perfect for swimming, picnicking and sunbathing. Goddard Park also has a range of other activities, like saltwater fishing, hiking, boating and horseback riding along nearly 20 miles of trails. Warwick City Park has hiking trails, sports fields and a beach. The city also has an extensive network of walking paths, ranging from short, scenic strolls at Barton Farm and Belmont Park to longer routes at Dawley Memorial Forest, Rocky Point Park and the Washington Secondary Bike Path. Get your toes wet by exploring the coastline and sailing or deep-sea fishing with several marinas. The area has several playgrounds for families, including the inclusive imPossible Dream Playground designed for children of all abilities.
sections.restaurants: Take a bite out of the local dining scene. Seafood lovers can indulge in fresh catches at places like Dockside Seafood Marketplace, while Italian cuisine is well-represented, and cozy, family-owned restaurants provide a welcoming environment. Beyond the diverse dining establishments, the city embraces local traditions and specialties, and new eateries are emerging, contributing to the evolving food scene. Popular spots like The Shanty and The Social House are local favorites offering a variety of cuisines. What is one of the best places to eat in Warwick, RI? Iron Works is an American restaurant housed inside of a historic foundry. This savory spot serves up coastal favorites like calamari and clam chowder but also offers other bites, like birria ramen and bacon-infused meatloaf. What is the best coffee shop in Warwick, RI? Presto Strange O Cafe is a beloved local favorite for a hearty cup of coffee — and perhaps a pastry (or two) if you plan to linger. Where is the best place for happy hour in Warwick, RI? Try one of the sours or IPAs at Proclamation Ale Company, a fun brewery with a retro arcade. There are also food trucks on the property if you get snacky.
sections.education: Rhode Island students are ready for the future. Warwick Public Schools is the primary school district here, with a student population of more than 8,000. The district offers a range of programming and school types, including traditional and specialized options. The district has a strong Career and Technical Education (CTE) program at Warwick Area Career and Technical Center, which offers students hands-on training in various fields. Private schools are excellent and offer religious, non-sectarian and college prep options. faqs.a: Try one of the sours or IPAs at Proclamation Ale Company, a fun brewery with a retro arcade. There are also food trucks on the property if you get snacky.

faqs.a: Warwick is home to the Community College of Rhode Island.

SC-charleston.description: All hail the Holy City! Charleston embraces Southern charm and is where history comes alive, from its cobblestone streets to radiant rainbow homes. Known for its unparalleled culinary scene, entrepreneurial spirit and outstanding amenities, Charleston captivates with its blend of creativity and coastal living.

sections.location: Charleston is a historic gem perched on the South Carolina coast, where the Ashley and Cooper rivers meet the Atlantic Ocean, giving it a stunning waterfront. It’s well-connected by Interstate 26 and U.S. Route 17, making exploring easy. The nearest airport, Charleston International Airport, is just 12 miles northwest of downtown, perfect for quick getaways. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Savannah, GA, is just 105 miles south, where you can stroll through the historic district or relax in Forsyth Park. Charlotte, NC, sits 210 miles northwest, offering the NASCAR Hall of Fame and the U.S. National Whitewater Center. Atlanta is about 305 miles west, packed with exciting attractions such as the Georgia Aquarium and the World of Coca-Cola.
sections.economy: At first glance, Charleston may seem like a laid-back coastal town, but the city has invested in things like infrastructure, technology and business incentives, making it an attractive destination for major employers and entrepreneurs alike. There are several major employers in the area, including the U.S. Department of Defense, Medical University of South Carolina, Boeing, Bosch and Mercedes-Benz, which account for the vast array of job opportunities in the city ranging from tourism and health care to education and engineering. The cost of living in Charleston is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Charleston is $463,644, and the average household income is $137,062. South Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and Charleston residents can expect a 9.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Charleston, SC? MUSC Health-University Medical Center is a teaching hospital affiliated with the Medical University of South Carolina and is regarded as the best hospital in Charleston. Roper Hospital is also a significant provider, nationally ranked in one specialty, a high performer in 12 procedures and conditions and regionally ranked as one of the best hospitals in the Low Country.
sections.outdoors: Charleston residents can capitalize on the city’s coastal setting and diverse range of outdoor activities. Beachgoers can enjoy the sun and surf at nearby beach towns, including Folly Beach, Isle of Palms and Kiawah, Seabrook and Sullivan’s islands. Waterfront parks like Riley Waterfront Park provide scenic spaces for picnics, walks and bikes. For those seeking adventure on the water, kayaking, paddleboarding, boating and fishing charters are readily available. Beyond the beach, Charleston has plenty of golf courses, including the Wild Dunes Harbor Course, offering seaside play with views of lagoons and marshlands. An adrenaline junkie? Check out the Charleston Adventure Forest. Take the kids to one of the many playgrounds around town. Family favorites are Hazel Parker Playground, Gadsdenboro Park and Tiedemann Park.
sections.restaurants: Charleston is a culinary hot spot, blending Southern traditions and coastal comforts. The city has an impressive array of restaurants, ranging from upscale fine dining to casual eateries. Seafood is naturally a highlight, with several restaurants offering fresh catches, including Lowcountry shrimp and grits, oysters and she-crap soup. Local favorites like Home Team BBQ showcase Southern flavors, while spots like Park & Grove and The Grocery emphasize fresh, locally sourced ingredients. Beyond its restaurants, Charleston has a delightful brewery and cocktail scene. From rooftop lounges like Élevé Lounge and craft breweries like Holy City Brewing, there are plenty of places to kick back and enjoy a drink. What is one of the best places to eat in Charleston, SC? Shrimp and grits are to Charleston what bagels are to New York City. Located on the popular King Street, The Darling Oyster Bar is one of the best spots in the city to get your grit fix, as well as many other seafood favorites. Another favorite is in the historic Elliotborough neighborhood, where you can enjoy a dock-to-table meal at Chubby Fish, where menus depend on what fishermen caught that day. What is the best coffee shop in Charleston, SC? Spending a long day working remotely from a coffee shop and want to cap your day off with a cocktail? Look no further than the downtown location of Island Provisions, where you can start your day with an americano and breakfast sandwich and end it with a glass of wine or hand-crafted cocktail. Where is the best happy hour in Charleston, SC? Happy hour is called tequila and taco hour at Pink Cactus, where you can get $7 margs and Palomas to pair with $5 tacos.
sections.education: Families have excellent educational options for finding the perfect school in Charleston. The city is primarily served by the Charleston County School District, one of the state’s largest and most highly ranked districts. For private education, Charleston offers a variety of religious, college prep and independent schools. And for residents in nearby Mount Pleasant, there are excellent options there, too. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: MUSC Health-University Medical Center is a teaching hospital affiliated with the Medical University of South Carolina and is regarded as the best hospital in Charleston. Roper Hospital is also a significant provider, nationally ranked in one specialty, a high performer in 12 procedures and conditions and regionally ranked as one of the best hospitals in the Low Country.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: After a long day, enjoy a sunset stroll across the Arthur Ravenel Jr. Bridge. The bridge has a 2.7-mile bike and pedestrian path and offers stunning views of the Charleston Harbor.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Charleston, SC, with kids?
faqs.a: Visit the Children’s Museum of the Low Country, which has an art room, a pirate ship, a garden and more.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: King Street is a bustling shopping destination with local boutiques, upscale retailers and charming shops. It’s a popular spot for anyone looking for clothing, jewelry and home goods.Or, for something a little less crowded, visit the Charleston City Market, one of the nation’s oldest city markets and home to more than 300 entrepreneurs. The market’s stalls are filled with local artisans, home goods and stops for snacks and libations.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: Many locals appreciate the diversity of performances at Charleston Music Hall, a historic venue housed in a 19th-century Gothic Revival building on John Street.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: Shrimp and grits are to Charleston what bagels are to New York City. Located on the popular King Street, The Darling Oyster Bar is one of the best spots in the city to get your grit fix, as well as many other seafood favorites. Another favorite is in the historic Elliotborough neighborhood, where you can enjoy a dock-to-table meal at Chubby Fish, where menus depend on what fishermen caught that day.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: Spending a long day working remotely from a coffee shop and want to cap your day off with a cocktail? Look no further than the downtown location of Island Provisions, where you can start your day with an americano and breakfast sandwich and end it with a glass of wine or hand-crafted cocktail.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: Happy hour is called tequila and taco hour at Pink Cactus, where you can get $7 margs and Palomas to pair with $5 tacos.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Charleston, SC?
faqs.a: Charleston has several colleges and universities, including the College of Charleston and The Citadel, a public military college.

SC-columbia.description: Southern charm meets sophistication in Columbia. This South Carolina city is known for its arts scene, educational opportunities and rich history, making it a hub of culture and education. Residents can enjoy an affordable cost of living, great job opportunities and scenic riverside parks.

sections.location: Columbia is the capital of South Carolina, nestled in the center of the state, about 115 miles northwest of Charleston, accessible via Interstates 26 and 20. Columbia Metropolitan Airport is just southwest of the city, offering flights to Atlanta and Charlotte. Nearby major cities include: Charleston, SC, sits 115 miles southeast, where you can stroll the Battery, visit Fort Sumter or explore Rainbow Row. Charlotte, NC, is just 95 miles north, home of the NASCAR Hall of Fame, U.S. National Whitewater Center and Discovery Place. Greenville, SC, is about 100 miles northwest, offering Falls Park on the Reedy, Peace Center and Greenville Zoo.
sections.economy: The city's economy is diverse and robust, with a mix of different industries fueling the economy. Major economic drivers include jobs in government, health care and education sectors, and the city’s central location within the state contributes to its overall stability. Some of the largest employers in Columbia are the State of South Carolina, the University of South Carolina, Prisma Health, Fort Jackson and BlueCross BlueShield of South Carolina. The cost of living in Columbia is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Columbia is $249,080, and the average household income is $96,118. South Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Columbia, SC? Lexington Medical Center, a high performer in nearly a dozen procedures and conditions, is one of the largest providers here. Residents can also access three different Prisma Health facilities in the area.
sections.thingsToDo: Columbia offers a choose-your-own-adventure. Love the arts? Visit the Columbia Museum of Art, which will house Keith Haring’s traveling pop art exhibit between September 2025 and February 2026. The museum also hosts live music, drag bingo and art classes. Music lovers have access to plenty of bars and theaters to see concerts, including The Senate, a concert hall that brings top-tier talent to the area. Columbia is also a bona fide theater city — there’s even the Columbia Marionette Theatre, which has a lineup of puppet shows. The Columbia Children’s Theatre puts on professional performances geared toward kids and plays featuring local youth. A big fan of the outdoors? Go for a walk or bike ride along the Columbia Canal path and get in a workout at the Riverfront Park, which has an outdoor gym. Saluda Shoals Park is a community favorite and features a dog park, a ropes course, a splash pad, disc golf and an inclusive playground. For fun with the family, visit the Riverbanks Zoo and Garden, home to 3,000 animals, plus a variety of botanical gardens and a kids’ play area called Waterfall Junction that stars everything from a treehouse to a life-size replica of T-Rex. What is something you can do for free in Columbia, SC? Take a free tour of the South Carolina State House and learn about the building’s architecture and art, including a beautiful mosaic of the state seal with 37,000 pieces of glass. What are some things to do in Columbia, SC, with kids? For a park day, head to Saluda Shoals, a 450-acre green space set on the banks of Saluda River that features a splash pad and inclusive playground. More fun is to be had at the Transmission Arcade (it’s open to kids each evening until 8 p.m.) and at the South Carolina State Museum, which has a Telescope Gallery to gaze up at the stars. Sign up for a kids’ mindful yoga class at FIT Columbia to get the wiggles out. Where do locals shop in Columbia, SC? Modeled after European street markets, the Soda Street Market takes to Main Street each Saturday. It’s a producers-only market, which means vendors create or grow everything being sold. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Columbia, SC? Theatergoers love Trustus Theatre, which has been a staple in Columbia for the past four decades and features a more intimate “Trustus Side Door Theatre” that seats 50 in addition to its main stage.
sections.outdoors: The city is home to some amazing outdoor activities thanks to a temperate climate. The city offers a wide variety of neighborhood, regional and specialty parks that are fit for all ages, stages and abilities. Learn how to play disc golf on a beginner-friendly course at Seven Oaks Park or make a tee time nearby at Cobblestone Park Golf Club, a regular on best golf course lists. Of course, there is water everywhere here! Rafting on the Saluda River, canoeing down the Congaree and walking along the water at Riverfront Park are just a few favorites. Lake Murray is nearby, offering boating, fishing, swimming and other water sports, too.Back on land and just out of town, you’ll feel a world away at Sesquicentennial State Park, which locals call “Sesqui.” Here, you can pitch a tent and camp, go for a hike or paddle on the 30-acre lake. The park also has a splash pad for kids.
sections.restaurants: From Southern staples to international restaurants, the dining scene has something for every palate. You’ll find a good lineup of restaurants in culinary hot spots like The Vista, which has dozens of restaurants and bars spread out across several blocks. Devine Street, which is lined with trees, is an excellent spot for al fresco dining at places like Il Giorgione Pizzeria & Wine Bar and Backstreets Grill, which also has a tailgating menu for perfect game days. Columbia and its environs also have a buzzing brewery scene; try the pecan brown ale from Steel Hands Brewing. What is one of the best places to eat in Columbia, SC? Everything that hits the plate at Motor Supply Company Bistro is sourced locally or homemade. Dishes rotate with the seasons, but you can expect offerings like pan-seared local fish. Come brunch, order a pimento cheese board for the table before diving into a Southern specialty like shrimp and grits. What is the best coffee shop in Columbia, SC? Drip serves up a variety of classic expresso-based beverages and custom in-house creations. Where is the best happy hour in Columbia, SC? Make out like a bandit at Bourbon, where you can get a burger, shot and beer for $12 during the “3B” happy hour.
sections.education: Columbia classrooms are primarily served by two public school districts: Richland County School District One and Richland County School District Two. Together, they serve more than 50,000 students. Richland One is known for advancing the health and well-being of its students: 48 schools in Richland One have been recognized as America’s Healthiest Schools, and the district is the leader in South Carolina with the most awards. Other points of pride: Class of 2024 graduates raked in $70 million in scholarship money. The city also has more than three dozen private school options. South Carolina also prizes diversity with its rich cultural programs. The state has been recognized for having an African American graduation rate in the top 10% of public universities, and over the past decade, African American freshman enrollment has grown by 85%, and both underrepresented minority and Hispanic freshman enrollment have increased by over 50%. Sign up for a kids’ mindful yoga class at FIT Columbia to get the wiggles out.

faqs.a: Make out like a bandit at Bourbon, where you can get a burger, shot and beer for $12 during the “3B” happy hour.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Columbia, SC?
faqs.a: The city is home to the state’s flagship university, the University of South Carolina, offering over 350 bachelor’s, master’s and doctoral degrees and career-enhancing certificates. More than five dozen of its programs are nationally ranked. There are also two historically black colleges (HBCUs) in Columbia — Benedict College and Allen University.

SC-greenville.description: A picturesque city nestled in the foothills of the Blue Ridge Mountains, Greenville offers a mix of natural beauty and urban charm. With a thriving economy, top-rated schools and an award-winning downtown,Greenville is a great place to plant roots.

sections.location: Greenville is nestled in the upstate region, right along Interstate 85 and Interstate 385, at the foothills of the Blue Ridge Mountains. The nearest airport, Greenville-Spartanburg International Airport, is about 15 miles east, making travel easy. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Charlotte is just 100 miles northeast, where you can visit the NASCAR Hall of Fame or the Mint Museum. Asheville sits 60 miles northwest, famous for the Biltmore Estate and the Blue Ridge Parkway. Atlanta is about 145 miles southwest, home of the Georgia Aquarium and the Martin Luther King Jr. National Historical Park.
sections.economy: The city used to be known as a textile powerhouse. But today, the region is home to major employers such as GE and Michelin. The titans of Greenville’s industry are manufacturing, health care and professional services. With a revitalized downtown and growing economy breathing new life into the area and its residents, this city is an excellent place for young professionals to begin their careers. The cost of living in Greenville is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Greenville is $420,025, and the average household income is $129,588. South Carolina residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Greenville, SC? Local residents are in great health! The city offers plenty of quality providers, including Bon Secours St. Francis Health System-Greenville and Prisma Health Greenville Memorial Hospital. Need Help? How to Find Your Career in Greenville, SCLife Sciences Industry Breeds Innovation in Greenville, South CarolinaGreenville, SC, Entrepreneurs Turn Their Dreams Into Reality
sections.thingsToDo: Prepare to have a lengthy Greenville bucket list because there’s so much to do and see in this city and its environs. Museumgoers can check out The Upcountry History Museum, which has family fun days and a monthly “lunchbox learning” lecture series for adults that features local, state and national historians. For a family night out, head to the ballpark to root for the minor league Greenville Drive (the affiliate of the Boston Red Sox) at Fluor Field, which is a mini replica of the iconic Fenway Park, or cheer on the Greenville Swamp Rabbits, the minor league professional hockey team. Other must-dos include catching a performance at the Carolina Ballet Theatre, seeing the exhibits at the Greenville County Museum of Art or taking kids for a show at the South Carolina Children’s Theatre. What is something you can do for free in Greenville, SC? Take a self-guided tour of the city’s first-rate public art scene with the help of this interactive online map, which features a black history art tour and a map of where you can find color-saturated murals around the city. Some more highlights include glass artist Dale Chihuly’s “Rose Crystal Tower” and artist Yuri Tsuzuki’s “Transformation,” which features hundreds of steel butterflies throughout Downtown Greenville. What are some things to do with kids in Greenville, SC? Take kids on a “Goodnight Moon”-inspired scavenger hunt! The Mice on Main public art exhibit features nine bronze mice statues hidden between the Hyatt Regency and Westin Poinsett hotels. When it comes to a fun day of play, the Children’s Museum of the Upstate is among the best of the best, with 80,000 square feet of play-based learning and exhibits like an upside-down land, animation station and a wheelchair-accessible outdoor playground. Or, take older kids to duckpin bowling and play indoor mini golf at Group Therapy Pub & Playground. Where do locals shop in Greenville, SC? Locals love the TD Saturday Market that runs on Saturday mornings from May through October and features 75 vendors selling everything from fresh fruits and veggies to small-batch Gouda cheese, carrot cake, house plants and fresh blooms. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Greenville, SC? The Peace Center is next to Falls Park and is the artistic heart of Greenville, hosting Broadway productions and touring musicians, comedians, speakers and dance companies. The center includes a 2,115-seat concert hall, a more intimate 439-seat theatre and The Pavilion, which offers outdoor seating for up to 1,200 along the overlook of the Reed River. Greenville, South Carolina, Weaves a Vibrant and Diverse Arts and Culture SceneHave Tons of Fun at These 4 Festivals in Greenville, SCGreenville, SC, Has a Variety of Sports for Amateur Athletes
sections.outdoors: Local residents love to get outside, from farmers markets to outdoor concerts, festivals and minor league baseball games. You’re in waterfall country here. In addition to the beautiful waterfall at Falls Park, there’s plenty more to see close to town, including Raven Cliff Falls in Caesars Head State Park. With a 420-foot drop, this South Carolina stunner is the tallest waterfall in the state! The 4.4-mile round trip Raven Cliff Falls Trail delivers hikers to a scenic overlook to see the waterfall. One of the city's most popular outdoor spaces, the 28-mile Prisma Health Swamp Rabbit Trail traces the bed of a former railroad. Run, walk or bike along the paved path that bisects Falls Park on the Reedy, a downtown oasis in its own right with a waterfall, gardens and green space. The trail also winds through Cancer Survivors Park and extends to Cleveland Park, the city's largest park and home of the Greenville Zoo, which is open year-round, as well as a fitness park with equipment for an al fresco workout! In the warmer months, a 10-minute drive from downtown Greenville delivers you to Paris Mountain State Park, which features four lakes, including one with a swimming area and on-site rentals for kayaks, canoes and pedal boats. The park also has a playground, 15 miles of hiking and biking trails and a campground, and it's home to Paris Mountain, a monadnock (or a mountain that rises from otherwise flat lands!) 5 Great Outdoor Experiences in Greenville, South CarolinaOutdoor Recreation Spots Abound in Greenville, South Carolina9 Ways to Get Out and About in Greenville, SC
sections.restaurants: The city has gained national recognition from travel guides and magazines as one of the top food cities in the United States. One of the best ways to get to know the dynamic culinary scene is by becoming a regular at the city’s food festivals like euphoria, a fall food festival that’s one of the best in the Southeast and includes tasting events, live music, cooking demonstrations and wine seminars. Your tastebuds can travel the world in Greenville, too, as the cosmopolitan food scene represents 34 countries. One more thing: The founder of Duke’s Mayonnaise is from here, and Eugenia Duke’s Unofficial Mayo Guide to Greenville reveals restaurants that use the condiment, from the Smoked Carolina Fish Dip at The Jones Oyster Co. to the tangy coleslaw at Bobby’s BBQ. What is one of the best places to eat in Greenville, SC? Pita House, a Middle Eastern restaurant and market, is one of the top-rated local places to eat. The menu includes pita sandwiches, shish kebabs, and tasty sides like hummus and baba ganoush (an eggplant dip). What is the best coffee shop in Greenville, SC? Methodical is a coffee connoisseurs’ dream — with coffee classics and delicious in-house pastries. Where is the best place for happy hour in Greenville, SC? Actually, Oak And Honey calls its happy hour “classy hour,” and this hot spot known for its craft cocktails and local fare serves $7 featured cocktails from 3 to 6 p.m. Tuesdays through Thursdays along with snacks like chicken adobo wings and crispy Brussels sprouts. Go Global With Great Places to Eat in Greenville, SCWhy We Launched Restaurants in Greenville
sections.education: Greenville County Schools are among the top 10 districts in South Carolina and the top 50 largest in the nation. There are nearly 100 schools and 78,000 students within the district. Still, there is a favorable student-to-teacher ratio of 15:1. The district provides dual-credit and AP courses, gifted and talented programs, International Baccalaureate studies, special education and other specialized programs. The district also has five career centers, including a Fine Arts Center and the new CTE Innovation Center that focuses on growing fields like aerospace technology, automation and robotics, clean energy technology and more. Plenty of preparatory and religiously affiliated schools offer private school options. Bob Jones Academy, which was founded in 1927, is a well-known fixture in the community.
