SD-rapid-city.description: Rapid City residents have it all. The city stuns against the backdrop of the Black Hills region, and it's easy to get around the different neighborhoods in this affordable city. Add in a friendly community, excellent schools and a strong economy, and it's easy to see why people are rushing to Rapid City.

sections.location: Known as the “Gateway to the Black Hills,” Rapid City is a rugged, adventure-ready city tucked into the western edge of South Dakota, where Interstate 90 cuts through. Rapid City Regional Airport is 10 miles southeast, offering flights to Denver, Minneapolis and more. When you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Deadwood, SD, is just 40 miles northwest, where you can step back in time at this historic gold-rush town, visit the Adams Museum or try your luck at a casino. Sioux Falls, SD, is 400 miles east, boasting Falls Park, the SculptureWalk and a great historic downtown. Denver, CO, is 400 miles southwest, with the Denver Art Museum, 16th Street Mall and plenty of outdoor activities.
sections.economy: Locals in Rapid City are proud of their resilient and diversified economy. Major industries range from health care and education to tourism and public service. As the largest city in the state’s western region, Rapid City is often considered an economic hub for the area, equating to various employment opportunities. The local health care sector is especially strong thanks to institutions like Monument Health and the Rapid City Regional Hospital. The tourism industry is another boon to the community because of the city’s proximity to Mount Rushmore and Custer State Park. The city is also an attractive home base for military families stationed nearby at the Ellsworth Air Force Base. New to town and looking to establish roots? Or thinking of moving to town and don’t know where to start? Recruit the help of a Dakota Roots job advisor to help you find the perfect role so you can make your move to Rapid City. The cost of living in Rapid City is lower than the national average. The median home value in Rapid City is $316,354, and the average household income is $99,487. South Dakota residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.2% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Rapid City, SD? Monument Health Rapid City is the region’s leading health care provider, offering a wide range of medical services. It is also one of the largest hospitals in western South Dakota.
sections.thingsToDo: Living in Rapid City means a never-ending roster of things to do. Downtown Rapid City is a hub of activity featuring the City of Presidents — a unique outdoor exhibit of life-size bronze presidential statues — and Art Alley, a street art space. Explore local culture at the Prairie Edge Trading Co., which showcases Native American art and heritage, and experience the world’s only Pawnseum. Main Street Square hosts year-round events, including summer festivals and winter Markets. For family fun, WaTiki® Indoor Waterpark Resort offers year-round aquatic entertainment, and curious kiddos will love the collection of reptiles and the botanical gardens at Reptile Gardens. Storybook Island, a free children’s theme park, is another local gem. Residents can gather at unique events like the Black Hills Stock Show and Rodeo, the Black Hills Powwow and the Great Downtown Pumpkin Festival. Just beyond the city limits, residents can appreciate South Dakota experiences like the Buffalo Roundup, and the Journey Museum & Learning Center provides insights into the region’s history and geology. What is something you can do for free in Rapid City, SD? Explore an ever-evolving exhibit of colorful, eye-catching murals in “Art Alley” between 6th and 7th and Main and Saint Joseph streets. What are some things to do in Rapid City, SD, with kids? See the lifesize, cartoon-like replicas of half a dozen dinos and an extinct reptile at Dinosaur Park. Set on a sandstone ridge, the concrete and steel reproductions built in 1936 are a fun throwback. During the warmer months, visit Storybook Island, an amusement park with a carousel and train rides. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Rapid City, SD? Aby’s is a full-service bar and live music venue that hosts open mic nights, upcoming talent and even karaoke. 5 Best Things to Do in Rapid City, SD
sections.outdoors: From relaxing in city parks to exploring the beauty of the Black Hills, Rapid City is a recreational wonder, and residents can reap the benefits of living in a city with dozens of parks and even more outdoor activities. Green spaces like Founders Park, with 60-plus acres for picnicking and sports, and Memorial Park, which features memorials, a playground and access to Rapid Creek, are popular spots. For more active pursuits, the city has facilities for disc golf and a state-of-the-art indoor climbing and fitness center, Black Hills Basecamp. Beyond the city limits, outdoor enthusiasts can immerse themselves in the natural wonders of the Black Hills, which provide hiking, biking and wildlife viewing opportunities. Other destinations within reach include Mount Rushmore, Crazy Horse Memorial, Custer State Park and Badlands National Park.
sections.restaurants: Local favorites like Wall Drug provide a roadside experience before you spend your day exploring the great outdoors, while Tally’s and Colonial House have hearty breakfast, lunch and dinner. For a taste of the region, Dakotah Steakhouse features game meats like elk and buffalo, and Murphy’s Pub & Grill offers South Dakota staples like buffalo meatloaf. For international flavors, Kathmandu Bistro offers Nepalese, Indian and Tibetan dishes, and Que Pasa Cantina serves modern Mexican fare. Delmonico specializes in dry-aged steaks and seasonal dishes for upscale dining, and Juniper at Vertex, atop the Hotel Alex Johnson, provides a refined dining experience with locally sourced ingredients and ever-changing menus. Rapid City also has a growing craft beverage scene. Independent Ale House offers a wide selection of rotating taps and bottled beers. Firehouse Brewing Company serves unique brews and is housed in a historic fire station. What is one of the best places to eat in Rapid City, SD? Dakotah Steakhouse serves up a variety of local game and fish, including steak, elk, buffalo and pheasant. What is the best coffee shop in Rapid City, SD? Dixon Coffee Company is a local staple. They serve classic coffee drinks but also put a twist on some beloved favorites. We recommend the Salted Carmel Devout. Where is the best happy hour in Rapid City, SD? Sidle up to the copper-top bar at Paddy O’Neill’s for “Paddy Hour.” The Irish pub is inside Hotel Alex Johnson.
sections.education: The Rapid Area Schools District, the central public school district serving more than 12,000 K-12 students, is one of the largest in the state and offers a range of programs and schools. What sets Rapid City Schools apart is that the district focuses on career pathways, with health sciences, engineering and the arts academies. These programs let students try different hands-on experiences and explore career interests. There are a couple of private schools here too, too, including the Rapid City Catholic School System, which prepares students from preschool to high school graduation. One thing to consider about schools in Rapid City is that South Dakota has open enrollment policies, allowing families to apply to schools outside their assigned district, including charter schools. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Rapid City, SD?
faqs.a: Monument Health Rapid City is the region’s leading health care provider, offering a wide range of medical services. It is also one of the largest hospitals in western South Dakota.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Rapid City, SD?
faqs.a: Explore an ever-evolving exhibit of colorful, eye-catching murals in “Art Alley” between 6th and 7th and Main and Saint Joseph streets.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Rapid City, SD, with kids?
faqs.a: See the lifesize, cartoon-like replicas of half a dozen dinos and an extinct reptile at Dinosaur Park. Set on a sandstone ridge, the concrete and steel reproductions built in 1936 are a fun throwback. During the warmer months, visit Storybook Island, an amusement park with a carousel and train rides.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Rapid City, SD?
faqs.a: Aby’s is a full-service bar and live music venue that hosts open mic nights, upcoming talent and even karaoke.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Rapid City, SD?
faqs.a: Dakotah Steakhouse serves up a variety of local game and fish, including steak, elk, buffalo and pheasant.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Rapid City, SD?
faqs.a: Dixon Coffee Company is a local staple. They serve classic coffee drinks but also put a twist on some beloved favorites. We recommend the Salted Carmel Devout.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Rapid City, SD?
faqs.a: Sidle up to the copper-top bar at Paddy O’Neill’s for “Paddy Hour.” The Irish pub is inside Hotel Alex Johnson.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Rapid City, SD?
faqs.a: The city has a handful of colleges and universities, including the South Dakota School of Mines & Technology, Western Dakota Technical College and National American University.

SD-sioux-falls.description: Sioux Falls enchants residents with its friendly community, abundant parks and recreation options and a strong economy with ample job opportunities. Surrounded by the natural beauty of the Big Sioux River and rolling plains, it offers a serene setting to establish roots.

sections.location: Sioux Falls is a vibrant city in the southeastern part of the state, right along Interstate 29 and Interstate 90. It’s the largest city in South Dakota and is known for its stunning Falls Park. Sioux Falls Regional Airport is right in the city, making travel easy. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Omaha, NE, is just 180 miles south, where you can visit the Henry Doorly Zoo or the Durham Museum. Minneapolis, MN, sits 235 miles east, offering the Walker Art Center and the Chain of Lakes. Des Moines, IA, is about 270 miles southeast, home to the Iowa State Capitol and the Blank Park Zoo.
sections.economy: The city has a well-rounded economy, with thriving industries like health care, retail trade and finance and insurance. It's a major medical care hub with major players like Sanford Health and Avera Health headquartered here. The financial sector is well-represented, too, with many national banks and insurance companies operating in the city. Raven Industries and Sonifi Solutions are both technology companies headquartered here. The cost of living in Sioux Falls is lower than the national average. The median home value in Sioux Falls is $284,520, and the average household income is $108,740. South Dakota residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.20% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Sioux Falls, SD? Because Sanford Health is headquartered here, it should be no surprise that the health care options here are top-notch. For example, Sanford USD Medical Center is nationally ranked in one adult specialty and rated high-performing in two additional specialties. Other options include Avera McKennan Hospital & University Health Center, a Level II trauma center, and Children’s Hospital & Medical Center, specializing in pediatric care.
sections.thingsToDo: There are tons of things to do in Sioux Falls. The arts scene is lively, with the Washington Pavilion’s museums and galleries and SculptureWalk’s 80-plus downtown sculptures, like the Arc of Dreams. Residents can explore local art at the Eide/Dalrymple Gallery and Full Circle Book Co-Op and enjoy free cultural events like First Fridays and performances at Levitt at the Falls. There is also a full-size replica of Michelangelo’s “David” in Fawick Park and family-friendly destinations such as the Butterfly House & Aquarium and the Great Plains Zoo. The city provides numerous outdoor and recreational opportunities. Residents can enjoy live music at the Levitt at the Falls — which hosts 50 free concerts each summer — and McKennan Park, catch a baseball game with the Sioux Falls Canaries or participate in the bustling Falls Park Farmers Market. Parks offer hiking and outdoor activities, and events like Moonlight Movies and the St. Patrick’s Day Parade bring people together. The downtown area has shopping experiences with locally owned boutiques, and the Denny Sanford PREMIER Center hosts a variety of large-scale entertainment events. What is something you can do for free in Sioux Falls, SD? Thanks to a collection of several dozen impressive sculptures, the city feels like an outdoor museum. The Arc of Dreams is a must-see; the colossal stainless steel sculpture stretches across the Big Sioux River downtown and is nearly the length of a football field. What are some things to do in Sioux Falls, SD, with kids? Wild Water West doesn’t just have slides and a wave pool; there’s also an adjacent campsite, mini golf and full-throttle Go Karts. Where do locals shop in Sioux Falls, SD? Artists are celebrated at Full Circle Book Co-Op, which has an impressive collection of artwork and books from local and regional creatives. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Sioux Falls, SD? Washington Pavilion on Main Street is a historic venue that combines everything from art galleries and science exhibits to theatrical performances. Their diverse calendar features symphony orchestra performances, dance competitions, festivals and more.
sections.outdoors: Living in Sioux Falls means having access to tons of outdoor amenities. The city’s extensive park system features over 80 parks, including the iconic Falls Park, which provides ample opportunities for hiking, biking and picnicking. The paved Big Sioux River Greenway offers 30 miles of scenic trails, perfect for cycling and leisurely walks, while locations like McKennan Park and Terrace Park offer diverse recreational amenities. Water activities are popular, with paddle boarding and kayaking available on Lake Lorraine and fishing along the Big Sioux River. The Japanese Gardens are beautiful in spring, and Good Earth State Park provides insights into Native American history and scenic trails. Winter brings ice fishing, skiing, snowboarding and tubing at Great Bear Recreation Park. The Mary Jo Wegner Arboretum and the Outdoor Campus are great for exploring the area's natural beauty. The city focuses on outdoor fun, with well-kept parks and trails.
sections.restaurants: Sioux Falls has a surprisingly diverse and growing food scene, moving beyond typical Midwestern fare. While steakhouses remain a staple, the city has embraced a range of international flavors and modern culinary trends. Downtown Sioux Falls, in particular, has seen a surge in independent restaurants offering everything from authentic Mexican cuisine at places like Tortilleria Hernandez to flavorful Thai dishes at Sanaa’s Gourmet Mediterranean. For a taste of upscale dining, look to Parker’s Bistro, known for its seasonal menus and carefully crafted cocktails. Be sure to pick up fresh-baked loaves of artisan bread from Breadsmith, which has staples and a rotating selection that includes flavors like raisin cinnamon walnut and jalapeño cheddar. Local breweries like Fernson Brewing Company and Remedy Brewing Company also contribute to the city’s culinary landscape, offering craft beers that pair well with the diverse food options. Don’t miss the chance to try a “chipper” at a local diner, a Sioux Falls original. What is one of the best places to eat in Sioux Falls, SD? Phillips Avenue Diner, located downtown, is a classic diner with a fun and retro atmosphere. Locals often praise its wide range of comfort food, including burgers, shakes and hearty breakfast dishes, which make it a fun and easy-to-please place for dinner with family or friends. What is the best coffee shop in Sioux Falls, SD? Nestled in the historic section of downtown Sioux Falls, Josiah’s Coffeehouse is a chipper little coffee shop that boasts outdoor seating, excellent coffee, tasty pastries and a fireplace for cozy winter afternoons with a steaming drink. Where is the best place for happy hour in Sioux Falls, SD? Grab a half-priced drink at PAve, a downtown bar and restaurant with regular live music and a sizeable Instagrammable rooftop patio.
sections.education: Sioux Falls School District is one of the best and largest in the state, serving more than 25,000 students. Sioux Falls Schools offers a range of programs and schools, including traditional K-12 and some specialized options and programs, like the Sonia Sotomayor Spanish Immersion Elementary School. The district also has a strong Career and Technical Education (CTE) program that helps provide students with hands-on training in various fields. Private education is important here, too. There are several different college prep and religious schools here. One thing to consider about schools in Sioux Falls is that South Dakota has open enrollment policies, allowing families to apply to schools outside their assigned district, including charter schools. faqs.a: Phillips Avenue Diner, located downtown, is a classic diner with a fun and retro atmosphere. Locals often praise its wide range of comfort food, including burgers, shakes and hearty breakfast dishes, which make it a fun and easy-to-please place for dinner with family or friends.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Sioux Falls, SD?
faqs.a: Nestled in the historic section of downtown Sioux Falls, Josiah’s Coffeehouse is a chipper little coffee shop that boasts outdoor seating, excellent coffee, tasty pastries and a fireplace for cozy winter afternoons with a steaming drink.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for happy hour in Sioux Falls, SD?
faqs.a: Grab a half-priced drink at PAve, a downtown bar and restaurant with regular live music and a sizeable Instagrammable rooftop patio.

faqs.a: There are several colleges and universities here, including Augustana University, the University of Sioux Falls and Southeast Technical College.

TN-chattanooga.description: All aboard! Chattanooga offers outdoor adventures, a vibrant downtown area and plenty of delicious dining options. With a low cost of living, a strong job market and excellent schools, Chattanooga is the perfect place for families and young professionals to plant roots.

sections.location: Chattanooga is a scenic city in southeastern Tennessee, about 135 miles southeast of Nashville, accessible via Interstates 24 and 75. Chattanooga Metropolitan Airport is just east, offering flights to Atlanta and Charlotte. Nearby major cities include: Nashville, TN, is just 135 miles northwest, perfect for a day trip to catch live music, visit the Ryman Auditorium or explore the Frist Art Museum. Atlanta, GA, sits 120 miles south, where you can dive into the Georgia Aquarium, sip at the World of Coca-Cola or cheer on the Braves at Truist Park. Knoxville, TN, is roughly 110 miles northeast, boasting the Knoxville Zoo, Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame and delightful downtown Market Square.
sections.economy: Chattanooga is often referred to as Gig City because it has been a pioneer in the fiber internet space. This well-connected city is attracting lots of startups and tech firms. Big-name companies such as BlueCross BlueShield of Tennessee, Volkswagen Group of America and Erlanger Health System call Chatt home. Significant industries are health care, manufacturing and tourism, thanks to the city’s natural beauty. The cost of living in Chattanooga is lower than the national average. The median home value is $298,784, and the average household income is $99,972. Tennessee residents do not pay a state income tax, and residents can expect a 9.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Chattanooga, TN? When it comes to staying in good health, residents have several options. CHI Memorial Hospital, Erlanger Medical Center and Parkridge Medical Center have all earned high-performance ratings in several different procedures and conditions. 5 Reasons Greater Chattanooga is the Perfect Place to Build Your CareerChattanooga Residents Are in Good HealthPowering Chattanooga’s High-Tech Workforce with AI
sections.thingsToDo: You’ll want to make return weekend trips to Lookout Mountain, which is the spot for scenic views, epic hiking, and attractions like Rock City, the Incline Railway and Ruby Falls — an underground cave waterfall! After work or on the weekends, enjoy a walk, jog or bike ride along the Chattanooga Riverwalk, a 13-mile path that traces the Tennessee River. Looking for a fun date night? Learn a new skill by taking a glassblowing glass at Ignis Glass Studio or enjoy a comedy show at The Comedy Catch, which hosts touring comedians and open mic nights. Art lovers can check out the exhibits at the Hunter Museum of American Art, which overlooks the Tennessee River. The museum hosts yoga, art camps for kids and Black Professionals at the Hunter, a series of art-focused social events celebrating Black creativity. The Chattanooga Symphony and Opera performs at the historic Tivoli Theatre. Plus, there’s a whole host of festivals to fill your calendar all year long.All aboard! Take themed train rides at the Tennessee Valley Railroad. As a bonus, each train ticket grants you admission to the train museum. And, of course, you can’t miss the Chattanooga Choo Choo. What is something you can do for free in Chattanooga, TN? Enjoy live music during the Nightfall Free Music Series, a favorite Friday night tradition that in 2025 will run from early May through early July at Miller Plaza. The stage draws artists worldwide, and Miller Park is set up with lawn games and family-friendly activities. What are some things to do in Chattanooga, TN, with kids? Check out the animals at Chattanooga Zoo, which recently expanded with its new Cape of Africa, home to porcupines, warthogs and the zoo’s giraffes. At Creative Discovery Museum, kids can pilot a riverboat, monkey around in a treehouse, learn how to basketweave, and take in panoramic views of the city from the observation deck on the Lookout Tower. At the Tennessee Aquarium, kids can watch penguins play and check out sharks, lemurs, jellyfish and more. Where do locals shop in Chattanooga, TN? The Chattanooga River Market features live music, local eats, arts and crafts and more on Saturdays. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Chattanooga, TN? Catch plays at the Chattanooga Theatre Centre, which also holds classes and a summer camp for kids. The Centre is one of the country’s oldest and busiest community theaters and produces over a dozen shows yearly. 10 Fun Things To Do in Chattanooga, TNThe Arts are Alive in Greater Chattanooga6 Reasons to Call Greater Chattanooga Home
sections.outdoors: There are more than 80 neighborhood parks in Chattanooga and dozens of other parks, trails and greenways, meaning there are plenty of places to explore. The sky’s the limit for outdoor adventure, which draws all kinds of outdoors lovers, from rock climbers to mountain bikers to those who love adventures on the water. Some adventures include whitewater rafting along the Ocoee, with three sections offering everything from wild rapids to calm waters ideal for flatwater paddling or a leisurely float. Climbers also flock to the area, one of the country’s best rock climbing destinations, with climbing hot spots like Tennessee Wall and Stone Fort. Hikers could pretty much visit a new trail every weekend: There are 50 trailheads all within 30 minutes of downtown, like the 10-mile Big Daddy Loop that takes you pretty far up Lookout Mountain. Sunset Rock, as its name implies, also shows off incredible views. Chattanooga is Calling All Nature Lovers10 Must-Do Outdoor Activities in Greater ChattanoogaAdventures Start Here: Chattanooga’s New Parks, Trails and Playgrounds
sections.restaurants: Chattanooga’s food and beverage scene has flourished in recent years, with beloved newcomers like Wooden City offering comforting American cuisine and Calliope, a modern Levantine restaurant, winning over locals. On Main Street, Hello Monty is a favorite spot, boasting an onsite brewery and tasty dishes like carrots with curry crème fraîche, brat skewers, steaks and more wood-fired American fare. Chatt Taste is an excellent way to get an introduction to the city’s food scene, with specialized tours like one that dives into the historic Southside and another that is focused on the city’s brunch scene. What is one of the best places to eat in Chattanooga, TN? Alleia is an Italian restaurant that incorporates local seasonal ingredients, so the menu frequently changes. But regulars rave about the ricotta bruschetta with truffle honey and the pizzas that cook in a 750-degree Fahrenheit handmade masonry oven. What is the best coffee shop in Chattanooga, TN? Enjoy the greenery and grounds at Sleepyhead Coffee. The space is known for its cozy vibe and potted plants and serves everything from a classic pour-over to cappuccinos and cortados. Plus, the menu is full of delicious vegan options. Where is the best happy hour in Chattanooga, TN? Oyster happy hour is from 4 to 5 p.m. daily at Easy Bistro, a delectable spot with a raw bar, handmade pasta, four-course chef dinners and more. Savor the Flavors of Greater Chattanooga: A Global Culinary Journey5 Breweries to Visit in the Greater Chattanooga Area
sections.education: Roughly 46,000 students in Chattanooga are enrolled within the Hamilton County School District, an above-average public school district. It’s one of the largest school districts in Tennessee and offers a range of programs and school types, including magnet and charter schools. The city also has a significant number of private schools, many of which are religiously affiliated. The city is well-known throughout the state for the fierce rivalry between McCallie School and Baylor School, an independent day and boarding school. Girls Preparatory School is also a top-notch choice and is an independent college prep school for girls. The Baylor School is another popular option. It is a co-ed independent college preparatory boarding and day school on 690 acres overlooking the Tennessee River Gorge. faqs.a: Catch plays at the Chattanooga Theatre Centre, which also holds classes and a summer camp for kids. The Centre is one of the country’s oldest and busiest community theaters and produces over a dozen shows yearly.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Chattanooga, TN?
faqs.a: Alleia is an Italian restaurant that incorporates local seasonal ingredients, so the menu frequently changes. But regulars rave about the ricotta bruschetta with truffle honey and the pizzas that cook in a 750-degree Fahrenheit handmade masonry oven.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Chattanooga, TN?
faqs.a: Enjoy the greenery and grounds at Sleepyhead Coffee. The space is known for its cozy vibe and potted plants and serves everything from a classic pour-over to cappuccinos and cortados. Plus, the menu is full of delicious vegan options.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Chattanooga, TN?
faqs.a: Oyster happy hour is from 4 to 5 p.m. daily at Easy Bistro, a delectable spot with a raw bar, handmade pasta, four-course chef dinners and more.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Chattanooga, TN?
faqs.a: The area has a diverse education landscape, with several options ranging from a large public university — the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga (UTC) — to several smaller private colleges and technical schools, like Chattanooga State Community College, which offers 150 degrees and certifications, and the Tennessee Transfer Pathways (TTP) allow community college graduates to transfer to four-year institutions in Tennessee.

faqs.q: What are your favorite things to do outdoors in Chattanooga?
faqs.a: One of the great things about Chattanooga is that there's a little something for everyone, no matter how you like to spend your time. Whether you enjoy the outdoorsy activities like hiking Signal Mountain, browsing at the Bluff View Art District, or cultural activities like visiting the Hunter Museum of American Art, you can always find something to do. As a sports fan myself, I love attending University of Tennessee at Chattanooga games with the family or playing a round at one of our many local golf courses. There's never a dull moment in Chattanooga, if you know where to look for fun.

faqs.q: Why do people move to Chattanooga?
What makes Chattanooga appealing to families?
What are your favorite things to do outdoors in Chattanooga?
faqs.a: We are truly blessed with beautiful surroundings in Chattanooga's one of the best places to live if you love being outside. There's something for everyone, whether you're into peaceful river walks or heart-pumping adventures. You can kayak or paddleboard along the Tennessee River or hike the many trails around Lookout Mountain. Raccoon Mountain Caverns is a fun underground adventure, and Rock City and Ruby Falls are local favorites that never get old, especially if you've got kids or visitors in town. Even just spending an afternoon walking the Tennessee Riverwalk gives you that perfect blend of city energy and natural beauty. It's hard not to fall in love with the outdoors when you live in a place like this.

faqs.q: Why do people move to Chattanooga?
What makes Chattanooga appealing to families?
What makes Chattanooga appealing to families?
faqs.a: As someone who is doing it myself, I can say wholeheartedly that Chattanooga is the perfect place to raise a family. Beyond just being a beautiful, scenic city, we have safe neighborhoods, reasonably priced homes, and excellent schools and universities right here in the city. There's always something to do with the family on the weekends, from spending the day learning something new at the Tennessee Aquarium or the Creative Discovery Museum to plenty of shopping options and places to eat. Overall, I would say that Chattanooga is a friendly, vibrant community for you and your family to call home.

faqs.q: Why do people move to Chattanooga?
faqs.a: Chattanooga truly offers the best of both worlds. We've got all the perks of city living while maintaining that warm, small-town vibe in our neighborhoods. Nestled along the Tennessee River and framed by Lookout Mountain, the city's natural beauty makes it easy to fall in love with. Plus, its convenient location means you're just a short drive from other major hubs like Knoxville and Atlanta. Our more affordable cost of living can also make setting down roots here even more appealing. Outdoor enthusiasts will find no shortage of adventures, from hiking scenic trails to exploring the Tennessee River. Having colleges like the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga here adds some youthful energy and educational opportunities to the mix. With all of that to boast about, it's no wonder Chattanooga is becoming an "it city."

faqs.q: What makes Chattanooga appealing to families?

TN-knoxville.description: Strangers ain’t coming down from Rocky Top, and it’s easy to see why. Knoxville’s affordability, close proximity to the Great Smoky Mountains, calendar full of festivals and friendly community make it a dynamic, welcoming city.

sections.location: Knoxville is a lively city in eastern Tennessee, about 180 miles east of Nashville, accessible via Interstate 40 and I-75. McGhee Tyson Airport is 12 miles south, offering flights to Atlanta and Charlotte. Nearby major cities include: Nashville is just 180 miles west, perfect for a day trip to catch live music, visit the Ryman Auditorium or explore the Frist Art Museum. Chattanooga sits just 110 miles southwest, perfect for families visiting the Tennessee Aquarium, Ruby Falls and the Chattanooga Choo Choo. Asheville, NC, is roughly 115 miles east, boasting the Biltmore Estate, Blue Ridge Parkway and an eclectic downtown.
sections.economy: Thanks to the city’s growth across several diverse industries, it’s easy to see why so many students come here for college and stay to build a career. Advanced manufacturing, education and research, and technology are three large industries contributing to Knoxville’s robust economy. The University of Tennessee, Tennessee Valley Authority, and Pilot Company are some of the major companies operating here. Additionally, companies like Scripps Network Interactive and Regal Cinemas are headquartered in the area. The median home value in Knoxville is $276,074, and the average household income is $81,895. Tennessee residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Knoxville, TN? Knoxville has several top-tier providers, including Parkwest Medical Center and the University of Tennessee Medical Center. East Tennessee Children’s Hospital is also in the city, keeping the entire family healthy. We’re Hiring: 5 Industries in Knoxville, TN, That Are GrowingKnoxville, TN, Preps for Next Industrial Revolution with The 2030 ProtocolWhy Young Professionals Thrive in Knoxville, TN
sections.thingsToDo: Rocky Top is home sweet home to many, and it’s easy to see why. You’ll never spend a day with nothing to do in Knoxville, from the city’s thriving arts and culture scene to many events and festivals. There are many museums, galleries and venues, including the Tennessee and Bijou Theatres, and Market Square and Gay Street offer various shopping options. Knoxville is known for its events and festivals, like the Dogwood Arts Festival and the Big Ears Music Festival. There are plenty of attractions, like the World’s Fair Park, home to the iconic Sunsphere, and sports fans can cheer on the University of Tennessee Volunteers. Speaking of sports, Knoxville is home to the Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame. Knoxville is super kid-friendly, too. Take them to check out the World’s Largest Rubik’s Cube or Zoo Knoxville, which specializes in conserving and protecting endangered species. When you’re downtown during the summer, expect to see Concerts on the Square in the form of Jazz on Tuesdays and Variety on Thursdays. What is something you can do for free in Knoxville, TN? Soak up the arts with a tour of the Knoxville Museum of Art featuring works by regional and national artists. It’s no surprise that this is a city where music matters, considering visitors are invited to sit in on a live Blue Plate Special performance of roots music for WDVX public radio station during their lunch hour at the Knoxville Visitors Center studio. What are some things to do in Knoxville, TN, with kids? If you’re visiting the city with children, you can’t miss Zoo Knoxville. Spanning 53 acres and home to 1,200 animals, this unexpectedly awesome zoo is worth an entire day’s visit for animal lovers. History and science buffs and generally curious folks will love the McClung Museum of Natural History and Culture, with exhibits that help explain the history of Tennessee and several other places. And both admission and parking are free! Where do locals shop in Knoxville, TN? Explore the sidewalks of Market Square, home to an unparalleled farmers market and various local boutiques and restaurants. Browse the stacks at locally owned, independent Union Avenue Books, which carries many titles and hosts events throughout the year. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Knoxville, TN? Ask any of the locals, and they’ll quickly report that Bijou Theatre is the place to go for live performances of all sorts. This beautiful centerpiece of the city boasts a storied history that began in 1909 and has grown ever since. For theater lovers, the Clarence Brown Theatre and Ula Love Doughty Carousel Theatres on the Tennessee campus can’t be missed. 15 Knoxville Festivals You Don’t Want To MissKnoxville, TN, is a Sports Lover’s DreamGo There: How to Spend the Perfect Weekend in Knoxville, TN
sections.outdoors: Its location in the foothills of the Smoky Mountains means there are plenty of places to explore. The Knoxville Urban Wilderness, a sprawling area encompassing Ijams Nature Center and Mead’s Quarry, offers many recreational opportunities to those living in Knoxville, including hiking, biking, climbing, swimming, paddling and ziplining at Navitat’s treetop adventures. Ijams Nature Center has more than 10 miles of trails and water activities, while Baker Creek Preserve caters to mountain biking enthusiasts with its 10 miles of trails. Pack a picnic and spend an afternoon at the Knoxville Botanical Garden and Arboretum. The city features over 100 miles of greenways and trails, including popular spots like Lakeshore Park, Suttree’s Landing and World’s Fair Park. And, thanks to its location along the Tennessee River, residents can enjoy a vast number of water activities, including paddling in the historic Mead’s Quarry. Looking to make friends? Knox County Parks & Recreation offers tons of intramural sports and various public and private golf courses. Outdoor Adventures Are Calling From Knoxville, TN
sections.restaurants: Foodies rejoice when eating their way through Knoxville. The downtown area, particularly Gay Street, has a wide variety of dining options, from Southern comfort to Colombian cuisine. Iconic local eateries like Petro’s, a World’s Fair original, and Yassin’s Falafel House, recognized as one of America’s “Nicest Places,” highlight the city’s culinary identity. Take the kids to Cruze Dairy Farm, a family farm with two ice cream shops and a pizza barn that hosts drive-in movie nights. Or, enjoy a campus classic by grabbing a steamed sandwich from Gus’ Good Times Deli. You might even run into football legend Peyton Manning, who stops every time he is here. The craft beer scene has exploded in recent years, evolving from traditional brewpubs to innovative spots like Balter Beeworks and Crafty Bastard Brewery, which emphasize locally sourced ingredients and unique brewing styles. Breweries like Printshop Beer Company and Pretentious Beer Company offer creative experiences, combining craft beer with art workshops and glassblowing. Looking to sip on something on the softer side? There are tons of coffee shops around town, too. What is one of the best places to eat in Knoxville, TN? Knoxville is a foodie city with nearly 100 restaurants, most locally owned, within a square mile of downtown. J.C. Holdway is a popular dinner spot owned by the former executive chef at Blackberry Farm and James Beard Award-winning chef Joseph Lenn. The menu features veggie-forward, Appalachian-inspired dishes that change seasonally. What is the best coffee shop in Knoxville, TN? Whether you are going through the farmers market, strolling down Jackson Avenue or desperately need a delicious cold brew, Awaken is a must-try. Enjoy a coffee flight with your choice of four specialty coffees to ensure you get the full experience! Where is the best happy hour in Knoxville, TN? Head to Old City, home to dozens of restaurants, distilleries and nightlife options. Grab a slice and enjoy live music at Barley’s, or sip on craft beers and cocktails while enjoying elevated comfort food at Southern Grit. In Knoxville, Craft Beer, Live Music Go Hand-in-Hand5 Foodie-Recommended Restaurants to Visit in Knoxville, TN
sections.education: Local residents are highly educated, and students can receive a smart start through Knox County Schools. It is one of the largest districts in Tennessee, serving more than 60,000 students, and offers both traditional and specialized options like magnet and charter schools. There is a significant number of private schools here, many of which are religiously affiliated. Webb School of Knoxville, Knoxville Catholic and Christian Academy of Knoxville are among the highest-ranking private schools. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: Knoxville has several top-tier providers, including Parkwest Medical Center and the University of Tennessee Medical Center. East Tennessee Children’s Hospital is also in the city, keeping the entire family healthy.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: Soak up the arts with a tour of the Knoxville Museum of Art featuring works by regional and national artists. It’s no surprise that this is a city where music matters, considering visitors are invited to sit in on a live Blue Plate Special performance of roots music for WDVX public radio station during their lunch hour at the Knoxville Visitors Center studio.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Knoxville, TN, with kids?
faqs.a: If you’re visiting the city with children, you can’t miss Zoo Knoxville. Spanning 53 acres and home to 1,200 animals, this unexpectedly awesome zoo is worth an entire day’s visit for animal lovers. History and science buffs and generally curious folks will love the McClung Museum of Natural History and Culture, with exhibits that help explain the history of Tennessee and several other places. And both admission and parking are free!

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: Explore the sidewalks of Market Square, home to an unparalleled farmers market and various local boutiques and restaurants. Browse the stacks at locally owned, independent Union Avenue Books, which carries many titles and hosts events throughout the year.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: Ask any of the locals, and they’ll quickly report that Bijou Theatre is the place to go for live performances of all sorts. This beautiful centerpiece of the city boasts a storied history that began in 1909 and has grown ever since. For theater lovers, the Clarence Brown Theatre and Ula Love Doughty Carousel Theatres on the Tennessee campus can’t be missed.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: Knoxville is a foodie city with nearly 100 restaurants, most locally owned, within a square mile of downtown. J.C. Holdway is a popular dinner spot owned by the former executive chef at Blackberry Farm and James Beard Award-winning chef Joseph Lenn. The menu features veggie-forward, Appalachian-inspired dishes that change seasonally.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: Whether you are going through the farmers market, strolling down Jackson Avenue or desperately need a delicious cold brew, Awaken is a must-try. Enjoy a coffee flight with your choice of four specialty coffees to ensure you get the full experience!

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: Head to Old City, home to dozens of restaurants, distilleries and nightlife options. Grab a slice and enjoy live music at Barley’s, or sip on craft beers and cocktails while enjoying elevated comfort food at Southern Grit.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Knoxville, TN?
faqs.a: A college town if there ever was one, this city is home to the University of Tennessee — otherwise known as Rocky Top — with more than 35,000 students that bring a youthful vibe to the city.

faqs.q: Why do people move to Knoxville?
faqs.a: People move to Knoxville for the perfect mix of city life and outdoor adventure. We have a vibrant downtown and great restaurants, and you can easily travel to other places like Gatlinburg or even Nashville. The cost of living is affordable compared to other cities, and we have a real sense of community here. Whether you're into hiking through Knoxville's Urban Wilderness, live music at the Tennessee Theatre, or college football at Neyland Stadium, Knoxville's got something for everyone. Plus, the pace of life strikes a nice balance – it's busy enough to be exciting but laid-back enough to feel like home.

faqs.q: What are your favorite things to do outdoors in Knoxville?
faqs.a: Knoxville has a ton of variety when it comes to outdoor activities. You can visit our parks and greenways for over 50 miles of paved pathways, where you can bike, hike, or stroll with the family. If you're looking for something with a little more adventure, try Navitat Knoxville, an aerial adventure park with ziplines, bridges, and climbs. If you're more of a nature observer, visit Seven Islands State Birding Park, home to over 180 species of birds, or the scenic views from Sharp's Ridge Veterans Memorial Park. No matter what you or your family enjoy, Knoxville has something special for you.

faqs.q: What are your favorite things to do in Knoxville?
faqs.a: Nothing beats catching a University of Tennessee game. College football is a big deal in Knoxville, as you'll find out when we play in Neyland Stadium, but don't forget about the rest of UT's teams, like basketball, baseball, and all our women's sports teams. Go, Vols! I also love to head to Market Square or the Old City with the family for an afternoon enjoying the sights of the city, visiting shops, and eating dinner together at one of our many restaurants. Seeing a concert at the Tennessee Theatre or the Bijou Theatre is another activity you shouldn't miss out on.

faqs.q: What makes Knoxville appealing to families?
faqs.a: One of the biggest draws to Knoxville for families is our top-notch education opportunities. We have an excellent public school system, in addition to several private schools to choose from if that's what your family prefers. And of course, your kids won't have to go far to get an excellent college education with the University of Tennessee, as well as Pellissippi State Community College, right in your backyard. We also have great neighborhoods, tons of things to do, and a welcoming community that makes it easy to feel that you belong here. My family and I are happy to call Knoxville our home.

faqs.q: What makes Knoxville appealing to families?
faqs.a: Knoxville is a great place for families for many reasons, from our great schools and neighborhoods to the more affordable cost of living. There is also a lot for families to keep busy and entertained around town or close by, from the Knoxville Zoo to the East Tennessee History Center, to nearby Dollywood and the Great Smoky Mountains National Park. Whether your family enjoys the arts, history, culture, the outdoors, or good food, Knoxville is an amazing city to live in. Our friendly Southern spirit and sense of community are some of my favorite qualities about Knoxville, and I'm proud to be raising my own family here.

faqs.q: What are your favorite things to do in Knoxville?
Why do people move to Knoxville?
What makes Knoxville appealing to families?
faqs.a: One of the best things about Knoxville is that, while we're a pretty big city, you don't have to go far to experience the great outdoors. Treetop Family Fun, Ijams Nature Center, and Knoxville Urban Wilderness are just a few places you can go for an adventure. There are tons of places to hike or bike within easy driving distance, and you can paddle or kayak the Tennessee River or Meads Quarry. Personally, I'm a fan of taking the family out for a fishing trip along the Holston River or enjoying a University of Tennessee football game at Neyland Stadium.

faqs.q: Why do people move to Knoxville?
faqs.a: It doesn't get much more "Tennessee" than Knoxville. It's a great place to move, whether you're a family, a young person just starting out, or someone a little older looking for a new place to call home. There's opportunity for everyone in Knoxville. Our lower cost of living, mild climate, access to the outdoors, and more draw many people here. We're home to the University of Tennessee, a short drive from the Great Smoky Mountains, and have a great food and entertainment scene. Top it all off with Southern charm and friendly people, and Knoxville should definitely be on your list of places to move.

TN-murfreesboro.description: Murfreesboro is just outside the Nashville city limits and is a thriving community with small-town Southern charm and endless amenities. Murfreesboro is known for its strong economy, lower cost of living and the ease with which residents can get around town, whether to visit the historic downtown district or one of the countless green spaces.

sections.location: Murfreesboro is located about 35 miles southeast of Nashville, accessible via Interstates 24 and 840. The nearest airport, Nashville International Airport, is approximately 30 miles away, offering convenient connections to destinations nationwide and internationally. Nearby major cities include: Nashville is 35 miles northwest, home of the Tennessee Titans and Nashville Predators, a replica of Athena at the Parthenon and plenty of places to hear live music. Chattanooga sits 110 miles southeast, where you can marvel at Lookout Mountain, ride the scenic Incline Railway or walk along the Riverwalk. Clarksville is located 50 miles northwest, a doable trip to discover historic downtown, visit Fort Defiance or catch a show at the Roxy Theater.
sections.economy: Murfreesboro’s convenient location within the Nashville metropolitan area results in a diverse and robust economy with several different industries. Health care, retail trade and education are among the largest industries here, and some of the largest employers in town are Rutherford County Government, Middle Tennessee State University and Nissan North America. The National HealthCare Corporation (NHC) is also headquartered in the city. The cost of living in Murfreesboro is lower than the national average. The median home value in Murfreesboro is $398,921, and the average household income is $107,554. Tennessee residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9.75% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Murfreesboro, TN? Due to Murfreesboro’s convenient location in Middle Tennessee and proximity to Nashville, residents can access various top-notch providers, including Ascension St. Thomas Rutherford Hospital, which has high-performing orthopedics and obstetrics departments.
sections.thingsToDo: You’ll never run out of things to do in Murfreesboro! The city is full of entertainment options, from live music at local venues — like Puckett’s and Hank’s Honky Tonk — to community gatherings at Hop Springs Beer Park. There are plenty of festivals throughout the year that will keep your calendar full, like Uncle Dave Macon Days Roots Rendezvous, the Greenway Art Festival, Jazz Fest and the International Folkfest. Step back in time and explore Stones River National Battlefield, Oaklands Mansion or the restored 19th-century buildings that dot the downtown area. Enjoy the arts? There is plenty of that, too! The Center for the Arts is a popular spot for performances and the Discovery Center at Murfree Spring is a combined children’s museum, nature center and wetlands boardwalk, offering themed exploration areas for children and adults alike. Root, root, root for the home team at any one of the Blue Raiders sporting events at MTSU. What is something you can do for free in Murfreesboro, TN? Take a class at the Linebaugh Public Library. They offer everything from a writer’s group to one-on-one tech help and even coding and master gardening courses. During the warmer months, music fans can attend the free Friday Night Live concert series, which is staged on Friday nights from June to September in downtown Murfreesboro. Dive into local history at Cannonsburgh Village. The exhibits represent approximately 100 years of early life in Tennessee and include a gristmill, schoolhouse, chapel, general store and more. What are some things to do in Murfreesboro, TN, with kids? Murfreesboro has an outstanding arts scene. The cultural network includes sites like the Discovery Center at Murfree Spring, a hands-on children’s museum that attracts about 120,000 children and families annually. Then burn off some energy and go roller skating at SkateCenter West, preferably on a Saturday night when there’s a pizza buffet. Where do locals shop in Murfreesboro, TN? Fountains at Gateway has shops and restaurants and a gorgeous green space. The Plaza is an expansive lawn and gathering place with a splash pad, outdoor movies and an ice skating rink come winter. During the summer months, stock up on local produce at the Rutherford County Farmers Market. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Murfreesboro, TN? See Broadway musicals, plays, magic shows and more at The Center for the Arts, located in a beautiful Italianate building that once housed a post office. For $300, you can get a full or flex season pass and see nearly a dozen shows in a year. Explore 8 Fun Things to Do in Rutherford County, TN5 Can’t-Miss Arts and Culture Experiences in Rutherford County, TennesseeCreativity Transforms Rutherford County into a Colorful Canvas
sections.outdoors: It’s easy to explore the great outdoors when in Murfreesboro, thanks to the city’s wealth of outdoor recreational opportunities available catering to a wide range of interests and ages. Barfield Crescent Park is a fixture in town with its extensive mountain biking trails, a disc golf course and several sports complexes on site, while the Murfreesboro Greenway System is more than 17 miles of trails, bridges and boardwalks perfect for walking, jogging or cycling. Take a step back in time exploring the paths of Stones River National Battlefield. Or, hit the links at several golf courses around town, including Cedar Crest, Indian Hills and Old Fort. Cool off on the water at Percy Priest Lake, which serves as a hub for boating, fishing and water sports. For those who want to blend urban amenities with the outdoors, The Plaza is a green space at the Fountains at Gateway that features an ice skating rink from Thanksgiving to mid-February and a splash pad in the warm-weather months. The Richard Siegel Soccer Complex is a Murfreesboro mainstay for families with young children, with nearly two dozen regulation soccer fields. Recreation in Rutherford County: Fun in the Sun10 Best Running Trails in the Nashville Area
sections.restaurants: It is hard to go hungry with so many delicious dining options in this city. Offering everything from Southern comfort staples at City Cafe and Puckett’s to international flavors at Parthenon Grille and Rock N Roll Sushi. Other restaurants in Murfreesboro, like Jack Brown’s Beer & Burger Joint, cater to burger enthusiasts, while barbecue lovers can savor the flavors of Single Tree and Slick Pig BBQ. Five Senses Restaurant and The Bistro are beloved favorites for a nice night out. Curb your coffee cravings at Red Bicycle Roasting Co. The cocktail scene is excellent, too. The Velvet Room is a members-only speakeasy offering a selection of cocktails, wine and small plates. Hop Springs Beer Park is a massive venue filled with craft beer and live music. What is one of the best places to eat in Murfreesboro, TN? Upscale dining and casual hospitality meet at Primrose Table. Southern staples are elevated, and menu mainstays include deviled eggs with lobster filling and cornbread panzanella. What is the best coffee shop in Murfreesboro, TN? Get your caffeine fix at the Dapper Owl, a locally-owned shop with plenty of space to sit and stay awhile. Be sure to get a house-made bagel or pastry to accompany one of their signature lattes. Where is the best happy hour in Murfreesboro, TN? One of Nineteen Cocktails and Raw Bar is a great place to imbibe in craft cocktails. They have a list of classic cocktails, including several martini options, and live up to their name with 19 different bourbon barrel picks. Rise and Shine in the Greater Nashville RegionFill Your Day with Great Places to Eat in Murfreesboro and Rutherford CountyMeet the Small Town in Tennessee Serving Up Big Brews
sections.education: Middle Tennessee State University, with more than 20,000 students — the largest undergraduate population in the state — brings excitement to the city with its many sports and cultural offerings. Go, Blue Raiders! Rutherford County Schools, which has more than 50,000 students, consistently ranks among the best school districts in the state and continues to grow, providing state-of-the-art facilities and student experiences. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: Due to Murfreesboro’s convenient location in Middle Tennessee and proximity to Nashville, residents can access various top-notch providers, including Ascension St. Thomas Rutherford Hospital, which has high-performing orthopedics and obstetrics departments.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: Take a class at the Linebaugh Public Library. They offer everything from a writer’s group to one-on-one tech help and even coding and master gardening courses. During the warmer months, music fans can attend the free Friday Night Live concert series, which is staged on Friday nights from June to September in downtown Murfreesboro. Dive into local history at Cannonsburgh Village. The exhibits represent approximately 100 years of early life in Tennessee and include a gristmill, schoolhouse, chapel, general store and more.

faqs.q: What are some things to do in Murfreesboro, TN, with kids?
faqs.a: Murfreesboro has an outstanding arts scene. The cultural network includes sites like the Discovery Center at Murfree Spring, a hands-on children’s museum that attracts about 120,000 children and families annually. Then burn off some energy and go roller skating at SkateCenter West, preferably on a Saturday night when there’s a pizza buffet.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: Fountains at Gateway has shops and restaurants and a gorgeous green space. The Plaza is an expansive lawn and gathering place with a splash pad, outdoor movies and an ice skating rink come winter. During the summer months, stock up on local produce at the Rutherford County Farmers Market.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: See Broadway musicals, plays, magic shows and more at The Center for the Arts, located in a beautiful Italianate building that once housed a post office. For $300, you can get a full or flex season pass and see nearly a dozen shows in a year.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: Upscale dining and casual hospitality meet at Primrose Table. Southern staples are elevated, and menu mainstays include deviled eggs with lobster filling and cornbread panzanella.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: Get your caffeine fix at the Dapper Owl, a locally-owned shop with plenty of space to sit and stay awhile. Be sure to get a house-made bagel or pastry to accompany one of their signature lattes.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: One of Nineteen Cocktails and Raw Bar is a great place to imbibe in craft cocktails. They have a list of classic cocktails, including several martini options, and live up to their name with 19 different bourbon barrel picks.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Murfreesboro, TN?
faqs.a: The city is home to Middle Tennessee State University, one of the state’s largest undergraduate universities.

faqs.q: What makes Murfreesboro appealing to families?
faqs.a: Murfreesboro is a fantastic place for families because of our blend of educational opportunities and recreational activities. Our city is home to top-rated grade schools known for their academic excellence, as well as Middle Tennessee State University. There are even more colleges and universities just a short drive away in Nashville. I love taking my young kids to the Discovery Center, a children's museum with interactive, hands-on exhibits. If your family is outdoorsy, the Murfreesboro Greenway System provides miles of trails perfect for biking and walking. There's a lot to do, see, and get involved in for your family in Murfreesboro, which makes it a great environment for young and growing families alike.

faqs.q: What are your favorite things to do outdoors in Murfreesboro?
faqs.a: No matter what your interests are when it comes to outdoor activities, Murfreesboro probably has it. The Stones River Greenway has miles of scenic trails perfect for walking, running, or biking along the picturesque Stones River. If you're a history enthusiast, a neat place to explore is the Stones River National Battlefield, where hiking trails weave through significant Civil War sites. Barfield Crescent Park has everything from hiking paths and disc golf to campgrounds, so it's a great place to take the family for an outing. I would also recommend spending time along the boardwalk of Murfree Spring Wetlands to experience a unique ecosystem. It's right next to the Discovery Center children's museum, which is another fun activity.

faqs.q: What are your favorite things to do in Murfreesboro?
faqs.a: My family and I are always looking for fun things to do around town, and we've discovered some that offer a great time for everyone. Climb Murfreesboro is the largest indoor rock-climbing gym in the entire state of Tennessee, and with a dedicated kids' area plus fitness classes for the adults, it's an amazing place to burn some energy as a family. We also enjoy visiting the Earth Experience at the Middle Tennessee Museum of Natural History, where they have a working paleontology lab. GO USA Fun Park is another must-do, with go-karts, batting cages, mini golf, and more—you can easily spend a whole Saturday there.

faqs.q: Why do people move to Murfreesboro?
What makes Murfreesboro appealing to families?
Why do people move to Murfreesboro?
faqs.a: Murfreesboro has a wonderful blend of small-town charm and modern amenities, which makes it an appealing place for all kinds of people to call home. We have a lower cost of living compared to the national average, with reasonably priced housing options that appeal to families and young professionals alike. We're also fortunate to have Middle Tennessee State University, which offers educational opportunities and a lot of cultural events for the community. Many students attend MTSU and enjoy the area so much that they end up staying long-term. We also have lots of parks and green spaces, which is great if you're into the outdoors. Plus, being just a short drive from Nashville, Murfreesboro makes it easy to access big-city entertainment while having a more relaxed, suburban atmosphere for your day-to-day life.

faqs.q: What are your favorite things to do in Murfreesboro?
Why do people move to Murfreesboro?
What makes Murfreesboro appealing to families?
faqs.a: What I think makes Murfreesboro appealing to families is that it offers that ideal mix of suburban comfort and urban amenities. We have plenty of parks and recreational facilities to get outdoors, such as the expansive Barfield Crescent Park, which features hiking trails, playgrounds, and picnic areas. Middle Tennessee State University adds a lot to our cultural scene with concerts, theater productions, and sporting events. Plus, Murfreesboro's proximity to Nashville means you and your family can enjoy all the big-city attractions when you want to, while living in a community known for its safety and strong neighborhood connections.

faqs.q: Why do people move to Murfreesboro?
faqs.a: Many people move to Murfreesboro to attend Middle Tennessee State University or because it's close to Nashville but more affordable. I believe a reason many people stay is our community-focused lifestyle. We're a college town, so our student population helps keep the city lively. There's always something to do, from local festivals to farmers markets, and our "melting pot" community means we have a lot of diverse dining options to choose from. As a fast-growing city, there's a lot of opportunity for those willing to work for it, which adds to Murfreesboro's appeal. If you're looking for suburban charm with close access to a major city, Murfreesboro may be the place for you.

sections.location: Nashville sits in north-central Tennessee along the Cumberland River, within a half-day’s drive of much of the eastern United States. Interstates 40, 24 and 65 converge here, making road trips to Atlanta, Louisville, Memphis and the Great Smoky Mountains straightforward. Nashville International Airport (BNA) is a major regional hub with nonstop flights to dozens of U.S. destinations and several international gateways. That connectivity is a big perk for professionals who travel frequently, touring musicians, and families who want easy access to both coasts without living in an ultra-expensive megacity.
sections.economy: Nashville has become one of the country’s standout growth stories, evolving from “just” a music town into a diversified economic powerhouse. Health care is the anchor: the regional health care industry contributes tens of billions of dollars annually and supports hundreds of thousands of direct and indirect jobs, helped by heavyweights like HCA Healthcare and Vanderbilt University Medical Center. Beyond medicine, the city’s economy spans automobile production, finance and insurance, higher education, logistics, corporate headquarters and a rapidly expanding tech and innovation scene. Major employers and investors include Vanderbilt University and Medical Center, Amazon’s Operations Center of Excellence, and a cluster of health-tech and software companies, with Oracle and others building sizable presences. This mix keeps unemployment relatively low and professional pathways diverse, from bedside nursing to product management.
sections.thingsToDo: Nashville’s live music reputation is legendary, but the experience is broader than just honky-tonks on Lower Broadway. You’ll find intimate writers rounds, jazz sets, indie rock shows and church-choir-level performances in venues scattered across neighborhoods like Midtown, The Gulch, East Nashville and Wedgewood-Houston. Districts such as Printer’s Alley and Demonbreun Street deliver dense clusters of nightlife, while Music Row offers a behind-the-scenes peek at the industry through historic studios and landmarks.​ Beyond the stage, the city’s cultural life includes art galleries, public murals, museums and festivals that celebrate everything from film to food to local makers. Families can spend weekends exploring the Nashville Zoo, visiting science and history museums, or heading downtown for kid-friendly events and riverfront play spaces. For many residents, the real joy is the constant calendar of low- or no-cost happenings, from neighborhood street fairs to seasonal outdoor concerts.
sections.outdoors: For a city best known for neon lights and guitar riffs, Nashville offers a surprising amount of green. An expanding greenway system follows rivers and creeks, linking parks and neighborhoods and giving runners, cyclists and stroller-pushers miles of off-street paths.​ Nearby state parks, lakes and rolling hills make it easy to trade skyline views for trailheads on any given weekend. Residents regularly kayak or paddleboard on area waterways, hike wooded trails, or head to regional parks for camping and mountain biking, taking advantage of the city’s mild climate and central location.
sections.restaurants: Nashville’s food scene has grown far beyond hot chicken, although you’ll find plenty of that, too. Neighborhoods like East Nashville, Germantown, 12South, The Nations and Hillsboro Village are packed with chef-driven restaurants, neighborhood coffee shops, craft breweries and dessert spots, each with its own character.​ From classic “meat-and-three” diners to sleek rooftop bars and global street food, the city gives residents lots of ways to eat well at different price points. Many spots double as community gathering places – think live music on patios, trivia nights at breweries and cafés that host readings or maker pop-ups – reinforcing that social, collaborative energy Nashville is known for. The Soul of the City What ultimately sets Nashville apart is the mindset. The city has long attracted creative people – songwriters, designers, founders, medical innovators – and that has shaped a culture that’s collaborative rather than cutthroat. Newcomers often remark on how easy it is to meet people, plug into professional networks or find a volunteer role that matters. For young professionals, families and career-changers alike, Nashville offers a mix of big-city opportunity and deeply human scale. It’s a place where you can chase ambitious goals, still recognize your neighbors at the coffee shop, and spend a lot of evenings outdoors or listening to live music that costs less than a big-ticket arena show.
sections.education: Nashville is a serious education hub, home to one of the nation’s most respected universities and a broad mix of public and private options. Metro Nashville Public Schools serves families across the city with neighborhood schools, magnet programs and specialized academies, while an array of independent schools offer faith-based, college-prep and Montessori-style options. On the higher-ed side, Vanderbilt University anchors the landscape with nationally ranked programs and a major research footprint, while Belmont University, Tennessee State University, Lipscomb University and several other institutions add depth in fields like music, business, health care and education. These campuses collectively attract talent from around the world and fuel the city’s innovation ecosystem, internships and early-career opportunities.

faqs.q: What is Nashville best known for?
faqs.a: Nashville is best known as “Music City,” the global center of country music and a major hub for songwriting, recording and live performance, but it’s also recognized as a leading health care and higher-ed capital.​

faqs.q: Is Nashville a good place to move for jobs?
faqs.a: Yes. Nashville’s economy is anchored by health care, higher education, corporate headquarters, logistics and a growing tech sector, with major employers like Vanderbilt University Medical Center, HCA Healthcare, Amazon and others expanding their footprints.

faqs.q: What is the cost of living like in Nashville?
faqs.a: The cost of living is higher than many smaller Southern cities, especially for housing, but remains more affordable than coastal megacities with similar job opportunities. Many residents feel the strong job market and lack of state income tax help balance the equation.​

faqs.q: What are some popular neighborhoods in Nashville?
faqs.a: Popular areas include Downtown and The Gulch for walkable, urban living; East Nashville and Germantown for historic homes and creative energy; 12South and Hillsboro Village for a village-like feel; and West End and Midtown for proximity to Vanderbilt, Music Row and nightlife.​

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Nashville?
faqs.a: Nashville has hot, humid summers, mild winters and long, comfortable shoulder seasons in spring and fall, making outdoor festivals and patio dining possible much of the year.

faqs.q: Are there good schools and universities in Nashville?
faqs.a: Yes. Families have access to Metro Nashville Public Schools, numerous private schools and several higher-ed institutions, including Vanderbilt University, Belmont University and Tennessee State University.

faqs.q: What cultural attractions does Nashville offer beyond music?
faqs.a: In addition to its music venues, Nashville features museums, public art, galleries, theaters, a zoo and a busy calendar of food, arts and neighborhood festivals across the city.​

faqs.q: How far is Nashville from the mountains or the beach?
faqs.a: The Great Smoky Mountains are roughly a 3.5–4-hour drive east, while Gulf Coast beaches are typically a full-day drive, making both long-weekend and extended-vacation trips manageable.​

faqs.q: Is Nashville family-friendly?
faqs.a: Yes. Parks, greenways, family attractions, strong health care access and a wide range of schools, plus an emphasis on community events, make Nashville an appealing choice for families at many life stages.​

TX-abilene.description: A small city with a big personality, Abilene, TX, is quintessentially Texas. With affordable housing, friendly neighborhoods and plenty of places to explore, residents enjoy a relaxed pace without sacrificing excellent amenities and a top-notch quality of life.

sections.location: Abilene is a charming city in west-central Texas, approximately 180 miles west of Fort Worth, easily accessible via Interstate 20. Abilene Regional Airport is just 6 miles from downtown, providing easy connections to major cities via American Eagle and other carriers. Nearby major cities include: Fort Worth can be found 180 miles east, where you can explore the Stockyard, Sundance Square and the Fort Worth Zoo. Dallas sits 200 miles east, home of the Sixth Floor Museum, Dallas Museum of Art and AT&T Stadium. Lubbock is about 200 miles northwest, offering the Buddy Holly Center, National Ranching Heritage Center and the Lubbock Symphony Orchestra.
sections.economy: Health care, education and manufacturing and information technology are a few of the significant industries here; however, the city has a diverse economy where industries all over the board are growing. Some of the largest employers in the area include Dyess Air Force Base, Abilene Independent School District and Hendrick Health. Thanks to its massive network of young professionals injecting the city with innovation, Abilene has an ever-changing and evolving economy. The city is proud to claim the largest network of young professionals in West Texas, and there are more than 570 members in Abilene Young Professionals. The cost of living in Abilene is below the national average. The median home value in Abilene comes right in at $182,377, and the average household income is $61,083. There’s no state income tax in Texas. In 2025, the sales tax rate in Abilene is 8.25%. Who are the major health care providers in Abilene, TX? Local residents are in good health, thanks to Hendrick Medical Center, which is recognized as one of the best hospitals in Texas and one of the region’s largest. Why Abilene, TX, Is a Mecca for Young ProfessionalsWe’re Hiring: 4 Growing Industries in Abilene, TXWhy Abilene, TX, Is a Great Place To Launch Your Small Business
sections.thingsToDo: As they say, everything is bigger in Texas, including the endless number of things to do in Abilene, TX. You can live out your Western dream by attending a rodeo, visiting a working ranch or two-stepping at an authentic dance hall. But, of course, there are myriad of other ways to have fun here. The city has a massive arts presence and plenty of big-city amenities, like museums and galleries, performing arts organizations like Broadway, a ballet, and a philharmonic orchestra, not to mention public art installations and coworking spaces for artists at the Center for Contemporary Arts (CCA). The city is also home to the Historic Paramount Theatre. Abilene is a great place for music lovers to plant roots, as the city is home to nearly a dozen different live music venues, a monthly ArtWalk and the annual Outlaws & Legends Music Fest, which features two days of live country music on The Back Porch of Texas. For families, there’s no better way to get to know the city than by embarking on a Storybook Sculpture Scavenger Hunt. There’s history on every corner here, from the historic downtown to the Swenson House mansion and grounds. What is something you can do for free in Abilene, TX? Check out the downtown ArtWalk, which happens on the second Thursday of every month and includes live art demonstrations and music. What are some things to do in Abilene, TX, with kids? Hand feed giraffes at Abilene Zoo or bounce around on trampolines at Maxx Air. Where do locals shop in Abilene, TX? The city is known for its treasure trove of vintage and antique stores. There are dozens of shops stocked with items from days gone by, from mall-style outlets, like Vintage Marketplace, to boutique-style stores, like Living Chic. Abilene even has a handful of flea markets for those who like a mix of old and new. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Abilene, TX? The city has a vast arts presence for a city of its size, with museums, galleries, performing arts organizations, a ballet and a philharmonic orchestra. A staple in the community is the Paramount Theatre, operated by a nonprofit organization that showcases classic, family and art films and live theater productions. 12 Things You Must Do in Abilene, TXAbilene, TX, is for Art LoversThings to Do After Work in Abilene, TX
sections.outdoors: There are plenty of things to do outdoors in Abilene, TX, from hiking and biking on numerous trails to golfing and fishing at local lakes. Summers here can be warm, but there are plenty of ways to cool off. Picnic on the shady banks of Elm Creek, rent a kayak at Lake Abilene or take a dip in the historic swimming pool at Abilene State Park. Located less than 20 miles from downtown, the 530-acre state park next to Lake Abilene provides ample opportunities for hiking, swimming, boating, fishing, horseback riding and camping. Fishing and boating are allowed at Lake Kirby Park & Nature Play Area, which also features pavilions and a beautiful boardwalk that’s popular among birdwatchers. At Lake Fort Phantom, you can swim, fish, ski and even sail and windsurf. You can also cool off at Adventure Cove, a pirate-themed waterpark with slides, a lazy river, a pool and concessions. 10 Activities for Outdoor Lovers in Abilene, TX
sections.restaurants: The local dining scene isn’t just steak and potatoes. Foodies appreciate the wide range of locally-owned eateries and can find award-winning restaurants, international cuisine, coffeehouses and food trucks. Foodies can find a sampling of Texas flavors with a twist, including world-famous Texas barbecue. Cowboy-style, mesquite-smoked, fall-apart-tender meats define Texas barbecue, and naturally, the city offers several choice establishments to sample its unique flavors. But, of course, Abilene goes beyond the basics, too. There is a huge foodie scene here with everything from farm-to-table favorites to international delights. Don’t know where to start? That’s okay! Abilene Food Tours pairs craft beers, wines and cocktails with samplings from local restaurants. Venture down to Abilene’s SoDA district, where you’ll find more than a dozen wineries, bars, breweries, cafes and restaurants. What is one of the best places to eat in Abilene, TX? The Mill is a wine bar with great outdoor seating. Sangrias, beer, cigars and snacky dinner items (think charcuterie, sliders, flatbreads) are also on the menu. What is the best coffee shop in Abilene, TX? Mezamiz Coffee House is a gathering place that also serves wine and beer and hosts events like open mic nights and doodle nights so that you can draw in a sketchpad alongside others. 7 Foodie-Recommended Restaurants to Visit in Abilene, TX
sections.education: Abilene schools get an A for effort — and for test scores! The Abilene Independent School District (AISD) and the Wylie Independent School District (WISD) primarily serve the city. Because of the deep military roots in the region, schools like Dyess Elementary School are recognized for their commitment to supporting the military community and students and their families. There are also several religiously affiliated and preparatory schools in the area.
