faqs.a: The city is home to both Hardin-Simmons University and Abilene Christian University.

TX-college-station.description: A dynamic city with a strong sense of community, College Station is anchored by Texas A&M University and is all about the Aggies. The city offers excellent schools, an affordable cost of living and a thriving job market. But there's plenty of fun to be had with family-friendly amenities and great dining options; it's a great place to live and grow.

sections.location: College Station is a vibrant city in east-central Texas, right along State Highway 6, with U.S. Route 190 nearby. The nearest airport — Easterwood Airport — is right in the city, making travel easy. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Houston is 95 miles southeast, where you can tour Space Center Houston or the Houston Museum of Natural Science. Austin sits 105 miles west, famous for the Texas State Capitol and the live music scene on Sixth Street. Waco is about 90 miles north, home to the Magnolia Market at the Silos and the Dr. Pepper Museum.
sections.economy: Those interested in finding a job here are in luck. Texas A&M University is obviously the most significant employer in the community, with about 13,000 employees, but you can also find many positions in the biotechnology, manufacturing, agricultural and tourism industries. But, back to education. Education is the largest sector in the area, with companies like the College Station Independent School District and Aggie Athletics, and it is home to the Texas A&M University System’s administrative offices. Texas A&M University opened in 1876 as the state’s first public institution of higher learning. Today, Texas A&M is a leading research institution. The university’s triple designation as a land-, sea- and space-grant university is a point of pride and demonstrates the breadth of research on campus. The Texas A&M College of Engineering is consistently among the nation’s top public undergraduate and graduate engineering programs in U.S. News & World Report’s annual rankings. Fun fact: The U.S. Postal Station designated this Texas city as College Station in 1877 because of the train station located west of the campus. The cost of living in College Station is generally lower than the national average. The median home value in College Station is $292,882, and the average household income is $89,799. Texas residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in College Station, TX? Baylor Scott and White Medical Center-College Station is a high-performance provider in the region.
sections.thingsToDo: It’s hard not to get swept up by the energy of game day at Kyle Field, the home of the Aggies! Locals can join in on all the storied campus traditions like tailgating and the “Midnight Yell” that’s held at the stadium the night before every home game, hyping up fans. About an hour and a half before kickoff, more than 2,500 Corps of Cadets and Fightin’ Texas Aggie Band members march in formation from the Quad through the Fan Zone and into Kyle Field. With nearly 103,000 seats, Kyle Field is the largest stadium in the SEC — and the fourth-largest in the country! The arts scene is also worth cheering for. Stay up to date with the Brazos Valley Symphony Orchestra’s concert calendar. The orchestra puts on some fantastic shows, like one that incorporated cirque arts and another that imagined what it would be like if The Beatles performed alongside a symphony orchestra. Locals can also grab tickets to Broadway musicals and performances at Opas at Texas A&M University. The city is also home to the George H.W. Bush Presidential Library, where you can see a section of the Berlin Wall, and the Museum of the American G.I., which has an impressive collection of WWI and WWII military vehicles. What is something you can do for free in College Station, TX? Pay a visit to Bottle Cap Alley, a quirky stretch on the Texas A&M campus paved with hundreds of thousands of beer and soda bottle caps that have been collected over the decades. What are some things to do in College Station, TX, with kids? Kids can learn to climb on indoor walls at Stone Co. Climbing, which also has classes, private lessons, competitions and yoga sessions for adults or play with their friends at a spot like Stephen C. Beachy Central Park, which has a splash pad, batting cages, playgrounds and shaded picnic areas. During the holiday season, visit The North Pole’s Texas outpost — Santa’s Wonderland — that twinkles with 4 million dazzling lights and has fun activities like train rides, a snow playground, tubing, plus country Christmas music. In the summer, beat the heat at a spot like the Adamson Lagoon, which is a tropical-themed mini water park with a giant Olympic-sized swimming pool and a couple of water slides. Where do locals shop in College Station, TX? Markets on The Green is a monthly vendor market at Century Square where you can find everything from local artisan goods to Lucchese boots. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in College Station, TX? Wolf Pen Creek Amphitheater is an open-air concert venue with lawn seating for up to 7,000 people set in a 63-acre park.
sections.outdoors: A perk of living in a college town is having access to all the fun amenities on the campus. Visit The Gardens, a 27-acre public teaching space at Texas A&M University. Some must-sees include the wine grape garden (the Texas wine scene is booming, y’all!), the colorful rose garden and the Mexican Heritage garden rich with Mexican and Spanish architecture and horticulture. If the gardens spark some creativity, you can take a class at the Benz School of Floral Design. Participate in a friendly competition at the Research Park & Disc Golf Course, which features an 18-hole course as well as some scenic ponds where you can spot adorable ducks and turtles wading through the water. Nature lovers can enjoy a close-to-home camping getaway at Sam Houston National Forest (90 miles from College Station), where you can hike, swim, fish and recharge in the great outdoors.
sections.restaurants: You’re in the land of barbecue! Chow down at some down-home barbecue joints like 1775 Texas Barbecue and Cooper’s Old Time Pit Bar-B-Que, which serves slow-smoked meats and delectable sides. Some other local favorite restaurants run the gamut from the hot chicken and ribs at The Cookshack to Cajun and Creole dishes at Remnants of Nawlins in neighboring Bryan and Chinese and Thai food at Shun De Mom. For a night out, the Northgate Entertainment District is an excellent spot for beer, cocktails and dinner with friends. You can also sample the Texas wine scene at Messina Hof Estate Winery, which pours award-winning vino from the Lone Star State. What is one of the best places to eat in College Station, TX? Make brunch a weekend ritual at Stella Southern Cafe, where you can enjoy fluffy, powder sugar-dusted New Orleans-style beignets, veggie frittatas and, hear us out, French toast made with biscuits. What is the best coffee shop in College Station, TX? Sweet Eugene’s is a College Station staple. From coffee classics to specialty sips, like a white chocolate raspberry espresso, there’s something to satisfy your morning cravings. Where is the best place for happy hour in College Station, TX? Pair margs or ranch waters (tequila + sparkling water) with nachos and street-style tacos for happy hour bliss at Chimy’s. P.S. You’ll find the happiest of all happy hours on Mondays and Tuesdays when the deals run all day long.
sections.education: Thanks to the university’s popularity and approximately 73,000 enrolled students, College Station has a college-town energy that rolls over into the city’s highly rated grade schools and safe neighborhoods. The amenities and quality of life available here have made the city increasingly attractive to young families, who can pick from more than a dozen private schools in the College Station and Bryan area. The College Station Independent School District (CSISD) serves the city’s public education needs and has a student-to-teacher ratio of 14:1. TX-flower-mound.description: Flower Mound might be a perfect place to live with suburban charm and endless amenities. With top-rated schools, a strong economy and plenty of parks, Flower Mound is ideal for families and professionals.

sections.location: Flower Mound offers the perfect blend of suburban peace with quick access to big-city excitement. It’s tucked just northwest of Dallas, where State Highway 121 runs through, keeping it well-connected to the sprawling Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex. The Dallas Fort Worth International Airport is just 10 miles away, offering a massive hub for domestic and international flights to get you wherever you need to go. Geographically, the city sits among the gently rolling hills of North Texas. Ready to venture out? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Dallas is just 28 miles southeast, where you can wander the vibrant arts district, cheer on the Mavericks at the American Airlines Center or shop till you drop at NorthPark Center. Fort Worth sits 33 miles southwest, home to the historic Stockyards, the Kimbell Art Museum and the Fort Worth Botanic Garden. Denton is about 15 miles north, buzzing with the University of North Texas energy, a lively music scene and the quaint Denton Square.
sections.economy: More than 2,000 businesses operate out of Flower Mound, meaning the city has a diverse and robust economy. There are a variety of industry jobs available in the area, but three of the largest sectors are technology, manufacturing and professional services. Some of the major employers include Lewisville Independent School District, Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital-Flower Mound and Communications Test Design, Inc. (CTDI). The cost of living here is slightly higher than the national average; however, some aspects of the cost of living, like utilities, are lower. The median home value is $476,609, and the average household income is $216,000. There’s no state income tax in Texas. In 2025, the sales tax rate is 8.25%. Who are the major health care providers in Flower Mound, TX? Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital-Flower Mound is the major hospital. The city is also centrally located, and residents can access various Dallas-Fort Worth providers.
sections.thingsToDo: Living here is fun and there are endless things to do. This family-friendly town is ideal for those looking to establish roots in a place with plenty to offer, especially those who love spending time outdoors or seek a close-knit community. The city’s historic downtown is lined with everything from coffee shops and art galleries to wine shops and food markets. The Riverwalk at Central Park is a 158-acre mixed-use development with a central tree-lined waterway connecting residential and retail spaces and an amphitheater and shared community chapel. The Shops at Highland Village is the area’s premier shopping center. Other spots, like Parker Square, a shopping and dining district, are home to local boutiques and restaurants. Outside of shopping, residents can participate in an endless number of community-centric events and activities. There are a number of annual events and festivals in town, including Concerts in the Park and the Arts Festival at Heritage Park. What is something you can do for free in Flower Mound, TX? Take a 3-D printing, sewing or embroidery class in the Creation Lab at the Flower Mound Public Library. What are some things to do with kids in Flower Mound, TX? The Flower Mound StoryWalk at Heritage Park allows little ones to begin on the first page of a story and follow numbered signs around the park. There is a new selection each month. Where do locals shop in Flower Mound, TX? The Flower Mound Farmers Market takes place every Sunday from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. and offers fresh produce, artisanal goods and live music by the Parker Square gazebo. What is the best place for live music or performing arts in Flower Mound, TX? Enjoy live music and monthly Pickers Circles at Guitars and Growlers. The Flower Mound Community Orchestra and Philharmonic frequently holds community events in various musical styles, from classical to contemporary.
sections.outdoors: Outdoor amenities are abundant. The city is situated on the northern edge of Grapevine Lake, an 8,000-acre reservoir where people can swim, sail and fish. Or splash around at the Community Activity Center’s Outdoor Water Park all summer long. The CAC Park includes two large water slides, a 60-gallon dumping bucket, interactive water features, a lazy river, a waterplay structure and more. Prefer to keep dry? The city has access to several top-notch golf courses, including Bridlewood Golf Club and Tour 18 Dallas. Residents can stretch their legs along the impressive trail system that runs through Flower Mound. The 64 miles of multi-purpose trails link together parks and neighborhoods, providing spots for walking, jogging and biking. Family-friendly fun extends to the local parks system, too. The city has 57 different parks and 35 playgrounds, providing great places to burn off some energy. Along the north shore of Grapevine Lake is Twin Coves Park and Campground, which offers playgrounds, pavilions, volleyball courts, disc golf, nature trails and camping grounds. Green Acres Farm Memorial Park is a great spot to birdwatch and see monarchs, native plants and a prairie restoration area. Murrell Park is located on the north side of Grapevine Lake and provides visitors with picnic and fishing areas, campgrounds and hiking and biking trails.
sections.restaurants: Restaurants in Flower Mound have plenty of flavors to savor, from casual to upscale dining. There is a diverse culinary scene that will cater to every craving, from Texas barbecue to farm-to-table fare. Stroll along Restaurant Row at the Flower Mound River Walk or take in Tex-Mex favorites like Anamia’s, Mi Dia from Scratch and Mena’s Grill. Underdogs and Whiskey & Smoke can’t be missed for classic Southern staples. The local area also offers several fine dining spots, including Tycoon, Rustico and 1845 Taste Texas. Local Pint is a locally-owned restaurant with plenty of craft beer options, perfect for happy hour. The Tavern at Lakeside offers an impressive selection of craft beer and cocktails and has a patio perfect for enjoying warm Texas weather. What is one of the best places to eat in Flower Mound, TX? Enjoy dinner and a movie (simultaneously) at Moviehouse & Eatery Flower Mound, which has a fun menu with pink palomas, bacon onion jam burgers and more. What is the best coffee shop in Flower Mound, TX? 151 Coffee is known for its good vibes and signature macchiato made with salted caramel-infused milk. Where is the best place for happy hour in Flower Mound, TX? Fiori is an excellent spot for food and drink specials, from a daily happy hour to half-price bottles of wine on Wednesdays. Be sure to book dinner on Tuesday for a pizza, salad and bottle of wine for $40.
sections.education: Schools in Flower Mound provide families with access to excellent educational opportunities. Several highly rated school districts serve the area, though most of the city is served by the Lewisville Independent School District (LISD) and Argyle Independent School District (AISD). These districts consistently receive high marks for academic excellence, graduation rates and overall quality. Residents have access to several quality private and charter schools as well. faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Flower Mound, TX?
faqs.a: Although no colleges or universities are located in the city itself, several major higher-ed institutions, including Southern Methodist University, Texas Christian University and The University of North Texas, are within driving distance.

TX-midland.description: Standing as a beacon against the vast expanse of West Texas is Midland, a community with a booming energy sector, diverse cultural heritage and a thriving economy, where innovation meets tradition. From stunning sunsets to warm hospitality, Midland perfectly captures authentic Texas living.

sections.location: Midland is a bustling city in west Texas, about halfway between Fort Worth and El Paso, right along Interstate 20. The nearest airport, Midland International Air and Space Port is right in the city, making travel super convenient. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Odessa is just 20 miles west, known for the Presidential Archives and Leadership Library. Abilene sits 150 miles east, offering the Grace Museum and the Abilene Zoo. Lubbock is about 120 miles north, home to Texas Tech University and the Buddy Holly Center.
sections.economy: Located in the nation’s largest oil-producing area, the oil industry understandably dominates Midland’s economy; however, other sectors in the region thrive as well. There are plenty of opportunities in industries like mining, health care and education, as well as professional services like business and finance. Some of the largest employers include Chevron, Pioneer Natural Resources and Midland Independent School District. The cost of living in Midland is lower than the national average. The median home value in Midland is $291,451, and the average household income is $137,120. Texas residents do not pay a state income tax, and Midland residents can expect a 7.5-8.5% sales tax rate, depending on whether they’re in the city or county in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Midland, TX? Midland residents can access several regional health care providers, including Midland Memorial Hospital and nearby Odessa’s Medical Center Hospital.
sections.thingsToDo: Everything’s bigger in Texas, including the amount of things to do in Midland! The city has several museums, including the Permian Basin Petroleum Museum, which showcases the area’s oil industry history, and the Museum of the Southwest, which features art, history and science exhibits, including the Marian Blakemore Planetarium. The George W. Bush Family Home gives a glimpse into the lives of the former president and his family, who grew up in Midland. The Haley Library & History Center offers insights into Texas and Southwestern history. For entertainment, residents can catch a Midland RockHounds baseball game, enjoy a classic drive-in movie at Big Sky Drive-In Theater or attend a performance at the Yucca Theater’s Summer Mummers. Midland is a community-minded city, hosting several events throughout the year — like the Celebration of the Arts festival — and unique shopping experiences at the Micro Market. Looking for something kid-approved? Families can enjoy the Fredda Turner Durham Children’s Museum and Cinergy Cinemas, which have tons of arcade games and an escape room to accompany your movie. There are also plenty of pieces of public art around town, and the Midland Downtown Farmers Market provides a delicious experience. What is something you can do for free in Midland, TX? Spend some time soaking up the sun and appreciating the animal art at the Sculpture Garden at the Museum of the Southwest. What are some things to do in Midland, TX, with kids? Take your kiddos out to the ballgame to see the Midland RockHounds play. Or visit the Doug Russell Aquatic Center, which has slides, cabanas, splash pads and plenty of room to play Marco Polo. Where do locals shop in Midland, TX? Holiday cheer abounds year-round at Miss Cayce’s Wonderland, a seasonal home decor shop that’s gained quite a following in Midland and beyond. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Midland, TX? The Wagner Noël Performing Arts Center is a state-of-the-art facility that hosts everything from Broadway productions to plays, comedians and musicians. Go There: Experience Rich History and Unforgettable Memories in Midland, TX
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities are abundant in Midland, and residents can enjoy the region’s natural beauty. There are 40 parks around town, spanning 1,364 acres. Families love Centennial Park, which features a splash pad, a playground and open green spaces perfect for picnics and a game of tag. For nature lovers, the Sibley Nature Center in Hogan Park provides 49 acres of trails, wildlife viewing areas and educational exhibits showcasing the flora and fauna of the Llano Estacado. The I-20 Wildlife Preserve is an 86-acre urban playground where visitors can hike, birdwatch and observe native wildlife. Play a round of disc golf at E.P. Cowden Park and Beal Park, and the Hogan’s Run Off-Leash Dog Park provides plenty of space for both pets and their owners to enjoy the outdoors.
sections.restaurants: From craft cocktails and local art at the Blue Door to authentic Mexican flavors from Taco Divino, Midland residents are well-fed, thanks to the city’s growing foodie scene. For something sweet, Susie’s South Forty Confections Candy Factory is a local legend, while Texas Sun Winery offers a selection of wines and a picturesque patio. Eat surrounded by the sounds of waterfalls and lush greenery inside a greenhouse at Cafe at the Gardens, located inside Alldredge Gardens. The Tailgate is one oversized patio that features food trucks to satisfy your cravings, big screens to stream sports and a large stage for live music. Midland has a growing craft beverage scene, with breweries like Tall City Brewing offering West Texas-inspired brews. (And it’s pet-friendly!) Trendy brunch spots like Fair to Midland and Wall Street’s Sunday Brunch provide a lively atmosphere to accompany your meal. Midland residents are serious about their love of coffee, and there are several cozy spots around town, like Mulberry Cafe. What is one of the best places to eat in Midland, TX? Enjoy fine, fresh, creative dining at Opal’s Table. The restaurant pairs Texas mainstays with a clever twist. The tuna nachos are great for sharing with a group of friends. What is the best coffee shop in Midland, TX? Far West Coffee is a Midland staple. What started as a farmers market favorite has grown into a beloved shop with two locations in town. Where is the best happy hour in Midland, TX? Deemed Midland’s “original cocktail bar,” The Blue Door features an ever-changing menu of drinks and an all-season, dog-friendly patio.
sections.education: Midland residents can take their pick from more than a dozen private schools, a large public school district and schools that focus on different learning styles, including charter schools. Midland Independent School District (MISD) has seen marked improvement over the last several years. faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Midland, TX?
faqs.a: The Wagner Noël Performing Arts Center is a state-of-the-art facility that hosts everything from Broadway productions to plays, comedians and musicians.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Midland, TX?
faqs.a: Enjoy fine, fresh, creative dining at Opal’s Table. The restaurant pairs Texas mainstays with a clever twist. The tuna nachos are great for sharing with a group of friends.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Midland, TX?
faqs.a: Far West Coffee is a Midland staple. What started as a farmers market favorite has grown into a beloved shop with two locations in town.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Midland, TX?
faqs.a: Deemed Midland’s “original cocktail bar,” The Blue Door features an ever-changing menu of drinks and an all-season, dog-friendly patio.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Midland, TX?
faqs.a: Midland has a few options for higher education, including Midland College; however, there are a few other institutions in nearby Odessa, including the University of Texas Permian Basin (UTPB), which is part of the University of Texas System.

TX-new-braunfels.description: Everything's bigger in Texas — including the charm of New Braunfels. Located between Austin and San Antonio, New Braunfels has a rich German heritage, and residents can enjoy the city's close-knit community, excellent schools and easy access to outdoor adventures.

sections.location: New Braunfels, TX, is a charming city perfectly positioned about 30 miles northeast of San Antonio and 50 miles southwest of Austin, blending small-town coziness with easy access to big-city fun. Interstate 35 keeps residents connected, and the closest airport, San Antonio International Airport, is just 30 miles away. And when you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: San Antonio is just 30 miles southwest, where you can stroll the River Walk, visit the Alamo or dive into shopping and culture. Austin sits 50 miles northeast; the Texas capital is packed with live music, quirky vibes, and unique shops and restaurants to check out along South Congress Avenue. Houston is about 175 miles east, a doable trip for checking out museums, NASA’s Space Center or even the Gulf Coast.
sections.economy: The area has a dynamic and rapidly growing economy thanks to its location. The major industries here are health care, retail and food services. Significant employers include the Comal Independent School District, Resolute Health Hospital and HEB. Thanks to the Schlitterbahn Waterpark and Resort, tourism is also an economic driver here. The cost of living in New Braunfels is generally lower than the national average. The median home value in New Braunfels is $329,097, and the average household income is $125,381. Texas residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in New Braunfels, TX? Resolute Baptist Hospital, New Braunfels ER & Hospital and Post Acute-Warm Springs Specialty Hospital of New Braunfels all keep residents in good health.
sections.thingsToDo: Living in New Braunfels is an absolute blast, with plenty of year-round fun. Texans are pros when it comes to beating the heat. Floating in tubes down the spring-fed Comal River or along the scenic Guadalupe River is a beloved pastime. The city is also home to Schlitterbahn, a top water park in the country. It is unique because it has throwback rides like tube chutes and modern water rollercoasters. You could even rent a treehouse to spend the night inside the waterpark for the coolest staycation. No coastline, no problem. At Texas Ski Ranch, locals can wakeboard, water ski and play on giant inflatables in the water. Summer 2025 will bring the addition of the Stoke Factory Wave Pool to the Ranch, churning out waves for surfers of all levels. Theme park lovers will have a blast riding the rollercoasters like “Batman: The Ride” and splashing around at the Six Flags Fiesta Texas waterpark in nearby San Antonio. For something a little less action-packed, arrange for a tour of the New Braunfels Fire Museum, which is set in the old firehouse and has antique fire trucks. Or, take a step back in time on a self-guided walking tour of downtown. On the weekends, catch a flick at Stars & Stripes Drive-In Theatre. There are nearly a dozen different museums in town, too. What is something you can do for free in New Braunfels, TX? Pack a picnic and play disc golf at Prince Solms Park along the banks of the Comal River. The spot is also a popular launching point for floating on the river. What are some things to do with kids in New Braunfels, TX? Let your kid’s imagination go wild at the McKenna Children’s Museum, which has adorable playscapes like a camp set up, a space exhibit, the “Lend-A-Hand” ranch and more. New Braunfels Performing Arts puts on many engaging shows for kids and teens. The recent calendar has ranged from “Six Teen Edition” to “Wizard of Oz” and others that bring Dr. Seuss and Spongebob Square Pants to the stage. Where do locals shop in New Braunfels, TX? The Gruene Historic District is packed with cute shops like Cosmic Plant Company, Gruene Hat Co. and Currents, a stylish home decor spot. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in New Braunfels, TX? You’ll find live music any night of the week at bars, restaurants and venues throughout town, but a show at Gruene Hall should be a rite of passage (it’s Texas’ oldest dance hall!). Legends like George Strait, Loretta Lynn, Garth Brooks, the Chicks and Lyle Lovett have all performed here. This Texas Town is Home to the Best Water Park in the Country
sections.outdoors: Texans beat the heat by floating on the Comal River, a summertime tradition. For a smooth ride, work with a local outfitter who can rent you a tube and provide a shuttle service. The Guadalupe River is known for its trout fishing and attracts anglers from all over the country. Feeling adventurous? You can tour the underground caverns at Natural Bridge Caverns or try out the Twisted Trails zip rail and ropes course in a six-story tall complex. There are more than 40 parks here. At 62 acres, Fischer Park is the city's largest public park and this hilltop spot features wide open green spaces, fishing ponds, hiking trails, splash pads, a playground and a Nature Education Center. Landa Park is another favorite, offering ample outdoor activities like hiking, playgrounds, picnic areas and even a mini golf course!
sections.restaurants: Of course, New Braunfels has great Texas barbecue. Try the legendary homemade sausage and Norma Jean’s beans at Black’s Barbecue. But you might be surprised by what else is on the culinary circuit here, including standout German spots like Alpine Haus and Krause’s Cafe, which has a Biergarten and 100 beers on tap. For a fun evening out, enjoy live music while you chow down on a burger at Freiheit Country Store or catch a band at Guadalupe Brewing Company, where you can enjoy craft beer paired with pizza (your pick if you want Detroit or New York style). Wine lovers can get an introduction to Texas wine in nearby Fredericksburg, a cute town with German roots boasting over 75 wineries, vineyards and tasting rooms. What is one of the best places to eat in New Braunfels, TX? Grab brunch at Texas Bistro, which has sweet dishes like creme brulee French toast and savory ones, too, like the tostada tower. What is the best coffee shop in New Braunfels, TX? Enjoy a latte in the morning and an old fashioned in the evening at Kinnor Coffee & Cocktails, a cute shop where records spin with plenty of outdoor seating. Where is the best happy hour in New Braunfels, TX? The Root Cellar Cafe offers happy hour all day long Tuesdays through Fridays.
sections.education: Everything’s bigger in Texas, including the number of educational institutions located here. The New Braunfels Independent School District (NBISD) and the Comal Independent School District (CISD) primarily serve 40,000 students in the area and have above-average graduation rates. There are plenty of private schools to pick from, too. Grace Preparatory Academy, for example, is a 6-12 school focusing on multimodal instruction in a blended learning environment. faqs.a: There are a handful of smaller colleges located in the area, but residents can also take advantage of the higher-ed opportunities in nearby San Antonio.

TX-round-rock.description: Round Rock is a small town that makes a big bang. The city's vibrant economy, excellent schools, numerous parks and recreation activities and low cost of living blend together seamlessly. And the city is just a stone's throw away from Austin, making it easy to get around.

sections.location: The city is parked just north of Austin in Williamson County — Interstate 35 links it to the capital and beyond. The closest airport, Austin-Bergstrom International Airport, is roughly 25 miles away. Ready to venture out? Here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Austin is just 20 miles south, where you can tour the Texas State Capitol, catch live music on Sixth Street or paddle Lady Bird Lake. Georgetown sits 10 miles north, boasting the historic Georgetown Square, Blue Hole Park and Inner Space Cavern. San Antonio is about 90 miles southwest, tempting with the Alamo, the River Walk and SeaWorld thrills.
sections.economy: The area saw a massive uptick in growth and development during the Austin tech boom in the 1990s. While many locals continue to commute into the city for work, opportunities within Round Rock proper have increased recently. The city has cultivated a robust economy with various industries, including technology, education and health care. Round Rock is home to the global headquarters of Dell Technologies, Emerson Automation Solutions and Toppan Photomasks, Inc. Additionally, thanks to the city's proximity to Austin, it's a short drive away from several major Fortune 500/1000 technology companies, like Google, Apple and Oracle. The cost of living in Round Rock is lower than the national average. The median home value in Round Rock is $463,739, and the average household income is $140,726. Texas doesn't have a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Round Rock, TX? St. David's Round Rock Medical Center and Seton Medical Center Williamson are two of the largest hospitals here. Baylor Scott & White Medical Center is also a high performer in several categories, including national recognition for patient safety and stroke care.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents have access to various activities, entertainment and arts. Sports enthusiasts can catch a Round Rock Express baseball game at Dell Diamond, and families can splash around at Kalahari Resorts & Conventions’ indoor waterpark. Another family-friendly option is Flix Brewhouse by Galaxy, which offers a unique movie-going experience where parents can enjoy a drink from the in-house brewery, and kids can enjoy a movie during the Flix Jr. Summer Series, which offers $5 kids meals. Head to downtown Round Rock to explore the local shops and live entertainment, and the Round Rock Premium Outlets cater to those seeking retail therapy. The city has a thriving arts scene, and highlights include the City Arts Center and live music events like Music on Main, which showcase local talent. Even better? The city earned a designation as a “Music Friendly Community,” which further enhances its appeal with numerous venues and events that feature live music. What is something you can do for free in Round Rock, TX? Start your morning with a visit to Round Rock Memorial Park, a family-friendly spot with a great playground and many open spaces for picnics and outdoor games. Then, spend some time learning about local history at Chisholm Trail Crossing Park, ripe with educational markers and sculptures commemorating its role as a Chisholm Trail cattle crossing. If you still have some oomph left, peruse the downtown area with its many local shops, landmarks and murals. What are some things to do in Round Rock with family? Book a staycation at Kalahari Resort and Conventions and enjoy the rides, slides, lazy river and surf simulator at the indoor water park. Or, take aspiring ballers to enjoy a night at the ballpark cheering on the Round Rock Express game, the AAA affiliate of the Texas Rangers. Where do the locals shop in Round Rock, TX? The historic downtown area is a favorite shopping destination, where wanderers can explore locally owned boutiques, specialty shops and art galleries. The vibrant local farmers markets, like the Round Rock Farmers Market, are also great for supporting local growers and artisans. The Round Rock Market Days are held on the first Saturday of each month in Prete Plaza. With vendors, live music and plenty of fresh produce, you can stock up on locally produced products from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Round Rock, TX? Sam Bass Theatre is the place to be if you’re in the market for community plays and performances. Founded in 1980, this nonprofit has long been a steady presence here and is named after the notorious outlaw Sam Bass.
sections.outdoors: Hit the pavement — the area is home to nearly 40 developed parks spanning 2,305 acres and trail corridors. The city’s extensive trail system, which includes the Brushy Creek Regional Trail, provides ample opportunities for hiking, biking and leisurely walks. Old Settlers Park is a family favorite, featuring sports fields, picnic areas, a fishing pond, miles of trails and Rock ‘N River Waterpark, a 150,000 square-foot park with a lazy river, slides and a water playground. Meadow Lake and Lake Creek Parks offer a serene setting for water activities like fishing, kayaking and nature walks. The Inclusive Play for All Park is a fun and accessible playground for children of all ages and abilities. Beyond the trails and parks, the community has an excellent golfing scene, with courses like Forest Creek Golf Club and Teravista Club. Cast a line in local freshwater spots or play disc golf on the 18-hole course at Old Settlers Park.
sections.restaurants: From classic Texas flavors to international influences, foodies find their footing here. A visit isn’t complete without trying Round Rock Donuts, known for their yellow yeast-risen treats. For brunch, options range from picture-perfect plates from Urban Eat. Drink to mimosa-fueled moments at Finley’s. Coastal cuisine is readily available at Salt Traders for those who love seafood. Gino’s Italian Restaurant and Palermo Pasta House are must-visit spots if you love Italian. Cap off the night with a cocktail at The Ruby Hotel or Swallow Wine. What is one of the best places to eat in Round Rock, TX? Cork & Barrel Craft Kitchen + Microbrewery is where a traditional Irish pub meets Texas. The menu is expansive and delicious, and patrons can enjoy an onsite microbrewery and a large outdoor patio. What is the best coffee shop in Round Rock, TX? Mi Mundo Coffeehouse & Roastery is ideal for grabbing a cup of joe. The coffeehouse is known for its delicious beans, authentic tamales and breakfast tacos. Mi Mundo is a community-driven company that redirects 25% of its profits to local and international charities. Where is the best happy hour in Round Rock, TX? Catch a vibe with live music, rooftop cocktails and flatbreads at Urban Rooftop.
sections.education: The city is served by the Round Rock Independent School District, one of Texas’ top districts. The quality of public and private schools here is a huge draw for young families. TX-sugar-land.description: Life in Sugar Land is sweet. In the heart of Texas, Sugar Land is a blend of Southern charm and big-city convenience. From top-rated schools to a delicious dining scene, Sugar Land offers the ideal balance of comfort and conveniences.

sections.location: Sugar Land’s location is sweet — right where U.S. Highway 59 cuts through — linking the city straight to the heart of Houston in roughly 20 minutes. The closest airport, William P. Hobby Airport, is roughly 25 miles away. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Houston is just 20 miles northeast, where you can tour the Space Center, dive into the Museum District or catch a show in the Theater District. Galveston sits 50 miles southeast, with sandy beaches, the Strand District and the fun-filled Moody Gardens. Austin is about 160 miles northwest. The Texas capital offers live music, quirky vibes and South Congress Avenue.
sections.economy: Thanks to its proximity to Houston, the city has no shortage of opportunities and jobs. Sugar Land’s business-friendly climate attracts companies from several industries, such as biotech, insurance, manufacturing and more. However, there are also many job opportunities and a close-knit community for entrepreneurs in Houston, one of the biggest U.S. cities. Sugar Land’s largest industries are health care, oil and gas, and advanced manufacturing. Minute Maid, Accredo Packaging, Inc. and Champion X’s Chemical Technologies branch are headquartered here. The cost of living in Sugar Land is higher than the national average. The median home value in Sugar Land is $402,094, and the average household income is $184,570. There’s no state income tax in Texas. In 2025, the local sales tax rate is 8.25%. Who are the major health care providers in Sugar Land, TX? The city is home to many health care providers, the largest of which are Houston Methodist Sugar Land Hospital and Memorial Hermann Sugar Land Hospital.
sections.thingsToDo: Sugar Land is much more than a Houston suburb; the city offers plenty of quality amenities to its locals. The arts are alive and well at the Art Museum TX, and entertainment is aplenty at the Smart Financial Centre at Sugar Land. For families, there are many places to explore, including the Houston Museum of Natural Science at Sugar Land, which features a unique saltwater aquarium, and the Fort Bend Children’s Discovery Center with various interactive and engaging exhibits. Catch a game at Constellation Field, home to the Sugar Land Space Cowboys, the Triple-A Minor League Baseball affiliate of the Houston Astros. Looking to stimulate the local economy? The city has over 30 distinct shopping areas, from established retailers to local shops. Or, spend your Saturday pursuing the stalls at the Farmer’s Market at Imperial Park, where the family-friendly atmosphere includes food trucks and live music. What is something you can do for free in Sugar Land, TX? The Lawn at First Colony Mall is a green open-space concept where families can relax, play games or watch a movie on the big screen. In addition to movie screenings, The Lawn offers other free events throughout the year, including yoga and live music. What are some things to do with kids in Sugar Land, TX? Kids can monkey around on zip lines, jungle swings and obstacle courses at the Go Ape Zipline and Adventure Park. Where do locals shop in Sugar Land, TX? Sugar Land Town Square is a mixed-use development specialty shopping center and home to half a dozen international dining choices within one block. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Sugar Land, TX? There are several show-stopping venues here, including Smart Financial Centre at Sugar Land, Inspiration Stage, the Sugar Land Auditorium and even outdoor performances at Sugar Land Town Square.
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities in Sugar Land are plentiful. Those seeking a more active lifestyle can also take advantage of the city’s numerous trails and parks, encompassing an impressive 1,174 acres of developed green space. Spend an afternoon walking along the trails at Sugar Land Memorial Park along the Brazos River, or visit one of Sugar Land’s more than two dozen parks. Some standouts include Oyster Creek Park, Brazos River Park and Cullinan Park. Both Imperial and Brazos River parks offer 9-hole disc golf courses as well. When it comes to water sports and activities, there are plenty of places where you can get your feet wet. Several parks in the area offer kayaking or fishing for those with a license. The Splash Pad at River Park is a family favorite for cooling off on a hot day, with water fountains, dumping buckets and a misting rainbow.
sections.restaurants: It might be a secret to some that Sugar Land is located in one of the most ethically diverse counties in the United States, and as a result, the city has an eclectic and lively culinary scene. A wide range of restaurants offer various cuisines — including the area’s signature Tex-Mex — which reflects the melting pot makeup of the locals. Foodies will be equally pleased with the dozens of establishments offering everything from barbecue and burgers to Japanese and Korean. Sugar Land also prides itself on being known as the ‘Sweetest City in Texas.’ From Mon Cheri Macarons to Gelato Picks, dozens of spots will satisfy the sweetest of sweet tooths. What is one of the best places to eat in Sugar Land, TX? Japaneiro’s menu includes sushi boats, large-format rolls ideal for sharing and several vegan options. What is the best coffee shop in Sugar Land, TX? Rang Coffee is the perfect place to grab a cup of joe. They are known for authentic Vietnamese coffee and serve various flavored coffee concoctions. Where is the best happy hour in Sugar Land, TX? Sip a cocktail on the patio at State Fare Bar and Kitchen.
sections.education: The primary public education provider in Sugar Land is the Fort Bend Independent School District (FBISD), one of the largest and most diverse districts in Texas. The district serves nearly 80,000 students, and though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable student-to-teacher ratio. Several different private school options offer a wide range of learning styles and curriculums. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: The city is home to many health care providers, the largest of which are Houston Methodist Sugar Land Hospital and Memorial Hermann Sugar Land Hospital.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: The Lawn at First Colony Mall is a green open-space concept where families can relax, play games or watch a movie on the big screen. In addition to movie screenings, The Lawn offers other free events throughout the year, including yoga and live music.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: Kids can monkey around on zip lines, jungle swings and obstacle courses at the Go Ape Zipline and Adventure Park.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: Sugar Land Town Square is a mixed-use development specialty shopping center and home to half a dozen international dining choices within one block.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: There are several show-stopping venues here, including Smart Financial Centre at Sugar Land, Inspiration Stage, the Sugar Land Auditorium and even outdoor performances at Sugar Land Town Square.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: Japaneiro’s menu includes sushi boats, large-format rolls ideal for sharing and several vegan options.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: Rang Coffee is the perfect place to grab a cup of joe. They are known for authentic Vietnamese coffee and serve various flavored coffee concoctions.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: Sip a cocktail on the patio at State Fare Bar and Kitchen.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Sugar Land, TX?
faqs.a: While there are no significant colleges or universities in Sugar Land, there are several higher-ed institutions near the city, including the University of Houston.

TX-tyler.description: Things are coming up roses in the Rose Capital. Tyler, TX, offers a variety of affordable housing options, a lower cost of living and a diverse economy that thrives in several sectors. Southern hospitality and Texas charm blend to create a tight-knit community full of scenic spots.

sections.location: Tyler is a charming city in east Texas, about 100 miles east of Dallas, accessible via U.S. Route 69 and U.S. Route 271. The nearest airport, Tyler Pounds Regional Airport, is just 3 miles west, offering flights to Dallas and beyond. Geographically, the city sits on rolling hills with flat stretches, and Lake Tyler nearby is perfect for boating, fishing or a lakeside picnic. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Dallas is 100 miles west, home of the Sixth Floor Museum, Dallas Museum of Art and AT&T Stadium. Shreveport, LA, is 100 miles east, home to the R.W. Norton Art Gallery, the Louisiana State Exhibit Museum or the Riverfront. Houston sits 200 miles south; tour the Houston Museum of Natural Science, visit the Houston Zoo or catch a show at the Houston Theater District.
sections.economy: Tyler attracts all manner of major employers and industries thanks to its convenient location and reputation as a hub for Northeast Texas. The local economy is robust and diverse thanks to its well-rounded manufacturing, retail, and oil and gas industries. Major employers in the Tyler area include Trane Technologies, John Soules Food, Kent Water Sports and Tyler Pipe Company. The city may be known as the Rose Capital of America, but it's beginning to also turn heads in the medical field. Thanks to institutions like the University of Texas at Tyler School of Medicine, the region is a great launching pad for people looking to get into the medical field. The school has emerged as a center of innovation and excellence in a region that hasn’t necessarily been associated with cutting-edge medical research in the past. The cost of living in Tyler is generally lower than the national average. The median home value in Tyler is $228,736, and the average household income is $98,764. Texas residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.25% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Tyler, TX? The city is home to several hospitals known for their quality health care and wide range of services, including UT Health Tyler, Christus Trinity Mother Frances Hospital – Tyler and Tyler ContinueCARE Hospital, an acute care, long-term hospital. Tyler, TX, is Ready for WorkTyler, TX: A Great Place to Learn and Launch Your Medical CareerEntrepreneurs Find Opportunities for New Business in Tyler, TX
sections.thingsToDo: Everything’s bigger in Texas, including the amount of things to do in Tyler. Learn about the city’s history through the Historic Tyler Walking Tour and museums like the Texas African American Museum and the Tyler Museum of Art. The R. Don Cowan Fine & Performing Arts Center at the University of Texas at Tyler provides a hub for performing arts, hosting Broadway shows and other cultural events. Try the Ye Olde City Antique Mall and The Loft Downtown Mall for shopping and a treasure trove antiques. Families will enjoy the Caldwell Zoo, with its 3,000-plus animals. Explore hands-on exhibits at the Discovery Science Place. Outdoor enthusiasts can explore the scenic Azalea District, particularly beautiful during springtime blooms, or attend events like the Tyler Film Festival and Spirits of Oakwood. Downtown Tyler’s Square has local shops and restaurants, and the Rose City Farmers Market has local goods. The city also hosts various festivals, including the Red Dirt BBQ & Music Festival and the Texas Rose Festival, both of which celebrate the region’s culture and heritage. What is something you can do for free in Tyler, TX? Bergfeld Park has everything you want in a park, from a dolphin fountain at the “sprayground” to an amphitheater for concerts and al fresco movie nights. What are some things to do in Tyler, TX, with kids? Pick your own strawberries, blueberries and sunflowers at Tyler Berry Farm. Where do locals shop in Tyler, TX? The city is a retail hub for East Texas. Swann’s Furniture & Design is a local institution that’s been around for over a century. It once delivered furniture in horse- and mule-drawn wagons. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Tyler, TX? The Tyler Civic Theatre has two performance spaces offering patrons various productions, including off-Broadway productions. Tyler, TX, Offers Fun for All AgesWhere to Enjoy Live Music in Tyler, TX
sections.outdoors: Natural beauty and sunny weather characterize the outdoor scene here. With its 1,900 acres, Tyler State Park provides ample opportunities for hiking, biking, boating and fishing while also offering camping and swimming. Parks like Faulkner, Rose Rudman, and The Children's Park have playgrounds, walking trails and picnic areas, making them ideal spots for families to run and play. Hit the water at Lake Tyler. There’s a skate park and golf courses like Tyler Municipal and Pine Springs for recreational sports. The Mineola Nature Preserve nearby is a peaceful spot for hiking and birdwatching. The city is also known for its beautiful gardens, such as the Tyler Municipal Rose Garden, and scenic trails, like the Legacy Trail and those in Tyler State Park. The Half Mile of History in downtown Tyler offers outdoor activities for learning about the city. Tyler, TX, Promotes Health and Wellness with a Slew of Outdoor Attractions
sections.restaurants: Taste the flavors of Tyler, a food scene that’s growing and changing, mixing East Texas food with international flavors and craft drinks. The city features classic barbecue joints like Stanley’s Famous Pit Bar-B-Q, known for its smoked meats, alongside more upscale dining options like Jack Ryan’s Steak & Chophouse. There’s also international food, with Mexican at La Plazita and Italian at Bruno’s Pizza & Pasta. Beyond its restaurants, the city has a burgeoning craft beer scene, with breweries like ETX Brewing Co. and True Vine Brewing Company offering local brews and community gatherings. Residents can enjoy unique dining experiences, such as the family-friendly atmosphere at The Grove Kitchen + Gardens and diverse options at places like The Foundry Coffee House. Tyler’s food scene caters to various tastes and preferences, from casual eateries to fine dining. What is one of the best places to eat in Tyler, TX? Stanley’s Famous Pit BBQ is revered for its smoked meats and live music calendar. What is the best coffee shop in Tyler, TX? Brady’s Coffee is a cozy spot with local art adorning the walls. Try the cinnamon dolce latte — it’s a house specialty. Where is the best happy hour in Tyler, TX? The Grove Kitchen & Gardens is a farm-to-table spot with weekly specials and events. How Breweries in This Texas Town are Crafting a CommunityTyler, TX, is Home to Smoking Hot Music & FoodSip Your Way Across East Texas
sections.education: The Tyler Independent School District (TISD) serves nearly 20,000 students in the area and maintains an average 15:1 student-to-teacher ratio. Tyler ISD also partners with local colleges to provide opportunities for students to earn dual credit and get a jump start on their postsecondary plans. There are more than 20 private schools in town, too. UT-salt-lake-city.description: Surrounded by mountain views and the shimmering waters of the Great Salt Lake, Salt Lake City offers captivating beauty and amenities. A thriving arts scene, friendly community and outdoor activities provide a dynamic lifestyle with a low cost of living.

sections.economy: --> --> --> Weather in Salt Lake City, UT Average Temperatures 64 high / 44 low Average Annual Rainfall 19 in Average Annual Snowfall 53 in

faqs.q: What is the weather like in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Salt Lake City residents are treated to all four seasons. The average highs and lows are 87°F and 65°F (summer) and 41°F and 28°F (winter).

faqs.q: Does Salt Lake City get a lot of snow?
faqs.a: The average annual snowfall in Salt Lake City is significantly higher than the national average at more than 54 inches each year.

faqs.q: Does Salt Lake City get a lot of rain?
faqs.a: The average annual rainfall in Salt Lake City is around 19 inches each year.

faqs.q: How many sunny days does Salt Lake City get?
faqs.a: Salt Lake City averages 222 sunny days a year.

faqs.q: What are some things to do outside in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Pack a picnic and climb Ensign Peak, which provides a panoramic view of Salt Lake City. The out-and-back hike is a 1-mile trip. If you’re up for a challenge, hike up Mt. Olympus. With a 4,000-foot elevation gain, it’s a calf-burner but offers great views of the Salt Lake City skyline.

faqs.q: What is something free to do in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Explore Temple Square in downtown Salt Lake City. Beautifully landscaped and covering more than 10 acres, this is home to the Salt Lake Temple, Tabernacle and other historic buildings important to the region's history.

faqs.q: What are some fun things to do with kids in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Hogle Zoo is a great family-friendly haunt, especially in the warmer months. At the same time, winter is a great time to visit the Clark Planetarium if your children are interested in learning more about space and science.

faqs.q: What is the best open secret about Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Salt Lake City has seen a spike in unique and eclectic restaurants in recent years. You can find everything from traditional comfort food in dives tucked in the mountains to innovative, chef-driven menus downtown. In addition to the dining melting pot, the city has a diverse array of architecture. At 210 feet tall, the six-spire, neo-Gothic-style Salt Lake Temple is the world's largest Mormon Temple. Temple Square tours are offered in more than 40 languages.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Locals often shop at City Creek Center, a downtown shopping and dining combo. It features a mix of well-known brands and local shops while keeping you near many of the city's prime highlights.

faqs.q: What is the best restaurant in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: The Copper Onion is a popular downtown Salt Lake City restaurant known for its modern American cuisine. The menu features locally sourced ingredients and plenty of variety to appeal to each guest.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: The George S. and Dolores Doré Eccles Theater, which includes the Delta Performance Hall, seats 2,468 people and has a smaller black box theater that seats 150 to 250, is the place to go for Broadway shows, concerts and other live performances in Salt Lake City.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Whiskey Street on South Main is a vintage lounge featuring creative cocktails, innovative snacks like bourbon bacon caramel popcorn, and a vast whiskey selection. Happy hour here is worth the trip, but reservations are not accepted.

faqs.q: What are the three largest industries in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: Information technology, health care and Outdoor Activities are among the largest industries in Salt Lake City. Major employers include the University of Utah, Delta Airlines, Salt Lake City School District and the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

faqs.q: What is the sales tax rate in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: In 2024, the sales tax rate in Salt Lake City is 7.75%.

faqs.q: Does Utah have a state income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, Utah has a state income tax.

faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: The University of Utah Hospital is nationally ranked in three specialties, including cancer and ophthalmology. It is also regionally ranked as the best hospital in Northern Utah and nationally recognized as high-performing in two specialties and more than a dozen procedures and conditions.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Salt Lake City?
faqs.a: There are several colleges and universities in Salt Lake City, including the University of Utah and Salt Lake Community College.

VA-lynchburg.description: Breathtaking views of the Blue Ridge Mountains and endless outdoor amenities are just a few reasons Lynchburg, VA, is a natural fit. Residents in Lynchburg can enjoy affordable housing, top-rated schools, a robust economy and a high quality of life without breaking the bank.

sections.location: Lynchburg is a historic gem in central Virginia, about 110 miles west of Richmond, along U.S. Route 29 and U.S. Route 460. Lynchburg Regional Airport is 5 miles north, offering flights to Charlotte and Washington, D.C. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Roanoke is just 55 miles west, boasting the Taubman Museum of Art, the Mill Mountain Zoo and the Roanoke Star. Richmond sits 110 miles east, home to the Virginia Museum of Fine Arts, the Science Museum of Virginia and Monument Avenue. Charlottesville is roughly 70 miles north; explore the University of Virginia, Monticello and the Downtown Mall.
sections.economy: Lynchburg is known for its innovative environment, and several industries in the area are growing rapidly; however, the largest in the region are health care and social assistance, education, and manufacturing. More than 6,000 residents work in the manufacturing sector, which is 22% higher than the national average. Some of the largest employers include Liberty University, Centra Health and Lynchburg City Schools. Lynchburg is also a great place to pursue entrepreneurship, thanks to the business resources available. The cost of living in Lynchburg is below the national average. The median home value in Lynchburg is $227,528, and the average household income is $83,633. Virginia residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 5.3% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Lynchburg, VA? Several highly rated health care providers are in the greater Central Virginia area. Lynchburg residents have easy access to the Centra Lynchburg General Hospital, which is known for its cardiology department. Job Searching? Consider a Career in Lynchburg, VA5 Growing Industries in Lynchburg, VAI Built My Nuclear Technology Career in Lynchburg, VA
sections.thingsToDo: Life in Lynchburg is where old meets new, mixing historical hot spots with newer attractions. The downtown area, particularly Main Street, is a hub for local boutiques, diverse dining options and places like the Academy Center of the Arts. The Old City Cemetery and Federal Hill are must-see landmarks for history buffs, and the Lynchburg Museum tells the city’s story. Families love life here, thanks to attractions like Amazement Square, a hands-on children’s museum, and outdoor spaces like Peaks View Park and Riverside Park. The Lynchburg Community Market is one of the oldest farmers markets in the country, offering local goods and produce. Plus, residents can take advantage of events and festivals all year long. What is something you can do for free in Lynchburg, VA? See a stellar collection of paintings and photographs at the Maier Museum of Art at Randolph College. Admission is free, but there are also some fantastic workshops for kids and adults that charge small fees. Additionally, Downtown Lynchburg comes to life on the first Friday of every month with art and music highlighting organizations that help bring art to the community. What are some things to do in Lynchburg, VA, with kids? Local kids love Amazement Square, a hands-on museum where they can form a band and rock out on stage or paint on the walls in a paint box. The museum has four spacious floors with interactive exhibits, workshops and educational programming. Amazement Square is also a Certified Autism Center. Where do locals shop in Lynchburg, VA? The Lynchburg Community Market began in 1783 and is one of the country’s longest-running farmers markets. Visitors can shop for fresh fruits and vegetables and beautiful art pieces or grab lunch at one of the many restaurant vendors. Farmers come year-round on Wednesdays and Saturdays, offering locally produced goods and specialty items. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Lynchburg, VA? Local musicians set the soundtrack at Three Roads Brewing Company, which took over a former car dealership. The brewery also makes artisan sodas. 12 Things to Do in Lynchburg, VA, Before Graduating CollegeLynchburg, VA: Public Art Tour
sections.outdoors: Thanks to the Blue Ridge Mountains and the James River, the city offers plenty of places to stretch your legs. They’ve got over 40 miles of city trails to explore and spots like Blackwater Creek and Percival’s Island, great for walking, biking and running. The James River is great for water sports, and parks like Riverside and Peakland have playgrounds and picnic spots. The Bluffwalk gives views of the river and mountains and connects to restaurants and public art. Looking for more adventure? The nearby Blue Ridge Parkway has hiking and stunning vistas, including the popular Sharp Top Trail at Peaks of Otter. Hit the water on the James River in the warmer months, and winter sports are available year-round at Liberty’s Snowflex Centre. Golfers can enjoy the scenic London Downs Golf Club, and history buffs can explore the restored grounds of Thomas Jefferson’s Poplar Forest. 10 Spots to Enjoy the Outdoors in Lynchburg, VA
sections.restaurants: Lots of spots in Lynchburg contribute to the city’s dynamic dining scene. The foodie culture is growing, mixing Southern staples and innovation. Downtown has more than 40 spots for eating, from quick bites to fine dining at places like Shoemakers American Grille. The Lynchburg Community Market is a long-time favorite for fresh food and local goods. Places like The Water Dog and The Cavalier Store have cool, dog-friendly patios. You can find all kinds of food here, with spots like RA Bistro and No. 7 Rooftop Bar offering happy hour specials and scenic views. The city also embraces its agricultural roots, with many restaurants emphasizing fresh, local ingredients. The craft beverage scene is thriving, with long-time establishments like Beale’s and Rising Silo Farm Brewery offering locally brewed beers and spirits. What is one of the best places to eat in Lynchburg, VA? Mizumi Japanese Bistro is the spot in town for hibachi and tonkotsu ramen. What is the best coffee shop in Lynchburg, VA? Mission House Coffee is a local favorite. Grab a cup of their freshly roasted, ethically sourced coffee or a salad or sandwich and spend the afternoon working in the cafe. An added bonus? They give back to local nonprofits in the community. Where is the best happy hour in Lynchburg, VA? After a long day, unwind with rooftop views of the James River and seasonal sips and spritzes at 7 Rooftop Bar. Foodie-Recommended Restaurants in Lynchburg, VA
sections.education: Life is easy in Lynchburg, especially when there are so many excellent educational institutions. The Lynchburg City Public Schools system serves nearly 8,000 students and has a student-to-teacher ratio of 13:1. The city's public schools are also some of the most diverse in the state. There are a dozen private school options in the area, and more than 40% of students in Lynchburg are educated in private schools. Virginia Episcopal School is a college prep boarding and day school for grades 9-12 emphasizing a rigorous academic program and many extracurricular activities. faqs.a: Mission House Coffee is a local favorite. Grab a cup of their freshly roasted, ethically sourced coffee or a salad or sandwich and spend the afternoon working in the cafe. An added bonus? They give back to local nonprofits in the community.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Lynchburg, VA?
faqs.a: After a long day, unwind with rooftop views of the James River and seasonal sips and spritzes at 7 Rooftop Bar.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Lynchburg, VA?
faqs.a: Several colleges and universities are located here, including Liberty University, the University of Lynchburg and Central Virginia Community College.

VA-richmond.description: Richmond residents are no strangers to a high quality of life. Top-rated schools, a diverse economy with plenty of job opportunities, delicious dining options and history on every corner add to the charm of Virginia's capital city.

sections.location: Richmond is the state capital, located in central Virginia on the banks of the James River, accessible via Interstate 95 and Interstate 64. Richmond International Airport is just 5 miles east, offering convenient flights. When you’re ready to explore beyond, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Washington, D.C., is 100 miles north, home to the National Mall, Smithsonian museums and the U.S. Capitol. Norfolk sits 90 miles southeast, where you can tour the USS Wisconsin, visit the Chrysler Museum or catch a show at the Scope Arena. Charlottesville is roughly 70 miles northwest, where you can explore the University of Virginia, Monticello and the Downtown Mall.
sections.economy: Local key industries include advanced manufacturing, technology and information technology. Capital One is one of the largest employers here, followed by Virginia Commonwealth University Health System and several other health care providers. Several major employers, including Fortune 500 giants like Dominion Energy and Altria Group, make it a good place to put down roots for anyone hoping to find and hold onto stable employment. The city’s proximity to government institutions and the financial sector in Washington, D.C., and nearby cities like Norfolk and Virginia Beach also opens up a great number of career opportunities. The cost of living in Richmond is lower than the national average. The median home value in Richmond is $354,664, and the average household income is $103,187. Virginia residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Richmond, VA? Residents have access to the nationally ranked VCU Medical Center, which is ranked in both children’s and adult specialties. The flagship hospital of the VCU Health System is known for its comprehensive range of medical services.
sections.thingsToDo: Residents in Richmond get a great mix of history, culture and family-friendly fun. As one of the oldest major cities in the country, local residents can access lots of local history, with spots like the Virginia State Capitol, the American Civil War Museum and the Edgar Allan Poe Museum, all telling stories from the past. The arts scene is massive, with places like the Virginia Museum of Fine Arts and the Institute for Contemporary Art, plus lots of galleries. Events like the Richmond Folk Festival show off the city’s different cultures. Richmond is a community-oriented place offering a family-friendly lifestyle. Kids are entertained at attractions like the Science Museum of Virginia, the Children’s Museum of Richmond and the Lewis Ginter Botanical Garden, all with fun learning activities. Downtown is bustling with live music venues, theaters and plenty of public art — there are more than 100 murals! The South of the James Farmers Market offers local products, showcasing the region’s produce and artisanal goods. What is something you can do for free in Richmond, VA? Get in touch with your artsy side by perusing the more than 70 art galleries that call the city home, or explore the more than 100 murals that dot the city’s walls. Become a repeat visitor to the free Virginia Museum of Fine Arts, which has more than 50,000 works of art representing nearly every major culture in the world. What are some things to do in Richmond, VA, with kids? Kids have so much to explore at the Children’s Museum of Richmond, including exhibits on caves, banks, dinosaurs, Wegman’s grocery store and more. The museum even has $3 carousel rides. Where do locals shop in Richmond, VA? The delightful South of the James Farmers Market, run by GrowRVA, is held every Saturday all year long in the Forest Hill neighborhood. The open-air, producer-only market features farmers and artisans selling locally grown and handmade items. PSA: No visit is complete without grabbing a donut to go! Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Richmond, VA? Watch Shakespeare plays in the outdoor courtyard of a 16th-century manor home.
sections.outdoors: Recreation in Richmond is endless, capitalizing on its urban landscape and natural surroundings. The James River is a big deal here — where residents can kayak, raft or swim. The James River Park System also offers over 550 acres of trails for hiking, biking and rock climbing. Belle Isle, an island in the river, is a good place for relaxation and exploration. There are plentiful parks, including Maymont Park, Huguenot Park and Pocahontas State Park, which are popular places for picnicking and playing. The Virginia Capital Trail is more than 50 miles of bike paths that connect Richmond to Williamsburg. Events like Dominion Energy Riverrock Festival celebrate the outdoors and music, highlighting the city's active outdoor culture.
sections.restaurants: The city’s food scene has been getting a lot of attention, mixing classic Southern foods with new and global dishes. There are more than 900 restaurants, most of which are locally owned, and the city has earned accolades for its foodie-forward culture. From James Beard Award-nominated establishments like L’Opossum and The Roosevelt to beloved local spots like Perly’s, a Jewish deli, Richmond offers a diverse range of dining experiences. Plus, there are food trucks, steakhouses and international eateries catering to an array of palates. The city is big on craft drinks, with over 30 breweries, including acclaimed spots like Mekong and Hardywood. Its thriving beer culture has earned it a place on USA Today’s list of top 10 beer scenes. You can also enjoy a variety of cideries, meaderies, distilleries and craft cocktail bars, which contribute to Richmond’s reputation as a hub for innovative and high-quality drinks. Rooftop bars like Q at the Quirk Hotel offer great views while you imbibe. What is one of the best places to eat in Richmond, VA? Pair tacos and margs at Lolita’s, and save room for desserts like espresso flan. What is the best coffee shop in Richmond, VA? Reviresco Coffee Company is a micro-roastery with a quintessentially cozy coffee shop environment. Where is the best place for happy hour in Richmond, VA? Enjoy a local craft brew with friends at Hardywood. Be sure to grab a slice from their pizza kitchen, too.
sections.education: Richmond is considered a family-friendly place to live. The city is home to many top-rated public and private schools, and the diverse cultural scene provides a wealth of enriching opportunities for children of all ages. The Richmond City Public Schools (RCPS) district serves more than 20,000 students across grades PK-12. Though performance varies from school to school, the district maintains a favorable student-to-teacher ratio. The city is also home to a significant number of private schools — nearly 80! Roughly 20% of all K-12 students here attend private schools, and several impressive options exist.
