VA-roanoke.description: Nestled in the heart of the Blue Ridge Mountains, Roanoke is a picturesque city that offers residents an affordable cost of living and plenty of job opportunities. It’s an ideal place for young families and professionals, with hiking and biking and plenty of places to wine and dine.

sections.location: Roanoke is a charming city in the Blue Ridge Mountains of western Virginia, about 240 miles west of Richmond, accessible via Interstate 81. Roanoke-Blacksburg Regional Airport is just north, offering flights to Atlanta and Charlotte. Nearby major cities include: Lynchburg, VA, is just 55 miles east, boasting the Lynchburg Museum, Liberty University and Blackwater Creek Trail. Richmond, VA, is roughly 185 miles east, home to the Virginia Museum of Fine Arts, the Science Museum of Virginia and Monument Avenue. Charlotte, NC, sits 180 miles south, home of the NASCAR Hall of Fame, U.S. National Whitewater Center and Discovery Place.
sections.economy: Historically, Roanoke served as a hub for the railroad and manufacturing sectors, but today, the economy has diversified to include prominent sectors such as health care, education and technology. Roanoke is a regional hub for Southwest Virginia, and some of the largest employers are Carilion Clinic, Norfolk Southern Railway and the City of Roanoke. Advanced Auto Parts has a headquarters here and is a major employer in the area. If you’re new to town and want to connect with other professionals, Get2KnowNoke has a quarterly Welcome Wagon event, local ambassadors to connect with and a treasure trove of helpful resources. The cost of living in Roanoke is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Roanoke is $197,134, and the average household income is $82,258. Virginia residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 5.3% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Roanoke, VA? The Carilion Roanoke Memorial Hospital and Children’s Hospital are major health care providers in the area. The Memorial Hospital has earned a high-performance ranking in one specialty and 16 different procedures and conditions.
sections.thingsToDo: A peaceful retreat, Mill Mountain Park draws visitors and locals. It’s home to the Roanoke Star, which was initially created as a Christmas decoration 75 years ago and remains a beloved symbol of the city today. As the world’s largest, free-standing illuminated star, it twinkles over the city, and those as far as 60 miles away can see it. The park also features a wildflower garden, a playground, the Discovery Center with interactive exhibits and a zoo home to animals like Frank, an adorable red panda and Opie, a silly Virginia opossum. The arts are alive and well here, from bluegrass and funk music to impressionist paintings and ballet. Museums and galleries around town feature special exhibits highlighting local and world-renowned artists’ work. Tap your toes along to the tunes of Busk Roanoke in downtown Market Square or snap a selfie in front of the David Ramey Gainsboro Mural Wall. Other fun ways to spend a weekend include visiting the Roanoke Pinball Museum, an interactive museum with playable games made over the past century, or taking climbing lessons on the indoor walls at River Rock Climbing. What is something you can do for free in Roanoke, VA? General admission is always free at the Taubman Museum of Art. You can also enjoy free art before you head out on vacation because the museum recently installed 20 pieces of artwork at the Roanoke-Blacksburg Regional Airport. What are some things to do in Roanoke, VA, with kids? Go to check out books at Roanoke’s public libraries, but stay for all the engaging activities like Play-Doh parties, storytimes, DIY bath bomb glasses and writing workshops. At The Virginia Museum of Transportation, check out antique automobiles and trucks. At the Science Museum of Western Virginia, kids can participate in dino digs in a giant sandbox, feed parakeets, visit the planetarium and more. Where do locals shop in Roanoke, VA? Open year-round in the heart of downtown, the Historic Roanoke City Market is a great place to pick up locally grown fruits and veggies, flowers, fresh meats, cheeses, baked goods and more. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Roanoke, VA? Originally built as a high school a century ago, the Jefferson Center is a cultural hub in the Roanoke Valley that hosts live performances.
sections.outdoors: Did you know that one of the largest municipal parks in the United States is in Roanoke? Carvins Cove Natural Reserve boasts 12,000 acres of forest and a 630-acre reservoir for kayaking and paddleboarding. You can also hike and mountain bike on the 60 miles of trails that braid the park or enjoy a picnic and the scenery. In addition to Carvins Cove, the city’s parks department oversees more than 50 parks, half a dozen greenways and two outdoor swimming pools. Enjoy walks and bike rides along the Roanoke River Greenway, a 10-mile stretch of paved trail in town. Off the Blue Ridge Parkway, Explore Park is also a special place to hike, mountain bike, play disc golf and try out aerial obstacles at Treetop Quest or kayak, tube or stand-up paddleboard along the Roanoke River Gorge. The park’s onsite Twin Creeks Brew Pub is a brewery and restaurant where you can order burgers and sip Virginia wine and ciders by outdoor fire pits.
sections.restaurants: The city's diverse dining scene covers a lot of ground. Order pastries or a loaf of bread from Bread Craft and enjoy a focaccia panini for lunch when you pop in for pickup, or start your day off with award-winning flaky, buttery biscuits at Scratch Biscuit Co. Savor Jamaican Jerk Jackfruit Nachos and other tasty dishes from Queen’s Vegan Cafe. Order ice cream sundaes or old-fashioned sodas at Pop’s Ice Cream and Soda Bar. Get a taste of the local food scene on the Downtown Roanoke Food, History and Cultural Tour or by checking out the local vendors at the Roanoke City Market. In addition to a diverse dining scene, residents have plenty of craft breweries to choose from, like Golden Cactus Brewing, a cheerful, dog-friendly spot inside a former soda bottling factory. What is one of the best places to eat in Roanoke, VA? Enjoy ramen, rice bowls, hibachi steak, and mochi ice cream at Ike’s Kitchen, a favorite Japanese restaurant. What is the best coffee shop in Roanoke, VA? One of the top-rated coffee shops in Roanoke is Chris’s Coffee & Custard. In addition to a stellar lineup of coffee drinks, the shop has custards (including rotating seasonal flavors) that you can order in a waffle cone bowl. The shop provides opportunities to young adults with intellectual, developmental and physical disabilities. Where is the best happy hour in Roanoke, VA? What’s better than $7 martinis and $10 endless truffle fries? Nothing. Except maybe the stunning views of the Roanoke Valley that accompany your happy hour at Six and Sky.
sections.education: ​​An affordable cost of living and a strong job market make Roanoke an equally appealing place for growing families. The area is primarily served by two districts: Roanoke City Public Schools (RCPS) and Roanoke County Public Schools (RCPS). Roanoke County Public Schools serve nearly 14,000 students and are some of the best in the state, with incredible teachers who love teaching in the district. There are also more than a dozen private schools in town, too. --> --> --> Browse the Top 100 Best Places to Live in the U.S. Roanoke, VA 636 St. Louis, MO 636 Orlando, FL 638 Anchorage, AK 639 Gainesville, FL 639 Santa Fe, NM 641 Greenville, NC 643 Little Rock, AR 644 Tulsa, OK 644 Athens, GA 646 Columbia, SC 646 Green Bay, WI 648 Knoxville, TN 648 Peoria, IL 651 Redding, CA 653 Wilmington, NC 653 Champaign, IL 655 Worcester, MA 656 Abilene, TX 659 Lynn, MA 661 Murfreesboro, TN 661 Davenport, IA 662 Wichita, KS 662 Billings, MT 663 Columbia, MO 663 Auburn, AL 666 Manchester, NH 669 Salem, OR 671 Tampa, FL 673 Greensboro, NC 674 Huntsville, AL 674 Tyler, TX 676 Winston-Salem, NC 676 Louisville, KY 678 Duluth, MN 679 Richmond, VA 680 Spokane, WA 683 Minneapolis, MN 685 Lynchburg, VA 687 Nampa, ID 687 Fayetteville, AR 689 Bloomington, IN 690 Danbury, CT 690 Tempe, AZ 692 Bethlehem, PA 696 Eugene, OR 696 Kennewick, WA 697 Colorado Springs, CO 698 Asheville, NC 699 Aurora, CO 699 Gresham, OR 700 Madison, WI 700 Omaha, NE 701 Appleton, WI 704 Frederick, MD 704 Sioux Falls, SD 704 Fargo, ND 706 Lincoln, NE 707 Iowa City, IA 709 Durham, NC 710 Greenville, SC 710 College Station, TX 711 Norman, OK 711 Midland, TX 712 Ann Arbor, MI 715 Rio Rancho, NM 716 Coral Springs, FL 717 Cedar Rapids, IA 718 Charleston, SC 719 Rochester, MN 722 Bloomington, IL 727 Pittsburgh, PA 727 Nashua, NH 730 Clovis, CA 731 Vancouver, WA 731 Loveland, CO 744 Bloomington, MN 755 New Braunfels, TX 758 Raleigh, NC 758 Evanston, IL 759 Warwick, RI 761 Hoover, AL 763 Virginia Beach, VA 770 Clifton, NJ 771 Edmond, OK 778 Gilbert, AZ 791 Round Rock, TX 793 Olathe, KS 799 Columbia, MD 828 Troy, MI 829 Overland Park, KS 831 Roswell, GA 838 Naperville, IL 841 Sugar Land, TX 854 Carmel, IN 872 Flower Mound, TX 875 Augusta, GA 633 Chattanooga, TN 634 Rapid City, SD 634 Cincinnati, OH 636 Return to Main List Explore All Top 100 Best Places Cities Top 100 Cities Map

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Roanoke, VA?
faqs.a: When it comes to higher-ed options in the city, there are several institutions to choose from. Some of the most prominent are Holins University, Roanoke College and Virginia Western Community College, one of the largest community colleges in Virginia.

VA-virginia-beach.description: The coast is calling in Virginia Beach. Sun-soaked beaches, a welcoming coastal lifestyle and abundant outdoor activities contribute to Virginia Beach’s reputation as a city where residents can enjoy a stellar work-life balance, all while maintaining a low cost of living.

sections.location: The city is a coastal gem parked on the southeastern edge of the state, right along Interstate 264, tying it to nearby Norfolk. The closest airport, Norfolk International Airport, is around 20 miles away, serving up flights for your getaways. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Norfolk is just 20 miles west, where you can tour the USS Wisconsin, visit the Chrysler Museum or catch a show at the Scope Arena. Chesapeake sits 15 miles west, offering the Great Dismal Swamp, Chesapeake Arboretum and Northwest River Park. Richmond is about 100 miles northwest, boasting the Virginia State Capitol, Civil War Museum and Maymont Park.
sections.economy: Tourism and hospitality, as well as military and real estate, are among the largest industries in Virginia Beach. In addition, the community is home to several military installations, including Naval Air Station Oceana and Joint Expeditionary Base Little Creek-Fort Story, making the military a significant employer. Dollar Tree and STIHL, Inc. are headquartered here, and GEICO has a regional office in the area. The cost of living in Virginia Beach is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Virginia Beach is $376,063, and the average household income is $125,987. Virginia residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 6% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Virginia Beach, VA? Residents have access to several highly ranked hospitals and providers at Sentara Virginia Beach General Hospital, one of the region’s highest-ranking hospitals. Additionally, the Virginia Beach-Norfolk-Newport News area has a variety of providers in different specialties, so there is something for everyone.
sections.thingsToDo: This city is a haven for people who love to get things going, blending coastal charm and cultural experiences. The iconic Virginia Beach Boardwalk, which is lined with shops, restaurants and entertainment venues, is a great place to stroll, bike and enjoy live music. History enthusiasts love spending time at the Cape Henry Lighthouses, including the original 1792 structure and the First Landing Cross, commemorating the arrival of English settlers. The Town Center offers a fun mix of retail therapy, dining and community events, featuring venues like the Sandler Center for the Performing Arts. Beyond the beaches and Boardwalk, residents can delve into the city’s history at the Military Aviation Museum, and kids will love the Virginia Aquarium & Marine Science Center. The King Neptune Statue is a must-see landmark, too. The area hosts numerous annual events, including the Neptune Festival and the Virginia Beach Chocolate Festival, so you’ll never run out of fun things to do. What is something you can do for free in Virginia Beach, VA? Become a regular at the Virginia Museum of Contemporary Art, which offers new exhibitions every few months. Or create your own tour. From world-class installations to colorful creations, numerous outdoor art pieces are waiting to be explored. For example, “Seashore Cathedral” is a series of murals on the underpass columns of Rudee Walkway. Be sure to grab some shots for Insta. What are some things to do with kids in Virginia Beach, VA? Climb and zipline in the treetop trails at The Adventure Park at Virginia Aquarium. Where do locals shop in Virginia Beach, VA? The Hilltop area is a popular shopping destination. It features a mix of locally owned stores, boutiques and national retailers. Other favorite spots include La Promenade Distinctive Shoppes and the Virginia Beach Farmers Market. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Virginia Beach, VA? There are several live music and performing arts venues here. Veterans United Home Loans Amphitheater, Sandler Center for the Performing Arts and Zeiders American Dream Theater are popular spots for locals and tourists alike.
sections.outdoors: Capitalize on the city’s coastal location and natural landscapes by enjoying the more than 200 parks and park facilities and over 7,000 acres of natural areas Virginia Beach offers. The iconic coastline provides plenty of space to swim, surf, boat and fish, while the Virginia Beach Boardwalk has a 3-mile trail. Nature enthusiasts can explore the expansive First Landing State Park, a registered natural landmark with miles of trails, or venture to the more secluded False Cape Park. Chesapeake Bay beaches and Sandbridge Beach offer alternative coastal experiences. Beyond the Boardwalk and beaches, residents can kayak through the Back Bay National Wildlife Refuge looking for dolphins or hike the trails of Mount Thrashmore Park, a reclaimed landfill turned recreational spot with lakes. Take the family to enjoy Great Neck Park’s grilling areas, sports facilities and playground.
sections.restaurants: From fresh seafood to farm-to-table fare, this coastal location has every flavor you can think of. Locals can enjoy casual beachfront grills and upscale dining venues, where restaurants highlight the fresh catch of the day. Outdoor dining is a popular choice around town, with several restaurants offering waterfront views. The Town Center of Virginia Beach is a culinary hub, offering everything from quick bites to leisurely meals. The area has a growing craft beer scene too — think creative brews like a pineapple IPA. There is also a lively bar scene with craft cocktails. What is one of the best places to eat in Virginia Beach, VA? The Blue Seafood and Spirits is a chef-owned casual restaurant that serves high-quality fresh seafood. Be sure to order the Chesapeake Bay blue crabs and oysters, if available. Then, top it off with a visit to Salty Paws Ice Cream and Bakery, which is dog-friendly and creates unique ice cream treats and baked goods designed specifically for canines. What is the best coffee shop in Virginia Beach, VA? Spend the day working from Three Ships Coffee. When it’s quitting time, you are less than three blocks from the beach. Where is the best place for happy hour in Virginia Beach, VA? Burgers, seafood, beers and cocktails are calling your name at South Beach Grill.
sections.education: The Virginia Beach City Public Schools (VBCPS) district serves more than 65,000 students and is one of the top five best districts in the state. VBCPS also ranks as one of the state’s most diverse and best districts for teachers. There is also a significant number of private schools here. There are more than 60 private schools, ranging from independent to co-educational schools and faith-based curriculums to STEM-focused. --> --> Read the Magazine --> Browse the Top 100 Best Places to faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: Residents have access to several highly ranked hospitals and providers at Sentara Virginia Beach General Hospital, one of the region’s highest-ranking hospitals. Additionally, the Virginia Beach-Norfolk-Newport News area has a variety of providers in different specialties, so there is something for everyone.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: Become a regular at the Virginia Museum of Contemporary Art, which offers new exhibitions every few months. Or create your own tour. From world-class installations to colorful creations, numerous outdoor art pieces are waiting to be explored. For example, “Seashore Cathedral” is a series of murals on the underpass columns of Rudee Walkway. Be sure to grab some shots for Insta.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: Climb and zipline in the treetop trails at The Adventure Park at Virginia Aquarium.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: The Hilltop area is a popular shopping destination. It features a mix of locally owned stores, boutiques and national retailers. Other favorite spots include La Promenade Distinctive Shoppes and the Virginia Beach Farmers Market.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: There are several live music and performing arts venues here. Veterans United Home Loans Amphitheater, Sandler Center for the Performing Arts and Zeiders American Dream Theater are popular spots for locals and tourists alike.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: The Blue Seafood and Spirits is a chef-owned casual restaurant that serves high-quality fresh seafood. Be sure to order the Chesapeake Bay blue crabs and oysters, if available. Then, top it off with a visit to Salty Paws Ice Cream and Bakery, which is dog-friendly and creates unique ice cream treats and baked goods designed specifically for canines.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: Spend the day working from Three Ships Coffee. When it’s quitting time, you are less than three blocks from the beach.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for happy hour in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: Burgers, seafood, beers and cocktails are calling your name at South Beach Grill.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Virginia Beach, VA?
faqs.a: Several colleges and universities are located here, including Regent University, Virginia Wesleyan University, South University – Virginia Beach, Bryant & Stratton College, and The Art Institute of Virginia Beach.

WA-kennewick.description: Three cheers for the Tri-Cities! Affordable housing, top-rated schools, and a family-friendly environment make Kennewick a great place to raise a family. Stunning Columbia River views and endless outdoor options keep nature lovers captivated, too.

sections.location: Kennewick is a lively city in southeastern Washington, right along the Columbia River, and is part of the Tri-Cities area. It’s accessible via Interstate 82 and U.S. Route 395, keeping it connected. The nearest airport, Tri-Cities Airport in Pasco, is just 5 miles north. When you’re ready to roam, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Spokane, WA, is just 140 miles northeast, where you can visit Riverfront Park or the Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture. Yakima, WA, sits 80 miles west, known for its wineries and the Yakima Valley Museum. Portland, OR, is about 215 miles southwest, home to the Oregon Museum of Science and Industry and the Lan Su Chinese Garden.
sections.economy: Kennewick is the largest of the Tri-Cities and boasts a robust economy fueled by its location in the heart of southeastern Washington State. Several significant industries support the local economy, including agriculture and food processing, retail and health care. Tyson Foods and Lamb Weston, major food processing companies, have facilities in the area and are two of the largest employers in town. Other major employers are Kadlec Regional Medical Center, Kennewick School District and Benton PUD. The cost of living in Kennewick is lower than the national average. The median home value in Kennewick is $381,322, and the average household income is $105,691. Washington residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.7% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Kennewick, WA? Kennewick’s location in the Tri-Cities area of Washington State gives residents access to a great variety of health care providers and facilities. Trios Health Southridge Hospital is one of the leading providers in the area, offering a comprehensive range of services.
sections.thingsToDo: Kick back in Kennewick, a city that blends family-friendly activities with arts and culture. Explore the city’s history at the East Benton County Historical Museum and the 9/11 World Trade Center Memorial Monument, or enjoy artistic experiences through the Kennewick Public Art Collection and visits to local galleries in Historic Downtown Kennewick. The Toyota Center hosts live events for entertainment. For nostalgic family fun, visit Gesa Carousel of Dreams. Shop til you drop at the Columbia Center Mall or find unique items at the seasonal Kennewick Farmers Market. Families can enjoy interactive experiences at the Quake Family Fun Center or visit Sandollar Farms & Alpacas for animal encounters. The nearby Pasco Farmers Market, Washington’s largest open-air produce market, offers fresh local goods. What is something you can do for free in Kennewick, WA? Go for a walk along the Columbia River and bring a picnic to enjoy in Columbia Park. What are some things to do in Kennewick, WA, with kids? The Playground of Dreams is a premier park with a cable bridge, lighthouse and fun play structures. The park was designed with inclusive features so kids with wheelchairs or mobility limitations can play on the equipment. Where do locals shop in Kennewick, WA? Grab some fresh-grown food and spend an afternoon at the nearby Pasco Farmers Market, which is Washington’s largest open-air fresh produce market. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Kennewick, WA? The Toyota Center is a multipurpose arena that hosts comedy shows, performing arts acts, and concerts by nationally touring artists and local bands alike.
sections.outdoors: Washington is full of outdoor wonders, and Kennewick is no different. The city offers a variety of outdoor activities, capitalizing on its scenic location along the Columbia River and its mild climate. Columbia Park provides riverfront access with walking trails, picnic areas and picturesque views, while the Zintel Canyon Greenway offers hiking and biking opportunities through natural landscapes. Residents can visit the Southridge Sports Complex Aquatic Playground for water-based activities or enjoy panoramic river views from the Clover Island Lighthouse. Beyond traditional parks, the city features unique outdoor experiences like the Manhattan Project National Historical Park, where visitors can tour the B Reactor National Historic Landmark. Highlands Grange Park offers a more relaxed setting with gardens and walking paths, and the Columbia Park Golf Tri-Plex features foot golf. The city’s numerous parks and trails and riverfront location make it a destination for outdoor aficionados.
sections.restaurants: Deeply influenced by its location in the heart of Washington Wine Country, Kennewick offers an ever-expanding culinary landscape. With over 200 wineries within an hour’s drive, the city provides ample opportunities for wine tasting and food pairings, particularly at destinations like the Columbia Gardens Urban Wine & Artisan Village and Badger Mountain Vineyard. The local food scene emphasizes fresh, locally sourced ingredients, with restaurants embracing farm-to-table concepts and showcasing the region’s agricultural bounty. Beyond wine, the city has a thriving craft beer culture, with breweries like Ice Harbor Brewing Company and diverse dining options. From authentic barbecue at Porter’s Real Barbecue to international flavors spanning Mexican, Mediterranean and Japanese cuisines, the city caters to a variety of palates. Local favorites like Foodies Brick and Mortar offer comforting American fare, while food trucks provide quick and diverse culinary experiences. The region’s proximity to Walla Walla Valley and other wine-producing areas further enhances the local appeal to food and beverage enthusiasts. What is one of the best places to eat in Kennewick, WA? Europa allows diners to take a culinary tour of Spain and Italy with its vast menu from pasta to paella. What is the best coffee shop in Kennewick, WA? Flying X is a cozy coffee shop with high-quality, organic options, including espresso tea and pastries. Where is the best happy hour in Kennewick, WA? Start the afternoon early at Picante Mexican Taqueria’s happy hour, where you can enjoy margaritas, mezcal mules and small bites.
sections.education: Kennewick School District is the primary public school district here, with an enrollment of more than 19,000 students. Kennewick Schools offers several different programs and schools, including traditional options, specialized choice schools and dual language programs. Delta High School (STEM-focused) and Phoenix High School (project-based learning) provide different learning options. Private schools are primarily targeted at early education, but families can also find excellent private high schools nearby in Richland. WA-spokane.description: Surrounded by the stunning beauty of the Inland Northwest, Spokane offers abundant outdoor activities, a friendly community and excellent educational opportunities. Affordability is a priority here, and Spokane is one of the most affordable cities on the West Coast.

sections.location: Spokane is a beautiful city in eastern Washington, about 280 miles east of Seattle, accessible via Interstate 90 and U.S. Route 2. Spokane International Airport is just 5 miles west, offering convenient flights. Geographically, the city sits in the Inland Northwest, with rolling hills and the majestic Rocky Mountains in the distance, providing a scenic backdrop for hikes and explorations. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Seattle is 280 miles west, offering the Space Needle, Museum of Pop Culture and Pike Place Market. Portland, OR, sits 300 miles southwest, home to the Oregon Museum of Science and Industry and the Lan Su Chinese Garden. Coeur d’Alene, ID, is just 30 miles west, boasting the scenic beauty of Lake Coeur d’Alene, the Coeur d’Alene Resort and the Tubbs Hill Natural Area.
sections.economy: Newcomers to Spokane often find diverse opportunities in everything from health care and public services to manufacturing and education. The community has also recently seen a rise in the finance and technology sectors. Some of the largest employers are Providence Health & Services, MultiCare Health System, Gonzaga University and Fairchild Air Force Base. The cost of living in Spokane is roughly right at the national average. The median home value in Spokane is $404,436, and the average household income is $97,540. Washington residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 9.0% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Spokane, WA? Residents have access to several highly regarded hospitals, including Providence Sacred Heart Medical Center, MultiCare Deaconess Hospital and Providence Holy Family Hospital.
sections.thingsToDo: Spend five minutes in Spokane, and you’ll notice all this city has to offer. The city boasts a vibrant arts scene, featuring museums like the Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture and performance venues like the Martin Woldson Theater at the Fox, Spokane Civic Theatre and The Bing Crosby Theater. Take a trip back in time at the Campbell House or a self-guided tour to see the city’s historic buildings. For outdoor enthusiasts, Spokane provides plentiful parks and trails, including Riverfront Park, with its iconic Looff Carrousel and views of the Spokane Falls. The green gardens at Manito Park are a can’t-miss. Residents also enjoy annual events like Hoopfest, the world’s largest 3-on-3 basketball tournament, and the Lilac Bloomsday Run. Shopping and dining options abound, with the Garland District and River Park Square offering a mix of local boutiques, galleries, shops and eateries. What is something you can do for free in Spokane, WA? Spend an afternoon walking or biking the Centennial Trail, a 40-mile paved trail that crosses the border between Washington and Idaho. What are some things to do in Spokane, WA, with kids? Head to Riverfront Park for a day of fun. A former rail yard, the urban oasis was cleaned up to host the 1974 World’s Fair. Now, it offers activities for all ages. The skating ribbon is free with your own wheels (helmets required). There’s also the 114-year-old, hard-carved Looff Carrousel, multiple playgrounds, and the Skyride cable car that takes you in front of Spokane Falls and gives excellent city views. Where do locals shop in Spokane, WA? The Garland District is one of the neighborhoods known for local businesses, shops and eateries. It’s a great area to explore and find unique items in vintage stores and specialty boutiques. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Spokane, WA? The Bing Crosby Theater, originally built in 1914, is a historic venue now considered a cultural hub here. The Knitting Factory Concert House is another popular venue for local and touring artists.
sections.outdoors: Spokane is positively scenic. Riverfront Park, a historic site from the 1974 World’s Fair, is a central hub featuring the Spokane Falls, Looff Carrousel and the Numerica SkyRide, which provides breathtaking views. The park also has the world’s largest Radio Flyer wagon, making it a popular spot for families. The 40-mile Centennial Trail is perfect for walking, running and biking and connects downtown to Riverside State Park. Outside the city, residents can access lakes and rivers for canoeing, kayaking and fishing. Manito Park offers botanical gardens and serene walking paths, while Riverside State Park provides 9,000 acres for hiking, rock climbing and horseback riding. In winter, ski or snowboard at Mount Spokane or ice skate at Riverfront Park’s Numerica Skate Ribbon. Plus, there are lots of golf courses known for their scenic beauty and well-maintained greens.
sections.restaurants: Spokane is serious about eating local, and luckily, the region has so much to offer, from salmon caught in Eastern Washington rivers to fresh vegetables grown on local farms. The local food scene is a tasty mix of fresh, local food, craft drinks and global flavors. Restaurants pride themselves on sourcing local ingredients from the surrounding farms, offering everything from classic American comfort food at Frank’s Diner to innovative Italian cuisine at Gander and Ryegrass and a wide array of international options. Coffee culture is big here, with spots like Atticus Coffee & Gifts and Indaba Coffee Roasters. The craft beverage scene is equally impressive, with a booming brewery culture. There are over 35 breweries along the Spokane Ale Trail, like No-Li Brewhouse and Iron Goat Brewing. Washington’s wine is top-notch, and Spokane has plenty of wineries and tasting rooms. Plus, distilleries like Dry Fly add to the variety. There’s something for everyone to eat and drink. What is one of the best places to eat in Spokane, WA? Baba Spokane is an innovative Mediterranean restaurant with a reputation for great entrees and even better cocktails. It has a minimalist, modern vibe, serves vegan dishes and has a wine club. What is the best coffee shop in Spokane, WA? In a city of great coffee shops, it’s difficult to go wrong, but 1st Avenue Coffee stands out with its massive coffee bar, pour-over options, locally roasted beans, house-made syrups and in-house gluten-free bakery. Specialty lattes change seasonally but are always well-balanced and delicious. Where is the best happy hour in Spokane, WA? Grab a specialty cocktail like huckleberry lemon drop or a familiar favorite, like the Osprey old-fashioned from Osprey.
sections.education: Spokane students have options. The Spokane Public Schools district is the largest in town, serving nearly 30,000 K-12 students, with a strong focus on STEM education. The area has many private schools, ranging from religiously affiliated to independent institutions. WA-vancouver.description: Located along the Columbia River, Vancouver perfectly blends natural beauty and urban amenities. With a friendly community, strong job market and excellent schools, not to mention a low cost of living and plenty of outdoor activities, Vancouver residents can appreciate a high quality of life.

sections.location: Vancouver is a vibrant city in southwestern Washington, situated on the Columbia River’s north bank — across from Portland — right along Interstate 5. Portland International Airport is about 10 miles away, a busy spot for flights near and far. When you’re set to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Portland, Oregon, is just 10 miles south, where you can enjoy the Rose City’s arts scene, craft breweries and Powell’s City of Books. Seattle sits 165 miles north, offering the Space Needle, Museum of Pop Culture and Pike Place Market. Tacoma is about 140 miles north, tempting with the Museum of Glass, Point Defiance Zoo and Ruston Way Waterfront.
sections.economy: Manufacturing, health care and technology are three of the area's largest industries. Small businesses make up nearly 95% of the city’s economy. A few major employers include health care giants like PeaceHealth and Banfield Pet Hospital, Evergreen Public Schools and startups like RS Medical. Nearby Portland offers even more opportunities for anyone interested in commuting to work for name brands like Nike and Columbia Sportswear. The cost of living in Vancouver is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Vancouver is $483,547, and the average household income is $112,845. Washington residents do not pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect an 8.7% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Vancouver, WA? PeaceHealth Southwest Medical Center is the largest hospital in the city and is a high performer in 9 procedures and conditions. Legacy Salmon Creek Medical Center is also a high-performing facility in the area, and residents have access to even more health care providers and options in nearby Portland.
sections.thingsToDo: Variety is the spice of life in Vancouver, blending history and outdoor recreation into the day-to-day lifestyles of residents. History enthusiasts can recount the Lewis & Clark expedition to establish Fort Vancouver through museums and historical sites. There are also plenty of opportunities for artists to appreciate local talent, with galleries and performance venues. Family-friendly is the name of the game, too; there are tons of attractions like the Vancouver Community Library’s children’s discovery zone and the scenic waterfront, which offers shopping, dining and water activities. Downtown Vancouver is vibrant and full of unique shops, galleries and eateries, particularly in Uptown Village. The community-focused city holds several annual events and festivals, such as the Vancouver Farmers Market, Clark County Fair and Vancouver Wine & Jazz Festival. What is something you can do for free in Vancouver, WA? Grab your four-legged friend and spend the weekend exploring. In addition to several dog parks, Vancouver has waterfront walking trails and pet-friendly patios. At the end of the outdoor pool season, Lake Shore Athletic Club holds a “Doggie Dive,” allowing dogs to swim in the pool. What are some things to do with kids in Vancouver, WA? There’s always something going on at Esther Short Park, a charming 5-acre square that’s the oldest public park in the state and a place where kids can splash around in a water feature. Residents gather in the park for farmers markets, summer concerts and unique events. A 69-foot bell tower with 35 cast-bronze bells chimes throughout the day. Where do locals shop in Vancouver, WA? Larson’s Bakery is a great place to find a mouthwatering Danish pastry. Continue your foodie tour with a visit to the Vancouver Farmers Market, a favorite local spot. You can even get a Market Box subscription packed with fruits, veggies, recipes and prep ideas. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Vancouver, WA? The Kiggins Theatre stands out as a local favorite for all things art. Tucked in the heart of downtown Vancouver, this historic theater not only screens films but also hosts a variety of live events, such as concerts, comedy shows and theatrical performances.
sections.outdoors: Get out and about in Vancouver, which caters to all seasons and interests. Residents can explore tons of scenic trails and waterfalls or hike along the Columbia River Gorge and Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument. Vancouver is an outdoor oasis with over 90 parks, 20 natural areas and over 20 miles of trails for walking, running, cycling, rollerblading and more. Hit the water and enjoy kayaking, canoeing and paddleboarding on the Columbia River and Vancouver Lake Regional Park. The Waterfront Renaissance Trail offers scenic walks and rollerblading, and Esther Short Park offers family-friendly spaces for picnics and play. Beyond the parks are numerous golf courses and campgrounds, and Mount Hood is a great spot to hit the slopes.
sections.restaurants: The city has a rapidly growing culinary scene that shines. Unique spots like Bleu Door Bakery, known for its French-inspired pastries, and Larson’s Bakery, famous for its Danish pastries, are favorites. The local craft beer scene is booming, with over two dozen breweries and taprooms. The city is also part of Southwest Washington’s wine country. Fortside Brewing, Loowit Brewing and Heathen Brewing Feral Public House add to the city’s dynamic food and drink culture. Looking for something alcohol-free? Coffee is a culture here, and locals love spots like Kafiex Roasters, Compass Coffee and Relevant Coffee. What is one of the best places to eat in Vancouver, WA? Check out The Smokin’ Oak Barbecue Restaurant on Columbia Street for a laid-back and fun-filled atmosphere. Texas-style barbecue and a swath of satisfying sides make this well worth a trip — especially when you need a place to meet up with friends at the end of the work week. What is the best coffee shop in Vancouver, WA? Like its big-city neighbors, Seattle and Portland, ‘The Couve’ boasts a remarkable coffee scene. To keep your energy up all day, we recommend trying a Mexican Mocha from Kafiex Roasters. Where is the best happy hour in Vancouver, WA? Happy hour at Gustav’s is a local favorite. The German spot serves fresh, seasonal and high-quality cuisine, and its happy hour menu has a collection of small bites, wines and cocktails on tap. 5 Delicious Reasons to Eat and Drink in Vancouver, WA, Right Now
sections.education: Vancouver residents are well-educated, and families can appreciate the many choices regarding early childhood education. Three public school districts serve the area: Vancouver Public Schools, Evergreen Public Schools and Washougal School District. Though performance varies from school to school and district to district, a ton of variety is available, including specialized programs in STEM and dual language. Residents can also choose between charter school and private school options. The Gardner School of Arts and Sciences is a private school focusing on arts and science education, offering students a well-rounded curriculum. faqs.q: Who are the major health care providers in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: PeaceHealth Southwest Medical Center is the largest hospital in the city and is a high performer in 9 procedures and conditions. Legacy Salmon Creek Medical Center is also a high-performing facility in the area, and residents have access to even more health care providers and options in nearby Portland.

faqs.q: What is something you can do for free in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: Grab your four-legged friend and spend the weekend exploring. In addition to several dog parks, Vancouver has waterfront walking trails and pet-friendly patios. At the end of the outdoor pool season, Lake Shore Athletic Club holds a “Doggie Dive,” allowing dogs to swim in the pool.

faqs.q: What are some things to do with kids in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: There’s always something going on at Esther Short Park, a charming 5-acre square that’s the oldest public park in the state and a place where kids can splash around in a water feature. Residents gather in the park for farmers markets, summer concerts and unique events. A 69-foot bell tower with 35 cast-bronze bells chimes throughout the day.

faqs.q: Where do locals shop in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: Larson’s Bakery is a great place to find a mouthwatering Danish pastry. Continue your foodie tour with a visit to the Vancouver Farmers Market, a favorite local spot. You can even get a Market Box subscription packed with fruits, veggies, recipes and prep ideas.

faqs.q: Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: The Kiggins Theatre stands out as a local favorite for all things art. Tucked in the heart of downtown Vancouver, this historic theater not only screens films but also hosts a variety of live events, such as concerts, comedy shows and theatrical performances.

faqs.q: What is one of the best places to eat in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: Check out The Smokin’ Oak Barbecue Restaurant on Columbia Street for a laid-back and fun-filled atmosphere. Texas-style barbecue and a swath of satisfying sides make this well worth a trip — especially when you need a place to meet up with friends at the end of the work week.

faqs.q: What is the best coffee shop in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: Like its big-city neighbors, Seattle and Portland, ‘The Couve’ boasts a remarkable coffee scene. To keep your energy up all day, we recommend trying a Mexican Mocha from Kafiex Roasters.

faqs.q: Where is the best happy hour in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: Happy hour at Gustav’s is a local favorite. The German spot serves fresh, seasonal and high-quality cuisine, and its happy hour menu has a collection of small bites, wines and cocktails on tap.

faqs.q: What colleges and universities are in Vancouver, WA?
faqs.a: Several colleges and universities are located here, including Clark College, Washington State University and Charter College.

WI-appleton.description: Appleton's location in the picturesque Fox River Valley epitomizes Midwestern charm. This Wisconsin city is welcoming and family-friendly, with a strong sense of community, highly ranked schools and a bustling downtown area that contributes to the area's robust economy.

sections.location: Appleton is a charming city in east-central Wisconsin along U.S. Route 41. It’s part of the Fox Cities region and known for being the childhood home of Harry Houdini. The nearest airport, Appleton International Airport, is just west of the city, making travel a breeze. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Green Bay is just 30 miles northeast, where you can visit Lambeau Field or the National Railroad Museum. Milwaukee sits 100 miles south, offering the Milwaukee Art Museum and the Harley-Davidson Museum. Madison is about 100 miles southwest, home to the Wisconsin State Capitol and the Henry Vilas Zoo.
sections.economy: Manufacturing, health care and retail are among the largest industries in Appleton. Some major employers include ThedaCare, the Appleton Area School District, Kimberly-Clark and Miller Electric, a welding equipment manufacturer headquartered in the city. Affordability is the name of the game here. The reasonably low housing costs and diversity of home choices make the area appealing for families and young professionals who want to purchase their first home. The cost of living in Appleton is lower than the national average. The median home value in Appleton is $220,113, and the average household income is $110,357. Wisconsin residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 5.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Appleton, WI? Local residents are in good health, and top providers include ThedaCare Regional Medical Center-Appleton and the Children’s Hospital of Wisconsin-Fox Valley in nearby Neenah. 5 Reasons to Build Your Career in Fox Cities, WIWhy Do Business in Northeast Wisconsin? Ask These Owners5 Reasons Your Business Should Call Fox Cities, WI, Home
sections.thingsToDo: Amenities abound in Appleton, and residents have access to ample activities that range from family-friendly fun to creative pursuits. The arts scene is dynamic, with spots like the Fox Cities Performing Arts Center, Trout Museum of Art and Hearthstone Historic House Museum. Residents come together at events like Octoberfest and the Mile of Music Festival, where roughly 200 musicians rock out at 40 venues during the multiday summer festival, and the performances are free. Families can have fun at spots like the Building for Kids Children’s Museum — a hands-on museum with interactive exhibits like a fire truck — and the Fox River Mall. Beyond family-friendly fun and cultural offerings, residents can appreciate outdoor events. Visit the open-skate hours at Community First Champion Center to burn off some energy, or enjoy an outing to a Timber Rattlers game. The Badger Sports Park is a must-visit, with 5.5 acres of indoor and outdoor all-season fun, including a go-kart track, an 18-hole mini-golf course, batting cages, mini-bowling alleys and a laser-tag arena. Entertainment venues such as the Fox Valley Symphony and Fox Cities Performing Arts Center — home to Broadway Across America and Boldt Arts Alive! Series — and the lively atmosphere of downtown ensure there’s always something to do. What is something you can do for free in Appleton, WI? Volunteers at The Gordon Bubolz Nature Preserve have taught visitors about environmental ethics, appreciation of nature and conservation for 45 years. Visitors not attending one of the several education programs can walk, hike, bike or cross-country ski through the 775-acre preserve. What are some things to do with kids in Appleton, WI? The Building for Kids Children’s Museum has all kinds of interactive exhibits, including a kids’ town with a grocery store, doll hospital and semi-truck depot. Where do locals shop in Appleton, WI? For more than 40 years, The Book Store, a funky, independently owned shop, has been supplying more than 80,000 new and used books to the bookworms of Wisconsin. During the summer, browse the stalls at the Appleton Downtown Farmers Market. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Appleton, WI? The Fox Cities Performing Arts Center is a significant venue in the area for anyone hoping to catch a Broadway show or concert in downtown Appleton. 10 Must-Do Family-Friendly Activities In Fox Cities, WIFun in the Fox Cities: Why Tourists Love This Wisconsin Region
sections.outdoors: Outdoor activities in Appleton are plentiful, from scenic trails and parks to water-based adventures and seasonal events. The city is home to more than three dozen parks, and residents can explore the 700-acre Bubolz Nature Preserve or hike the trails at High Cliff State Park. Hit the water at the Fox River for boating, kayaking and fishing, or visit Plamann Lake Water Park during the summer to enjoy their slides and pools. There are tons of parks, playgrounds and greenspaces to enjoy, including Lindenwood Park and Appleton Memorial Park, perfect for picnics and family outings. In the winter, snowshoe, cross-country ski or ice skate. The city is located near the Niagara Escarpment and Lake Winnebago, providing further opportunities for outdoor fun. A Season for Every Adventure: Exploring Year-Round Fun in Appleton, Wisconsin
sections.restaurants: Eat your way through Appleton, where restaurants blend classic Midwestern comforts and innovative culinary experiences. The city has a range of dining options, from traditional supper clubs to cozy brewpubs like the Appleton Beer Factory to globally inspired spots. Brunch is a highlight, with places like Fratello’s Riverfront Restaurant offering views of the Fox River. There is an emphasis on fresh, local ingredients in the city, and the food scene has something for everyone. Unique beverage experiences abound with Mondo! offering a curated wine selection and McFleshman’s Brewing Co.’s British ales. Cleo’s celebrates Christmas year-round, and its signature “dirty snowball” drink adds to the city’s quirky charm. Pet-friendly patios are also located throughout the city, including The Stone Yard. What is one of the best places to eat in Appleton, WI? Spats is a popular place to go to dinner. It is known for its diverse menu, which includes everything from steak and seafood to innovative cocktails. The casual, fun atmosphere makes Spats an excellent spot for dinner with family or friends. What is the best coffee shop in Appleton, WI? Copper Rock Coffee Company is a local favorite for coffee enthusiasts. It has a cozy atmosphere, an expansive coffee menu, tasty pastries, a solid collection of board games and live music. Where is the best place for happy hour in Appleton, WI? Grab a cocktail or a glass of wine at Cleos. This festive watering hole is known for its daily drink deals and creative cocktails — like a jellybean martini! A Foodie’s Guide to the Northeast Wisconsin Dining Scene
sections.education: Appleton is known for having a tight-knit community and family-friendly amenities. The city’s top-rated school system appeals to families seeking a place to put down roots. The Appleton Area School District (AASD) serves roughly 15,000 students and excels in diversity and extracurriculars for students. AASD also has a large number of charter schools, many of which have a specific focus, like fine arts or technology. There are also many different private schools in town, ranging from independent schools to college prep to religiously affiliated. WI-green-bay.description: Home of the Green Bay Packers and cheese curds, need we say more? If the answer is yes, there's plenty to love about Green Bay, including excellent schools, a strong job market, waterfront views and a strong community spirit.

sections.location: Green Bay is a spirited city perched in northeastern Wisconsin, about 120 miles north of Milwaukee, where Interstate 43 and U.S. Route 41 meet. Green Bay Austin Straubel International Airport is 6 miles southwest, offering flights to Chicago, Minneapolis and beyond. When you’re ready to venture out, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Appleton sits just 30 miles southwest, boasting the Hearthstone Historic House, the Fox River Mall and the History Museum at the Castle. Milwaukee is 120 miles south, where you can stroll the Riverwalk, visit the Milwaukee Public Market or cheer on the Bucks. Madison lies 140 miles southwest, boasting the Wisconsin State Capitol, Lake Mendota and the Olbrich Botanical Gardens.
sections.economy: Life is good in Green Bay, thanks to ample opportunities to create a career. Some of the largest industries in Green Bay are manufacturing, health care and retail. Schneider National, Georgia-Pacific and the University of Wisconsin-Green Bay are all major employers. It should also come as no surprise that the Packers impact the economy, with the Green Bay Packers team being one of the city’s largest employers and significantly affecting employment, tourism and related activities. The cost of living in Green Bay is significantly lower than the national average. The median home value in Green Bay is $206,179, and the average household income is $83,328. Wisconsin residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 5.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Green Bay, WI? Several high-performing providers are located in the area, including Bellin Memorial Hospital and HSHS St. Vincent Hospital. Bellin is a large, locally-owned health care system known for its comprehensive services and Level II trauma center. HSHS St. Vincent offers specialized care in areas like cardiology and oncology. Why Do Business in Northeast Wisconsin? Ask These Owners
sections.thingsToDo: Get going in Green Bay! The city’s strong sense of community is evident from its numerous annual festivals and community celebrations. Celebrate local arts at Artstreet or the holidays at Prevea Green Bay Holiday Parade and Christkindlmarket. The arts are alive and well, thanks to spots like the Meyer Theatre, Weidner Center for the Performing Arts and Resch Center hosting Broadway shows, concerts and national acts. The Art Garage and the Green Bay Film Festival provide platforms for local and national artists, while events like the Festival of Cultures celebrate the city’s diverse communities. Check out the summer music series at parks during the warmer months, and CityDeck offers laid-back family fun. Speaking of family-friendly, this Wisconsin city is full of things for kids of all ages, including the historic Zippin Pippin roller coaster at Bay Beach Amusement Park and numerous museums and parks. Shop ’til you drop at the numerous boutique shops in downtown Green Bay. The city’s farmers markets, like the Farmers Market on Broadway and Saturday Farmers Market, offer fresh produce and local goods. What is something you can do for free in Green Bay, WI? The Waterboard Warriors Water Ski Show Team provides free shows twice a week on the Fox River during the summer. Have your camera ready because the water skiing pyramid is a fantastic trick! What are some things to do in Green Bay, WI, with kids? Take the kids to explore the New Zoo Adventure Park. You can even zipline, too! Where do locals shop in Green Bay, WI? The Attic is quite literally a one-stop shop. The storefront is a triple threat and houses a used book store, an espresso bar and a tea shop. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Green Bay, WI? The Meyer Theatre, located in downtown Green Bay, is a historic venue that hosts a variety of live performances. For something a little larger, Epic Event Center hosts live music and other events, like national and regional tours. 14 Fun Things To Do in Green Bay, WIGreater Green Bay, WI: Live Like a Local7 Reasons to Live in Green Bay, WI
sections.outdoors: Explore Green Bay’s great outdoors, with plenty of activities catering to warm and cold climates. During the warmer months, residents can enjoy watersports and fish on the Bay of Green Bay and Fox River and golf at various courses around town. Fisk and Tank parks offer splash pads for families, and the Fox River State Recreational Trail provides scenic routes for biking and walking. The Green Bay Botanical Garden and Bay Beach Wildlife Sanctuary offer beautiful natural spaces for exploration and relaxation. When the temperature drops, Green Bay residents can still explore the wintertime wonders of Wisconsin with cross-country skiing, snowshoeing and fat-tire biking. Ice fishing is a popular pastime, and the Titletown recreation complex offers ice skating and tubing. Year-round, residents can explore the Ice Age Trail for hiking and enjoy the natural beauty of the Niagara Escarpment at parks like Bay Shore Park and Fonferek’s Glen. The city’s numerous parks and trails — 69 parks and 15 greenways, to be exact! — provide ample space for residents to connect with nature. Find Year-Round Outdoor Activities in Greater Green Bay, WIEnjoy 4 Seasons of Outdoor Fun in Northeast Wisconsin
sections.restaurants: Green Bay has plenty of restaurants, whether you’re looking for a casual bite or fine dining. Foodies can enjoy Midwestern comforts and international flavors. Locals love supper clubs — a Wisconsin tradition — and favorites are Club Chalet and Kropp’s. There are several different ethnic cuisines across town, and favorites are Angelina Restaurant, Los Banditos and Taste of India. Green Bay’s beer scene is a real highlight, with breweries like Badger State Brewing, Titletown Brewing and Stillmank Brewing offering a range of award-winning brews. What is one of the best places to eat in Green Bay, WI? Los Banditos has been a Greater Green Bay favorite for over three decades. The kitchen turns out a weekly taco special with rotating flavors and a killer chile con queso appetizer. What is the best coffee shop in Green Bay, WI? Grounded Coffee has delicious drinks and low prices, and they also work with the Aging and Disability Resource Center to give people job training and life skills. Where is the best happy hour in Green Bay, WI? Enjoy a beer and some pinball at Titletown Brewing Co. Eat Your Way Through Greater Green Bay, WI’s Restaurant SceneA Foodie’s Guide to the Northeast Wisconsin Dining Scene
sections.education: Local families and potential transplants appreciate the quality of the Green Bay Area Public School District (GBAPS). Most students here attend schools within the system, with an enrollment of nearly 19,000 students. The district offers specialized programs, specialty schools and charter schools. For private education, there are several highly rated schools in the city, many of which are religiously affiliated. WI-madison.description: Madison is a natural escape, nestled between two lakes and dotted with more than 200 hiking and biking trails. School spirit runs deep here, and the college town is known for its vibrant arts and music scene, farm-to-table culinary experiences and commitment to sustainability.

sections.location: Madison is located in south-central Wisconsin along Interstate 90 and Interstate 94. It’s known for its beautiful lakes and the University of Wisconsin-Madison. Dane County Regional Airport is right in the city, making travel a snap. When you’re ready to explore, here’s how close you are to some major nearby cities: Milwaukee is just 80 miles east, where you can visit the Milwaukee Public Museum or the Pabst Mansion. Chicago, IL, sits 145 miles southeast, offering the Art Institute of Chicago and Navy Pier. Minneapolis, MN, is about 270 miles northwest, home to the Minneapolis Sculpture Garden and the Guthrie Theater.
sections.economy: Those hoping to put down roots somewhere they can build a career or support a family will be pleased with Madison’s diverse and robust local economy, which comprises several industries, including technology, advanced manufacturing and health care. University of Wisconsin–Madison, Epic Systems and American Family Insurance are among the largest employers in the city. Because the city is also business-friendly, several startups, tech companies and entrepreneurs have moved into the community. The cost of living in Madison is slightly higher than the national average. The median home value in Madison is $361,084, and the average household income is $112,542. Wisconsin residents pay a state income tax, and local residents can expect a 5.5% sales tax rate in 2025. Who are the major health care providers in Madison, WI? Local residents are in excellent health thanks to various providers, including UW Health and UnityPoint Health. UW Health University Hospital is nationally ranked in four adult and one pediatric specialty, rated high performing in five adult specialties and rated high performing in 18 procedures and conditions. Why I Own a Business in the Madison Region (and You Should, Too!)Minority Entrepreneurs Thrive in the Madison, WI, RegionYour Health is in Good Hands in the Madison Region
sections.thingsToDo: Welcome to Madison, a lively city with culture, family fun and plenty of arts and entertainment. The city’s cultural landscape is enriched by the University of Wisconsin-Madison, contributing to a dynamic atmosphere. Residents can explore renowned institutions like the Chazen Museum of Art and the Overture Center for the Arts or enjoy live performances at the Orpheum Theater. State Street Pedestrian Mall is a dining and shopping hub right by campus. Henry Vilas Zoo and Madison’s interactive museums, such as the Madison Children’s Museum, offer engaging experiences for all ages. A pride and joy of Madison is the Dane County Farmers’ Market, which brings the community together on “the Square” to shop for local goods. Arts and film are important here, evidenced by events like the Wisconsin Film Festival and Cinematheque at UW-Madison. Historical exploration is available through walking and biking tours offered by the Madison Trust for Historic Preservation, as well as museums like the Wisconsin State Historical Museum. Live music events like Concerts on the Square and Jazz at Five make the city even more fun. What is something you can do for free in Madison, WI? A spirit of volunteerism keeps many local amenities free and open to the public. For example, spend time perusing the Chazen Museum of Art, a free museum on the UW campus that houses 23,000 works of art from ancient Greece to 18th-century Japan and modern Africa. The local list of free things to do is extensive! What are some things to do in Madison, WI, with kids? Make repeat visits to the Madison Children’s Museum, which has a kid-approved playground and engaging exhibits, like the Urb Garden, where children can learn how worms turn food waste into compost. Where do locals shop in Madison, WI? State Street is a bustling pedestrian mall stretching from Capitol Square to the University of Wisconsin-Madison campus. A Room of One’s Own is a queer- and trans-owned bookstore that’s been a local staple since 1975. Where is the best place for live music or performing arts in Madison, WI? The Majestic Theatre on King Street near Capitol Square is one of Madison’s best places for live music and performing arts. This historic venue, which opened in 1906, seats 600 and has a reputation for hosting a diverse range of events throughout the year. 10 Things You Must Do in Madison, WIMore to do in the Madison Region: Fun for the Whole Family6 Arts and Entertainment Attractions Near Madison, Wisconsin
sections.outdoors: Situated on an isthmus between two lakes, it should come as no surprise that Madison is an outdoor lover’s dream, with tons of parks, trails and, of course, water. There are 260 parks, including Tenney Park, Elver Park and the beautiful Olbrich Botanical Gardens. Over 200 miles of trails are perfect for hiking, biking and snowshoeing. Lakes Mendota and Monona are great for paddling in summer and snowshoeing in winter, and Picnic Point is a scenic spot for walks and runs. Beyond the city limits, nearby attractions like Devil’s Lake State Park and Blue Mound State Park offer hiking, rock climbing and camping. Madison’s bike-friendly infrastructure, recognized as a platinum-level bike community nationally, encourages residents to explore the city on two wheels. Trails like the Badger State Trail and the Ice Age Trail provide longer routes for hiking and biking, while the UW-Madison Arboretum offers a chance to discover local flora and fauna. Madison makes it easy to enjoy the outdoors. 5 Ways to Enjoy the Outdoors Near Madison, WisconsinMadison, WI, Region Is an Outdoor Wonderland
sections.restaurants: The lively food scene is fully focused on farm-to-table and locally sourced ingredients. Being surrounded by farms means the farmers markets are great, especially the Dane County Farmers’ Market. Places like Graze and A Pig in a Fur Coat have menus that change with the seasons, and The Old Fashioned Tavern & Restaurant has classic Wisconsin dishes. The city’s commitment to fresh, regional produce extends to numerous dining options, including those with on-site herb gardens and a strong emphasis on farm-fresh eats. You can find all kinds of food here, from sushi and grass-fed burgers to vegan and vegetarian options, many with lake views. Brunch is a big deal, with creative takes on breakfast classics. The craft drink scene is booming, with breweries like Ale Asylum and Capital Brewing, distilleries like Old Sugar and wineries like Botham offering tours. You can also find unique spots like Fromagination for cheese and food tours to try local favorites. What is one of the best places to eat in Madison, WI? One favorite dinner spot is The Old Fashioned near Capitol Square. This popular restaurant celebrates Wisconsin’s supper club tradition with a menu featuring classic comfort foods, local specialties and an extensive selection of Wisconsin beers. What is the best coffee shop in Madison, WI? Broken Board — located inside a board shop — is known for its attention to detail and delicious craft coffee. Where is the best happy hour in Madison, WI? Slurp $2 oysters and enjoy other seafood snacks at Tempest Oyster Bar. The best table in the house is a booth that looks like it’s part of a fan shell. Cento Ristorante serves classic Italian cocktails for $5 a pop. Craft Breweries Find Success in the Madison, WI, Region
sections.education: The University of Wisconsin-Madison anchors the city's vibrant cultural scene, and that love of learning extends into the city’s schools. The Madison Metropolitan School District (MMSD) serves the city, with over 25,000 students across numerous elementary, middle and high schools. With a highly favorable 13:1 student-to-teacher ratio, local schools also have a reputation for being diverse and having great programs for athletes. The city has dozens of private schooling options, including Madison Country Day School, which was recognized as the best private school in Wisconsin this year.
